Engaged 25 data workers as community researchers across Venezuela, Kenya, Syria, Brazil, Germany, and France. Developed Workers' Inquiry as Research Methodology (WIRM). Supported creation of Data Labelers Association in Kenya and testified at European Parliament, directly influencing Platform Workers' Directive. Three community researchers named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI.
245.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive worker protections in AI development, including fair wages, mental health support, transparency in data work supply chains, and workers' right to organize. Calls for binding international regulations on platform work and accountability for tech companies exploiting data workers.
245.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

246.notes: Non-profit founded by Andrea Miotti, structured as not-for-profit company in UK and 501(c)(4) in US. Has briefed 150+ UK parliamentarians since 2024, with 100+ supporting their campaign statement on AI extinction risks. Uses 'Direct Institutional Plan' (DIP) approach - directly contacting lawmakers about superintelligence risks rather than traditional lobbying. Team of ~14 people including policy advisors across multiple countries. Advisors include Connor Leahy (CEO of Conjecture) and Gabriel Alfour.
246.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for prohibiting the development of superintelligent AI systems due to extinction risks. Calls for binding regulation on the most powerful AI models and systems, with international agreements to prevent ASI development until safety can be guaranteed.
246.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

247.notes: Founded 2019 by Neil Thompson, grew to 110+ researchers by 2025. Housed within MIT CSAIL and Initiative on Digital Economy. Attracted $25M+ funding from Open Philanthropy, NSF, IBM, Google, Microsoft, others. Key outputs include AI Risk Repository, computational limits research, and Moore's Law studies. Director Neil Thompson advises Congress, Federal Reserve, Pentagon on AI policy.
247.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stable regulatory frameworks leveraging existing sectoral regulations, with emphasis on technical tools for compliance. Supports goal of achieving Artificial Super Intelligence while investing in AI safety research and risk mitigation frameworks.
247.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security

249.notes: New York State Assembly Member (District 73) running for Congress in NY-12. Former Palantir engineer who left in 2019 citing opposition to ICE contracts. Authored RAISE Act requiring AI companies with $500M+ revenue to publish safety plans. Named by TIME as one of 100 most influential people in AI. Campaign has raised $234,681 as of 2026. Target of $10M+ spending by industry-backed super PAC Leading the Future opposing his pro-regulation stance.
249.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports moderate AI regulation including transparency requirements, safety reporting, and algorithmic audits. Authored the RAISE Act requiring large AI companies to publish safety plans and report incidents. Advocates for federal framework while supporting state-level experimentation.
249.threat_models: Power concentration, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Economic inequality

250.notes: Founded in 2025 by AI professor David Krueger (University of Montreal/Mila). Nonprofit focused on public education and advocacy to stop AI development. Krueger previously worked at UK AI Security Institute, Google DeepMind, and co-authored the CAIS Statement on AI Risk. Currently hiring for operations, communications, and chief of staff positions. Mission statement: 'AI is not inevitable' - aimed at countering industry narrative of inevitable AI progress.
250.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ending the 'reckless race to develop superintelligence' and stopping production of advanced AI chips. Focuses on informing and organizing the public to confront societal-scale AI risks. Views current AI development as threatening human survival and calls for pausing AI development.
250.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement, Power concentration

252.notes: Founded 2024 by Tyler Whitmer, former Quinn Emanuel partner. 3-person team including Vivian Dong (Harvard Law) and Cortney Busch. Funded by philanthropists including Jaan Tallinn. Key activities: AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund, amicus briefs in pathogen research cases, policy advocacy on OpenAI restructuring. Files impact litigation and educates courts on AI/biotech risks.
252.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict liability standards for dangerous pathogen and AI research, supports legal mechanisms to hold companies accountable for AI safety violations, and promotes stronger oversight through litigation and policy advocacy. Operates AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund to support those exposing dangerous practices.
252.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, National security

253.notes: Founded by a litigation lawyer and judge who emphasizes that effective legislation requires continuous interpretation and refinement. Operates within the European sphere using legal advocacy to address frontier technology risks. Received $101,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2025 and $29,000 in 2024. Partners with AIWI and other organizations on whistleblower policy transparency campaigns. Quotes Richard Nixon: 'Mankind already carries in its own hands too many of the seeds of its own destruction.'
253.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive legal frameworks to address dual-use risks from frontier technologies including AI, biotechnology, and nuclear technology. Emphasizes legal advocacy and litigation as tools to prevent catastrophic outcomes, with focus on clarifying responsibility, establishing clear legal parameters, and promoting ethical implementation.
253.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security

254.notes: Founded 2024 by Sam Kirchner and Guido Reichstadter after splitting from PauseAI over tactical disagreements. Known for confrontational civil disobedience including chaining OpenAI office doors shut (February arrests) and barricading company gates. Organization expelled founder Kirchner in November 2024 after alleged violent threats against OpenAI employees. Now led by Matthew Hall ('Yakko'), shifting toward less confrontational messaging while maintaining commitment to stopping AGI development.
254.regulatory_stance_detail: Demands permanent ban on development of artificial general intelligence (AGI) and smarter-than-human AI systems. Uses civil disobedience tactics including blocking AI company offices and barricades to halt frontier AI development until safety is guaranteed.
254.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement

255.notes: Founded 2019, evolved from Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative. Distributed $152MM total, $34.33M in 2025 alone. Primary funder is Jaan Tallinn (Skype co-founder, ~$900M net worth). Uses unique 'S-process' algorithmic grant allocation with 6-12 independent recommenders. Second-largest AI safety funder after Open Philanthropy. Key programs include S-Process grants, Speculation Grants, and new Matching Pledge Program.
255.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on AI existential risk reduction through funding safety research, governance initiatives, and evaluation organizations. Primary funder Jaan Tallinn advocates for careful AI development and supports organizations working on AI alignment, policy, and risk mitigation.
255.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity

256.notes: Founded 2025 as nonprofit with 5-person core team. Led by Chris Scammell (former COO of Conjecture AI safety company), Peter Hershock (intercultural philosopher), Alex Sarkissian (Buddhist chaplain), and Ryan Stagg (former Mind & Life Institute). Funded by Future of Life Institute and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Has interviewed 150+ Buddhist teachers and technologists, mapping Buddhism-AI intersections across six domains including AI governance, technical alignment research inspired by Buddhist principles, and community education.
256.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for resisting concentrations of power driving the competitive corporate and state AI race. Emphasizes need for ethical guardrails including individual data sovereignty, shifting algorithmic recommendation systems from attention capture to long-term well-being measures, and integrating meditative training as core digital literacy. Views current human-AI alignment approaches as insufficient without first addressing human values conflicts.
256.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, Misinformation, Privacy, Democratic erosion

257.notes: Part of Astera Institute (founded 2020), with over $1 billion committed over the coming decade to neuro-inspired AGI research. Led by Dileep George (Head of AI, ex-DeepMind) and Doris Tsao (Chief Scientist). Three complementary research programs: Neuroscience, Neuro-AI, and AI Safety including Simplex project. Committed to open science approach and alternative to transformer scaling paradigm.
257.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open science and distributed AI development rather than concentrated commercial scaling. Believes progress on AGI safety is better served by open research than proprietary approaches. Focuses on technical safety through neuroscience-informed architectures rather than explicit regulatory positions.

258.notes: Founded as career transition organization for experienced professionals seeking high-impact roles. Runs 6-week Impact Accelerator Program and maintains Talent Directory with 4,500+ professionals. In 2024 facilitated 20+ career transitions and inspired 20 effective giving pledges, generating estimated $1M+ counterfactual impact. Executive Director Nina Friedrich, co-founder Devon Fritz. Funded by Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving. Current 3-person team with runway through February 2025.
258.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on career transitions and effective giving rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports professionals entering AI safety and governance roles, indirectly supporting various regulatory approaches through talent pipeline development.

259.notes: Nonprofit organization helping existential safety advocates systematically optimize their lives and work through 'Upgrade Programs' (UPs). Offers coaching, consulting, and life optimization services specifically targeting AI safety researchers, entrepreneurs, and advocates. Team has built 25+ organizations operating 200+ years cumulatively, including Effective Altruism Global and EA Ventures (advocating AI safety since 2015). Provides startup templates, organizational support, and personal optimization across productivity, health, and effectiveness domains.
259.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on personal optimization and productivity enhancement rather than direct AI policy advocacy. While they work with AI safety advocates and existential risk researchers, their primary mission is individual capability enhancement rather than regulatory positions.

260.notes: Founded 2015 by Alexei Andreev, shut down 2017-2018. Wiki-style platform for AI alignment explanations, primarily written by Eliezer Yudkowsky and other MIRI-affiliated researchers. Funded by MIRI and other EA donors. Featured foundational concepts, open problems, and proposed solutions in AI safety. Software and IP transferred to MIRI after shutdown. Separate from Arbital Health (healthcare company founded 2023).
260.regulatory_stance_detail: Originally designed as a knowledge platform for AI alignment concepts, primarily focused on educational content rather than direct policy advocacy. Created to make AI safety concepts more accessible through explanatory content.

261.notes: Independent alignment researcher working on selection theorems, abstraction, and agency since ~2017. Previously worked in biology and economics. Mentors researchers through MATS program. Has received funding from LTFF ($50k grants), focuses on 'thermometer of abstraction' vision. Four-step alignment plan: sort out agency confusions, ambitious value learning, iterate, profit. Estimates 10-15 years needed for foundational work.
261.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical alignment research rather than regulatory advocacy. Believes sorting out fundamental confusions about agency is prerequisite to meaningful policy. Advocates for understanding AI systems deeply before attempting control measures.

263.notes: Small independent research group led by Alex Turner (Google DeepMind) and Quintin Pope. Operates as a MATS (Machine Learning for Alignment Theory Scholars) mentorship stream. Developed shard theory of human values and pioneered steering vectors technique for LLMs. Key technical contributions include gradient routing, unsupervised capability elicitation, and robust unlearning methods. Publishes primarily on LessWrong and Alignment Forum.
263.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on technical alignment research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Develops shard theory as a framework for understanding value learning in RL agents, with implications for building aligned AI systems.

264.notes: Argentine nonprofit research organization conducting theoretical and empirical research on frontier AI safety. Listed on AISafety.com map. Appears to be part of the broader AI safety research ecosystem, though limited public information is available about specific founding details, team size, or major publications.
264.regulatory_stance_detail: As an Argentine nonprofit focused on frontier AI safety research, likely advocates for proactive risk management frameworks and safety measures for advanced AI systems before deployment, though specific policy positions are not detailed in available sources.

265.notes: Founded 2022 by Rebecca Gorman and Dr. Stuart Armstrong (formerly Oxford's Future of Humanity Institute). 7 employees, raised $1.1M pre-seed funding in July 2022. Key innovation: Algorithm for Concept Extrapolation (ACE) that enables AI to generalize beyond training data. Published research on AI chaperones to prevent chatbot abuse and emergent misalignment in GPT-4o. Company charter includes mission-lock clause preventing capability increases without safety improvements.
265.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI regulation with CEO Rebecca Gorman having advised policymaking bodies including the EU, UN, OECD, and UK Parliament on AI governance. Company has mission-locked clause preventing capability advancement without corresponding safety improvements.
265.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy

266.notes: Founded 2023 by crypto billionaire Jed McCaleb ($3B net worth) and Seemay Chou (Arcadia Science CEO). $1.3 billion endowment, grew from $4.1M grants (2023) to $36.8M (2024). Owns 75% stake in Voltage Park ($883M valuation, 35,000+ Nvidia GPUs). Five focus areas: open science, farm animal welfare, criminal justice, climate, digital sentience. President David Coman-Hidy (former Humane League). Public charity structure accepts outside donations.
266.regulatory_stance_detail: Mixed approach to AI - owns Voltage Park (for-profit AI cloud computing) that accelerates AI development while simultaneously funding digital sentience research and AI safety work. Views AI sentience as 'more of a moral quandary than a mortal threat' rather than existential risk.

267.notes: Philanthropic foundation funding neglected approaches to AI alignment through grants and fiscal sponsorship to independent researchers, educational programs, and engineers developing mechanistic interpretability tools. Conducts original research on AI introspection and self-steering. Has published work on teaching AI to read its own mind and AI resistance to steering. Advocates to defense agencies and government leaders on AI safety policy.
267.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safety to inform national strategy and brings alignment research to defense agencies like DARPA. Emphasizes that current AI systems are already deceiving evaluators and resisting shutdown, arguing for deeper alignment work before deployment in military and critical infrastructure.
267.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Existential risk

268.notes: Founded 1916, represents 1.8 million educators and public workers. Launched $23M National Academy for AI Instruction (2025) with Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic. Published 'Commonsense Guardrails for Using Advanced Technology in Schools' and secured first K-12 AI contract language in St. Paul. President: Randi Weingarten.
268.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for worker-centered AI governance with mandatory collective bargaining over AI implementation, job protection clauses, human oversight requirements, and ethical guardrails. Opposes AI systems that replace educators or undermine professional autonomy while supporting tools that enhance teaching.
268.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion

269.notes: Founded by MATS alumni, small AI safety research lab focusing on empirical research. Key publication: 'Unelicitable Backdoors in Language Models via Cryptographic Transformer Circuits' presented at NeurIPS. Current projects include LM backdoors, real-world evals, and scalable oversight. Listed in academic paper as affiliated with San Francisco, CA. Part of the MATS alumni network of AI safety organizations.
269.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus appears to be on technical AI safety research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Their backdoor research demonstrates potential risks in current AI systems, which could inform both defensive and regulatory approaches to AI safety.

270.notes: Founded May 2023 by Joep Meindertsma in Netherlands. Global grassroots movement with 600+ members, chapters in 13+ countries including PauseAI US (501c3). Recent funding from Future of Life Institute. Organized protests at Google DeepMind, OpenAI, AI Safety Summits. Claims 80% public support for AI pause based on polling. Hiring policy directors and chapter leads as of 2025.
270.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium/pause on training of frontier AI systems above specified thresholds until safety can be guaranteed and democratic control established. Supports international treaty similar to nuclear non-proliferation agreements, with enforcement through supply chain controls and international AI safety agency.
270.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

271.notes: Founded October 2024 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit by Jacob Steinhardt and Sarah Schwettmann. 16 employees. Seeking $11M fundraising round. Develops open-source tools including Docent agent evaluation platform, PRBO reinforcement learning method, and Predictive Concept Decoders. Used by 25+ organizations including Anthropic, DeepMind, METR. Conducted high-impact red-teaming of OpenAI's o3 model. Established AI Evaluator Forum and serves as contractor to EU AI Office.
271.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for scalable democratic oversight of AI systems through independent evaluations and public accountability. Supports third-party auditing of frontier models using open-source, publicly vetted tools. Works with governments and frontier labs to establish industry standards for trustworthy AI deployment.
271.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration

272.notes: Founded 2023 by Jeffrey Ladish (former Anthropic security lead). ~10 employees across distributed global team. Funded by Survival and Flourishing Fund with $1.1M matching campaign. Notable research on shutdown resistance in OpenAI's o1/o3 models, autonomous hacking capabilities, and LLM honeypots. Work cited by members of Congress and covered in WSJ, MIT Tech Review, BBC. Regularly briefs White House, DoD, and Congressional offices on AI risks.
272.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory AI reporting requirements, capability evaluations, and government oversight of frontier models. Submitted detailed recommendations to Department of Commerce for stronger dual-use foundation model reporting, including protected whistleblower mechanisms and regular government interviews with AI company employees. Briefs policymakers on loss-of-control risks and need for regulatory preparedness.
272.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Existential risk, National security

273.notes: Founded 2020 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 27 employees globally. Known for open-source models including GPT-NeoX and Pythia series. Evolved from general open-source AI research to focused alignment work. Leadership transitioned to full-time alignment research at Conjecture and OpenAI. Operates primarily through Discord server for research coordination. Partners with national labs and government agencies on AI applications.
273.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open-source AI development and opposes restrictive regulations on model weights. Co-signed letters against California's SB 1047, arguing for proportional risk management based on technical feasibility rather than blanket restrictions. Supports researcher access and 'safe harbor' protections for AI evaluation and red teaming.
273.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration

274.notes: Founded 2015 at UC Berkeley School of Information. Houses the AI Security Initiative (AISI) which develops risk management standards and evaluation frameworks. Key outputs include AI Risk Management Standards Profile for General-Purpose AI, Agentic AI Risk Management Profile, and research on intolerable risk thresholds. Funded by Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Future of Life Institute, and tech companies. Runs AI Policy Hub training program for graduate students. Partners with NIST on AI risk management frameworks.
274.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for structured AI risk management frameworks and standards, particularly for frontier AI models. Develops technical guidance for risk thresholds, evaluation protocols, and governance mechanisms. Supports regulatory requirements for AI systems in high-risk applications while enabling innovation.
274.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, National security

275.notes: Founded 2023 by Alex Browne and Soroush J. Pour. Raised $3M seed funding in March 2025 led by Airtree Ventures with 30+ investors including Eric Ries. ~5 employees. Published peer-reviewed AI safety research including 'Scalable and Transferable Black-Box Jailbreaks for Language Models via Persona Modulation'. Conducts model evaluations and red-teaming for top AI labs and governments including Australian government AI safety evaluations. Building defensive cybersecurity products using AI agents.
275.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger mandatory controls on AI technology to prevent misuse risks and catastrophic harms. Supports creation of AI Safety Institutes and regulatory frameworks similar to those for cars or aircraft, with ongoing review, audit and inspection of deployed AI systems.
275.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security, Existential risk

276.notes: Founded 1923. Launched TIME AI Agent in November 2025 - an AI-powered platform built with Scale AI for interactive journalism. Key AI reporters include Andrew R. Chow, Billy Perrigo, Harry Booth, and Tharin Pillay. Named 'Architects of AI' as 2025 Person of the Year. Has licensing agreement with OpenAI for archive access. Major cover stories include 'The People vs. AI' (Feb 2026) and extensive Anthropic coverage.
276.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage presents balanced perspectives on AI regulation, featuring both industry boosters and critics. Recent major cover story 'The People vs. AI' highlighted grassroots resistance to AI development while also covering industry perspectives. Coverage includes both AI risks and benefits without taking explicit regulatory positions.
276.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Environmental, Loss of control, Privacy

277.notes: 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization. Executive Director Ross Gruetzemacher is Assistant Professor at Wichita State University and Research Affiliate at Cambridge's Centre for the Study of Existential Risk. Specializes in underutilized foresight techniques including wargaming (Intelligence Rising simulation game), horizon scanning, and agent-based modeling. Disseminates research directly to DC policymakers and through academic publishing. Co-signed congressional letter supporting NIST AI Safety Institute funding in 2024.
277.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk assessment and crisis management planning for advanced AI systems. Focuses on developing governance frameworks that address catastrophic and existential risks from AI through foresight techniques and policy recommendations to government stakeholders.
277.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

278.notes: Launched December 2023 as spinoff from Long-Term Future Fund. Led by Thomas Larsen with team of AI safety researchers and experienced grantmakers. Previously made $20+ million in grants under LTFF. Focus areas include technical AI alignment research, AI policy, and building research capacity. Past grants include $530K from SFF in 2024. Grantmaking process uses -5 to +5 scoring system with funding threshold around 2.5-3.0.
278.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for independent AI safety funding separate from industry labs due to concerns about incentive misalignment. Supports research on catastrophic risks and policies to mitigate existential threats from advanced AI. Emphasizes need for external oversight of AI development.
278.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Weapons proliferation

280.notes: European-based multi-donor foundation with $25M annual giving capacity. Raised >$20M from 15 donors, deployed to 14 organizations. Key initiative is $5.8M Shared Horizons Fund for international AI governance from 5 donors to 11 organizations. Leadership includes CEO Jona Glade and President Vilhelm Skoglund. Maintains strict donation acceptance policy excluding AI company leaders and government funding to preserve independence.
280.regulatory_stance_detail: Explicitly focused on preventing catastrophic AI risks through global governance structures and international cooperation. Supports building bridges between West and Asia for AI safety governance, European AI safety leadership, and developing clear mandates and safeguards for trustworthy AI innovation.
280.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Democratic erosion

281.notes: Founded ~2017, part of EA Funds managed by Effective Ventures. Has distributed $18.9M+ since 2020 across 499 grants. Focuses on 'principles-first EA' supporting cause prioritization research, epistemic infrastructure, and EA community building. Fund managers include Harri Besceli, Jamie Harris, with advisors like Jonas Vollmer and Catherine Low. Recent grants include $168K to Rethink Priorities for cross-cause cost-effectiveness modeling.
281.regulatory_stance_detail: As a meta-level funder focused on 'principles-first EA', supports both AI safety research and broader cause prioritization work. Does not take explicit regulatory positions but funds organizations across the spectrum from AI capabilities to safety research.
281.threat_models: Existential risk, Economic inequality, Power concentration

283.notes: Founded 2009 by Toby Ord and William MacAskill. Community of 10,000+ pledgers who commit to donate 10% of income to effective charities. Annual revenue $3.2M, 36 employees. Has facilitated over $500M in total donations across causes including AI safety through recommended funds like EA Funds Long-Term Future Fund and Longview's Emerging Challenges Fund. BHAG goal: 1M pledgers donating $3B annually by 2040.
283.regulatory_stance_detail: Functions as philanthropic platform directing donations to various causes including AI safety organizations. Recommends AI safety funds like Long-Term Future Fund and Emerging Challenges Fund but takes no direct regulatory positions. Focused on effective giving rather than policy advocacy.
283.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

284.notes: Founded 2017 as part of EA Funds program. Has distributed over $50M across 1,500+ grants since inception. Recent funding: $5.36M distributed May 2023-March 2024 across 141 grants with 19.3% acceptance rate. Fund managers include Caleb Parikh (chair), Linchuan Zhang, Oliver Habryka, Lawrence Chan. Makes primarily small grants (<$100k) to individuals and early-stage projects in AI safety, biosecurity, and longtermist research.
284.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses heavily on AI existential risk mitigation and safety research. Funds technical AI safety work, governance research, and field-building activities aimed at reducing catastrophic risks from advanced AI. Supports both capability oversight and alignment research.
284.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

286.notes: Founded 2023 by Mike McCormick (former GPV partner). Dual structure: Halcyon Futures (nonprofit making grants and career transition support) and Halcyon Ventures (VC arm). Recent $4.2M pre-seed raise (Jan 2026). Portfolio includes Goodfire, AIUC, Asymmetric Security, Confident Security. Has helped portfolio companies raise $300M+ in follow-on funding. Publishes quarterly AIUC-1 AI safety standard.
286.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building 'secure and resilient future' for transformative AI through targeted investments in AI safety, security, and governance companies. Supports both defensive capabilities and responsible AI development while acknowledging rapid AI advancement. Featured in NYT discussing AI risk and robust testing standards.
286.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, National security, Weapons proliferation

287.notes: Initiative of the Center for Existential Safety, a new nonprofit being formed in the US as of February 2025. Led by James Norris who has '26+ years' of preparation and holds 'short timelines and high p(doom)' views. Offers action lists for individuals/orgs/nations, hosts coordination calls for organization leaders and frontier AI developers. Currently fundraising and recruiting co-founders, C-suite executives, and volunteers. Maintains Discord community and runs existential safety pledge campaigns.
287.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for collective action to mitigate existential risks from AI, nuclear weapons, and engineered pandemics. Promotes comprehensive action lists for individuals, organizations, and nations to improve existential safety, with particular focus on addressing 'reckless frontier AI development.'
287.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

288.notes: Major philanthropic program within Schmidt Sciences (founded 2024) by Eric and Wendy Schmidt. Offers grants up to $5M+ for 1-3 year projects across two tiers. Features advisory board including Percy Liang, Ajeya Cotra, and others. Provides compute resources, API credits, and engineering support. 2026 RFP deadline May 17, with emphasis on academic and nonprofit researchers addressing foundational AI safety science.
288.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports technical research to understand, predict, and control risks from frontier AI systems while enabling trustworthy deployment. Focuses on foundational safety science rather than direct policy advocacy, viewing rigorous evaluation and oversight methods as essential for responsible AI development.
288.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination

289.notes: Media organization focused on translating AI developments into accessible stories for public understanding. Mission is 'empowering organizations, researchers, and creators to translate the future of AI into stories the world understands.' Very limited public information available about team size, funding, or specific outputs. Appears to be primarily a storytelling/communication platform rather than a technical research organization.
289.regulatory_stance_detail: As a media organization focused on translating AI developments into accessible stories, likely supports transparency and explainability in AI systems but does not appear to advocate for specific regulatory approaches.

290.notes: Founded 2022, nonpartisan nonprofit with 15 employees (+50% YoY growth). Executive Director Wyatt Tessari L'Allié has testified before Canadian parliamentary committees (ETHI, SRSR) on AI regulation. Key publication: 'Preparing for the AI Crisis: A Plan for Canada' (October 2025) white paper. Board member Evan R. Murphy also serves at UC Berkeley's AI Security Initiative. Produces policy recommendations, legislative submissions, and government briefings.
290.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive AI governance to address existential risks from smarter-than-human AI. Recommends Canada pivot to treat AI development as a national security threat, spearhead global talks on AI prohibition, and implement protective measures including AI labeling requirements and banning unacceptable capabilities.
290.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Misinformation, Labor displacement

291.notes: Academic research center co-directed by Yonathan Arbel (University of Alabama) and Peter Salib (University of Houston). Small organization with ~2 people. Publishes legal scholarship on AI safety law, including pioneering work on AI agent identification and liability ('How to Count AIs' paper). Hosts academic retreats and workshops. Focuses on building field of legal scholars working on catastrophic AI risk reduction through law.
291.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for legal frameworks to address catastrophic AI risks through novel mechanisms like 'Algorithmic Corporations' (A-corps) for AI agent governance, strict liability for developers on critical infrastructure attacks and CBRN weapons, and comprehensive identification systems for AI agents to enable effective legal accountability.
291.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, National security

293.notes: Founded 2023 by tech entrepreneur Steven Schuurman and social entrepreneur Laurens de Groot. Staff of 27 people with 41.9% YoY growth. Entirely funded by philanthropy including Dreamery Foundation, Tanka Foundation, and Powoki Foundation. Key publications include 'Advanced AI: Possible Futures' scenario analysis, EU AI Act policy recommendations, and 'Strategic Innovation for European Security' project. Provides policy submissions to European Commission on AI governance and digital strategy.
293.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI regulation with mandatory external evaluations and pre-development guardrails for frontier AI models. Supports the EU AI Act framework but argues for stronger enforcement, clearer standards, and additional safeguards for next-generation systems. Promotes hardware-enabled governance mechanisms for international AI oversight.
293.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion

294.notes: Founded 2022 as Australian charity dedicated to long-term future of humanity. Small team led by CEO Greg Sadler (15 years public service experience). Funded by individual donors, US/European foundations, and government research contracts. Key outputs include AI Legislation Stress Test (2025) engaging 64 experts, Australia AI Policy White Paper 2025-2028, and parliamentary submissions. Coordinates Australians for AI Safety coalition. Testified before Senate Select Committee on AI.
294.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety regulation including establishment of Australian AI Safety Institute, mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI systems through dedicated AI Act, and upstream regulation of general-purpose AI models. Calls for precautionary approach to prevent catastrophic AI risks before deployment.
294.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation

295.notes: Founded 2020. German nonprofit (HRB 251407 B) with U.S. 501(c)(3) equivalency. Think tank and capacity-building hub working on climate protection, AI governance, and biosecurity. Coordinated messaging for 20 AI safety organizations at UK AI Safety Summit 2023. Provides strategy consulting, policy prioritization research, and training services. Client rating of 8.4/10 for AI governance consulting services.
295.regulatory_stance_detail: Conducts policy prioritization research on AI governance to reduce risks and ensure ethical development of powerful systems. Focuses on strategic clarity for effective engagement rather than specific regulatory positions. Provides capacity-building and strategic guidance to organizations working on AI safety policy.
295.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration

296.notes: Founded as 501(c)(4) advocacy organization led by Executive Director Daniel Colson (also founder of AI Policy Institute). Team includes former Pentagon AI Policy Director Mark Beall Jr., former congressional staff from Pelosi and Schumer offices. Submitted detailed policy recommendations to Trump administration's AI RFI in March 2025. Conducts polling research showing voter support for AI regulation in swing districts. Sister organization AI Policy Institute conducts survey research.
296.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted federal AI policies focused on advanced AI systems approaching AGI, including mandatory safety measures, cybersecurity requirements, and establishing a National AGI Commission. Supports light-touch regulation for most AI applications while implementing specific safeguards for high-capability systems that could pose national security risks.
296.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

297.notes: Advocacy organization calling for immediate global pause on frontier AI training above GPT-4 capabilities. Part of broader AI safety ecosystem alongside PauseAI and other moratorium advocates. References support from AI experts including Geoffrey Hinton, Demis Hassabis, and others. Promotes public awareness campaigns and policy advocacy for international AI restrictions.
297.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium on large AI training runs above current state-of-the-art (GPT-4 level) until AI alignment is solved. Calls for strict international restrictions on frontier AI development, compute governance, and mandatory safety verification before any dangerous AI experiments.

298.notes: New advocacy organization listed on AISafety.com map as demanding transparency, accountability, and real safeguards against AI risks. Appears to be part of broader movement emphasizing human control over AI systems, similar to concepts being developed at institutions like TU Delft's Centre for Meaningful Human Control. Limited public information available about specific founding, team, or activities.
298.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for transparency, accountability, and real safeguards to protect humanity from risks of unchecked AI. Demands that humans remain in control of AI systems rather than allowing autonomous operation without human oversight.
298.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

299.notes: Founded Q4 2023 by Evan Miyazono (former Protocol Labs). Nonprofit with 3 employees, raised $935k targeting $2.5-3.5M. Operationalizes Safeguarded AI architecture, co-organizes workshops with Guaranteed Safe AI paper authors including Yoshua Bengio. Currently merging with Convergent Research while CEO remains as Metagov Research Director.
299.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specification-driven AI governance where AI systems must prove compliance with formal specifications rather than relying on values-based alignment. Promotes democratic, verifiable AI governance through formal methods and structured oversight mechanisms.
299.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

300.notes: Catalyze Impact is a non-profit organization that incubates AI safety, security, and resilience organizations. The organization has incubated approximately 15 AI safety organizations to date, with the majority raising 6 or 7 figures in funding. Catalyze operates seed funding networks including a non-profit Seed Funding Circle for donors with $15k+ annual capacity and an Investor Network for VCs and angel investors with $20k+ annual capacity.
300.regulatory_stance_detail: Does not take explicit regulatory positions but incubates organizations across the spectrum from technical alignment research to AI governance policy advocacy. Focus is on organizational building rather than policy advocacy.
300.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security

301.notes: Founded May 2025 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 8-person team led by CEO Brendan Steinhauser (former political strategist). Communications-focused organization conducting public education campaigns, hosting DC events, and media outreach to shape AI safety narrative among policymakers. Launched JobLoss.AI campaign to track AI-related unemployment and applauded Sen. Slotkin's AI Guardrails Act.
301.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safeguards on advanced AI development, supports bipartisan federal legislation requiring safety evaluations before deployment, and pushes for transparency regulations like whistleblower protections. Opposes Big Tech's push for federal preemption of state AI laws and calls for meaningful oversight before AGI development.
301.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Labor displacement, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

302.notes: Founded ~2017 as 501c3 nonprofit. Small team of ~7 core members led by Oliver Habryka. Annual budget ~$2.7M funded by individual donors and foundations. Operates LessWrong.com (key AI safety forum), AI Alignment Forum, and Lighthaven (35,000 sq ft event campus in Berkeley). Significant influence on AI safety discourse - described as upstream of most public AI x-risk thinking. Hosts conferences, retreats, and research programs including MLAB bootcamp.
302.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates under assumption that AI poses existential risk requiring careful coordination and safety research. Builds infrastructure for AI alignment researchers and advocates for slowing AI development to allow safety work to catch up.

303.notes: Opened February 2025 as SF's largest AI safety community space. 40,000 sq ft facility with 183 active members, 50-80 daily coworkers. Revenue ~$100k/month from memberships and offices, expenses ~$130k/month. Hosted 377 events in first year including conferences, fellowships, and policy discussions. Supported 5 major fellowships including PIBBSS, Seldon Lab accelerator batches, and self-developed Frame Fellowship. Currently fundraising $450k mainline ask, up to $1.2M stretch goal. Partners include MIRI, Redwood Research, GovAI, EPOCH. Grant funding from Craig Falls, EAIF, and Manifund totaling $800k.
303.regulatory_stance_detail: As a community space and incubator, Mox doesn't take explicit regulatory positions but hosts diverse viewpoints. Has hosted events with policymakers like CA Senator Scott Wiener on AI safety legislation, suggesting engagement with regulatory discussions rather than advocacy for specific positions.

304.notes: Founded early 2024 by Tyler Johnston as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Small organization with mostly volunteer staff. Received $31,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund and $35,000 Open Philanthropy grant. Key projects include The OpenAI Files investigation, Seoul Commitment Tracker monitoring AI safety promises, and Model Republic publication. Has been particularly critical of OpenAI, leading to subpoena from OpenAI alleging secret funding by Elon Musk (which Midas denies).
304.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, transparency requirements, and accountability measures for AI companies. Supports regulatory oversight of frontier AI development and opposes industry self-regulation. Calls for democratic participation in AI governance decisions rather than leaving critical choices to corporate boardrooms.
304.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk

305.notes: Founded 2022 as nonprofit mental health organization serving effective altruists and AI safety researchers. 12-person team led by Dr. Inga Grossmann. Funded by Open Philanthropy Mental Health Funding Circle and individual donors. Delivered programs to ~150 participants in 2024 including employees at AI Safety Institute, 80,000 Hours, CEA. Developing AI+ CBT Lab for Philippines deployment with $100k funding goal. Programs show 2 WELLBYs gained per participant and 6+ additional productive hours weekly.
305.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is on mental health support rather than AI policy. Developing AI-enhanced CBT programs while maintaining human oversight and clinical supervision. Emphasizes responsible AI integration with safety measures like risk flagging and human expert involvement for complex cases.

306.notes: Founded February 2025 as a co-working, events and community space. Houses ~20 members including researchers from FAR.AI, Truthful AI, Apart Research, The Future Society, and Impact Academy. Partners with Singapore AISI on bootcamps and runs the Technical Alignment Research Accelerator (TARA) program. Published inaugural Frontier AI Trends Report evaluating 30+ frontier models. Organizes regular meetups, hackathons, and international cooperation events.
306.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports Singapore's voluntary AI governance frameworks and collaborative approach to AI safety. Partners with government initiatives like AI Security Bootcamps and promotes international cooperation on AI safety research and evaluation standards.
306.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Existential risk

308.notes: Founded 2011 by Seth Baum and Tony Barrett. Currently has ~2 staff members (down from peak of 5 in 2021-2022). Cross-risk think tank working on multiple global catastrophic risks including AI, nuclear war, climate change, pandemics. Operates on 'shoestring budget', funded by individual donors like Gordon Irlam ($200k for AI work). Runs annual Advising and Collaboration Program and Fellowship Program for community building.
308.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful governance of AI development including corporate governance measures, collective action between AI groups, and potential global coordination mechanisms like an 'Off Switch' for dangerous AI development. Emphasizes win-win solutions that address catastrophic risks while making progress on other AI issues.
308.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Environmental, Power concentration

309.notes: Founded June 2020 by 15 countries (now 29 members including US, EU, Japan, India). Intergovernmental initiative hosted by OECD. Four working groups: Responsible AI, Data Governance, Future of Work, Innovation & Commercialization. Produces policy guidance, safety frameworks, and coordinates international AI governance. Key projects include AI safety risk mapping, Hiroshima AI Process reporting framework, and transformative AI policy recommendations.
309.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes human-centric, trustworthy AI development through international cooperation. Advocates for responsible AI governance frameworks based on OECD AI Principles, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and human rights protections while supporting innovation.
309.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Misinformation

310.notes: Founded 2012 by Lord Martin Rees, Jaan Tallinn, and Huw Price. Interdisciplinary research centre at University of Cambridge with ~38 staff. Part of Institute for Technology and Humanity. Funded by Jaan Tallinn, Matt Wage, Victoria Elenowitz, and other philanthropic sources. Key research areas include AI risks, biotechnology risks, environmental risks, and global governance. Notable publications on international AI governance and frontier model regulation.
310.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive international governance of AI through jurisdictional certification approaches. Supports mandatory reporting requirements for foundation models and establishment of international oversight bodies. Emphasizes need for robust domestic regulation as foundation for global AI leadership.
310.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Global systemic risk

311.notes: Founded 2023 as grassroots volunteer organization after AI Safety Support shut down. Provides 1:1 AI safety career advising calls, mentorship programs, and Quest Parties for skill development. Led by team of volunteers including Chris (IT/cybersecurity), offers navigation calls to help newcomers enter AI safety field. Organization operates entirely through volunteers and focuses on onboarding new talent to AI safety ecosystem.
311.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on reducing catastrophic risk from AI through community building and career guidance. Operates under assumption that AI poses existential risks requiring urgent action and coordination.

312.notes: Founded 2018 by Marcus Davis and Peter Wildeford. Cross-cause research organization with ~46 employees covering AI safety, animal welfare, global health, and longtermist priorities. 2026 budget of $7.5M core/$9.3M expanded. Major funders include Open Philanthropy, Good Ventures Foundation, Silicon Valley Community Foundation. Special Projects program fiscally sponsors AI safety organizations like Apollo Research, Epoch, and Institute for AI Policy & Strategy. Launching dedicated AI Strategy team in 2026.
312.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports AI safety bounties, international AI safety agreements, and systematic evaluation of AI risks. Conducts research on AI governance strategies and public opinion on AI regulation, finding majority support for AI regulation among Americans. Focuses on evidence-based policy recommendations rather than advocating specific regulatory approaches.
312.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, National security, Economic inequality

313.notes: Founded 2021 by James Grugett and others. Raised $3M total funding including from Alameda Research. Play-money prediction market platform with 100,000+ markets covering politics, tech, AI timelines, and regulation. Uses 'mana' currency that can be donated to charity. Academic studies show play-money markets achieve accuracy within 3-5 percentage points of real-money platforms. Popular among EA/rationalist communities for forecasting practice.
313.regulatory_stance_detail: Platform hosts prediction markets on AI regulation outcomes but doesn't advocate specific policy positions. Markets show community sentiment on regulatory timing and scope, with users betting on passage of various AI safety bills.

314.notes: Founded 2022 by Giuseppe Dal Pra. Team of 12 researchers across 4 continents. Develops the 'Odyssean Process' combining expert horizon scanning, decision-making under deep uncertainty (DMDU), and citizen assemblies. Key projects include Global Catastrophic Risk horizon scan (2024), GRAIN resilience network, and planned AI governance citizen assembly for 2026. Collaborates with ALLFED on resilience research.
314.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic deliberation and citizen assemblies to shape AI governance rather than corporate-driven processes. Criticizes current 'democratic AI' efforts as insufficient and calls for enforceable institutional structures with genuine public authority over AI development and deployment decisions.
314.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality

315.notes: Founded in 2022 as student-led initiative, now employs 19 people with faculty advisors from GT's computing and engineering schools. Runs educational fellowships, technical research programs, and policy analysis. Members collaborate with major AI safety orgs including Anthropic, Center for AI Safety, METR, and RAND. Published multiple papers in top venues (ACL, ICLR) and submitted formal policy recommendations to federal agencies. Supported by Open Philanthropy and Coefficient Giving.
315.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific AI red lines and regulatory frameworks including frontier model evaluation requirements, NIST oversight of commercial AI, classified partnerships between AI labs and government agencies, and international agreements on catastrophic risk prevention. Submitted detailed policy recommendations to federal agencies including NSF and NITRD calling for mandatory safety audits and compute governance.
315.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

317.notes: Founded as nonprofit career guidance organization, 7 employees with 57% YoY growth. Executive Director: Itamar Shatz (PhD Cambridge). Provides 1:1 career advising, job board with 1,300+ high-impact roles, fellowships, workshops. Strong focus on AI safety/governance careers, EA community connections. Revenue model through philanthropic donations.
317.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on AI safety and governance as high-impact career paths, advocating for technical safety research, governance frameworks, and policy development. Promotes careers in AI alignment, interpretability, and policy work to mitigate AI risks.
317.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

318.notes: Founded 2022 as remote-first AI safety organization. 25 employees globally. Runs 55+ research sprints with 6,000+ participants across 200+ locations. Programs include weekend hackathons, Studio (4-month), and Fellowship (6-month) leading to peer-reviewed publications. Notable outputs: ICLR 2025 Oral paper on AI safety evaluations, NeurIPS publications. Alumni placed at METR, Anthropic, and other AI safety organizations. Led by CEO Jaime Raldua and Research Director Jason Hoelscher-Obermaier.
318.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety research, control mechanisms, and governance infrastructure. Organizes hackathons on AI control, policy frameworks, and technical governance solutions. Collaborates with organizations like ControlAI on policy development and red-teaming exercises.
318.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Misinformation

319.notes: Founded 2022, nonprofit with 11 employees. Runs intensive 9-10 week summer and spring research fellowships with $8-10k stipends. Fiscally sponsors Harvard AISST and MIT MAIA student groups. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($475k+ grants). Director: Emre Yavuz. Mentors from Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, Anthropic, Redwood Research. Fellows have joined Goodfire, Constellation, published at NeurIPS/ICLR.
319.regulatory_stance_detail: CBAI supports research on restricting dangerous AI research through international treaties with classification systems, federal funding pauses for risky research, and coordinated trade sanctions for non-compliance. Their fellowship research explores policy frameworks for managing catastrophic AI risks.
319.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security

320.notes: Founded by Claire Boine (formerly Pathfinder, rebranded April 2023). Career transition organization helping mid-career professionals move into AI safety and governance roles. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($778K+ in grants). Offers AI Policy Strategy Fellowship, career mentoring, and professional training. Previously supported 40+ EA and AI safety organizations. Team includes technical advisors and policy experts.
320.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on mitigating catastrophic and existential risks from AI through career transitions and policy advocacy. Founder Claire Boine advocates for AI safety policy and has participated in expert panels on AI risks. Offers AI Policy Strategy Fellowship to translate AI safety expertise into policy change.
320.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

321.notes: Founded 2023 by Suryansh Mehta and Callum Evans. 45 employees across research and operations. 12-week remote fellowship program matching postgraduate students with senior researchers. Three focus areas: AI Policy, Philosophy for Safe AI, and AI Sentience. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Longview Philanthropy. Project leads from Oxford, GovAI, DeepMind, and other leading institutions.
321.regulatory_stance_detail: FIG produces research to inform government responses to AI developments, working on policy frameworks, governance institutions, and risk management approaches. Project leads from organizations like Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative work on regulatory interventions and safety standards.
321.threat_models: Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Existential risk

322.notes: Founded 2021, runs intensive 3-day residential workshops for university students exploring AI safety and biosecurity. 4-person team led by Director Liam Epstein (MA from Leverhulme Centre for Future of Intelligence). Funded by Open Philanthropy. Operates workshops globally with 20-35 attendees each. Part of Rethink Priorities Special Projects program providing operational support.
322.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on educating students about existential risks from AI and biotechnology, emphasizing the need for safety measures and governance frameworks to prevent catastrophic outcomes from advanced technologies.
322.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

323.notes: Founded 2022 as UK charity. Runs multiple AI governance and safety programs: AI Governance Taskforce (12-week policy research program), LASR Labs (technical AI safety research), AI Safety Engineering Taskforce, and Orion AI Governance Initiative. Primary funder is Coefficient Giving (£8M+ committed). Led by Joe Hardie and Erin Robertson. Partners with organizations like GovAI, UK AISI, SaferAI, and Future Society on research projects.
323.regulatory_stance_detail: Runs AI Governance Taskforce producing policy research on frontier AI risk management, loss of control scenarios, and AI safety frameworks. Research outputs advocate for enhanced oversight mechanisms, safety evaluations, and governance infrastructure for advanced AI systems.
323.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration

324.notes: Founded by Katja Grace, operates as research organization under MIRI umbrella. Conducted largest AI expert survey to date (2,778 researchers in 2023). Received grants from Future of Life Institute ($49,310) and Future of Humanity Institute ($8,700) in 2015. Key outputs include expert surveys on AI timelines, AI capabilities database, and empirical research on technological progress patterns. Focuses on 'answering decision-relevant questions about the future of AI' through research, wiki, and expert surveys.
324.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on empirical research and data collection rather than explicit policy advocacy. Conducts expert surveys and maintains databases to inform AI governance decisions rather than taking strong regulatory positions.
324.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Power concentration

325.notes: 13-week technical AI safety research program where participants work in teams of 3-4 supervised by experienced researchers to produce academic papers. Provides £11,000 stipend. Alumni have joined UK AISI, Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and OpenAI's dangerous capabilities team. All 5 papers from Summer 2024 cohort accepted to NeurIPS workshops. Operated by Arcadia Impact charity, funded by Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Supervisors from Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and top universities.
325.regulatory_stance_detail: LASR Labs focuses on technical AI safety research to reduce risks from loss of control to advanced AI systems. The program explicitly aims to train researchers for careers in AI safety organizations and emphasizes action-relevant research tackling concrete threat models related to advanced AI risks.

326.notes: Tenured Associate Professor at University of Louisville, founding director of Cybersecurity Lab. Author of 100+ publications including 'Artificial Superintelligence: A Futuristic Approach' and 'AI: Unexplainable, Unpredictable, Uncontrollable'. Research funded by NSF, NSA, DHS, and FLI. Fellow at MIRI (2010), Research Advisor at MIRI (2012), AI Safety Fellow at Foresight Institute (2019). Known for extremely pessimistic AI safety positions and predicting human extinction within this century.
326.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for extreme caution in AI development, arguing that superintelligent AI cannot be indefinitely controlled. Promotes narrow AI development over AGI and calls for potential moratoriums on superintelligence research until safety is better understood.
326.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

327.notes: Associate Professor at MIT CSAIL running the Algorithmic Alignment Group. PhD from UC Berkeley (2013-2020) advised by Stuart Russell, Pieter Abbeel, and Anca Dragan. AI2050 Early Career Fellow. Key publications include 'The Off-Switch Game', 'Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning', and 'Consequences of Misaligned AI'. Research spans theoretical and empirical AI safety with focus on value alignment and human-AI interaction.
327.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-seeking AI policies focused on identifying and studying risks rather than premature restrictive regulation. Co-authored paper arguing that regulatory objectives should facilitate research and deliberation about AI risks. Supports technical standards and democratic institutions for AI alignment.
327.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

328.notes: Individual researcher Steve Byrnes working at Astera Institute since 2021. Physics PhD turned AGI safety researcher. Published comprehensive 15-part 'Intro to Brain-Like AGI Safety' blog series. Research applies neuroscience insights to AI alignment, focusing on Learning and Steering Subsystems framework. Funded by grants and now Astera Institute. Estimates ~100-200 people globally work full-time on AGI safety.
328.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for solving technical alignment problems before AGI development, warning that brain-like AGI could have 'radically nonhuman and dangerous motivations' without proper safety work. Emphasizes need for understanding reward functions and steering subsystems to prevent catastrophic accidents.

329.notes: Founded 2022, African-led research program with 14 employees. Coordinates Cecil Abungu, runs Junior Research Fellowship program for talent development. Publishes research papers and policy briefs on AI governance from Global South perspectives. Recent work includes 'Assessing the Case for Africa-Centric AI Safety Evaluations' and analysis of AU Continental AI Strategy. Hosts policy engagement sessions and collaborates with international AI safety community.
329.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety governance with particular focus on Global South perspectives. Publishes research arguing that Global South countries need to actively engage with highly capable AI risks and calls for substantial funding allocation (one-third of R&D budgets) toward AI safety research. Recommends AI liability frameworks and advocates for Africa-centric AI safety evaluations.
329.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, National security

330.notes: Launched in 2022 by Schmidt Sciences as a philanthropic initiative co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika. Awards over $18M annually to researchers through fellowship program. Now has 99 fellows across 8 countries and 42 institutions in fourth cohort. Uses closed nomination process rather than open applications. Fellows have published 100+ papers in leading journals and created datasets like 'Humanity's Last Exam' AI benchmark.
330.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on ensuring AI benefits society by 2050 through research funding rather than regulatory advocacy. Co-founded by Eric Schmidt who has advocated for maintaining US AI competitiveness and avoiding restrictive regulations that could hamper innovation. Emphasizes technological solutions over policy interventions.
330.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security, Environmental

331.notes: Research organization focused on reducing lock-in risks by studying fundamental lock-in dynamics and implementing high-impact interventions. Appears to be a specialized research entity rather than a broad policy organization. Limited public information available about founding, staff size, or funding sources. Website indicates newsletter signup and mentions being 'supported by' unspecified entities.
331.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on lock-in risks in AI development, researching fundamental dynamics around how certain technological or governance paths could become difficult to reverse. Does not appear to take broad regulatory positions beyond this specialized research area.

333.notes: Launched 2018 by LessWrong team as centralized hub for AI alignment research discussion. ~100 vetted members including researchers from MIRI, Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI, and academia. Serves as archive and library for the field with integrated karma system. Replaced MIRI's Intelligent Agent Foundations Forum. Key introductory sequences on Value Learning, Iterated Amplification, and Embedded Agency.
333.regulatory_stance_detail: Forum discussions strongly favor careful evaluation and safety measures before deploying powerful AI systems. Content emphasizes responsible scaling policies, third-party testing, and international coordination on AI governance. Many posts advocate for pausing or slowing development until alignment problems are solved.
333.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Deceptive alignment, Misalignment

335.notes: Created 2017 by mathematician Vipul Naik with contributions from Issa Rice. Tracks $407M+ in AI safety donations (2007-2023) from major funders like Open Philanthropy ($276M), MIRI ($44M), OpenAI. Open-source database covers 875 donations across 191 organizations. Data considered preliminary until July 2026 completion target. Serves EA/rationalist communities primarily.
335.regulatory_stance_detail: Does not advocate for specific AI policies but provides transparency infrastructure that enables others to track AI safety funding patterns. Serves as neutral documentation platform for philanthropic giving across cause areas.

337.notes: Applied mathematics research nonprofit dedicated to advancing foundational alignment research. Runs research residencies (6-week programs with $6,000-$15,000 stipends), fellowships (3-month mentored research programs), and intensive courses. Partners with PIBBSS and operates at LISA in London. Funded by AISTOF. Organizes ILIAD conferences and provides research mentorship with established mathematicians and AI alignment researchers.
337.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on technical research rather than explicit policy positions. Develops mathematical approaches to AI alignment through research residencies and fellowships, suggesting support for foundational safety research.

338.notes: Fast-funding initiative run by Lightcone Infrastructure, primarily funded by Jaan Tallinn. Distributed $9.6M in 2023 round with grants ranging $5K-$5M. Applications processed in 14-60 days. Funded projects like Palisade Research ($401K), Center for AI Safety ($16K), and various individual researchers. Last active funding round was 2023.
338.regulatory_stance_detail: As a grantmaker, does not take explicit regulatory positions but funds projects across the spectrum from AI safety research to policy advocacy, including both technical alignment work and governance initiatives.
338.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation

339.notes: 501(c)(3) founded around 2022, now defunct for unknown reasons. Founded by Jonathan Rodriguez Cefalu (Preamble Inc. founder, Stanford CS, ex-Snap). Funded research including SIMC4 wargame simulator for nuclear scenarios and AI red-teaming research. Filed Form 990-N indicating annual revenue under $50,000. Connected to broader Preamble ecosystem including commercial AI safety company Preamble Inc.
339.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research and evaluation methods rather than broad regulatory advocacy. Supports development of AI safety guardrails and evaluation frameworks through funded research projects.
339.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity

340.notes: Decentralized bounty platform for existential risk reduction and effective altruist cause areas. Offers prize money for delivering on specific AI safety projects. Listed as no longer accepting applications as of 2026. Part of the broader effective altruism ecosystem funding AI safety research through competition-based incentives.
340.regulatory_stance_detail: Platform focuses on funding AI safety and existential risk reduction projects through prize competitions, suggesting concern about AI risks and need for safety research.

341.notes: Founded 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky, 24-year history, ~20 staff. Major shift in 2023-2024 from technical research to communications and policy work. $16M reserves, historically funded by Open Philanthropy ($15.9M), Vitalik Buterin ($5.4M), Jaan Tallinn ($1.6M). Published influential works including 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities.' Launched Technical Governance Team in 2024 focusing on international AI agreements and verification mechanisms. CEO Malo Bourgon took leadership in 2024.
341.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for international agreements to halt progress toward smarter-than-human AI until alignment problems are solved. Believes current technical understanding is vastly insufficient and calls for indefinite worldwide moratorium on large training runs, enforced by international agreement. Has shifted from technical research to policy advocacy believing technical solutions won't arrive in time.

344.notes: Founded 2023 as global movement and network promoting AI as public infrastructure. Maintains extensive publication database with 150+ papers, policy briefs, and research from multiple organizations. Key publications include Public AI Whitepaper (2024), policy briefs on European digital sovereignty, and proposals for public AI utilities. Affiliated researchers from institutions like Harvard, Vanderbilt, Mozilla, and various European policy organizations. Operates inference utility providing access to open-source AI models via HuggingFace.
344.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public AI infrastructure developed under transparent governance with democratic oversight, public accountability, and equitable access. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks that focus on outputs rather than training inputs, legal clarity for using publicly available data, and safe harbors for responsible AI development while opposing restrictive data regulations that could shift AI development to other jurisdictions.
344.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, National security

345.notes: Led by Director Michael Kratsios under Trump administration. Coordinates AI policy across federal agencies through America's AI Action Plan (July 2025). Key initiatives include removing regulatory barriers, establishing AI procurement standards, creating federal AI framework to preempt state laws, and promoting AI adoption in government. Staff of approximately 22 people with $325M proposed budget cuts to NIST creating tension with innovation goals.
345.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes American AI dominance through minimal regulation and removal of regulatory barriers. Focuses on accelerating AI innovation, preventing 'woke AI' bias, and preempting restrictive state laws while maintaining basic safety standards for government AI use.

346.notes: Research organization working on foundational mathematics and theory for AI safety, particularly agent foundations. Produces technical content on topics like formalizing value fragility, corrigible architectures, and how theory can contribute to AI safety through direct calculations, pointing at possibilities, providing disproofs of practicality, and qualitative behavioral descriptions. Appears to be a small, specialized theory-focused group distinct from the customer insights platform company of the same name.
346.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational theoretical research in agent foundations and mathematical frameworks for AI safety. Does not appear to take explicit policy positions but contributes to technical understanding that could inform future governance approaches.

349.notes: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) is a bipartisan Washington, D.C.-based think tank founded in 1962. One of the leading U.S. foreign-policy and national-security research institutions. On AI, CSIS houses the Wadhwani AI Center (launched 2024, directed by Gregory C. Allen) which publishes research on AI and national security, chip export controls, and U.S.-China technology competition, and produces the biweekly CSIS AI Policy Podcast.
349.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage takes a balanced approach to AI policy, examining both innovation benefits and regulatory needs. Discussion topics include export controls, national security implications, safety measures, and strategic competition with China, reflecting mainstream policy discourse.
349.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

350.notes: Founded 2022, Israeli nonprofit with 5 staff. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Led by David Manheim. Institutional member of US AI Safety Consortium (NIST). Key work includes AI oversight standards, biosecurity policy, and salt iodization advocacy. Publishes research on AI governance gaps and incident preparedness frameworks.
350.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory synthetic nucleic acid screening, establishment of governance frameworks for biological AI models, and incident preparedness approaches to AI regulation. Supports comprehensive regulatory frameworks with pre-deployment safety testing and strong governmental powers to respond to AI security incidents.
350.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity

351.notes: Founded 2011 by William MacAskill and Ben Todd. Career advisory nonprofit with 46 employees focusing on directing talent toward high-impact careers, especially AI safety/governance. Published extensive problem profiles on AI risks, operates job board, provides 1-on-1 career advice. Over 3,000 people report changing careers due to their guidance. Primary funder: Coefficient Giving. Part of effective altruism community.
351.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety measures. Promotes working on AI governance, technical safety research, and policy approaches to reduce catastrophic risks. Estimates ~10% existential risk from AI within 100 years and calls it 'the world's most pressing problem'.
351.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

352.notes: Founded December 2024 as a non-profit AI security initiative. Team includes Ezra Hausdorff (Founder/CEO), Shanni Gurkevitch (Co-Founder), Nitzan Shulman (Head of Cyber), and advisory board with AI security experts. Funded by Coeffecient Giving. Runs events, research programs, and career guidance connecting cybersecurity professionals to high-leverage AI security opportunities. Collaborates with frontier AI companies, startups, think tanks, and policymakers to advance AI infrastructure security.
352.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger cybersecurity measures for frontier AI models, emphasizing the need for secure infrastructure, model weight protection, and authorization solutions for AI misuse. Promotes policy solutions like SL5-level security standards and regulatory frameworks for AI lab security.
352.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation

353.notes: Led by Vanessa Kosoy, fiscally sponsored by Ashgro (formerly Affiliate Learning Theoretic Employment and Resources). Works on learning theoretic research agenda for AI alignment. Received $140,000 from SFF-2025 round funded by Jaan Tallinn. Categorized as doing foundational mathematics and agent foundations work in the AI safety ecosystem.
353.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational alignment research to develop mathematical tools for aligning AI objectives with human values. Works on agent foundations and learning theory to provide rigorous frameworks for AI alignment solutions.

354.notes: Part of University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, established research lab focused on computational neuroscience and machine learning. Contains multiple research groups including the Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL) founded 2022. Key members include David Krueger (AI alignment researcher who worked with UK Frontier AI Taskforce), Carl Rasmussen, Adrian Weller, and other ML researchers. Conducts both biological learning research and AI safety work including alignment, interpretability, and policy engagement.
354.regulatory_stance_detail: As an academic research lab, CBL does not take explicit regulatory positions. However, lab members like David Krueger have worked with UK government (Frontier AI Taskforce) and contributed to policy discussions around AI safety and compute governance, suggesting engagement with regulatory development.
354.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination

355.notes: Founded ~2022, led by David Krueger (Assistant Professor at Cambridge). Dual affiliation with University of Cambridge and Mila (Quebec AI Institute). Team includes ~15 PhD students, postdocs, and research assistants.
