Key publications include 'Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning' (ICML 2022), 'Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of LLMs' (2024). Runs paid AI safety internship programs. Previously based at University of Cambridge, now also affiliated with Mila.
355.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety evaluations. Co-authored major paper identifying 18 foundational challenges in LLM alignment and safety with 35+ researchers. Research focuses on technical solutions to prevent misaligned AI behavior before deployment.

356.notes: Founded 2022 as ARC Evals, rebranded to METR. 36 employees including CEO Beth Barnes (named to TIME's 100 most influential people in AI 2024). Funded by The Audacious Project, Open Philanthropy, Schmidt Sciences, and other philanthropic sources - notably does not accept funding from AI companies. Partners with OpenAI, Anthropic, and other labs on pre-deployment evaluations. Key research includes 'Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks' showing exponential capability growth. Part of NIST AI Safety Institute Consortium.
356.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for rigorous pre-deployment evaluations of frontier AI models, external third-party auditing rather than self-evaluation by labs, and mandatory safety assessments before release. Developed the Responsible Scaling Policy framework adopted by nine leading AI developers and provides technical assistance to regulators including the EU AI Office.
356.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security

357.notes: Founded 2019 as nonprofit research institute by Ozzie Gooen. Small team of ~5 full-time staff. Receives funding from Survival and Flourishing Fund ($62K in 2024), operates under Rethink Priorities fiscal sponsorship. Key projects include Squiggle probabilistic programming language, SquiggleAI, and research on AI for forecasting question resolution. Board includes AI policy experts like Abigail Olvera (Golden Gate Institute for AI).
357.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing epistemic tools and forecasting systems to improve decision-making rather than direct regulatory advocacy. Research on AI for question resolution and evaluation systems could inform governance frameworks, but no explicit policy positions identified.

358.notes: Small philanthropic foundation with long history in X-risk funding. Does not conduct direct research but identifies and financially supports individuals and organizations working on existential risk reduction. Part of broader AI safety funding ecosystem alongside organizations like Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Currently not accepting applications according to multiple sources.
358.regulatory_stance_detail: Dedicated to preventing human-created existential risks through funding researchers and organizations working on X-risk reduction. Operates as a philanthropic foundation that identifies and financially supports people doing the best work to prevent human extinction from technologies like AI, nanotechnology, and other emerging threats.
358.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

359.notes: Founded September 2024 with Beijing municipal government support. Led by Yi Zeng, CAS professor and UN High-Level Advisory Body on AI member. Core contributor to China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA). Published AGILE Index 2025 evaluating AI governance across 40 countries. Co-organized Beijing AI Safety International Consensus with signatures from Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell.
359.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks balancing development and safety through international cooperation. Supports tiered regulatory approaches, mandatory safety evaluations for frontier models, and establishment of global AI governance institutions with enforcement mechanisms.
359.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Misinformation

361.notes: Launched February 2025 at Paris AI Action Summit. Network of China's leading AI institutions including Tsinghua University, Beijing Academy of AI, Shanghai AI Lab, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and China Academy of Information and Communications Technology. Claims Chinese government backing but operates as collaborative platform rather than regulatory body. Primary function is international representation and dialogue coordination rather than domestic oversight or testing.
361.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI development and safety governance through international cooperation. Positions itself as China's counterpart to international AI safety institutes, emphasizing collaborative governance frameworks, voluntary industry safety commitments, and multilateral dialogue while avoiding restrictive regulatory approaches that could hinder development.
361.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, CBRN risks, National security, Misinformation

362.notes: Founded 2024 as nonprofit hosted by Whistleblower Netzwerk e.V. Staff of 7 with 200% growth. Offers 'Third Opinion' anonymous consultation service connecting AI insiders with independent experts. Launched 'Publish Your Policies' campaign with 35+ signatories demanding AI companies publish whistleblowing policies. Documented 22 whistleblower cases (6 named, 16 anonymous) in comprehensive resource. Provides legal support connections and policy advocacy in EU and US.
362.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger legal protections for AI whistleblowers, supports principle-based regulation that covers emerging risks not yet captured by existing law, and promotes transparency requirements for AI companies including mandatory publication of internal whistleblowing policies.
362.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, National security, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity

363.notes: Coworking space and field-building nonprofit based in Cambridge, UK. Houses projects like CAISH and the ERA:AI Fellowship. Runs 3-12 month Visiting Researcher Programme for AI safety and governance researchers. Partners with Cambridge AI Safety Hub. Provides workspace, community, and research support including assistance with grant applications to Open Philanthropy and other funders. Contact: visitingresearchers@meridiancambridge.org
363.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety and governance research through visiting researcher programs. Houses projects like CAISH and ERA:AI Fellowship. Supports research on technical alignment, governance, interpretability, forecasting, and other research ensuring AI benefits humanity.
363.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration

364.notes: Founded as nonprofit research center in Berkeley with 35 employees (+266% growth). Four core strategies: mobilization (placing talent), incubation (supporting new orgs), growth (helping orgs scale), and convening (facilitating coordination). Runs Astra Fellowship (3-6 month AI safety research program), incubator for AI safety startups, and visiting fellows program. Over 80% of first Astra cohort now work full-time in AI safety at organizations like Redwood Research, METR, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind.
364.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing AI safety talent and supporting key organizations rather than direct policy advocacy. Emphasizes coordination across the AI safety field and facilitating collaboration between researchers, policymakers, and industry.
364.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk

365.notes: Founded as fiscally-sponsored project of Social & Environmental Entrepreneurs (501c3). 10 employees across US, UK, Finland, Kenya, Italy. Led by Richard Mallah (also Principal AI Safety Strategist at Future of Life Institute). Conducts interdisciplinary research on risk management, policy, and technical safety. Co-authored 'Intolerable Risk Threshold Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence' and 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' papers. Mission to lower risks to humanity and biosphere from transformative AI.
365.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk management frameworks and intolerable risk thresholds for frontier AI systems. Co-authored research on 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' recommending mandatory safety evaluations, risk thresholds, and multi-stakeholder governance approaches before deployment of transformative AI.
365.threat_models: Research focuses on CBRN weapons risks, cyber attack capabilities, model autonomy, persuasion and manipulation, deception, and systemic risks from increasingly powerful AI systems. Develops risk assessment frameworks using probabilistic risk assessment techniques adapted from other industries.

366.notes: Founded 2022, runs 4-5 week intensive ML bootcamps 2-3 times per year. Each cohort accepts ~30 participants. Funded by Coefficient Giving and Open Philanthropy ($18,800 grant). Alumni outcomes include roles at Anthropic, OpenAI, METR, UK AISI, and MATS scholarships. Based at LISA in Shoreditch, covers all participant costs including travel, accommodation, and meals.
366.regulatory_stance_detail: ARENA trains technical AI safety researchers who typically advocate for cautious AI development. The program emphasizes AI alignment research and technical safety measures, with alumni going to organizations like Anthropic, METR, and UK AISI that promote careful AI governance.

369.notes: Academic research lab at NYU's Center for Urban Science and Progress, directed by Prof. Daniel B. Neill. Faculty includes experts in biostatistics, epidemiology, and applied statistics. Research applications include disease surveillance, opioid crisis response, criminal justice fairness, and urban analytics. Funded by NSF (including $1M NSF-Amazon Fairness in AI grant), collaborates with city agencies and public health organizations. Over 100 published papers on machine learning for social good applications.
369.regulatory_stance_detail: Research focuses on algorithmic fairness, AI bias detection and correction, and developing responsible AI methods for public sector applications. Lab develops technical methods to audit and mitigate systematic biases in AI systems used for urban policy decisions.
369.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Privacy

370.notes: Founded 2022 as EU Tech Policy Fellowship, renamed to Talos Network. Flagship 7-month program trains ~50 fellows annually in European AI governance through 8-week online curriculum, Brussels summit, and 4-6 month paid placements. 70% alumni transition rate to AI policy roles across EU AI Office, UK AISI, OECD, think tanks. Funded primarily by Coefficient Giving/Good Ventures, seeking €200k diversification funding in 2026. Managing Director: David Conrad.
370.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on 'safe and responsible deployment of advanced AI' through comprehensive European AI governance training. Curriculum covers regulatory approaches from principles-based to rules-based frameworks, emphasizing EU AI Act implementation and international coordination on AI risks.
370.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

371.notes: Founded 2013 by Kevin Gosschalk. 250+ employees. Raised $114M+ from SoftBank Vision Fund, Microsoft M12, PayPal, Sony Innovation Fund. Commercial cybersecurity company developing fraud prevention platform 'Arkose Titan' with specialized focus on AI agent detection and classification. This appears to be a different organization from the AI safety nonprofit 'Arkose' mentioned in the original notes that closed due to lack of funding.
371.regulatory_stance_detail: Focused on commercial fraud prevention and cybersecurity rather than AI safety regulation. Advocates for enterprise preparedness against AI-powered attacks and supports 'Know Your Agent' frameworks for distinguishing legitimate from malicious AI agents.

372.notes: Founded 2018, closed in 2025. Was an interdisciplinary research center at Oxford conducting foundational research on global priorities and longtermism. Received $3.3M from Open Philanthropy in 2020. Notable publications include 'AI alignment vs AI ethical treatment: Ten challenges' (2024) and research on longtermism paradigm. Staff included Leopold Aschenbrenner (former OpenAI researcher who later founded $1.5B hedge fund) and Bradford Saad who co-authored key AI ethics research.
372.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful consideration of AI development risks, including potential global moratoriums on advanced AI systems until adequate safety and ethical frameworks are established. Emphasizes the need for strategic funding of AI safety research and potential tax policies to discourage mistreatment of AI systems with moral status.
372.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation

373.notes: Founded 2023, 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization in rural Vermont. Offers fellowships for early-career researchers with $1500/month grants plus lodging. Received $88,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2023. Research community focused on AI safety and pandemic prevention, with researchers living and working together. Key publications include work on unelicitable backdoors in language models via cryptographic transformer circuits.
373.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing provable guarantees and safety techniques for AI systems, particularly in RL frameworks. Conducts research on unelicitable backdoors and alignment methods to ensure AI systems behave safely and as intended.

374.notes: Founded by MATS alumni Jacques Thibodeau and Ronak Mehta (merged with Vectis AI). Nonprofit developing research automation tools and AI systems to accelerate alignment work. Secured $110k+ in seed funding through Catalyze Impact's funding circle. Currently in alpha phase with research workflow tool called 'Seed'. Part of broader trend of AI safety organizations adopting automation to compete with capabilities research pace.
374.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found. Focus is on technical research automation and accelerating alignment research rather than policy advocacy. Appears to prioritize building tools and systems over regulatory interventions.

375.notes: Founded 2023 as Public Benefit Corporation. 18 employees across 11 countries with task-based contributor network. Revenue-generating through client contracts with METR, UK AISI, Redwood Research, and EU Commission. Specializes in evaluation implementation using frameworks like Inspect. Leadership includes CEO Chris Canal, CTO Daniel O'Connell, and AI Advisor Rob Miles. Raised $400k operating capital via Manifund SAFE agreement in 2025.
375.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports technical implementation of AI safety evaluations and compliance infrastructure. Through AgentStamp subsidiary, develops tools for EU AI Act compliance including transparency disclosures, audit trails, and regulatory reporting for AI agents.

376.notes: Founded June 2025 by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio. Nonprofit AI-safety lab launched with ~$30M in philanthropic funding from Jaan Tallinn, Eric Schmidt, Open Philanthropy, the Future of Life Institute, and others; ~15–30 employees at launch. Key research focus on 'Scientist AI' — non-agentic, Bayesian-inference-based systems intended to be safe-by-design and to provide trustworthy oversight of more powerful AI. Subsequently announced major Canadian government investment of over $100M.
376.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safety-first approaches through technical solutions rather than purely regulatory measures. Focuses on developing non-agentic 'Scientist AI' systems that can serve as guardrails for more dangerous agentic AI systems. Supports government regulation but emphasizes the need for technical safeguards built into AI systems by design.
376.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

377.notes: Founded 2025 by Jai Dhyani, senior FAANG ML engineer with decade of experience deploying AI at billion+ user scale. Non-profit organization building on Redwood Research's AI Control research. Received seed grant from AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund. Part of Catalyze's Winter 2024/25 London incubation cohort. Currently seeking $500K to grow Seattle team with SDEs and PM for coordination with research labs and frontier AI companies.
377.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing production-ready AI Control solutions for immediate deployment, emphasizing practical risk mitigation through oversight and management systems that prevent misaligned AI from achieving harmful goals.

378.notes: Founded 2023 by Joe Edelman. 30+ researcher network with 3 core team members. Funded by ARIA, OpenAI, SFF, and others. Key research areas include democratic fine-tuning (won OpenAI Democratic Inputs grant), moral graph elicitation, and 'thick models of value.' Released WiseLLaMa-8B model trained on values-based conversations. Collaborates with major AI labs on alignment research.
378.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'full-stack alignment' approaches that align not just AI systems but also markets and democratic institutions with human values. Focuses on technical solutions like democratic fine-tuning and thick models of value rather than restrictive regulation.
378.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

379.notes: Founded 2016 as nonprofit research lab. ~10 person team led by Andreas Stuhlmüller and Jungwon Byun. Raised $3.8M in 2020 from Open Philanthropy ($2.6M), Paul Christiano ($900K), and others. Built Elicit AI research assistant (spun off 2022 with $9M funding). Developed Interactive Composition Explorer (ICE) for factored cognition research. Key technical focus on supervising AI reasoning processes rather than outcomes.
379.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing process-based supervision and factored cognition approaches for AI alignment rather than advocating for specific regulations. Emphasizes building safer AI systems through technical research on interpretability-by-construction and supervising reasoning processes.

380.notes: Founded 2024 by Adam Shai (neuroscientist) and Paul Riechers (theoretical physicist). Small research team funded by Astera Institute. Key research: NeurIPS 2024 paper on transformer belief-state geometry, ICML 2025 work on attention mechanisms. Develops theoretical frameworks connecting next-token prediction to geometric structures in neural networks. Mentors fellows at MATS and PIBBSS programs. Hiring research scientists in Bay Area and London with $120k-200k salaries.
380.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for establishing scientific foundations for understanding AI systems' internal structures to enable better monitoring and control. Emphasizes need for interpretability research to address alignment challenges and promote evidence-based policy discussions rather than polarized debates between 'doomers and optimists.'
380.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation

381.notes: Founded 2015 as public benefit corporation. Staff of 30. Operates forecasting platform with pro forecasters and AI policy workshops. Partnership with Future of Life Institute on AI Pathways project supporting U.S. policymakers. Open Philanthropy commissioned AI progress tournament. Board member Peter Wildeford co-founded Institute for AI Policy and Strategy.
381.regulatory_stance_detail: Through AI Pathways workshops with Future of Life Institute, advocates for specific interventions including API restrictions to prevent recursive self-improvement, compute capacity restrictions, and required reporting of large training runs. Supports establishing government capability to evaluate AI systems for national security risks.
381.threat_models: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including potential for AI-caused global catastrophe. Research emphasizes recursive self-improvement risks, loss of human control over advanced AI systems, and national security implications of AI capabilities races between nations.

383.notes: Pooled multi-donor charitable fund managed by JueYan Zhang, former BlackRock portfolio manager and Founders Pledge investment committee chair. Raised over $10M since 2023 and committed 70+ grants. Structured for rapid deployment to capture emerging opportunities in AI safety landscape. Notable grantees include Luthien Research and other technical safety organizations. Praised by Open Philanthropy as filling important gaps in AI safety funding before major funders scaled up.
383.regulatory_stance_detail: Funds AI safety research, governance, and evaluation projects to reduce catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems. Supports both technical alignment work and policy initiatives aimed at ensuring AI development proceeds safely. Views current AI progress as potentially dangerous without proper safeguards.
383.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation

384.notes: Comprehensive analysis published on EA Forum mapping AI safety funding ecosystem from 2022-2025. Tracks $3.9B in total funding with Safe Superintelligence raising $3B across two rounds. Documents shift from philanthropic to venture funding in AI safety space. Referenced extensively by other funders and researchers as authoritative overview of funding landscape.
384.regulatory_stance_detail: Analysis advocates for increased AI safety funding and coordination among philanthropic funders. Emphasizes need for governance infrastructure and responsible development practices. Aligned with effective altruism's longtermist approach to existential risk mitigation.
384.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

387.notes: Founded 2018 by Natalie Cargill. 30 employees, directed over $133M toward AI risk reduction as of March 2026. Manages Frontier AI Fund ($20M raised, $11.1M disbursed to 18 orgs), Digital Sentience Fund ($2.5M), and Emerging Challenges Fund. CEO Simran Dhaliwal, serves major donors giving >$100K with bespoke philanthropy strategies.
387.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for AI safety regulations and governance frameworks. Manages multiple AI safety funds totaling over $22.5M, funding research, policy advocacy, and field-building to shape frontier AI development and regulation. Supports organizations working on AI governance in US and Europe.
387.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity

390.notes: Founded 2021, nonprofit think tank with 5 staff based in Geneva. Funded by Open Philanthropy and other EA-aligned donors. Specializes in international AI governance and multilateral diplomacy. Key work includes designing frameworks for UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, organizing briefings for diplomats, and publishing policy recommendations for international AI summits. Named after Herbert Simon, focusing on future-oriented policymaking tools.
390.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based multilateral AI governance through international institutions. Develops policy frameworks for AI safety assessments, promotes technical safeguards for frontier AI systems, and supports structured international cooperation mechanisms like the UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI.
390.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality

391.notes: Founded at University of Chicago, directed by Daniel Holz (professor of physics, astronomy, astrophysics and chairman of Bulletin of Atomic Scientists). Runs 10-week Summer Research Fellowship producing 60+ alumni since 2022 who have gone to positions at OpenAI, RAND, Google DeepMind, congressional offices, and leading PhD programs. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($301,800 in 2024). Fellows receive $10,000 stipend plus housing and compute credits.
391.regulatory_stance_detail: Laboratory focuses on understanding and mitigating existential risks including AI safety, advocating for increased attention to catastrophic risks that could result in hundreds of millions of deaths or permanently curtail civilization's potential. Research areas include AI governance, nuclear security, and biosecurity with emphasis on precautionary approaches.
391.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Nuclear weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, National security

392.notes: Founded 2014, nonprofit with 29 employees (72% growth YoY). Funded by Patrick J. McGovern Foundation ($170K grant 2023). Key activities include organizing The Athens Roundtable on AI and the Rule of Law, participating in EU AI Act Code of Practice development, conducting global consultations for France's 2025 AI Action Summit involving 10,000+ citizens and 200+ experts. Notable reports include comprehensive analysis of AI agents governance under EU AI Act and AI red lines governance framework.
392.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks including binding regulations, external pre-deployment assessments for high-risk AI systems, AI red lines for unacceptable capabilities (CBRN uplift, mass surveillance), and robust enforcement mechanisms. Supports both horizontal and sector-specific regulation rather than self-governance approaches.
392.threat_models: Existential risk, CBRN weapons proliferation, Mass surveillance, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security

393.notes: Founded 2024 as fiscally sponsored project of Rethink Priorities (EIN 84-3896318). Staff of 6 people with 60% YoY growth. Offers virtual courses on National Security Law and AI, AI Agents and the Law, plus in-person DC workshops. Trained 100+ law and policy students, researchers, and experts in 2025. Provides fellowships for independent research and research assistant positions. Co-Director Aishwarya Saxena acknowledged in academic papers on AI governance.
393.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on academic field-building and talent development in AI law and policy rather than advocating specific regulatory positions. Emphasizes informed policymaking through research, knowledge-sharing, and skill building to navigate AI challenges and opportunities.
393.threat_models: National security, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Loss of control

395.notes: Founded August 2023 as Filipino nonprofit. Team of 3 core staff (Clark Urzo, Brian Tan, Kyle Reynoso) plus advisors Callum McDougall (Google DeepMind) and Lee Sharkey (Apollo Research). Runs 5-month AI Interpretability Fellowship program in Manila. First cohort had 13 participants, with fellows winning hackathon prizes and gaining acceptance to programs like Apart Lab. Funded by Long-Term Future Fund and Manifund ($72,490 raised). Focuses specifically on mechanistic interpretability using curriculum from ARENA program.
395.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on technical AI safety research training rather than explicit policy advocacy. Emphasizes mechanistic interpretability as a path to safer AI systems, suggesting moderate approach to AI development with emphasis on understanding and transparency.

396.notes: Founded 2022, 501(c)(3) nonprofit providing fiscal sponsorship to AI safety projects. Revenue of $2.1M in 2023 from major funders including Founders Pledge ($455k), Silicon Valley Community Foundation ($388k), and Effective Ventures Foundation ($150k). Sponsors projects including Orthogonal (formal alignment research), Timaeus, and various individual researchers. Enables projects to access funding and reduces administrative burden.
396.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses primarily on fiscal sponsorship and operational support rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports various AI safety projects ranging from technical research to policy work, but does not appear to take unified regulatory positions as an organization.

397.notes: Founded 2017 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit providing operational support to university existential risk research groups. 5 employees (down from previous years). Primary funders are Open Philanthropy (84% of funding since 2017) and Jaan Tallinn. Currently supports 37+ collaborations including CHAI at UC Berkeley, CSER at Cambridge, and CLTC at UC Berkeley. Provides financial management, HR, grant administration and compliance services to enable academic researchers to focus on mission-critical work. 2024 goals include launching new programs beyond current collaborations model and diversifying funding base.
397.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports AI safety regulation through public statements and research collaboration. Predicted 75% probability of signing onto public statements calling for AI regulation in 2024. Partners with UC Berkeley Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity on AI risk threshold research and standards development.
397.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, CBRN weapons, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Deception

399.notes: Founded 2023, 11-person team providing operations consulting to high-impact nonprofits. Fee-based business model supporting 50+ clients including many AI safety organizations. Services include finance, recruitment, entity setup, due diligence, ops audits, and systems implementation. Co-founders have Founders Pledge to donate 50%+ of proceeds to effective charities. Supports ecosystem through SparkWell nonprofit accelerator.
399.regulatory_stance_detail: Operations consultancy that supports high-impact organizations including many AI safety groups, but does not take explicit policy positions. Focuses on empowering organizations through operational excellence rather than direct advocacy.

400.notes: Founded by Kat Woods and Emerson Spartz. Operates as an EA meta-organization providing funding, coaching, and career advice to reduce existential and suffering risks. Runs incubation programs (like EA hiring agency initiative) and researches talent multiplier interventions such as personal assistants, therapy, and coaching for high-impact AI safety researchers. Means-neutral approach focused on whatever most helps reduce x-risks.
400.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses primarily on AI safety field-building and talent development rather than explicit regulatory positions. Advocates for supporting existing AI safety researchers through multiplier interventions like personal assistants and coaching rather than direct policy advocacy.

401.notes: Founded October 2025 by Greg Colbourn (also founder of Centre for Enabling EA Learning & Research, formerly EA Hotel). Provides free accommodation, vegan food, and £50/week stipend for people dedicating 20+ hours weekly to AI pause advocacy. Located in Blackpool, England. Connected to PauseAI movement and broader AI safety community through effective altruism networks.
401.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium on AGI development until safety, alignment and interpretability research can catch up. Provides accommodation and support for individuals working on AI pause advocacy and awareness campaigns.

402.notes: Coworking space located at Zeughausstrasse 31, 8004 Zurich, offering public coworking on Tuesdays and Thursdays for people interested in effective altruism or AI safety. Regularly hosts events in collaboration with Zurich AI Safety and EA Zurich, including lightning talks and discussions. Provides modern facilities, meeting rooms, and a library of EA and AI safety books. Serves as a hub for independent researchers, visiting community members, and various organizations working on EA and AI safety projects.
402.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates as a coworking space and community hub supporting AI safety and effective altruism work rather than advocating specific policy positions. Provides infrastructure for researchers and organizations working on alignment and safety.

403.notes: Founded 2023 as AI safety game, pivoted to interpretability platform in 2024. Initially funded by EA fund grant. Open-sourced March 2025 with 4TB+ of interpretability datasets. Collaborates with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, EleutherAI on circuit tracing research. Provides interactive tools for sparse autoencoders, activation steering, and semantic search. Over 7,000 attribution graphs generated on platform.
403.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical alignment research through interpretability tools rather than advocating for specific regulations. Provides open-source infrastructure to democratize AI safety research and enable broader participation in mechanistic interpretability work.

404.notes: Founded and led by Zach Stein-Perlman. Maintains comprehensive scorecard evaluating major AI companies (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta, xAI, Microsoft, DeepSeek) on safety practices across risk assessment, scheming prevention, safety research, and misuse prevention. Funded by philanthropists including Jaan Tallinn. Website discontinued as of September 2025 but remains as reference. Produces detailed policy analysis and company advocacy tracking.
404.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for transparency requirements, mandatory safety evaluations, and third-party audits for frontier AI models. Supports responsible scaling policies and information sharing among companies but focuses more on accountability measures than restrictive regulations.
404.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Power concentration

405.notes: Founded by Kabir Kumar, 10-person team across 8 countries. Operates as contributable compendium ranking alignment plans by criticism scores. Runs regular hackathons including AI Alignment Evals Hackathon (Jan 2025), Critique-a-Thon (Mar 2024), and Law-a-Thon (Feb 2024). Platform allows community to add alignment plans and criticisms with voting system similar to LessWrong. Aims to identify best alignment approaches by crowdsourcing quality criticism.
405.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on evaluating and scoring AI alignment plans to identify problems and gaps. Advocates for evidence-based approach to alignment by crowdsourcing criticism of proposed safety plans. Runs hackathons on alignment evaluations and policy development.

406.notes: Founded early 2018 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to global catastrophic risk research. Small team with researchers from MIRI, OpenPhil and Numerai backgrounds. Key work includes AI timeline modeling presented at CHAI and strategic research on technological progress patterns. Spent ~$0 in 2018-2019, planned ~$170k spending in 2020. Focus on technological and geopolitical changes over next century.
406.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on research and modeling rather than explicit policy advocacy. Work on AI timelines and progress models may inform governance decisions but organization doesn't appear to take strong regulatory positions.

407.notes: Living document published October 2024 by Connor Leahy, Gabriel Alfour, Chris Scammell, Andrea Miotti, and Adam Shimi. Comprehensive worldview document arguing AI progress is rapidly converging on AGI driven by corporate race backed by Big Tech and nations. Estimates solving alignment requires Nobel-prize-level breakthroughs, billions/trillions in investment, and decades of work. Connected to ControlAI organization for public awareness and policy advocacy.
407.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive slowing and regulation of AI progress, implementing mechanisms to pause/stop AI development, and preventing the creation of AGI altogether. Supports international coordination through treaties and regulations to deter nation-states from racing to superintelligence. Endorses 'A Narrow Path' strategy to prevent superintelligence development for 20+ years.
407.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

408.notes: Founded 2017 by David Kristoffersson and Justin Shovelain, formalized as institute in 2024. Non-profit with 9-person interdisciplinary team spanning AI alignment, ethics, governance, and policy. Funded by Future of Life Institute, Open Philanthropy, and individual donors. Key programs: AI Clarity (scenario research), Governance Recommendations, and AI Awareness. Published major reports including '2024 State of AI Regulatory Landscape' and 'Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies'. Hosted Threshold 2030 conference on AI economics. Research has influenced US BIS dual-use AI reporting requirements and EU AI Act implementation.
408.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance through scenario planning and strategic policy recommendations. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks, AI model registries, incident reporting requirements, and international cooperation mechanisms. Emphasizes the need for proactive governance to address existential risks from transformative AI.
408.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation

409.notes: Founded by Rob Miles (AI safety researcher and YouTuber). Maintained by global team of volunteers from various backgrounds. Operates as comprehensive FAQ and educational resource with AI safety chatbot 'Stampy'. 501(c)(3) public charity (Ashgro Inc, EIN: 88-4232889). Part of Alignment Ecosystem Development network. Funded by Long-Term Future Fund and other EA sources including $50K+ grants for distillation fellowship program.
409.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on existential risk from AI and need for urgent safety research. Site emphasizes that 'smarter-than-human AI may come soon' and 'could lead to human extinction,' advocating for proactive safety measures and alignment research before deployment.

410.notes: Founded 2021 as ERA Cambridge, non-profit fellowship program. Runs 8-10 week fully-funded research fellowships (Winter and Summer cohorts) accepting 30-35 fellows per cohort. £34,125 annual salary equivalent (pro-rated), plus accommodation, meals, visa support. 38 employees, operates across 9 countries. Alumni now work at UK AI Security Institute, RAND, Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative, and other key AI policy institutions. Partners with University of Cambridge, provides mentorship from expert network.
410.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellowship focuses on training researchers in AI safety and governance to mitigate risks from frontier AI systems. Research streams explicitly target technical safeguards, governance frameworks, and policy solutions for advanced AI risks. Alumni work at institutions like UK AI Security Institute and RAND shaping AI policy.
410.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Existential risk

411.notes: Annual 4-day educational program held in Prague since at least 2022, organized by Alignment of Complex Systems research group and Center for Theoretical Study at Charles University. Led by Jan Kulveit and team. Attracts researchers, PhD students, and practitioners interested in AI alignment. Features speakers from major AI labs like DeepMind, Anthropic, and universities. Funded through EA/longtermist community channels including past Long-Term Future Fund grants.
411.regulatory_stance_detail: The summer school focuses on AI alignment research and safety concerns, emphasizing the need for AI systems to remain under human control as they approach or exceed human-level capabilities. Curriculum covers alignment theory, interpretability, and multi-agent safety frameworks.

413.notes: Founded 2023 by Vilhelm Skoglund and Sebastian Schmidt. Runs 3-6 month fully-funded research fellowships placing exceptional STEM talent at leading AI safety organizations. Placement partners include UC Berkeley CHAI, Conjecture, FAR.AI, Mila-Quebec AI Institute, and UK AISI. Funded by Open Philanthropy, Charlottes och Fredriks Stiftelse (Swedish family foundation), and US private donors. Also operates Axiom Futures program focused on Indian talent development. Provides comprehensive financial packages including salary, housing, and research costs.
413.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on reducing risks from advanced AI systems through technical safety research and governance interventions. Operates within the AI existential risk ecosystem advocating for careful development and safety measures before deployment of powerful AI systems.
413.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security

414.notes: 8-week mentored research program for London students run by Arcadia Impact. Operates 5 research streams: AI governance, technical AI safety, global health, biosecurity, and animal welfare. Led by Alicia Pollard. Awards £2,000 prize for best project. Part of broader Arcadia Impact ecosystem including LASR Labs and AI Safety Engineering Taskforce.
414.regulatory_stance_detail: Program trains students in AI governance and technical AI safety research, emphasizing risk mitigation approaches. Focus areas include addressing 'gravest risks from AI' through sound policy and corporate governance, suggesting support for proactive regulatory frameworks.
414.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

415.notes: Founded 2021 as independent research fellowship program. 146 employees across 18 countries, led by Co-Executive Directors Ryan Kidd and Christian Smith. Runs 12-week fully-funded research fellowships connecting scholars with mentors from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and other leading AI safety organizations. Has supported 450+ researchers with 80% of pre-2025 alumni now working in AI safety. Alumni founded organizations like Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and joined top AI safety teams. Funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and other philanthropic sources.
415.regulatory_stance_detail: MATS trains researchers in AI safety, alignment, interpretability, governance, and security with focus on reducing risks from unaligned AI. Program explicitly aims to address 'the world's most urgent and talent-constrained problem: reducing risks from unaligned artificial intelligence.' Research includes AI control, monitoring, evaluations, and governance policy work.
415.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Cybersecurity

416.notes: Founded 2022 as Effective Ventures project. 8-week online fellowship for ages 14-20 tackling global challenges. Awards up to $30,000 in scholarships annually, with over $400,000 distributed since founding. Funded primarily by Open Philanthropy. Alumni include UN speakers and winners of major science competitions. 40 employees, 1,856 LinkedIn followers.
416.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellowship program trains young researchers on pressing global challenges including AI safety and biosecurity, but does not explicitly advocate for specific regulatory positions. Focus is on research capacity building rather than policy advocacy.
416.threat_models: AI safety, Biosecurity, Climate change, Nuclear risk

417.notes: Founded 2021, operated by Pivotal Research organization with 16 employees. 9-week in-person fellowship program hosted at London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA). 7th cohort running 2026 Q1. Fellows receive £6-8k stipend plus housing/travel. Mentors from top AI safety organizations including Redwood Research, Google DeepMind, SecureBio, FAR AI. Program rated 8.7/10 by fellows with 88 Net Promoter Score. Research outputs include papers on AI liability, interpretability, safety evaluations.
417.regulatory_stance_detail: Fellows and mentors conduct research advocating for AI safety governance frameworks, compute governance, liability mechanisms, and international coordination. Research outputs focus on reducing risks from advanced AI through policy interventions and safety measures.
417.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

418.notes: Founded 2022, operates as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Runs 3-month summer fellowship program with ~20 fellows annually, targeting PhD/postdoc researchers from diverse fields for AI safety career transitions. Funded by SFF, LTFF, Cooperative AI Fund, Foresight Institute. Key outcomes include fellows transitioning to AI safety labs, UK AISI, and academic positions. 2024 program produced multiple publication-ready research outputs in mechanistic interpretability, cooperative AI, and formal safety methods.
418.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on AI safety research and facilitating career transitions into AI alignment work. Emphasizes understanding AI risks and developing interdisciplinary approaches to AI safety. Research projects include work on AI cooperation, mechanistic interpretability, and formal safety guarantees.
418.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Multi-agent coordination failures

419.notes: Founded 2022 by Olivia Jimenez and Akash Wasil. Ran research contests offering up to $100,000 in prizes for solutions to AI alignment problems. Funded by Open Philanthropy, fiscally sponsored by Player's Philanthropy Fund. Final contest deadline was May 1, 2023, with judging concluding afterward. Has Discord community for participants and maintains educational resources on alignment research.
419.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI alignment challenges, particularly corrigibility and goal misgeneralization problems. Emphasizes the importance of solving alignment before advanced AI systems become too powerful to control safely.

420.notes: Communications organization that connected journalists to AI safety experts and resources. Received $288,000 from Open Philanthropy in 2023 for general support. Key experts include Robert Trager, Lennart Heim, and Seán ÓhÉigeartaigh who co-authored proposal for International AI Organization. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map as of 2024.
420.regulatory_stance_detail: Facilitates communication between AI safety experts and journalists to increase public understanding of AI risks. Co-authored proposal for International AI Organization (IAIO) for civilian AI governance. Supports informed policy discourse through expert-media connections.

421.notes: Newsletter was part of AI Safety Support (AISS), founded 2020 by Linda Linsefors and JJ Hepburn. Australian registered charity providing fiscal sponsorship, career advice, and field-building activities. Newsletter discontinued due to lack of maintainer, but parent organization continues other AI safety support activities including managing Alignment Slack and running events like Virtual AI Safety Unconference.
421.regulatory_stance_detail: Part of AI Safety Support organization that advocates for careful AI development and existential risk reduction. Supports field-building activities to help researchers work on AI alignment problems.

422.notes: Founded 2023 as Australian nonprofit, now merged with Existential Risk Observatory. Conducted message testing campaigns, billboard advertisements, and policy submissions to governments worldwide including UN, US, UK, EU, and Australia. Organized international protests and collaborated with PauseAI movement. Ran international law student competition to draft AI moratorium treaty. Small team of 2 employees with focus on public advocacy and policy submissions.
422.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an indefinite worldwide moratorium on AI scaling beyond GPT-3 levels until safety can be demonstrated. Calls for shutting down large GPU clusters, criminalizing AGI/ASI development, and establishing international treaty similar to nuclear non-proliferation agreements. Supports strengthened liability rules for AI harms.
422.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation

423.notes: Personal research blog by janus, focusing on AI safety implications of large language models. Known for influential 'Simulators' post on LessWrong introducing new framework for understanding GPT-like systems. Last posted in 2023. Describes self as 'GPT cyborg' and conducts conceptual research on alignment challenges unique to generative models trained on self-supervised objectives.
423.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research through the 'simulators' framework for understanding GPT-like models. Argues that existing alignment theory, designed for agent-like AI, may not apply well to self-supervised generative models. Emphasizes need for new conceptual frameworks to understand simulator vs simulacra dynamics.

424.notes: Weekly podcast, YouTube channel and newsletter that provided updates on AI safety research and developments. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map, discontinued due to organizational reprioritization. Was featured on the Alignment Forum and served as a media source for the AI safety community to stay informed about latest research and policy developments.
424.regulatory_stance_detail: As a discontinued newsletter/podcast focused on AI safety updates, did not advocate for specific regulatory positions but covered developments in AI safety research and policy.

426.notes: Public education website focused on communicating AGI extinction risks to general audiences. Provides detailed policy proposals including global moratoriums, compute caps, and international AI safety institutions. Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map but website remains operational with recent content updates.
426.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for completely stopping development of AGI systems more powerful than GPT-3. Proposes bans on large training runs (>10^23 FLOP), rolling back existing models like GPT-4, requiring licenses for smaller AI development, and creating international oversight institutions with compute monitoring and shutdown capabilities.
426.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

427.notes: Volunteer-run nonprofit organization with 4 employees. Operates under AI Safety Awareness Foundation (also known as AISAP). Conducts workshops nationwide at libraries, universities, and community centers on AI basics, safety evaluations, policy analysis, and forecasting. Partners with Seattle Public Library and other institutions for public education programs. Featured in AISafety.com field map as advocacy organization.
427.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public awareness and education about AI risks and benefits through workshops and community engagement. Focuses on empowering citizens to have a voice in shaping AI development rather than specific regulatory prescriptions.
427.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Power concentration

428.notes: UK government R&D agency established 2023 by Act of Parliament, modeled after DARPA. £800M budget over 5 years (£184M allocated 2025-26). CEO Ilan Gur, 95 employees. Eight program directors with £50M+ budgets each. Major programs: Safeguarded AI (£69M), Scaling Compute (£50M), Precision Neurotechnologies. Portfolio includes AI hardware startups like Fractile (£5M) and Callosum (partnership). Operates independently from government intervention.
428.regulatory_stance_detail: Government R&D agency funding high-risk, high-reward research including AI safety programs. Safeguarded AI program seeks to build 'gatekeeper' systems with quantitative safety guarantees for frontier AI deployment. Complementary to UK AI Safety Institute's policy work, focused on fundamental research for 3-10 year horizons.
428.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security

429.notes: Founded as independent think tank, formerly Legal Priorities Project. Staff of 40+ researchers across 12 countries, led by Founding Director Christoph Winter. Publishes working paper series via SSRN, produces policy reports and research articles on AI governance. Notable recent work includes 'Legal Alignment for Safe and Ethical AI' and 'Automated Compliance and the Regulation of AI'. Hosts workshops on law-following AI and maintains research collaborations with academic institutions.
429.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for precise, legally-grounded AI regulation that addresses specific harms rather than broad technology bans. Proposes innovative regulatory mechanisms like 'automatability triggers' that activate regulations only when AI can automate compliance tasks, and emphasizes liability frameworks for AI companies causing harm.
429.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Power concentration, Misinformation, Economic inequality

431.notes: Founded 2025 as initiative of Center for Existential Safety. Being incorporated as nonprofit in Geneva. Launched petition calling for supranational AI governance organization, aiming for 1M signatures. Led by James Norris who has 26+ years preparing for Singularity. Expects AI 'warning shot' or catastrophe soon. Seeks to develop vetted treaty quickly as 'Plan B' to other multilateral efforts.
431.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for immediate pause on all large-scale frontier AI development until provably safe systems can be developed. Calls for creation of supranational organization to develop and enforce legally-binding Global AI Safety Treaty with stringent safety standards and mandatory fair distribution of AI-derived economic benefits.
431.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality

433.notes: Founded March 2024 by Charbel-Raphaël Ségerie and team. ~20 employees plus volunteers. Funded by Survival and Flourishing Fund, Longview Philanthropy, Swedish foundation, Effektiv Spenden. No AI company funding to avoid conflicts. Provides Europe's first university AI safety courses at ENS Ulm/Paris-Saclay. Initiated Global Call for AI Red Lines with 300+ signatories including Nobel laureates. Co-organized symposiums with Yoshua Bengio and Stuart Russell.
433.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for robust AI governance frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, third-party audits, and international coordination mechanisms. Contributed to EU AI Act Code of Practice implementation and supports establishing 'red lines' for unacceptable AI capabilities and uses.
433.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, National security

434.notes: Free 13-week startup accelerator program run by Fifty Years VC, helping scientists and engineers build AI safety/alignment companies. 445 participants to date, launched 105 companies with 96% fundraising success rate. Mentors include OpenAI co-founder Wojciech Zaremba, former OpenAI interim CEO Emmett Shear. UK program launched in partnership with ARIA in 2025. No equity taken, entirely free program focused on commercializing AI safety research.
434.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building AI safety infrastructure through market-driven solutions. Supports startups developing safety evaluation tools, interpretability platforms, alignment infrastructure, and governance systems rather than direct regulatory advocacy.
434.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination

435.notes: Founded 2018 by Greg Colbourn, registered charity in England & Wales since 2020. Provides subsidized accommodation and workspace in Blackpool for EA researchers and entrepreneurs at £750/month. Supports ~20 residents at a time with 4-6 month average stays. 66 grantees in 2025 (+36% YoY). Notable alumni include Chris Leong (Principal Organiser for AI Safety New Zealand) and Hoagy Cunningham (Stanford Existential Risks Initiative). Formerly known as 'EA Hotel', rebranded in 2020. Currently facing £330k funding gap and needs £30k for urgent roof repairs.
435.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on talent development and research support rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports researchers working on global catastrophic risks including AI safety, but does not appear to take explicit regulatory positions.

436.notes: The AI Governance and Safety Institute (AIGSI) is a nonpartisan 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization on a mission to ensure artificial intelligence and other technologies benefit humanity and are developed safely, securely, and in alignment with human values. The organization aims to improve institutional response to the risk that future AI might cause human extinction and ensure the enormous potential benefits of AI are realized. They conduct research, engage in outreach, and educate stakeholders and the general public. The organization focuses on key technical concepts including machine learning interpretability, outer alignment, inner alignment, convergent instrumental subgoals, and advanced agents.
436.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for preventing development of superintelligent AI until safety can be guaranteed. Emphasizes institutional response to existential risk through research, outreach, and education. Supports mandatory safety evaluations and oversight mechanisms.
436.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

437.notes: Founded 2024 by Supheakmungkol Sarin (former Google AI lead and World Economic Forum Head of Data & AI) and Edward Tsoi. Team of 13 employees across 7 countries. Has engaged 2,000+ AI governance professionals across 16 Asian countries. Co-hosted sessions at India AI Impact Summit 2026 on crisis diplomacy and contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026. Focuses on building durable governance infrastructure through regional research, structured peer learning, and implementation-oriented engagement.
437.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance frameworks tailored to Asian contexts. Co-hosted AI crisis diplomacy sessions focusing on cross-border incident coordination and joint safety testing between countries. Contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026 emphasizing need for demonstrable safety evidence and credible liability frameworks.
437.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality

438.notes: Founded 2023 by Claire Short. 10-week remote mentorship program with 1-week in-person retreat. Targets women transitioning into or early in AI alignment research careers. Funded through Manifund ($20k goal reached), Lightspeed grants. Requires 20+ hours/week commitment. Provides mentorship from established AI safety researchers, weekly research talks, networking opportunities, and potential placement in research groups post-program. Has run multiple cohorts with positive participant feedback.
438.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on technical AI alignment research capacity building rather than explicit policy positions. Aims to increase diversity in AI safety research field through mentorship and training.

439.notes: Nonpartisan think tank producing policy research on AI implications. Fellowship program runs 3 months with $15,000-$22,000 stipends, training professionals from varied backgrounds in AI policy. Alumni have secured positions at RAND, Institute for Progress, Center for Health Security, and government agencies. Recent research includes strategic visions in AI governance and policy recommendations for the AI Action Plan. Features mentorship from leading experts and produces concrete policy outputs like briefings to government officials and international conferences.
439.regulatory_stance_detail: IAPS focuses on practical policy solutions to address AI risks from today's advanced models to potential AGI and superintelligence, with emphasis on national security implications, government procurement, export controls, and international governance frameworks. Their research covers strategic visions for AI governance including government involvement and centralized development approaches.
439.threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

440.notes: Founded 2024 as part of Arcadia Impact. UK-based talent development scheme with 3 employees. Offers 9-week AI Policy Accelerator program, mentorship scheme, and internship placements. Has trained 57 participants across programs and placed 2 people into AI policy roles at think tanks. Applications for 2026 program closed January 6, 2026. Project manager is Callum Hinchcliffe, who previously worked on AI governance research and policy.
440.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on developing talent for AI governance careers with emphasis on managing risks from advanced AI systems. Curriculum covers policy levers including compute governance, model evaluations, and international AI governance frameworks.
440.threat_models: Power concentration, National security, Loss of control, Economic inequality

441.notes: Launched 2025 by Kairos, replacing Open Philanthropy's University Organizer Fellowship and Kairos's prior Fieldbuilder Support Program. Supports ~70 AI safety university groups worldwide with funding, mentorship, and resources. 12 staff across 7 countries. Funded by Open Philanthropy grant, fiscally sponsored through BERI. Provides $500+ startup funding plus ongoing support and graduate student stipends.
441.regulatory_stance_detail: Program supports AI safety and policy university groups globally, training students to work on AI alignment, governance, and existential risk reduction. Mentors have experience at organizations like METR, RAND TASP, and US AISI, indicating focus on safety-first approaches to AI development.
441.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security

442.notes: Founded 2024 by AI Safety Australia & New Zealand (AIS ANZ), led by Yanni Kyriacos. 14-week part-time program based on ARENA curriculum with weekly in-person Saturday sessions. Inaugural cohort targeted 25 participants across Australian cities. Funded by $14,901 grant from Ryan Kidd via Manifund. Expanding to Singapore, Manila, Tokyo, and Taipei in 2026. Program aims to develop technical talent for AI Safety Institutes and demonstrate community credibility to Australian government.
442.regulatory_stance_detail: Program explicitly aims to staff Australia's AI Safety Institute with safety-conscious technical talent and leverage Australia's strategic alliance with the US to promote robust global AI safety governance. Focus on preventing harmful impacts of AI through technical alignment research.

443.notes: Founded 2023, shut down in September 2025 due to lack of funding. Was a 501(c)(4) advocacy organization that conducted over 406 meetings with Congressional staffers, hosted 8 congressional briefings reaching 150+ staffers, and developed the 'Responsible AI Act' model legislation. Funded primarily by Survival and Flourishing Fund. Executive Director Jason Green-Lowe continues to offer legislative review services and seeks funding to revive the organization.
443.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for comprehensive regulation of frontier AI systems through their 'Responsible AI Act' model legislation, requiring independent third-party audits, deployment permits, hardware security standards, and government oversight before powerful AI systems can be deployed. Focused on preventing catastrophic risks through mandatory safety evaluations.
443.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Cybersecurity

452.notes: Stanford HAI (Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence) is an interdisciplinary research institute at Stanford University, founded in 2019 by Fei-Fei Li and John Etchemendy, along with James Landay and Chris Manning. The institute advances AI research, education, policy, and practice with the goal of ensuring AI benefits humanity. HAI publishes the annual AI Index Report — the most widely cited benchmark of global AI progress, tracking technical capability, industry investment, policy activity, and public opinion. It has invested over $40 million across 300+ Stanford researchers spanning computer science, social sciences, law, and medicine. In the U.S. AI policy ecosystem, HAI occupies an influential centrist position: its co-directors, fellows, and researchers regularly advise Congress, federal agencies, and international governance bodies. The institute advocates for AI that augments rather than replaces humans, and for targeted federal oversight rather than either blanket restriction or unchecked acceleration.

460.notes: Time magazine has extensively covered AI developments, with correspondents Andrew R. Chow and Billy Perrigo leading AI coverage through their newsletter 'In the Loop'. The publication has launched TIME AI initiatives including the TIME AI Agent, developed in collaboration with Scale AI, which provides personalized, interactive journalism experiences. Time has also partnered with OpenAI to advance global AI literacy and created AI-powered features like daily audio briefs and translation services. The magazine named 'The Architects of AI' as the 2025 Person of the Year, reflecting AI's significant impact.
460.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

461.notes: From AISafety.com map. Interdisciplinary research center at the University of Cambridge exploring the nature, ethics, and impact of AI.
461.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

533.notes: The UK AI Security Institute (AISI) is a government research organisation within the UK's Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, established in 2023 as the AI Safety Institute and renamed in February 2025. AISI's mission is to equip governments with scientific understanding of advanced AI risks — particularly catastrophic risks such as AI-assisted development of chemical and biological weapons, cyberattacks, and AI-enabled crime. Under Chair Ian Hogarth and Interim Director Adam Beaumont (formerly GCHQ's Chief AI Officer), the institute employs 100+ technical staff including alumni from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Oxford. Key programs include the £15m Alignment Project, £8m Systemic Safety Grants, and £5m Challenge Fund. AISI holds formal research MOUs with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, OpenAI, and Cohere, and co-led the International AI Safety Report (2024). It plays a central role in the international AI Safety Summit process and directly informs UK government AI policy.
533.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

540.notes: Public AI is a nonprofit initiative advocating for public-interest AI infrastructure as an alternative to purely commercial frontier AI development. Positions public AI as critical digital infrastructure (analogous to public broadcasting or libraries) and promotes government and civil-society funded AI projects, open models, and democratic oversight of large-scale AI systems.

542.notes: Stanford RegLab (Regulation, Evaluation, and Governance Lab) partners with government agencies to design and evaluate programs, policies, and technologies that modernize government. The organization uses machine learning and data science to improve compliance, mass adjudication, policy making, and government services. RegLab has an extensive track record working with local and national government agencies including the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Internal Revenue Service (IRS), Santa Clara County Public Health Department, and others. Daniel E. Ho is the Faculty Director of RegLab and William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law at Stanford University.

544.notes: 165-page essay series published June 2024 by Leopold Aschenbrenner, a former researcher on OpenAI's Superalignment team who was fired in April 2024. Argues AGI is "strikingly plausible" by 2027 by extrapolating GPT-2 → GPT-4 capability gains in compute, algorithmic efficiency, and "unhobbling," that a subsequent intelligence explosion could compress a decade of AI research into one year, and that the free world faces an existential race with the CCP over superintelligence. Became the defining national-security AI narrative in DC policy circles, heavily cited by hawks arguing for export controls, AI-lab security mandates, and a "Manhattan Project" posture on AI. Aschenbrenner has since launched an AGI-focused investment firm.
544.author: Leopold Aschenbrenner
544.key_argument: AGI is imminent; the US must win the race against China or face catastrophic consequences

545.notes: Seventh annual AI Index Report, published April 2024 by Stanford's Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI). The 2024 edition documented industry dominance of frontier ML research (51 notable models from industry in 2023 vs. 15 from academia), surging training costs (GPT-4 ≈ $78M, Gemini Ultra ≈ $191M), an ~8× jump in generative-AI private investment to $25.2B, a proliferation of national AI regulations, and introduced a new chapter on AI in science and medicine. Widely treated as the canonical reference on the state of the AI ecosystem; cited routinely in congressional testimony, executive-branch AI policy documents, and industry research.
545.author: Stanford HAI
545.key_argument: Comprehensive annual benchmarking of AI progress, investment, policy, and public opinion

546.notes: Essay by Marc Andreessen (June 2023) arguing that AI will be a profoundly positive force for humanity, countering doomer narratives with a case for accelerating AI development.
546.author: Marc Andreessen
546.key_argument: AI risk is overstated by incumbents seeking regulatory moats; AI will generate enormous prosperity

548.notes: White paper published May 25, 2023 by Microsoft under a foreword from Vice Chair and President Brad Smith. Part 1 lays out a five-point blueprint for AI public policy: build on government AI safety frameworks (e.g. NIST AI RMF), require "safety brakes" on AI systems controlling critical infrastructure, adopt a layered regulatory model distinguishing the applications / model / infrastructure layers of the tech stack, pursue transparency and a broader public AI governance architecture, and leverage AI to address societal challenges. Part 2 describes Microsoft's internal responsible-AI program. One of the first major corporate AI policy statements from a frontier-model developer; cited in congressional AI Insight Forums and in international AI governance coordination discussions.
548.author: Microsoft (Brad Smith)
548.key_argument: Proposes tiered governance framework based on AI capability levels

549.notes: One-sentence open letter published May 30, 2023 by the Center for AI Safety (CAIS): “Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside other societal-scale risks such as pandemics and nuclear war.” Signed by 350+ AI researchers and public figures, including Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, Dario Amodei, Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell, and Bill Gates. A watershed moment that moved existential AI risk from a fringe concern into mainstream policy discourse.
549.author: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
549.key_argument: Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside pandemics and nuclear war

550.notes: Voluntary framework published January 2023 by NIST (under Dept. of Commerce) providing guidance for organizations to manage risks from AI systems across the lifecycle. Built around four core functions — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage — and accompanied by the AI RMF Playbook and Generative AI Profile (July 2024). Widely adopted as the de facto U.S. industry reference; explicitly invoked in Biden's EO 14110 and in subsequent federal agency AI policies. Under the Trump administration's July 2025 AI Action Plan, NIST was directed to revise the RMF (removing references to DEI, misinformation, and climate change).
550.author: NIST
550.key_argument: Voluntary framework for managing AI risks throughout the AI lifecycle

551.notes: "On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big? 🦜" — peer-reviewed paper presented at ACM FAccT 2021 by Emily M. Bender (U. Washington), Timnit Gebru, Angelina McMillan-Major, and Margaret Mitchell. Argues that ever-larger language models carry four underappreciated costs: environmental impact from compute, unaccountable bias baked into uncurated web-scale training data, research opportunity cost, and downstream harms from fluent but meaning-blind outputs (the "stochastic parrot" metaphor — pattern matching without understanding). Foundational text of the algorithmic-harms / AI ethics wing of the field; internal dispute over the paper precipitated Gebru's forced exit from Google in December 2020 and Mitchell's firing in February 2021, becoming a watershed moment in the politics of industry AI research.
551.author: Emily Bender, Timnit Gebru, et al.
551.key_argument: Large language models pose environmental, bias, and misinformation risks that are inadequately addressed

552.notes: 2020 book by journalist and researcher Brian Christian (W.W. Norton, October 2020) structured in three parts: Prophecy (fairness, bias, and transparency failures in deployed ML systems — COMPAS, facial recognition, word embeddings), Agency (reinforcement learning, reward specification, and the difficulty of encoding objectives), and Normativity (imitation learning, inverse reward design, and long-horizon alignment of advanced AI). Widely cited as the most accessible bridge between mainstream ML-ethics literature and the AI-safety / long-term alignment community; frequently assigned as an introductory text in AI governance and technology-policy courses.
552.author: Brian Christian
552.key_argument: Comprehensive overview of the AI alignment challenge, from fairness to existential risk

553.notes: From AISafety.com map. Platform from the Center for AI Safety (CAIS) posting articles written by experts from a wide range of fields discussing the impacts of AI.

554.notes: From AISafety.com map. Publishes policy-relevant perspectives on frontier AI governance, including research summaries, opinions, interviews, and explainers.

555.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog by Eric Drexler on AI prospects and their surprising implications for technology, economics, environmental concerns, and military affairs.

556.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog covering many topics, including reasoning, science, psychiatry, medicine, ethics, genetics, economics, politics, and AI. Often book summaries and commentary on AI safety.

557.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog on AI safety by Jacob Steinhardt, a UC Berkeley statistics professor, analysing risks, forecasting future breakthroughs, and discussing alignment strategies.

558.notes: From AISafety.com map. Deep, thoughtful coverage on China, technology, and US-China relations – written by Jordan Schneider.

559.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog about transformative AI, futurism, research, ethics, philanthropy etc. by Holden Karnofsky. Includes the "Most Important Century" post series. Last posted in 2024.

560.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog by AI safety researcher Daniel Paleka curating and succinctly analyzing the latest research and news in AI safety. Posts about every two months.

561.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog from the Google DeepMind safety team discussing research ideas about building AI safely.

562.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog by Zvi Mowshowitz on various topics, including AI, offering detailed analysis, personal insights, and a rationalist perspective. Posts very frequently.

563.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog from ex-OpenAI (now independent) AI policy researcher Miles Brundage on the rapid evolution of AI and the urgent need for thoughtful governance.

564.notes: From AISafety.com map. Reporting and analysis on capitalism, great power competition, and the race to build superintelligence by freelance journalist Garrison Lovely.

565.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog on aligning prosaic AI by one of the leading AI safety researchers. No longer active but the archive is high quality.

566.notes: From AISafety.com map. Substack by Ajeya Cotra, researcher at METR, covering AI capabilities forecasting, AI agent benchmarks, timelines for automating AI R&D, and implications for AI safety.

567.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog by Helen Toner (director of CSET and former OpenAI board member) offering analysis on navigating the transition to advanced AI systems.

568.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog about building the fields of AI safety and Effective Altruism, discussing big-picture strategy and sharing personal experience from working in the space.

569.notes: From AISafety.com map. Top forecaster Peter Wildeford forecasts the future and discusses AI, national security, innovation, emerging technology, and the powers – real and metaphorical – that shape the world.

570.notes: From AISafety.com map. Publication by Anton Leicht on the political economy of AI progress, featuring weekly posts on how institutions and political incentives interface with fast technological change.

571.notes: From AISafety.com map. Blog by an AI safety researcher at Google DeepMind, covering alignment research, rationality, and personal productivity insights. Last AI post was 2023.

572.notes: From AISafety.com map. The AI safety space is changing rapidly. This directory of key information sources can help you keep up to date with the latest developments.

573.notes: From AISafety.com map. Johannes Gasteiger, an alignment researcher at Anthropic, selects and summarizes the most interesting AI safety papers each month.

574.notes: From AISafety.com map. Weekly newsletter listing newly-announced AI safety events and training programs, both online and in-person.

575.notes: From AISafety.com map. Newsletter listing newly announced funding opportunities for individuals and organizations working to reduce AI existential risk.

576.notes: From AISafety.com map. Newsletter from Concordia AI, a Beijing-based social enterprise, providing updates on AI safety developments in China.

577.notes: From AISafety.com map. Newsletter published every few weeks discussing recent developments in AI and AI safety. No technical background required.

578.notes: From AISafety.com map. Current-events newsletter for keeping up to date with FMxAI (formal methods and AI) with a gear toward safety, doing a mix of shallow technical reviews of papers and movement updates.

579.notes: From AISafety.com map. Weekly updates on the latest developments in AI research (including governance) written by Jack Clark, co-founder of Anthropic.

580.notes: From AISafety.com map. Infrequent newsletter aiming to inform readers about recent ML safety research, focussing on areas like adversarial robustness, interpretability, and control.

581.notes: From AISafety.com map. Aiming to help decision-makers understand what’s happening in AI and why it matters – through news roundups, explainers, features, and opinion pieces.

582.notes: From AISafety.com map. In-depth conversations about the world’s most pressing problems – including AI safety – and what you can do to help solve them.

583.notes: From AISafety.com map. Interviews with (mostly technical) AI safety researchers about their research, aiming to get a sense of why it was written and how it might reduce existential risk from AI.

584.notes: From AISafety.com map. Well-researched interviews with influential intellectuals going in-depth on AI and technology, and their broader societal implications.
