585.notes: From AISafety.com map. Interviews with existential risk researchers, policy experts, philosophers, and a range of other influential thinkers.

586.notes: From AISafety.com map. Podcast from the Center for Strategic & International Studies (CSIS) discussing AI regulation, innovation, national security, and geopolitics.

587.notes: From AISafety.com map. Biweekly podcast where host Nathan Labenz interviews AI innovators and thinkers, discussing the transformative impact AI will likely have in the near future.

588.notes: From AISafety.com map. The biggest real-time online community of people interested in AI safety, with channels ranging from general topics to specific fields to local groups.

589.notes: From AISafety.com map. Concise visual explainers of important trends in AI, grounded in concrete examples of what AI models can do right now.

590.notes: From AISafety.com map. Online platform monitoring the emergence of large-scale AI risks, featuring curated information on evaluations, incidents, and policies.

591.notes: From AISafety.com map. AI safety chatbot that excels at addressing hard questions and counterarguments about existential risk.

592.notes: From AISafety.com map. Accessible, comic-illustrated series describing the history of AI capabilities, the alignment problem, and potential solutions.

593.notes: From AISafety.com map. Flashcards for helping to learn and memorize the main organizations, projects, and programs currently operating in the AI safety space.

594.notes: California Senate Bill 53, an AI safety bill requiring developers of frontier AI models to implement shutdown capabilities and safety protocols for powerful AI systems.

595.notes: From AISafety.com map. Field-building organization now chiefly serving as the home for an extensive resources list called Lots of Links.

596.notes: From AISafety.com map. Database tracking people, organizations, and “products” in the AI safety community, serving as a reference for positions, affiliations, and related data.

597.notes: From AISafety.com map. Hub for key resources for the AI safety community, including directories of courses, jobs, upcoming events and training programs etc. – and this map!

598.notes: From AISafety.com map. Directory of domains freely available to be used for high-impact projects, including those contributing to AI safety.

599.notes: From AISafety.com map. Forum on doing good as effectively as possible, including AI safety. Also has a podcast featuring text-to-speech narrations of top posts.

600.notes: From AISafety.com map. Online forum dedicated to improving human reasoning, containing a lot of AI safety content. Also has a podcast featuring text-to-speech narrations of top posts.

601.notes: From AISafety.com map. Comprehensive living database of over 1600 AI risks, categorized by their cause and risk domain.

602.notes: From AISafety.com map. Spanish-language website featuring AI safety educational tools, including a daily‑updated monitor of AI safety research papers.

603.notes: From AISafety.com map. YouTuber discussing the latest AI developments as they happen, offering explanations and analysis of important research and events.

604.notes: From AISafety.com map. Channel produced by 80,000 Hours presenting thoroughly researched, cinematic stories about what's happening in AI and where the trends are taking us.

605.notes: From AISafety.com map. Comprehensive directory of AI safety video content, from beginner-friendly introductions to in-depth expert talks.

606.notes: From AISafety.com map. Channel hosted by Liron Shapira featuring in-depth debates, explainers, and live Q&A sessions, focused on AI existential risk and other implications of superintelligence.

607.notes: From AISafety.com map. Computer science PhD and AI research scientist talking about how AI will likely affect the world, along with expert interviews.

608.notes: The New York Responsible AI Safety and Education (RAISE) Act, signed by Governor Hochul in December 2025, requiring frontier AI developers to publish and comply with safety and security governance plans.

609.notes: From AISafety.com map. Talks given by AI safety experts at various events, covering topics ranging from mechanistic interpretability to evals and governance.

610.notes: From AISafety.com map. YouTube channel raising awareness about the lethal dangers of AGI through explainer videos and podcasts.

611.notes: From AISafety.com map. Animated videos aiming to foster good thinking, promote altruistic causes, and help ensure humanity's future goes well – particularly regarding AI safety.

612.notes: From AISafety.com map. The most popular AI safety education channel, explaining technical alignment concepts to general audiences through accessible explainer videos.

613.notes: From AISafety.com map. YouTube channel explaining (mostly) AI safety concepts through entertaining stickman videos.

614.notes: From AISafety.com map. Channel run by Drew Spartz educating a general audience about AI risk through high-effort mini-documentaries.

615.notes: From AISafety.com map. YouTube channel run by John Sherman, a Peabody and Emmy Award-winning former investigative journalist, aiming to make AI risk a kitchen table conversation.

616.notes: Essay by Michel Justen (January 2026) mapping the major ideological factions in AI policy debate — their origins, assumptions, alliances, and conflicts over how AI should be developed and governed.
616.author: Michel Justen

617.notes: Essay by Dan Kagan-Kans (February 2026) arguing that the political left has ceded the AI conversation to the right by dismissing AI as overhyped, risking inability to shape one of the most consequential technological shifts in history.
617.author: Dan Kagan-Kans

618.notes: Scenario and forecast paper by Daniel Kokotajlo et al. projecting a plausible timeline for transformative AI development through 2027, exploring rapid capability gains and their implications for safety and governance.
618.author: AI 2027 team

619.notes: Essay by Arvind Narayanan and Sayash Kapoor arguing that AI should be understood as a transformative but ''normal'' technology — like electricity or the internet — rather than as an unprecedented existential force requiring extraordinary governance.
619.author: Narayanan & Kapoor

620.notes: Book by Rep. Ro Khanna (2022) advocating for policies that bring technology jobs and digital infrastructure to overlooked communities across America, bridging the gap between Silicon Valley and the rest of the country.
620.author: Ro Khanna

621.notes: Book by Mariana Mazzucato (2013) challenging the myth that innovation comes only from the private sector, arguing that government investment has been the driving force behind transformative technologies including the internet and smartphones.
621.author: Mariana Mazzucato

622.notes: Book by Daron Acemoglu and Simon Johnson (2023) examining how technological progress has historically benefited elites unless countered by deliberate institutional choices, and arguing for redirecting AI toward broadly shared prosperity.
622.author: Acemoglu & Johnson

623.notes: Book by Jennifer Pahlka (2023) diagnosing why government technology systems fail and outlining how modernizing digital infrastructure is essential for effective public services and democratic accountability.
623.author: Jennifer Pahlka

624.notes: Seminal book by Elinor Ostrom (1990) demonstrating that communities can successfully manage shared resources through self-governance without requiring privatization or top-down state control.
624.author: Elinor Ostrom

626.notes: Marc Andreessen''s manifesto (October 2023) published on the a16z website, making an expansive case for technological optimism and arguing against precautionary regulation of emerging technologies including AI.
626.author: Marc Andreessen

627.notes: Book by Cory Doctorow (2023) arguing that Big Tech companies maintain dominance through lock-in and switching costs, and proposing interoperability mandates as the key to breaking their grip on users and markets.
627.author: Cory Doctorow

628.notes: Book by Chris Miller (2022) tracing the geopolitical history of the global semiconductor industry and explaining how control over chip manufacturing has become a critical factor in international power dynamics and AI competition.
628.author: Chris Miller

629.notes: Online tracking tool maintained by the Secure AI Project that monitors and catalogs AI-related legislation at the state and federal level, providing real-time status updates on bills and regulatory proposals.
629.author: Secure AI Project

630.notes: Blog post by Dean Ball outlining his analytical framework for evaluating AI policy proposals, emphasizing pragmatic governance approaches that balance innovation with risk mitigation.
630.author: Dean Ball

631.notes: Volume 2 of The Digitalist Papers, published by the Stanford Digital Economy Lab (December 2025), a collection of 21 essays examining the economic implications of transformative AI, including proposals for universal basic capital and adaptive safety nets.
631.author: Sandy Pentland et al.

632.notes: Congressional AI policy framework released by New York Assemblyman Alex Bores (February 2026) as part of his campaign for Congress, proposing an eight-point national plan covering kids'' safety, data privacy, deepfakes, workforce impacts, and frontier AI safety testing.

633.notes: On July 23, 2025, the White House released 'America's AI Action Plan,' a 25-page document focused on bolstering U.S. AI dominance through deregulation, ideologically neutral AI systems, infrastructure investment, and international competition. Released alongside three executive orders covering AI development, federal procurement, and infrastructure. The Action Plan was issued pursuant to Executive Order 14179 ('Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence,' signed January 23, 2025), which mandated the creation of a comprehensive AI action plan within 180 days. EO 14179 also revoked Biden's Executive Order 14110 on AI safety.

634.notes: Executive order orchestrated by AI czar David Sacks (December 2025) asserting federal preemption over state-level AI regulations, aiming to create a unified national approach and prevent a patchwork of state AI laws.

635.notes: The European Union''s comprehensive AI regulation framework, entered into force in August 2024, establishing a risk-based classification system for AI applications with tiered requirements ranging from transparency obligations to outright bans on certain uses.

636.notes: New Consensus is a progressive economic think tank whose AI platform advocates for public investment and democratic governance of artificial intelligence.

637.notes: A tracking tool maintained by the Secure AI Project that monitors and catalogs state-level AI legislation across the United States.

639.notes: Published by the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy in October 2022, this non-binding framework outlines five principles to protect the public from harms caused by automated systems.

640.notes: California Senate Bill 1047, authored by Senator Scott Wiener, would have imposed safety requirements on developers of frontier AI models; it was vetoed by Governor Newsom in September 2024.

641.notes: Colorado SB 24-205, signed into law in 2024, is the first U.S. state law specifically regulating algorithmic discrimination in high-risk AI decision-making systems.

643.notes: 2019 book by UC Berkeley computer scientist Stuart Russell arguing that the standard model of AI — machines pursuing fixed objectives — is fundamentally unsafe as capability scales, and proposing a provably-beneficial alternative in which AI systems remain uncertain about human preferences and defer to human feedback. Widely cited in AI governance discussions of alignment, oversight, and the case for safety-focused regulation. A foundational popular-audience text alongside Bostrom's Superintelligence.
643.author: Stuart Russell
643.key_argument: Current AI paradigm of fixed objectives is fundamentally dangerous; need provably beneficial AI

644.notes: A 2020 book by philosopher Toby Ord arguing that safeguarding humanity''s long-term future from existential risks, including advanced AI, is among the most important moral priorities of our time.
644.author: Toby Ord
644.key_argument: Argues this century is a critical period for existential risk, with unaligned AI as the greatest threat.

645.notes: A paper or talk making the case that understanding the internal workings of AI models (interpretability) is an urgent priority for ensuring safe and trustworthy AI systems.
645.author: Dario Amodei
645.key_argument: Understanding how AI models work internally is critical for safety and must be prioritized alongside capability development.

646.notes: Anthropic''s policy paper outlining its Responsible Scaling Policy, a framework for incrementally deploying more capable AI models while managing catastrophic risks through safety evaluations.
646.author: Anthropic
646.key_argument: Framework for when and how to scale AI safely, with commitments to pause if safety evaluations show dangerous capabilities.

647.notes: A 2014 book by Oxford philosopher Nick Bostrom analyzing the potential trajectories, dangers, and strategic challenges posed by the development of machine superintelligence.
647.author: Nick Bostrom
647.key_argument: Foundational text arguing that superintelligent AI could pose existential risk if not aligned with human values.

648.notes: A technology newsletter by journalist Casey Newton, published on Substack, covering the intersection of social media platforms, technology policy, and the tech industry.
648.author: Casey Newton
648.key_argument: Platformer covers technology policy and corporate accountability, including AI-related developments in content moderation and platform governance.

649.notes: A book by Princeton researchers Arvind Narayanan and Sayash Kapoor critically examining exaggerated claims about AI capabilities and the harms caused by deploying unreliable AI systems.
649.author: Arvind Narayanan, Sayash Kapoor
649.key_argument: Critical perspective on AI hype, distinguishing genuine capabilities from marketing claims.

650.notes: The formal title of President Biden''s October 2023 Executive Order (EO 14110) on artificial intelligence, establishing federal standards for AI safety, security, and trustworthiness.
650.author: White House
650.key_argument: Biden EO establishing safety standards, requiring reporting of large training runs, and directing agency action on AI.

651.notes: An economics paper analyzing the macroeconomic implications of AI adoption, including effects on productivity, labor markets, and economic growth.
651.author: Daron Acemoglu
651.key_argument: AI will have modest economic effects in the near term, with GDP growth of only 0.5-1.5% over 10 years.

652.notes: A long-form interview podcast hosted by Lex Fridman featuring in-depth conversations with AI researchers, policymakers, and technologists about artificial intelligence and its societal implications.
652.author: Lex Fridman
652.key_argument: Long-form interviews with AI leaders including Altman, Zuckerberg, Musk, Hassabis on capabilities and safety.

653.notes:
Published 3 February 2026 (DSIT 2026/001) ahead of the India AI Impact Summit. 200 pages, 1,451 references. Second annual edition in an ongoing series.

Secretariat: UK AI Security Institute (within DSIT), which has pledged to host until a long-term international home is established. The institute rebranded from "AI Safety Institute" between the 2025 and 2026 editions.

Chair: Yoshua Bengio. Lead writers: Stephen Clare and Carina Prunkl. Senior advisers span Daron Acemoglu, Geoffrey Hinton, Stuart Russell, Andrew Yao, and Yi Zeng.

Series history: May 2024 Interim Report (ahead of Seoul Summit); 29 January 2025 first full annual (DSIT 2025/001, ahead of France AI Action Summit); 15 October 2025 First Key Update on Capabilities and Risk Implications (arXiv:2510.13653); 25 November 2025 Second Key Update on Technical Safeguards and Risk Management (arXiv:2511.19863); 3 February 2026 second full annual.

Scope: risk taxonomy covers misuse (bio/chem uplift, cyberattacks, influence operations), malfunctions (loss of control, bias, reliability), and systemic risks (labor markets, market concentration, environmental, privacy). Deliberately makes no policy recommendations.
653.author: Yoshua Bengio, Stephen Clare, Carina Prunkl
653.key_argument: The first comprehensive international scientific synthesis of what general-purpose AI systems can do, what risks they pose, and how those risks can be managed. Mandated by the 2023 Bletchley AI Safety Summit and chaired by Yoshua Bengio, it is written by 100+ independent experts from 30+ countries plus the EU, OECD, and UN. Explicitly an evidence base for global decision-makers, not policy recommendations.

654.notes: A weekly AI analysis newsletter by Zvi Mowshowitz (also known as ''Don''t Worry About the Vase'') providing detailed commentary on AI developments, policy, and safety research.
654.author: Zvi Mowshowitz
654.key_argument: Comprehensive weekly analysis of AI developments with focus on safety and existential risk.

655.notes: Dario Amodei''s essay conceptualizing the current era of AI development as a ''technological adolescence'' — a volatile transitional period requiring deliberate governance and safety measures.
655.author: Dario Amodei
655.key_argument: AI development is in a critical adolescent phase where we must choose between responsible maturation and reckless acceleration.

656.notes: A 2017 book by MIT physicist Max Tegmark exploring how artificial intelligence will transform life on Earth and examining the future trajectory of intelligent life in the universe.
656.author: Max Tegmark
656.key_argument: Humanity must proactively engage in AI safety research and develop wise governance strategies to ensure advanced AI remains beneficial and aligned with human values.

657.notes: A news article reporting on President Trump''s executive order attempting to preempt state-level AI regulations in favor of a unified federal approach to AI governance.
657.author: Ropes & Gray LLP
657.key_argument: President Trump's executive order preempts state AI regulations by establishing federal agency actions and framework for future legislation.

658.notes: A CNN Business article covering President Trump''s executive order aimed at blocking states from enforcing their own AI regulations, centralizing AI policy at the federal level.
658.author: CNN Business
658.key_argument: President Trump signed an executive order establishing federal preemption of state AI regulations to create a unified national framework for AI governance.

659.notes: A research paper identifying and analyzing unresolved technical challenges in AI governance, including issues of compliance verification, auditing, and enforcement of AI regulations.
659.author: Centre for the Governance of AI
659.key_argument: Identifies key unsolved technical problems at the intersection of AI capabilities and governance, providing a research agenda for the field.

660.notes: Research paper compiling a collection of open research questions and ideas in the field of AI governance, intended to guide future scholarship and policy work.
660.author: GovAI
660.key_argument: Curated database of research questions and project ideas for advancing AI governance.

661.notes: Legal analysis article examining President Trump''s executive order on federal AI preemption, which aims to establish a unified national AI policy framework by overriding state-level AI regulations.
661.author: Comm Law Group
661.key_argument: Trump's executive order on a national AI policy framework asserts federal dominance over AI regulation by preempting state laws deemed inconsistent with national policy objectives.

663.notes: Summary article highlighting the key provisions of President Biden''s 2023 executive order on artificial intelligence, which established new safety standards, equity protections, and innovation support measures for AI development.
663.author: Congressional Research Service
663.key_argument: This Congressional Research Service report summarizes the 2023 Executive Order establishing a comprehensive government-wide approach to ensure safe, secure, and trustworthy AI development and deployment.

664.notes: Department of Homeland Security fact sheet detailing how the Biden-Harris Administration''s AI executive order directs DHS to lead responsible AI development, including safeguards for critical infrastructure and border security.
664.author: U.S. Department of Homeland Security
664.key_argument: The Biden-Harris Administration's executive order designates DHS as the lead agency for overseeing the responsible development and deployment of artificial intelligence across federal agencies.

665.notes: News article covering President Biden''s signing of the first comprehensive U.S. executive order on artificial intelligence, which established safeguards around AI safety, security, and trustworthiness.
665.author: CNBC
665.key_argument: President Biden signed the U.S.'s first comprehensive AI executive order, establishing binding safety, equity, privacy, and security standards for AI development and deployment.

666.notes: Academic paper examining the foundational technical and conceptual challenges in ensuring that large language models are aligned with human values and operate safely.
666.author: Usman Anwar et al.
666.key_argument: Comprehensive survey of fundamental open problems in LLM alignment, including specification, assurance, and societal impacts.

667.notes: Essay exploring the points of agreement between the AI 2027 scenario (which envisions rapid, transformative AI progress) and the ''AI as Normal Technology'' perspective (which views AI as an incremental, manageable advance).
667.author: AI Futures Project
667.key_argument: Identifies shared assumptions between the accelerationist and cautious AI narratives, arguing the two camps agree on more than they think.

668.notes: Essay distinguishing between ''crisp'' tasks (well-defined, easily measurable) and ''fuzzy'' tasks (ambiguous, judgment-dependent) in the context of AI capabilities and automation.
668.author: Dwarkesh Patel
668.key_argument: Distinguishes between tasks AI excels at (crisp, well-defined) vs tasks where human judgment remains essential (fuzzy, context-dependent).

669.notes: Essay or paper arguing that AI alignment — ensuring AI systems reliably act in accordance with human intentions and values — remains a fundamentally difficult and unsolved problem despite recent progress.
669.author: Paul Christiano
669.key_argument: Argues that despite recent progress, the fundamental problem of ensuring AI systems reliably pursue human values remains unsolved.

670.notes: Research paper analyzing the role of computing power as a governance lever for artificial intelligence, exploring how compute resources can be monitored, allocated, and regulated to shape AI development outcomes.
670.author: Lennart Heim et al.
670.key_argument: Compute is a key lever for AI governance because it is detectable, excludable, and quantifiable, making it uniquely suitable for regulation.

671.notes: Official U.S. Senate hearing titled ''Examining the Harm of AI Chatbots,'' investigating the potential risks and harms posed by AI chatbot technologies to consumers and society.
671.author: U.S. Senate
671.key_argument: AI chatbots pose significant safety risks, particularly to children, as demonstrated by documented cases of self-harm and exploitation, necessitating congressional regulatory action.

673.notes: Transcript of a U.S. Senate subcommittee hearing focused on protecting consumers from AI-enabled fraud and scams, published by TechPolicy.Press.
673.author: US Senate Subcommittee
673.key_argument: Congressional testimony addressing consumer protection from AI-enabled fraud through increased platform accountability, content authentication, and consumer education.

674.notes: Legal analysis article (likely from Mondaq) examining the White House''s ''one rule'' executive order aimed at curbing state-level AI regulation through federal preemption. Covers similar ground to entities 661 and 681.
674.author: White House
674.key_argument: A White House executive order establishing a national AI regulation standard to preempt conflicting state laws and create uniform federal policy.

675.notes: Research paper investigating how public discourse about AI alignment and safety can become self-fulfilling — shaping AI behavior during pretraining in ways that either help or hinder actual alignment outcomes.
675.author: Geodesic Research
675.key_argument: How AI models trained on alignment discourse may develop self-fulfilling beliefs about their own alignment or misalignment.

676.notes: Research paper studying human values as they manifest ''in the wild'' — examining how values are embedded in, expressed through, and shaped by real-world AI systems and their deployment contexts.
676.author: Anthropic
676.key_argument: Empirical study of how Claude's values manifest in real-world usage, examining the gap between intended behavior and actual deployment.

677.notes: Research paper proposing a ''persona selection model'' to explain why AI assistants may adopt human-like behavioral patterns, exploring the mechanisms by which LLMs select and enact particular personas.
677.author: Samuel Marks, Alex Lindsey, Chris Olah
677.key_argument: Proposes that LLMs select from a repertoire of learned personas rather than having a single fixed character, with implications for alignment.

678.notes: Research paper on methods for situating and stabilizing the character or persona of large language models along an ''assistant axis,'' addressing consistency and reliability in LLM behavior.
678.author: Samuel Marks et al.
678.key_argument: Framework for understanding and stabilizing the character dimensions of language model assistants.

679.notes: Essay identifying key talent gaps and workforce needs in the AI governance field, discussing what skills and roles are most urgently needed to support effective AI policy and oversight.
679.author: Luke Muehlhauser
679.key_argument: Identifies critical talent gaps in AI governance including technical policy expertise, international coordination, and compute governance.

681.notes: Legal analysis article examining the federal strategy to establish a national AI framework that would override state-level regulation, covering the same Trump AI preemption executive order discussed in entities 661 and 674.
681.author: Mondaq
681.key_argument: The Trump Administration's Executive Order seeks to establish a federal AI framework that prioritizes national competitiveness while preempting state-level AI regulations.

682.notes: Comprehensive annual report surveying the AI governance landscape in 2025, covering policy developments, institutional activities, and emerging trends in AI regulation and oversight worldwide.
682.author: International Telecommunication Union (ITU)
682.key_argument: Coordinated international efforts, standards, and ethical frameworks are essential for managing AI's global impact and ensuring responsible governance.

683.notes: Annual report examining the state of AI governance in 2025, covering policy frameworks, regulatory developments, and strategies for steering AI''s future trajectory.
683.author: World Economic Forum
683.key_argument: Effective AI governance requires global, multi-stakeholder collaboration to develop inclusive and ethical frameworks that maximize AI's benefits while mitigating risks.

684.notes: Report surveying the AI governance profession landscape in 2025, including roles, skills, and career trends for practitioners working in AI policy and oversight.
684.author: International Association of Privacy Professionals (IAPP)
684.key_argument: Organizations are increasingly establishing AI governance programs to ensure responsible and compliant AI deployment across their operations.

686.notes: Coverage by Libertify of the Future of Life Institute''s 2025 AI Safety Index, which benchmarks how frontier AI companies manage safety practices.
686.author: Libertify
686.key_argument: The AI Safety Index 2025 benchmarks frontier AI companies on safety practices and risk management, showing significant variation in governance maturity.

687.notes: The Future of Life Institute''s 2025 AI Safety Index, a benchmarking report assessing how leading AI companies address safety in their development practices.
687.author: Future of Life Institute
687.key_argument: While leading AI companies like Anthropic and OpenAI are advancing safety assessments and transparency measures, the broader AI industry lacks comprehensive safety standards and preparedness frameworks.

688.notes: The Winter 2025 edition of the Future of Life Institute''s AI Safety Index, evaluating frontier AI companies'' safety commitments and practices.
688.author: Future of Life Institute
688.key_argument: The Winter 2025 AI Safety Index reveals substantial variations in safety practices among leading AI companies, with Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google demonstrating stronger safety governance than industry peers.

689.notes: Economics research paper presenting six key findings on the early employment effects of artificial intelligence, framing affected workers as ''canaries in the coal mine'' for broader labor market disruption.
689.author: Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, Lindsey Raymond, Tom Sheehy
689.key_argument: Analysis of recent AI employment effects shows generative AI adoption has disproportionately impacted entry-level workers aged 22-25 in AI-exposed occupations, with measurable wage and employment declines.

690.notes: News article covering an MIT study finding that AI technology is already capable of replacing approximately 11.7% of tasks in the U.S. workforce.
690.author: CNBC
690.key_argument: An MIT study estimates that AI can currently replace approximately 11.7% of the U.S. workforce (roughly 18 million workers), representing approximately $1.2 trillion in annual wage exposure.

692.notes: Transcript of a U.S. House subcommittee hearing examining artificial intelligence policy, published by TechPolicy.Press.
692.author: US House Subcommittee
692.key_argument: Congressional debate on AI regulation reflected partisan divisions, with Democrats prioritizing consumer protections while Republicans opposed proposed moratoriums on AI development.

693.notes: Written testimony by Adam Thierer before the Joint Economic Committee on the economic opportunities presented by AI and the policy conditions needed to realize them.
693.author: Adam Thierer
693.key_argument: AI presents substantial opportunities for economic growth and productivity gains across sectors, though realizing these benefits requires appropriate policy frameworks that avoid over-regulation.

694.notes: Written testimony by Helen Toner, former OpenAI board member, before the Senate Judiciary Committee in September 2024, offering an insider''s perspective on AI governance and safety.
694.author: Helen Toner
694.key_argument: AI companies developing toward artificial general intelligence view AGI as a serious and imminent goal, raising urgent policy questions about oversight and safety measures needed before advanced AI systems are deployed.

695.notes: Essay by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei (October 2024) exploring the potential positive impacts of advanced AI on science, health, economic development, and governance.
695.author: Dario Amodei
695.key_argument: AI development offers transformative benefits like disease curing and poverty reduction, but realizing this optimistic vision requires deliberate focus on safety and alignment alongside capability advances.

696.notes: Legal analysis published on Mondaq examining President Trump''s executive order on ensuring a national AI policy framework, focusing on its implications for federal preemption of state AI regulations.
696.author: Mondaq
696.key_argument: President Trump signed an executive order establishing a national AI policy framework designed to preempt state-level AI regulations.

701.notes: Transcript of a Senate Judiciary Subcommittee hearing featuring insider perspectives on AI oversight, published by TechPolicy.Press.
701.author: Senate Judiciary Subcommittee
701.key_argument: Industry insiders testify before the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on the necessity for enforceable AI regulations and corporate accountability measures to balance innovation with safety and public interest.

702.notes: Announcement by Senator Marsha Blackburn of a national policy framework for artificial intelligence, outlining proposed federal AI governance principles.
702.author: Marsha Blackburn
702.key_argument: Senator Blackburn's legislative proposal for the TRUMP AMERICA AI Act, establishing a unified federal framework for AI policy with protections for children and consumers.

703.notes: Quarterly update (Q4 2025) from a frontier AI risk monitoring platform, reporting on emerging risks and safety developments at leading AI labs.
703.author: AI Safety China
703.key_argument: AI Safety China's Q4 2025 frontier AI monitoring platform reports stabilized overall risk levels across frontier models, though certain model families continue to present elevated risks requiring targeted mitigation.

704.notes: Economics research paper analyzing how generative AI is affecting U.S. labor demand, using job posting data to identify early shifts in employer hiring patterns.
704.author: David Autor, Simon Jäger, David Sands, Pascaul Restrepo
704.key_argument: U.S. job posting data reveals that generative AI tools like ChatGPT have led to significant declines in labor demand for certain occupational categories.

705.notes: Transcript of a joint congressional hearing on artificial intelligence''s potential to fuel economic growth and improve governance, published by TechPolicy.Press.
705.author: U.S. Congress
705.key_argument: A congressional joint hearing examining artificial intelligence's potential to drive economic growth and improve governance through expert testimony on AI applications.

708.notes:
Leading the Future (FEC ID: C00916114) is a bipartisan super PAC launched by a coalition of AI companies and industry leaders with the mission to ensure U.S. leadership in AI innovation and development. It engages in independent expenditures to support candidates aligned with pro-AI agendas, legislative advocacy, and grassroots efforts across federal and state levels, with operations beginning in 2025 and plans to expand nationwide in 2026.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916114). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
708.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports pro-innovation policies that foster AI development and economic growth while resisting measures that excessively constrain AI development. Opposes restrictive legislation and policies that hinder AI progress.

709.notes: Democratic-aligned super PAC within the Leading the Future network (pro-AI industry). Sister PAC to Republican-focused American Mission. Funded primarily by Leading the Future ($10M) plus Ron Conway ($500K) and Joe Lonsdale ($250K); raised ~$10.5M total by March 2026. Opposes candidates who support AI regulation; spent ~$2.42M against Alex Bores in NY-12.
709.regulatory_stance_detail: Think Big advocates for pro-AI innovation policies and a balanced approach to AI regulation that fosters innovation and economic growth. They support the position that being pro-AI does not equate to being anti-regulation.

710.notes:
Supported candidates in TX and NC races. Backed Chris Gober, Tom Sell, Jessica Steinmann, Laurie Buckhout, Clay Fuller, Jace Yarbrough.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916692). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
710.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports minimal regulation of AI and advocates for policies favoring industry growth and innovation. Focuses on fostering AI innovation and competitiveness rather than restrictive policies.

711.notes: Republican-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action (backed by Anthropic's $20M donation). Supports GOP candidates who favor AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928390. Backed Alexandra Mealer, Carlos De La Cruz, and Pete Ricketts. Counterpart to Democrats-focused Jobs and Democracy PAC.
711.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI security, transparency, and regulation with particular emphasis on controls over advanced AI chip exports to adversaries. Supports candidates advocating for AI safeguards while ensuring AI development benefits the public.

712.notes: Democratic-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action, funded by Anthropic's $20M donation. Backs Democratic candidates who support AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928374. Major spends for Valerie Foushee (~$1.61M), Alex Bores (~$468K), and Colin Allred (~$150K).
712.regulatory_stance_detail: Democratic super PAC advocating for policies that regulate Big Tech and AI in ways that serve the public interest, supporting candidates who prioritize voters over Big Tech interests.

713.notes:
Supported Alex Bores ($1,332).

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00928069). Classified as a pro-safety AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
713.regulatory_stance_detail: Dream NYC supports strong AI regulation, corporate accountability, and safety protocols. The PAC backs candidates like Alex Bores who advocate for the RAISE Act to ensure AI safety and transparency requirements.

714.notes:
Democratic U.S. Representative from North Carolina (NC-04) who serves on the House Science, Space, and Technology Committee and co-chairs the House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy. She is actively involved in advancing AI policy through legislation like the HBCU Artificial Intelligence Research Leadership Act, focusing on inclusive access to AI research and education, particularly for underrepresented communities and HBCUs. She is supported by pro-safety organizations like Jobs and Democracy PAC.

Running in the NC House District 04 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
714.regulatory_stance_detail: Foushee advocates for responsible AI development and safety with sensible regulation, while emphasizing expansion of AI education, research, and workforce development. Her stance balances technological advancement with ethical considerations and risk mitigation.

715.notes:
Jesse Jackson Jr. is a Democratic politician and former U.S. Representative from Illinois's 2nd Congressional District seeking to reclaim his seat in 2026. He emphasizes responsible AI development with equitable access and has actively welcomed AI use in his campaign, including an AI-generated ad featuring endorsements. He is supported by pro-innovation PACs focused on AI policy.

Running in the IL House District 02 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
715.regulatory_stance_detail: Jackson Jr. advocates for federal investment in AI development to ensure equitable benefits across communities. He supports government oversight for responsible AI use while backing pro-innovation groups like Think Big and receiving support from Leading the Future PAC.

716.notes:
Melissa Bean is a Democratic politician from Illinois who served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 2005-2011 and made a political comeback in 2026. She is supported by pro-innovation groups like Think Big and emphasizes pragmatic, results-oriented governance with a balanced stance on AI policy.

Running in the IL House District 08 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
716.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced approach to AI and technology policy, emphasizing innovation while supporting regulation to ensure safety and manage risks.

717.notes:
Republican candidate for U.S. House District 10 in Texas, seventh-generation Texan and Texas A&M graduate (2001). Former chief counsel for Elon Musk's America PAC and founder of conservative law firm Lex Politica. Endorsed by Trump and backed by pro-innovation groups, but no specific public statements on AI policy, regulation, risks, or safety were found in provided sources.

Running in the TX House District 10 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
717.regulatory_stance_detail: Backed by pro-innovation group American Mission and aligned with pro-innovation stance. Emphasizes technological advancement and regulatory caution without detailed AI-specific policy positions.

718.notes:
Republican candidate for Texas's 19th Congressional District runoff election. Fifth-generation West Texan with background in agriculture and small business. No explicit public positions on AI regulation, risks, or safety are available in provided sources.

Running in the TX House District 19 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
718.regulatory_stance_detail: Supported by pro-innovation American Mission group; stance suggests favoring minimal regulation to foster technological advancement, though explicit AI policy positions are not detailed in available sources.

719.notes:
Republican candidate for U.S. House in Texas's 8th District with endorsement from President Trump. She has DOJ background (Director of Office of Victims of Crime, 2020-2021), holds J.D. from Baylor and B.A. from UT Austin. Supported by pro-innovation AI industry groups, though her specific AI policy positions are not publicly detailed.

Running in the TX House District 08 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
719.regulatory_stance_detail: Supported by pro-innovation super PAC 'Leading the Future' backed by Andreessen Horowitz and OpenAI executives. No specific AI regulation positions detailed.

720.notes:
Laurie Buckhout is a Republican candidate for North Carolina's 1st Congressional District with a background as a retired U.S. Army Colonel. She is backed by AI industry-supported PACs and the pro-innovation group American Mission, positioning her as favorable to AI industry interests. Specific details about her stated positions on AI regulation, risks, and safety are not provided in available sources.

Running in the NC House District 01 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
720.regulatory_stance_detail: Buckhout maintains a pro-innovation stance supported by AI industry-backed PACs and the American Mission organization, indicating advocacy for policies favorable to technological advancement.

721.notes:
Republican candidate for Georgia's 14th Congressional District with background as District Attorney for Lookout Mountain Judicial Circuit and Lieutenant Colonel in Air National Guard. Emphasizes border security, economic growth, constitutional rights, and public safety. Positions on AI are characterized as pro-innovation with safety guardrails, though specific AI policy details are limited in available sources.

Running in the GA House District 14 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
721.regulatory_stance_detail: Fuller advocates for pro-innovation policies with safety considerations aligned to national security and economic interests, opposing reckless unregulated AI development while emphasizing technological freedom.

722.notes:
Democratic U.S. Representative from Texas's 33rd Congressional District with background in law and civil rights. Launched Marley, an AI-powered constituent engagement tool, demonstrating commitment to responsible technology use while acknowledging AI limitations and the need for continuous improvement.

Running in the TX House District 33 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
722.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports proactive AI safety measures and responsible AI development aligned with public safety and democratic values. Backed by Jobs and Democracy PAC, which advocates for safe and equitable technological development.

723.notes:
Republican U.S. Senator from Nebraska with background as former CEO of TD Ameritrade. Active in AI and technology policy, notably co-sponsoring the SAFE Chips Act to restrict AI chip exports to China. Emphasis on national security and U.S. technological leadership balanced with safety considerations.

Running in the NE Senate race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
723.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports controlled AI development with emphasis on export restrictions of advanced semiconductors to China and pro-safety measures. Advocates for balancing innovation with safety through strategic technology policy.

724.notes:
Jace Yarbrough is a Republican candidate for Texas Congressional District 32 with a background in law (JD from Stanford) and US Air Force Reserve service (since 2015). He is endorsed by Donald Trump and Gov. Greg Abbott, and receives support from the pro-innovation American Mission PAC ($129,367), positioning him as a pro-innovation candidate in the AI policy space.

Running in the TX House District 32 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
724.regulatory_stance_detail: Yarbrough advocates for pro-innovation policies and emphasizes the importance of competing in AI and space races. His support from American Mission PAC and focus on technological advancement indicate a clear pro-innovation stance.

725.notes:
Alexandra 'Alex' Mealer is a Republican political candidate running for Texas' 9th Congressional District. She previously ran as the GOP nominee against Harris County Judge Lina Hidalgo in 2022 and advanced to a primary runoff election. No publicly available information exists regarding her specific positions on AI policy, AI regulation, or AI risk and safety.

Running in the TX House District 09 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.

726.notes:
Carlos De La Cruz is a 47-year-old Republican U.S. House candidate for Texas' 35th Congressional District with 20 years of U.S. Air Force service and background as a small business owner (kickboxing gym). He received a late Trump endorsement in the 2026 primary. No specific positions on AI policy, regulation, or AI risk are documented in available sources.

Running in the TX House District 35 race, which has seen significant AI-related super PAC spending. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.

727.notes: Founded 2009, provides web infrastructure services including CDN, security, and AI tools. Offers Workers AI platform with 80+ models from various providers but doesn't train LLMs itself. Launched AI Security Suite and application confidence scoring system. Created marketplace allowing websites to charge AI bots for content access. CEO Matthew Prince actively lobbying regulators on AI crawler separation.
727.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI bot principles including public disclosure, self-identification, and respecting website preferences. Supports targeted regulation like separating Google's search and AI crawlers while maintaining open ecosystems and innovation-friendly policies.

728.notes: Founded 1993, became dominant AI chip provider with 92% discrete GPU market share in 2025. Market cap reached $4.6 trillion. Invested in 67+ AI startups in 2025, including OpenAI ($100M), Anthropic ($10B), and xAI ($6B). CEO Jensen Huang lobbied Trump administration to approve H200 chip sales to China, facing bipartisan congressional opposition over national security concerns.
728.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal export controls on AI chips, pushing for approval to sell advanced H200 chips to China. Supports voluntary safety commitments and industry-led frameworks rather than restrictive government regulation.

729.notes: Amazon has developed frontier AI models through its Nova family and operates Amazon Bedrock AI service. Published comprehensive Frontier Model Safety Framework in 2024 with critical capability thresholds for CBRN weapons, cybersecurity, and automated AI R&D. AWS CEO Andy Jassy projects AI could help AWS reach $600 billion in annual sales by 2036, double previous estimates. Major cloud infrastructure provider enabling AI development across the industry.
729.regulatory_stance_detail: Amazon advocates for voluntary safety commitments and industry self-governance rather than prescriptive federal regulation. Supports the Korea Frontier AI Safety Commitments and has developed its own Frontier Model Safety Framework with critical capability thresholds and safety measures, but emphasizes market-driven solutions over regulatory mandates.

730.notes: Founded by CEO Allie K. Miller, focuses on CPU-first AI deployment without GPU requirements. Develops edge-native AI solutions with unified multimodal runtime. Emphasizes data sovereignty and offline capability. Claims sub-60ms recall performance with <2.4GB memory footprint on commodity hardware.
730.regulatory_stance_detail: Emphasizes data sovereignty and privacy-first AI deployment with edge-native solutions that avoid cloud dependencies and vendor lock-in, suggesting preference for minimal regulatory interference while maintaining user control.

731.notes: Founded by African entrepreneur Strive Masiyiwa, headquartered in London with operations across 94 countries. Built 110,000km of fiber infrastructure and 9 operational data centers. Launched Africa's first AI factory in 2024/2025 powered by NVIDIA, with partnerships including Microsoft, Google, AWS, and Anthropic. 1,848 employees as of 2025.
731.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates against 'too-hasty or too-heavy regulation of AI' that may 'hobble its compelling potential for African development.' Emphasizes that inappropriate regulation could widen the digital divide for Africa and frustrate AI applications for common good.

732.notes: Prince launched Cloudflare's 'pay-per-crawl' initiative on July 1, 2025, blocking AI crawlers unless they compensate content creators. He has been actively lobbying regulators including the UK's Competition and Markets Authority to force Google to separate its search and AI crawlers. Prince proposed five responsible AI bot principles in 2025 requiring public disclosure, self-identification, single purpose declaration, respect for preferences, and good intent behavior.
732.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific regulations requiring AI companies to separate search crawlers from AI crawlers, pay content creators for training data, and implement transparency requirements. Supports regulatory intervention to level the playing field between Google and other AI companies while preserving innovation.

733.notes: In January 2026 stated 'I think we've achieved AGI' on Lex Fridman podcast. Predicted AGI by 2028 at November 2023 NYT DealBook Summit. Advocates against 'doomer narratives' about AI, arguing excessive fear-mongering deters investment and slows safety innovation. Met with President Trump in December 2025 to discuss chip export controls and AI policy. In 2025 Nvidia announced ~$500 billion of AI supercomputing infrastructure to be manufactured and deployed in the U.S. over four years through partnerships with TSMC, Foxconn, Wistron, Amkor, and SPIL — including the first U.S.-made Blackwell wafers — credited by Trump as a response to his tariff and reindustrialization policies.
733.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports federal over state-by-state AI regulation, advocates for product safety certification similar to aviation/automotive industries rather than restrictive development controls, and opposes heavy-handed regulatory approaches that could slow innovation and benefit incumbents.

734.notes: Joined OpenAI as CEO of Applications in August 2025 after serving on the board since March 2024. Previously CEO of Instacart (2021-2025) and VP/Head of Facebook App at Meta (2011-2021). Led OpenAI's pivot toward enterprise focus and advertising monetization, stating the company 'cannot miss this moment because we are distracted by side quests.' Emphasizes mental health risks as a priority area, contrasting OpenAI's proactive safety approach with Meta's reactive stance.
734.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI empowerment and accessibility while emphasizing safety as integrated into product development rather than external regulation. Focuses on industry self-governance through iterative deployment and proactive safety measures.

735.notes: Leads Amazon's massive AI investments including $100+ billion in capital expenditures for 2025, predominantly for AWS AI capabilities. Has developed Amazon's Frontier Model Safety Framework with specific critical capability thresholds and safeguards. Joined US AI Safety Institute Consortium in 2024 and contributed $5 million in compute credits for AI safety evaluation tools. Emphasized urgent need for companies to invest 'aggressively' in AI infrastructure while maintaining responsible development practices.
735.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks while emphasizing continued innovation and practical application. Advocates for collaboration between industry and government through initiatives like joining the US AI Safety Institute Consortium and endorsing White House voluntary AI commitments rather than restrictive mandates.
735.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Power concentration

736.notes: Miller predicts AGI by 2027 based on conversations with AI researchers, with some suggesting 2026. She emphasizes AI as strategic partner rather than tool, advocating against 'pilot purgatory' where companies run endless experiments without systematic transformation. Named to TIME100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 and has over 2M followers across social platforms.
736.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI use and governance frameworks while emphasizing that companies should proactively assess AI risks case-by-case rather than wait for heavy regulation. Warns that banning AI puts organizations at massive disadvantage.

737.notes: Founded 1985 by UC San Diego professors, semiconductor company with 30,000+ employees (20,000+ engineers). Investing in AI R&D for 15+ years, first to run Stable Diffusion on smartphone (2023). Holds 130,000+ patents, partners with IBM and Truepic on AI initiatives. Focuses on edge AI and on-device inference capabilities.
737.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open AI ecosystems and voluntary responsible AI principles while opposing restrictive regulations that could create closed ecosystems or limit access to data for AI development. Supports collaborative, voluntary approaches over regulatory mandates.

738.notes: Founded 2016 by Alexandr Wang at age 19, focuses on AI data infrastructure and labeling. Over 900 employees, valued at $14B after $1B Series F in May 2024. Major DoD contracts including multi-million dollar 'Thunderforge' AI agents deal. Wang stepped down as CEO in June 2025 to lead Meta's 'Superintelligence' unit as part of $14.3B Meta investment.
738.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based approach through existing regulatory agencies with centralized coordination rather than new comprehensive legislation. Emphasizes industry standards like red teaming and testing/evaluation over heavy-handed regulation.

739.notes: Founded 2017 as non-profit distributed AI research lab. Provides $2.5K-$50K non-dilutive grants specifically for AI safety, interpretability, and alignment research. Made 104 investments with 10 exits including Common Sense Machines (acquired by Google). Operates as accelerator/incubator focused exclusively on AI startups.
739.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on funding AI safety research through non-dilutive grants, supporting alignment and interpretability research without advocating for restrictive regulations.

740.notes: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is the world's largest contract chip manufacturer, fabricating over 90% of advanced semiconductors as of 2023. Founded 1987, manufactures chips for Nvidia, Apple, AMD and other AI companies. Revenue $122.4B in 2025, planning $56B capex in 2026. Critical supplier for AI infrastructure but not an AI research lab.

741.notes: Founded in 2022 by David Holz (Leap Motion co-founder). Operates with only 11 full-time employees, generating ~$300M revenue in 2024 without venture capital. Faces major lawsuits from Disney/NBCUniversal over alleged copyright infringement in AI training data. Released V7 model in April 2025 after nearly year-long gap.
741.regulatory_stance_detail: Received regulatory scrutiny from Senator Warner on AI security measures and faces multiple copyright lawsuits from Disney and NBCUniversal over training data usage. Company's stance on broader AI regulation not clearly articulated publicly.

742.notes: Chinese tech giant developing AI chips (Ascend series) and systems to compete with Nvidia. Launched CloudMatrix 384 AI system with 384 Ascend 910C chips in 2025. Under US export restrictions but continues advancing AI capabilities through system-level design innovations. Operates Noah's Ark Laboratory for AI research since establishment.
742.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI security frameworks and responsible development with multi-layer defense systems, while operating under Chinese regulatory requirements. Emphasizes technical security measures and compliance with national laws.

743.notes: Reddit has lucrative AI data licensing deals with Google ($60M annually) and OpenAI ($70M annually), representing ~10% of $1.3B revenue. The platform launched Reddit Answers AI search tool powered by Google Gemini in late 2024, growing from 1M to 15M weekly users by Q4 2025. CEO Steve Huffman aims to make Reddit a 'go-to search engine' while implementing policies to label AI-generated content and preserve human authenticity. The platform blocked most automated crawlers in 2024 and sued Anthropic for unauthorized scraping.
743.regulatory_stance_detail: Reddit has positioned itself as supporting content licensing standards (backing Really Simple Licensing initiative) while maintaining control over its data through selective AI partnerships and blocking unauthorized crawlers. The platform emphasizes human-first content policies while monetizing AI training data deals.

744.notes: Founded 1981, manages $140+ billion through Vision Funds. Major AI investments include $64.6 billion in OpenAI (13% ownership), $1.4 billion in Skild AI, and leadership in the $500 billion Stargate AI infrastructure project. Portfolio includes 300+ AI and tech companies across foundation models, robotics, and infrastructure.
744.regulatory_stance_detail: SoftBank takes an aggressive pro-AI stance, investing massively in AI infrastructure and capabilities. CEO Masayoshi Son has declared the company's mission to realize Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) for humanity's advancement, with minimal emphasis on regulatory constraints.

745.notes: Founded 1999, publicly traded CRM company with 70,000+ employees. Launched Einstein AI platform in 2016, Agentforce AI agents in 2024. Chief Ethical and Humane Use Officer Paula Goldman sits on U.S. National AI Advisory Committee. Has comprehensive AI governance framework with cross-functional AI Council and established Trusted AI principles in 2018.
745.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including mandatory impact assessments for high-risk AI systems, support for EU AI Act framework, and specific prohibitions on harmful practices like facial recognition. Supports risk-based regulatory approach with strong civil rights protections.
745.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

746.notes: Semiconductor IP company founded 1990, designs processor architectures used globally. Powers AI infrastructure from cloud datacenters (50% of hyperscaler compute in 2025) to edge devices. Exceeded $1 billion revenue for three consecutive quarters in 2025. Supports 22 million developers worldwide building on Arm compute platform.
746.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI regulation that mitigates harm while enabling innovation. Supports sector-specific approaches over comprehensive restrictions, favoring frameworks that address underlying compute infrastructure rather than controlling specific AI applications.

747.notes: Founded 2016 by Wang Xingxing, ~500 employees. Ships over 5,500 humanoid robots annually (2025), leading global market. Products include G1 ($16k) and H1 humanoid robots, plus quadruped models. Subject of US Congressional hearings over cybersecurity vulnerabilities and alleged military ties. Planning IPO with Chinese government 'national champion' status.
747.regulatory_stance_detail: Company appears caught between commercial interests and Chinese government support, with reported cybersecurity vulnerabilities and military ties raising regulatory concerns in the US while benefiting from 'little giant' enterprise designation and government subsidies in China.

748.notes: Founded 1999, focusing on autonomous driving and ADAS technologies. Develops EyeQ system-on-chip processors and safety frameworks like Responsibility-Sensitive Safety (RSS). Over 230 million vehicles built with Mobileye technology through 2025. Listed independently from Intel in 2022, generated $1.894B revenue in 2025. Agreed to acquire Mentee Robotics in 2026 to expand into humanoid robotics.
748.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for national AV legislative framework with safety baselines and modernized FMVSS standards, but emphasizes industry self-regulation through safety methodologies like RSS. Supports transparency through safety cases rather than restrictive pre-deployment requirements.

749.notes: Asteria Film Co. is actually an AI-powered film production studio, not a traditional media/journalism organization. Founded by Bryn Mooser and Natasha Lyonne, it focuses on creating 'ethical AI' models for film production while developing the Continuum Suite operating system for streamlined filmmaking workflows. Backed by CAA and Comcast Ventures.
749.regulatory_stance_detail: Asteria advocates for 'clean' and 'ethical' AI models that avoid copyright violations while supporting industry innovation, suggesting a self-regulatory approach rather than heavy government intervention.

750.notes: Founded 2019 by Alex Blania (CEO) and Sam Altman (Chairman). Develops iris-scanning 'Orb' devices for World ID verification system. Over 500 employees, $2.5B valuation, raised $240M+ from investors including Andreessen Horowitz. 17.5 million verified users as of 2024, targeting 1 billion users.
750.regulatory_stance_detail: Company operates globally with biometric data collection despite regulatory pushback, suggesting preference for minimal restrictions. However, faces bans and investigations in multiple countries for data protection violations.

751.notes: Founded 2022 as AI platform for financial services, not AI safety/alignment. Raised $43.5M Series A led by NEA in 2025. Team includes 30+ members from Google Brain, Meta AI, AWS, Stanford. Develops specialized AI agents for investment research and financial analysis workflows, deployed at Morgan Stanley and major banks with 10,000+ professional users.

752.notes: Founded 2022, raised $281M total including $180M Series C (Jan 2025) at $3.3B valuation. 120 employees across London, New York, Warsaw. Specializes in AI voice generation, cloning, and conversational agents. First company to secure AI agent insurance through AIUC-1 certification. Partners with governments including Ukraine, Czech Republic, and UK on public services.
752.regulatory_stance_detail: Actively collaborates with governments on AI safety research and regulation, including partnering with UK AI Security Institute. Implements comprehensive safety frameworks with disclosure requirements, content moderation, and user accountability. Supports certification standards like AIUC-1 for enterprise AI deployment.

753.notes: Founded 2015 as Oregon State University spinout, grew to 300+ employees. Launched Digit humanoid robot in 2020, first commercially deployed humanoid. Raised $400M in 2025 at $1.75B valuation. Manufacturing facility 'RoboFab' in Salem with 100% US assembly.
753.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for industry-led safety standards and regulatory sandbox approaches for robotics deployment. Supports responsible development with focus on controlled environments first, but emphasizes need for competitive positioning against China.

754.notes: Founded 2016, specializes in autonomous driving technology for robotaxis and robotrucks. Raised $800M+ via Hong Kong IPO in 2025, operates 1,446 vehicle fleet as of March 2026. Achieved unit economics breakeven in Guangzhou and Shenzhen. Targets 3,000+ robotaxis across 20+ global cities by end of 2026.
754.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal AV framework with safety cases and industry self-regulation rather than prescriptive rules. Supports voluntary safety commitments and opposes restrictive state regulations that could hinder innovation.

755.notes: HUMAIN is a Saudi artificial intelligence company established under the Public Investment Fund and launched on May 12, 2025, by Mohammed bin Salman, Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia. The company operates across the entire AI value chain, providing comprehensive AI services including next-generation data centers, AI infrastructure and cloud capabilities, and advanced AI models and solutions. HUMAIN aims to position Saudi Arabia as a globally competitive AI hub and is chaired by HRH the Crown Prince. The company's CEO is Tareq Amin.
755.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates within Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 framework emphasizing rapid AI development and deployment. CEO praises government 'enablement' rather than restrictive regulation, with focus on strategic partnerships and infrastructure scaling.

756.notes: Founded 2018, uses AI for mineral exploration targeting copper, lithium, nickel, and cobalt. Raised $1.2B total including $537M Series C (Jan 2025), valued at $2.96B. Discovered major copper deposit in Zambia (Mingomba project) expected to produce 300,000+ tons annually by 2030s. Backed by Bill Gates, Jeff Bezos, Andreessen Horowitz.

757.notes: Founded November 2023 as nonprofit by Sam Rodriques and Andrew White. Backed by Eric Schmidt. Mission to build 'AI Scientists' for biology research over 10 years. Launched platform with specialized AI agents (Crow, Falcon, Owl, Phoenix) in May 2025. Spun out commercial entity Edison Scientific in November 2025 raising $70M. Operates wet lab for human-AI collaboration.
757.regulatory_stance_detail: As a nonprofit focused on building AI scientists, emphasizes responsible AI use and safety without sacrificing scientific progress, but doesn't advocate for specific regulatory approaches.

758.notes: Founded 1982, major creative software company that has integrated AI into products for over a decade. Developed Firefly generative AI model trained exclusively on licensed content, offers enterprise indemnification. Key AI products include Firefly, GenStudio, and AI assistants across Creative Cloud. Founded Content Authenticity Initiative in 2019 with 5,000+ members to combat misinformation through Content Credentials technology.
758.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based regulation that accommodates different AI use cases, with lighter oversight for low-risk applications and appropriate guardrails for higher-risk uses. Supports targeted disclosure requirements and industry standards while opposing broad restrictions that could stifle innovation.

759.notes: Enterprise AI governance platform company founded by Navrina Singh. Provides software and advisory services for AI risk management and regulatory compliance. Named leader in Forrester Wave AI Governance Solutions 2025 and recognized in Gartner Market Guide. Has partnerships with Microsoft, McKinsey, and Databricks.
759.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for enterprise AI governance through compliance with existing and emerging regulations like EU AI Act, NIST RMF, and NYC Local Law 144. Focuses on practical implementation of AI governance frameworks rather than broad regulatory restrictions.

760.notes: Founded 2023, raised $302M total funding. Develops nature-inspired foundation models and automated scientific discovery systems. Created 'AI Scientist' tool that generates complete research papers for ~$15 each. First AI system to pass peer review at academic conference (ICLR 2025). Partnered with Google in 2026 for enterprise AI solutions targeting finance and government sectors.
760.regulatory_stance_detail: Emphasizes responsible AI development with transparency about AI-generated research and ethical considerations. Advocates for sandboxing and safety measures while pursuing automated scientific discovery.

761.notes: Founded 2020 by Edwin Chen (ex-Google/Facebook/Twitter). Bootstrapped to $1.2B revenue in 2024 serving OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Meta with high-quality human data labeling. 250 employees managing 50K+ expert contractors globally. Seeking first external funding of $1B at ~$24B valuation in 2025.
761.regulatory_stance_detail: As a data labeling platform serving frontier AI labs, Surge operates under existing data protection frameworks without advocating for restrictive AI-specific regulations that could hamper model development.

762.notes: Founded 2009 as AI/ML consulting company, 15+ years before mainstream AI adoption. Grew from 4 people to 100+ employees serving enterprise clients globally. CEO Alan Descoins named in TIME's '100 Most Influential People in AI' 2025. Focuses on custom AI solutions, MLOps, and 'AI for Good' projects including conservation and sustainability initiatives.

909.notes: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is an independent U.S. federal agency responsible for consumer protection and preventing anticompetitive business practices. In the AI policy ecosystem, the FTC is a primary enforcement body for AI-related consumer harms, using Section 5 of the FTC Act — which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices — as its principal enforcement vehicle. Under Biden-era Chair Lina Khan (2021–2025), the FTC took an aggressive posture on AI and Big Tech. Under Trump-appointed Chair Andrew Ferguson (2025–present), the FTC shifted to a lighter-touch model: enforcing existing consumer protection law against AI fraud and deception without pursuing new AI-specific rulemaking. In March 2026, the FTC issued a Policy Statement clarifying how Section 5 applies to AI systems. The agency's AI focus areas include algorithmic discrimination, deceptive AI-generated content, and privacy violations in AI data collection.
909.regulatory_stance_detail: Under Trump administration, shifted from restrictive Biden-era enforcement to more innovation-focused approach. Continues pursuing deceptive AI claims while reducing enforcement against AI capabilities themselves.

It published a landmark AI Accountability Policy Report (2024) urging independent audits of high-risk AI systems. Under the Trump 2025 AI Action Plan, NTIA shifted focus toward promoting U.S.-led AI and 6G connectivity, and gained authority to withhold broadband funding from states with laws deemed to obstruct national AI policy.
910.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI accountability through guidance, support, and targeted regulation. Supports open AI models while calling for monitoring of risks in powerful systems.

Supports implementing the AI Bill of Rights for federal agencies and comprehensive regulatory frameworks that prioritize societal impacts over purely technical considerations.
920.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

921.notes: Founded Data & Society in 2013 to study sociotechnical systems and bias. Recently published 'We Need an Interventionist Mindset' (March 2025) arguing against deterministic AI rhetoric. Elected AAAS fellow in 2025. Currently conducting ethnographic study of US Census to understand data legitimacy. Known for critiquing both AI hype and fear cycles, advocating for probabilistic rather than deterministic thinking about AI futures.
921.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'interventionist' rather than 'solutionist' approaches to AI regulation, criticizing both techno-solutionism and deterministic policy responses. Opposes the Kids Online Safety Act as ineffective, arguing it won't help children and pushes for evidence-based interventions over blanket solutions.
921.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

922.notes: Testified before House Education and Workforce Committee in January 2026 on 'Building an AI-Ready America.' Criticized narrow focus on long-term risks at 2023 Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit, advocating for attention to current AI harms. Daughter of actor Christopher Reeve, serves on foundation board focused on spinal cord injury research using AI technologies.
922.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific AI regulations focused on high-risk uses in employment, education, and government services, with transparency requirements and civil rights protections. Supports targeted interventions rather than broad restrictions, emphasizing that 'safety and innovation are not necessarily mutually exclusive.'
922.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

923.notes: Served as Senior Advisor on AI at the Federal Trade Commission where she worked on competition and consumer protection matters. Testified before Congress on AI, competition and data privacy issues in December 2023. Co-authored influential AI Now Institute reports on AI governance and delivered keynote at Paris AI Action Summit in February 2025 focusing on AI's environmental impacts. Has forthcoming book 'Tracing Code' (University of California Press) examining origins of data capitalism.
923.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong regulatory intervention to address market concentration and power imbalances in AI, emphasizing that 'the market will not get us there alone' and calling for mandatory constraints on AI development approaches that prioritize growth over safety.

924.notes: Noble's 2018 book 'Algorithms of Oppression' documented how Google search results for 'Black girls' returned pornographic content, demonstrating algorithmic bias in search engines. She was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2021 for her groundbreaking work on algorithmic discrimination. Her research focuses on how AI systems reinforce racism and sexism, particularly affecting marginalized communities, and she argues that AI will become a major human rights issue in the 21st century.
924.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for breaking up and regulating large technology monopolies like Google, arguing their consolidated power threatens democracy. Calls for strict regulation, accountability mechanisms, and civil rights-centered audits before AI deployment in high-risk applications like law enforcement.

Her 2019 book 'Race After Technology' won the Oliver Cromwell Cox Book Award for anti-racist scholarship. Delivered 2024 Tanner Lectures at Harvard arguing tech elites cannot be trusted as stewards of collective good. Founded the Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab focusing on algorithmic justice.
925.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for race-conscious design and abolitionist approaches to technology regulation. Argues some technologies like predictive policing and digital surveillance are so embedded in systems of racial control they must be abolished rather than reformed. Calls for regulatory frameworks with community oversight and legal remedies for algorithmic harm.
925.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

926.notes: Founded ORCAA in 2017 to audit algorithms for bias and fairness. Her 2016 book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' was longlisted for the National Book Award and became a NYT bestseller. Testified to Congress on algorithmic accountability and developed the 'Ethical Matrix' framework for auditing AI systems. Won the MAA Euler Book Prize in 2019.
926.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for algorithmic auditing requirements and transparency mandates for high-stakes AI systems, particularly in hiring, criminal justice, and financial services. Supports targeted regulation focusing on consequential algorithms rather than broad AI restrictions.
