927.notes: Eubanks' 2018 book 'Automating Inequality' investigates how AI systems in welfare, housing, and child welfare disproportionately harm poor and working-class people. She coined the term 'digital poorhouse' to describe technological systems that embed assumptions about poverty. Her work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has won multiple awards including the 2019 Lillian Smith Book Award and 2018 McGannon Center Book Prize.
927.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong regulatory constraints on AI systems that impact public services and vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need to address algorithmic bias and discrimination in automated decision-making systems used by government agencies.
927.threat_models: Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

928.notes: Led FTC's AI enforcement including OpenAI investigation (July 2023), Rite Aid AI facial recognition ban (December 2023), and joint interagency AI statement with DOJ/CFPB/EEOC (April 2023). Published op-ed 'We Must Regulate A.I.' in New York Times. Estimated 15% p(doom) according to sources. Known for aggressive antitrust enforcement and authored influential 'Amazon's Antitrust Paradox' as law student.
928.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive AI regulation using existing FTC authorities, has launched investigations into OpenAI, banned AI facial recognition at Rite Aid, and co-authored joint interagency statements emphasizing 'no AI exemption to laws on the books' with vigorous enforcement against AI-enabled fraud and discrimination.

929.notes: Led NTIA's AI Accountability Policy Report (March 2024) calling for improved transparency, independent evaluations, and consequences for AI systems imposing unacceptable risks. Currently working on assessment of benefits and harms of open-weight AI models per Biden's AI Executive Order, with report due to White House in July 2024. Co-leads President's Task Force on Kids Online Health and Safety and oversees $42 billion broadband infrastructure programs.
929.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on accountability, transparency, and independent evaluations of high-risk AI systems. Supports building an ecosystem around AI accountability similar to financial auditing, with requirements for independent audits of highest-risk AI systems while promoting innovation and competition.

930.notes: As NIST Director (2022-2024), Locascio launched the U.S. AI Safety Institute in November 2023 and oversaw release of the AI Risk Management Framework in January 2023. She participated in roundtables on AI safety and testified before Congress on NIST's AI priorities, securing $50M in FY2025 budget for AI safety work. Now leads ANSI, coordinating private sector standards development including AI standards.
930.regulatory_stance_detail: Locascio led implementation of Biden's AI Executive Order through NIST's AI Risk Management Framework and U.S. AI Safety Institute, emphasizing voluntary standards and collaboration with industry while building government capacity for AI oversight and safety evaluation.

931.notes: Iranian-born, joined NIST in 1999 after coming to US in 1994 for master's in electrical engineering. Principal architect of NIST Fingerprint Image Quality standard used by FBI. Led development of AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 released January 2023 that laid groundwork for Biden's AI Executive Order. Named by Time Magazine as one of 100 most influential people in AI in 2024.
931.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for voluntary, flexible frameworks like NIST's AI Risk Management Framework rather than prescriptive regulations. Emphasizes standards-based approach with measurement science and evaluation tools, supporting the Biden AI Executive Order's risk-based requirements while maintaining NIST's non-regulatory role.

932.notes: Led DOJ Antitrust Division 2021-2024, confirmed by Senate 68-29. Delivered remarks at Stanford AI workshop stating DOJ would scrutinize AI sector 'with urgency' to prevent monopoly consolidation. Worked with FTC to divide AI oversight responsibilities - DOJ handles chip manufacturers while FTC oversees software companies. Appointed DOJ's first Chief Technologist to enhance AI expertise for investigations.
932.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on antitrust enforcement in AI markets, examining potential monopolization, anti-competitive mergers, and market concentration. Stated DOJ is 'actively examining the AI ecosystem' and will enforce Sherman Act and Clayton Act against AI firms that violate antitrust laws.

933.notes: Served as CISA Director from July 2021 to January 2025. While CISA's mandate is primarily cybersecurity, the agency had a supporting role in implementing parts of Biden's Executive Order 14110 on AI safety (Oct 2023, since rescinded by Trump's EO 14179 in Jan 2025). Frequently warns that AI represents 'the most powerful weapon of our time' and advocates for 'secure-by-design' AI development. Named CEO of RSA Conference in January 2026 after leaving CISA. Rhodes Scholar and 20-year Army veteran who helped establish US Cyber Command.
933.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for urgent AI regulation with focus on cybersecurity and infrastructure protection. Supports mandatory cyber incident reporting within 24 hours and calls for legal frameworks to prevent AI weaponization while avoiding restrictions that handicap American AI companies versus China.

934.notes: No information found in search results for a Stefano Mazzocchi serving as Chief AI Officer at the Department of Commerce. Search results show other individuals with similar names in different roles.

935.notes: Led development of the EU AI Act (2021-2024) establishing world's first major AI legislation with risk-based approach. Known as aggressive antitrust enforcer against Big Tech companies including landmark cases against Google, Apple, Amazon, and Facebook. Served as EU Competition Commissioner 2014-2024 and Executive Vice President for Digital Age 2019-2024. Described by critics as implementing 'anti-American antitrust' policies that disproportionately target U.S. tech companies.
935.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI regulation focusing on use cases rather than technology itself, with strict obligations for high-risk applications and prohibition of unacceptable uses like social scoring. Champions the EU AI Act as 'pro-innovation' while maintaining that trust-building through regulation is essential for AI adoption.

936.notes: Led the 38-hour 'trilogue' negotiations that finalized the EU AI Act in December 2023. Sanctioned by the US in December 2024 with travel ban and asset freeze for his role in EU tech regulation, specifically the Digital Services Act. Previously wrote prescient sci-fi novels in the 1980s about fake news and generative AI. Resigned from the European Commission in September 2024 amid tensions with Ursula von der Leyen.
936.regulatory_stance_detail: Architected the EU AI Act, the world's first comprehensive AI regulation with strict bans on certain AI applications, transparency requirements for foundation models, and significant penalties up to €35 million. Promoted a risk-based approach with the motto 'regulating as little as possible, as much as needed.'

937.notes: Won four Hugo Awards, four Nebula Awards, and 2024 PEN/Malamud Award for short story excellence. Named one of Time's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Wrote influential New Yorker essays including "Why A.I. Isn't Going to Make Art" and "ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web" arguing AI cannot create true art due to lack of intentional choice-making. Studied computer science at Brown University, providing technical foundation for his AI commentary.
937.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues current AI hype is overblown and companies benefit from blurring distinctions between science fiction AI and reality. Believes generative AI will have "massive repercussions" primarily because companies will adopt it for cost-cutting before realizing its limitations, potentially destroying industries.
937.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

938.notes: Delivered major AI warnings at Davos 2026 WEF, predicting AI will create identity and immigration crises for every country. Warns that 'anything made of words will be taken over by AI' including laws, books, and religions. Has sold over 50 million books in 65 languages and regularly advises world leaders including Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel. Published 'Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI' in 2024.
938.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict safety standards and regulations on AI companies similar to pharmaceutical industry oversight, warns against granting AI legal personhood, and calls for immediate government action to prevent AI from gaining rights to start businesses or operate autonomously in financial systems.
938.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality

939.notes: Co-authored influential 2018 paper 'Datasheets for Datasets' with Timnit Gebru that has been integrated into the EU AI Act. Her work 'Anatomy of an AI System' with Vladan Joler won the Beazley Design Award 2019 and is in MoMA's permanent collection. Testified before U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary in 2023 arguing that AI half-measures like transparency and bias audits are insufficient. Co-founded AI Now Institute in 2017 to explore policy approaches to AI's social consequences.
939.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong regulatory frameworks for AI systems, particularly algorithmic impact assessments for government use, trade secrets exceptions for high-stakes domains, and specialized regulatory agencies. Argues that value alignment alone is insufficient without institutional change and that AI systems are not neutral technologies requiring rigorous oversight.
939.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

940.notes: Coined the viral term 'enshittification' to describe platform decay. Has upcoming book 'The Reverse Centaur's Guide to Life After AI' (Farrar, Straus and Giroux / Verso, June 23 2026) — title invokes the 'reverse centaur' figure forced by technology to work at an inhuman pace. Views AI as 'a collection of moderately useful tools being billed as a supernatural being' and argues the AI bubble will cause economic damage when it deflates. Warns that 'AI can't do your job, but an AI salesman can convince your boss to fire you and replace you with AI that can't do your job.'
940.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive antitrust enforcement against Big Tech monopolies, interoperability requirements, and tech worker unionization. Opposes broad AI regulation that serves incumbent interests, instead favoring targeted interventions to prevent AI-enabled worker displacement and corporate power consolidation.
940.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Economic inequality

941.notes: Published 2023 Guardian piece 'AI machines aren't hallucinating, But their makers are' arguing AI development represents 'the largest theft in human history.' Describes AI as creating a 'three-legged stool' of tech, fossil fuels, and state power that provides a lifeline to fossil fuel industries. Warns AI undermines belief in reality itself through deepfakes and generated content.
941.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues the current 'arms race' model of AI development is fundamentally incompatible with climate goals and sustainability. Calls for breaking up AI 'empires,' increasing transparency across AI supply chains, and redirecting development toward smaller, more specialized systems rather than massive build-outs.

Currently pursuing DPhil at Oxford under Chris Summerfield focusing on cognitive models of human decision-making for AI alignment. His book 'The Alignment Problem' was called 'the one book to read on AI' by The New York Times and bridges technical AI safety with present-day algorithmic bias issues.

Research shows early evidence of AI displacing entry-level workers, informing targeted labor and technology policy recommendations.

951.notes: Ash Center for Democratic Governance; Danielle Allen
951.regulatory_stance_detail: The Ash Center houses multiple programs with varying approaches to AI governance. 951.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

952.notes: Harvard research center on internet and society. Active AI governance research covering agentic AI, algorithmic accountability, and LLM transparency. Funded by foundations including MacArthur and Knight; no current corporate funding.
952.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for dynamic, collaborative public-private governance frameworks that combine ex-ante measures with adaptable mechanisms. Researchers propose legal alignment approaches and emphasize the need for regulatory clarity while fostering innovation and competition.

AI work focuses on antitrust frameworks for emerging AI markets, worker rights in AI deployment, and democratic governance of automation. Argues labor voice must be central to technology policy; affiliated scholar Ganesh Sitaraman covers industrial policy.
956.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for worker-centered AI governance with transparency requirements, human oversight mandates, and bright-line prohibitions on harmful uses like social scoring and surveillance pricing. Supports federal regulation while defending state-level protections.
956.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

957.notes: Windfall Trust is a research and advocacy project focused on the economic redistribution of gains from transformative AI, exploring mechanisms (equity stakes, dividends, public ownership) by which society could share in the windfall generated by advanced AI systems. Aligned with the post-labor-economics research community.
957.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive policy frameworks to manage AI's economic disruption, including wealth redistribution mechanisms, tax reform, and social safety net adaptations. Focuses on preparing institutions for transformative AI scenarios rather than restricting AI development itself.

958.notes: Nonprofit incubator for participatory AI governance models, co-founded by Divya Siddarth and Saffron Huang. Obtained independent 501(c)3 status in 2024.
958.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic governance and collective input into AI development through institutional innovation, evaluation frameworks, and multi-stakeholder processes rather than top-down restrictions.
958.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

959.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'plurality' approach to AI governance emphasizing democratic participation, transparency, and public infrastructure rather than corporate concentration. Supports public AI infrastructure and participatory governance mechanisms.

Active in AI policy discussions focused on collective decision-making, data dignity, and democratic governance of AI systems.
960.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for data coalitions and collective bargaining structures to redistribute AI power, while supporting open source models and targeted regulation through trusted data intermediaries rather than broad AI restrictions.

961.notes: Taiwan''s Ministry of Digital Affairs (MoDA), established August 2022, is the cabinet-level ministry responsible for digital development, cybersecurity, and AI policy in Taiwan. Audrey Tang served as its first minister (2022–2024) and made it a widely cited example of participatory digital governance, open-source civic tech, and democratic AI policymaking.
961.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes a principle-based approach to AI regulation through the AI Fundamental Act, establishing seven governance principles while promoting innovation through subsidies and regulatory sandboxes. Balances development with security through risk classification frameworks.

962.notes: Co-authored influential 2026 Hamilton Project report 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' defining framework for AI that augments rather than replaces human expertise. Served on National Academy committee for 2024 report 'Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work.' Recognized as 'academic voice of the American worker' by Economist magazine and received 2019 Carnegie Fellowship for work on globalization and technology impacts on labor markets.
962.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive government intervention to steer AI development toward 'pro-worker' applications through targeted sectoral policies, especially in healthcare and education, tax code reforms to reduce bias toward automation, and stronger safety nets like wage insurance for displaced workers.
962.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

963.notes: Leading labor economist studying AI's impact on employment through the 'Race Between Education and Technology' framework. His research shows AI may substitute for entry-level workers while complementing experienced workers with tacit knowledge. Named 2025 Citation Laureate alongside David Autor for influential work on wages, inequality, and technological change. Co-authored influential book 'The Race between Education and Technology' (2008) with Claudia Goldin documenting how educational expansion affects inequality.
963.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted policies to address AI-driven labor displacement, drawing parallels to past trade adjustment programs. Supports strengthening worker bargaining power and robust safety nets, while emphasizing the need for high-quality sectoral training programs to help displaced workers transition to new roles.

964.notes: Warns of AI bubble that could burst while simultaneously displacing workers, creating worst-case scenario without proper retraining infrastructure. Distinguishes between 'AI' (replacement) and 'IA' (intelligence assisting), advocating for latter approach. Signed Global Call for AI Red Lines in 2026. Predicts AI will primarily impact routine white-collar jobs while augmenting rather than replacing roles in education, healthcare, and skilled trades.
964.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for government regulation to prevent AI from exacerbating inequality and undermining information quality, while supporting AI as a tool for augmenting human capabilities. Calls for compensation mechanisms for news industry affected by AI scraping and international red lines for dangerous AI applications.

965.notes: Co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' (2022) framing AI as primarily a prediction technology with economic implications. Founded Creative Destruction Lab, one of the world's largest seed-stage programs for science-based companies with over 600 startups admitted in 2022. Member of the Order of Canada (2022) and recipient of University of Toronto's President's Impact Award (2023). Advocates for viewing AI through economic lens of falling prediction costs rather than focusing on AGI scenarios.

Supports applying existing regulations to AI rather than creating new broad frameworks.

966.notes: Published multiple NBER working papers on AI regulation including 'Can Socially-Minded Governance Control the AGI Beast?' (2024) concluding that socially-minded entities cannot minimize harm from unrestricted AGI products. Provides expert witness testimony in antitrust cases and has equity/advisory relationships with startup firms. Argues that ASI is unlikely to kill humans because we find AI economically valuable and wouldn't threaten its existence.
966.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues that ex post regulatory instruments like liability regimes outperform ex ante restrictions in many scenarios. Advocates for acceleration of AI adoption to surface and react to potential harms more quickly through 'learning by doing' rather than delayed adoption until harms are fully understood.
966.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Loss of control

967.notes: Co-authored 'The Future of the Professions' (2015) with father Richard Susskind examining technology's impact on professional work. His 2026 paper 'What Will Remain for People to Do?' explores which jobs might survive AGI, identifying 'general equilibrium limits', 'preference limits', and 'moral limits'. Previously worked in British government including Prime Minister's Strategy Unit and Cabinet Office. His TED Talk on the future of work has over 1.5 million views.
967.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for thoughtful policy responses to AI's labor market impacts, including consideration of Universal Basic Income and Job Guarantee Schemes. Emphasizes need for governments to prepare for AI-driven economic transformation rather than restrictive regulation of AI development itself.
967.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

968.notes: Co-authored influential paper 'Cheap Goods for Everyone? The Impact of Market Power in Artificial Intelligence on Welfare and Inequality' with Susan Athey (2025). Warns that AI monopolists could create scenario where wages fall to zero while goods remain expensive. Has consulted for Microsoft on competition topics unrelated to AI and regularly advises governments on confidential antitrust cases.
968.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for vigorous antitrust enforcement and competition policy to prevent AI monopolization, particularly through immediate enforcement action before markets concentrate. Supports pro-competitive regulation like interoperability requirements and open-source model promotion, while opposing broad bans in favor of targeted interventions.
968.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

969.notes: Author of 'What's There to Fear in a World with Transformative AI? With the Right Policy, Nothing' (2025), proposing three key policy areas: managing job transformation speed, implementing digital dividends as returns on collective data contributions, and building social trust. Served on Obama's Council of Economic Advisers and as Labor Department Chief Economist. Co-hosts 'Think Like an Economist' podcast and frequently appears in media discussing AI's economic impacts.
969.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for managing the speed and process of AI adoption through policy interventions like shifting taxes from labor to consumption/capital, implementing digital dividends, and ensuring competitive markets, while avoiding stopping AI development entirely.

970.notes: Testified before Congress on labor market competition (October 2019) and served as Principal Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024), helping integrate labor market analysis into 2023 Merger Guidelines. Published 'Resilient by Design: Dual Safety Nets for Workers in the AI Economy' (2025) proposing AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend programs. Co-authored economic modeling work on AI's wage and output impacts with complementarity between physical and intelligence sectors.
970.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation addressing AI's labor market impacts through safety nets and competition policy, proposing a two-tier system with AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend rather than broad AI restrictions.
970.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

971.notes: Designed and taught one of the first university courses on AI's economic impact at Stanford. In his 'Digitalist Papers' essay, he created a framework analyzing four potential AI futures: Titan's Dominion, Copilot Empire, Disruption Swarm, and Promethean Fire. As Assistant Secretary of State (2022-2024), he represented State Department on CFIUS and developed outbound investment security programs to protect AI technologies. His testimony shows focus on using AI to modernize government operations while addressing national security implications.
971.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for leveraging existing regulatory tools and authorities rather than rushing new regulations, emphasizing 'guardrails and triggers' approach that establishes foundational rules while preparing escalation mechanisms based on how AI technology and markets evolve.
971.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Loss of control

972.notes: Co-authored the comprehensive AI Risk Repository (August 2024) cataloguing 777 AI risks from 43 taxonomies, and developed AI Risk Mitigation Taxonomy (December 2025) organizing 831 mitigation strategies. Has advised businesses and government on Moore's Law and computing futures, previously worked at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Bain & Company, UN, and World Bank. Warns that smarter AI systems can more easily bypass controls and emphasizes the economic constraints of deep learning scaling.
972.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for significantly increased government investment in AI research and post-Moore's Law computing technologies, supporting the CHIPS Act as 'good first steps' but calling for 10-100 times more investment in next-generation computing. Emphasizes need for public sector capabilities to audit industry AI models and ensure public interest considerations.
972.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, Economic inequality

973.notes: Served as Chief Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division 2022-2024, focusing on AI and tech competition policy. Currently advises World Bank on AI implications for developing countries. Keynote speaker at 2024 IMF Statistical Forum on AI's economic impact. Research focuses on economics of digitization, causal inference with AI, and social impact applications of technology.
973.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive competition policy and interoperability requirements for AI platforms, emphasizing ex-ante regulation for systemically significant digital enterprises. Supports frameworks that preserve innovation while ensuring fair competition and preventing incumbent monopolization.
973.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

974.notes: Served as Obama's chief economist and played major role in economic policies 2013-2017. Co-authored 'AI and the Economy' (NBER 2018) and gave influential 2016 speech 'Is This Time Different?' arguing AI resembles previous technological advances. Currently consults for OpenAI and chaired UK's Digital Competition Expert Panel, leading to establishment of Digital Markets Unit. Advocates six principles for AI regulation including application-specific approaches and comparing AI performance to imperfect humans rather than perfection.
974.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for application-specific AI regulation through existing agencies rather than federal-level blanket rules, emphasizing competition policy and merger enforcement over broad restrictions. Supports pro-competitive regulation including codes of conduct for dominant platforms and data mobility requirements.

975.notes: Goldfarb testified before the U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2019 on digital advertising and data privacy, arguing that privacy regulation often creates trade-offs with innovation and competition. His research with Catherine Tucker found European privacy regulations reduced online advertising effectiveness by 65%. He co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' that frame AI economically as cheap prediction technology, widely cited in policy discussions.
975.regulatory_stance_detail: Goldfarb advocates for careful regulatory design that balances innovation with legitimate concerns, emphasizing that privacy regulations often reduce innovation and that regulatory consistency is crucial. He argues against broad AI research pauses and supports targeted approaches that mitigate negative consequences without stifling technological progress.

976.notes: Testified before U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum arguing against regulating AI technology itself while supporting application-specific regulations. Publicly opposed California's SB 1047 and the White House's Defense Production Act use for AI regulation. Coined the term 'agentic AI' in 2024 and has been vocal about dismissing AI extinction risks as implausible while supporting responsible AI development.
976.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for regulating AI applications rather than the underlying technology, opposing broad model-level regulations like California's SB 1047 and supporting targeted regulations for specific harmful uses like non-consensual deepfakes. Supports federal preemption of state-level AI regulations and opposes compute threshold-based reporting requirements.

977.notes: Co-authored 2020 MIT report concluding AI mass unemployment fears are unlikely to be realized, instead predicting new job creation. Focuses on 'physical AI' - AI systems that understand and interact with the physical world. Recently received 2025 IEEE Edison Medal for sustained leadership in modern robotics. Emphasizes AI as tool for human augmentation rather than replacement, advocating for policy frameworks that smooth workforce transitions.
977.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI development with emphasis on human-AI collaboration rather than replacement, supporting frameworks that ensure AI benefits are broadly distributed while addressing safety concerns through technical innovation and policy coordination.
977.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

978.notes: Coined the term 'surveillance capitalism' in her 2019 book. Testified before Congress in February 2022 on privacy law. Has spent over 40 years studying digital transformation and warns that AI represents surveillance capitalism's continued evolution and expansion. Argues for 'epistemic rights' as fundamental to human autonomy in the digital age.
978.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for abolishing surveillance capitalism's core mechanisms rather than just regulating them, arguing 'we can have surveillance capitalism, or we can have democracy, but we cannot have both.' Supports outlawing secret massive-scale data extraction and creating new legal frameworks for the digital century.
978.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control

979.notes: Served on U.S. National AI Advisory Committee (2022-2024) and chaired National Committee on Vital and Health Statistics Subcommittee on Privacy, Confidentiality, and Security (2019-2021). Ranked third most-cited U.S. legal scholar in Law & Technology field (2016-2020). Co-authored 'Licensing High-Risk Artificial Intelligence: Toward Ex Ante Justification for a Disruptive Technology' proposing regulatory pre-approval for high-risk AI systems. His work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has influenced AI law and policy discussions globally.
979.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ex ante licensing and regulatory approval for high-risk AI systems, proposing that firms should bear the burden of proving their AI is non-discriminatory and safe before deployment. Co-authored proposals for licensing high-risk AI with presumption of unlawfulness until justified.
979.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion

980.notes: Testified before US Senate on AI and privacy policy multiple times. Co-authored 2016 AI100 report 'Artificial Intelligence and Life in 2030.' Published influential 2017 'AI Policy Primer and Roadmap' in UC Davis Law Review. Testified July 2024 before Senate Commerce Committee on AI privacy threats, warning about the 'inference economy' where AI derives intimate information from available data.
980.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive, comprehensive AI regulation addressing specific harms rather than broad innovation-focused approaches. Supports sector-specific regulation combined with federal coordination, emphasizing protection from algorithmic bias, privacy violations, and corporate power concentration over speculative existential risks.

981.notes: Co-authored 'How AI Destroys Institutions' arguing AI systems erode democratic institutions through isolation, automation, and removal of human decision-making. Testified before Senate Judiciary Committee in September 2023 advocating for moving beyond 'AI half-measures.' Published extensively on privacy law including 'Privacy's Blueprint' (2018) and co-authored 'The Great Scrape' on AI data collection practices. Served on Massachusetts Special Commission on Facial Recognition.
981.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for substantive legal protections against AI 'half-measures,' calling for duties of loyalty, design rules, and outright prohibitions on dangerous AI systems like emotion recognition, biometric surveillance, and social scoring. Testified before Congress that procedural approaches requiring only transparency and consent are insufficient.

982.notes: Co-authored 'A Roadmap for Governing AI: Technology Governance and Power-Sharing Liberalism' (2025) proposing 17 specific AI governance recommendations. Advocates for 'power-sharing liberalism' framework that prioritizes human flourishing and democratic participation over purely technocratic approaches. Has testified and advised on AI policy while directing Harvard's Allen Lab for Democracy Renovation, focusing on using AI to enhance rather than replace democratic processes.
982.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal licensing of AI development firms similar to other high-risk labs, supports strengthening the U.S. AI Safety Institute as primary coordinating body, and promotes risk-based regulatory approaches with clear guidelines distinguishing high-risk from experimental activities. Emphasizes pro-social AI governance through power-sharing liberalism framework.
982.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

983.notes: Leading scholar on AI's impact on democracy who argues that panic over AI could harm democracy more than AI itself. Co-authored 'Social Media and Democracy' (2020) and teaches 'Governing Artificial Intelligence: Law, Policy, and Institutions' at Stanford. Recently published research comparing generative AI regulation to social media regulation, advocating for transparency, trust and safety investments, and global cooperation. Awarded Guggenheim Fellowship in 2020 for work on technology's impact on political communication.
983.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation informed by lessons from social media regulation, emphasizing transparency, accountability, researcher access, and public investment while warning against both under-regulation and panic-driven overregulation that could harm democracy.

984.notes: Leading First Amendment scholar who has written extensively on AI and free speech, including 'Freedom of Speech and AI Output' (2023) and 'Large Libel Models? Liability for AI Output' (2023). Argues that Section 230 protections don't apply to AI-generated content since it's created by the companies themselves, not third parties. Has testified as expert witness and provided analysis on California's AI training data disclosure law (AB 2013), finding it likely constitutional under First Amendment commercial speech doctrine.
984.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly supports First Amendment protection for AI output and opposes broad content restrictions. Argues that AI speech should be protected based on users' rights to listen and speak, even if AI companies lack direct speech rights. Generally skeptical of government regulation that would restrict AI expression.

985.notes: Co-authored influential Science paper 'Large AI models are cultural and social technologies' (March 2025) arguing against AGI narratives. Published 'AI as Governance' in Annual Review of Political Science (2025) examining AI's relationship to governance structures. Frequently critiques both AI 'doomers' and 'boomers' for missing real policy challenges. Won 2019 Friedrich Schiedel Prize for Politics and Technology.
985.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for treating AI as a social technology requiring complex governance reforms rather than simple restrictions, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary approaches that combine social science with computer science to address AI's integration into existing institutions like markets, bureaucracy, and democracy.
985.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

986.notes: Leading expert on democratic theory who has extended her work on citizen assemblies to AI governance. Served as advisor to OpenAI's Democratic Inputs to AI program in 2023 and received $1 million grant from Eric Schmidt Foundation for AI and democracy project with John Tasioulas. Author of 'Open Democracy' and advocates for 'politics without politicians' through sortition-based citizen rule.
986.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic governance of AI through citizen assemblies and participatory processes rather than pure industry self-regulation or heavy-handed state control. Supports binding democratic rules that apply across all AI companies to prevent race-to-the-bottom dynamics.
986.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Economic inequality

987.notes: Delivered keynote 'Are We Having the Wrong Nightmares About AI?' at NeurIPS 2025, arguing against AGI fixation in favor of addressing 'Artificial Good-Enough Intelligence' that breaks societal trust mechanisms. Co-authored 2025 paper on dimensional governance for AI systems. Known for early accurate predictions on technology impacts, including COVID-19 mask effectiveness and social media's democratic risks.
987.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for dimensional governance frameworks that can adapt to evolving AI capabilities rather than static categorical approaches. Emphasizes preparing society for AI's disruptive effects on institutional mechanisms rather than trying to contain the technology's spread through export controls.

988.notes: Founded MIT GOV/LAB in 2014 focusing on citizen engagement and government accountability. Key contributor to The Digitalist Papers (2024) examining AI's impact on democratic societies. Research spans governance in developing contexts, particularly Asia and Africa, with emphasis on how technology can enhance rather than replace democratic participation. Advocates for 'reserved sociability' in digital civic platforms and human-centric AI governance.
988.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance that enables innovation while protecting democratic values. Supports digital civic infrastructure with human oversight and emphasizes the need for contextual, adaptive governance models rather than rigid regulatory frameworks.

989.notes: Co-authored influential 2024 paper 'Plurality: The Future of Collaborative Technology and Democracy' with Taiwan's Digital Minister Audrey Tang. Invented quadratic voting mechanism and quadratic funding. Led Microsoft's relationship with OpenAI as part of Office of the CTO team. Co-authored 2024 Journal of Democracy article 'The Real Dangers of Generative AI' warning of twin risks of democratic collapse and power concentration.
989.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic governance mechanisms for AI rather than traditional regulation, supporting processes like collective constitutional AI and participatory decision-making frameworks. Co-authored Democracy Levels Framework for evaluating democratic decision-making in AI governance.
989.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control

990.notes: Testified before U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2023 advocating for AI regulation. Consistently argues that current LLMs have fundamental limitations in reasoning, generalization, and reliability that scaling won't solve. Has been a prominent AI safety critic since 2018, warning against premature deployment of unreliable systems. Co-authored 'Rebooting AI' (2019), listed as Forbes Must-Read book on AI.
990.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for FDA-style safety review boards for AI systems before widespread deployment, mandatory independent audits, transparency requirements for training data, liability laws, and layered oversight similar to aviation industry. Opposes Section 230 application to AI.

991.notes: Chomsky is highly critical of current AI approaches, particularly Large Language Models, arguing they lack genuine understanding and cannot tell us anything meaningful about human cognition or language. He distinguishes between AI engineering (which can be useful) and AI science (which he views as fundamentally flawed). His critique is grounded in his transformational-generative grammar theory and concerns about corporate influence over AI development.
991.regulatory_stance_detail: Chomsky advocates for careful regulation and human oversight of AI systems, warning against automation of critical decisions like patient care or missile defense systems. He supports the moratorium calls by AI researchers and emphasizes the need for safeguards before deployment.
991.threat_models: Misinformation, Loss of control, Power concentration

992.notes: Leading developmental psychologist who applies her research on children's learning to critique current AI systems, particularly LLMs. Argues AI systems are 'cultural technologies' like printing presses rather than intelligent agents, and that true AI intelligence would require embodied interaction with the world. Has written extensively for major publications including Wall Street Journal and New York Times, and co-authored papers on AI as cultural technology with political scientists Henry Farrell and James Evans.
992.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for gradual development of norms and regulations for AI systems, comparing it to how electricity required elaborate regulatory systems. Emphasizes need for thoughtful governance while rejecting panic-driven responses to existential AI risks.

993.notes: Founded Collective Intelligence Project to develop democratic governance models for AI. Led alignment assemblies with Taiwan's Digital Ministry that informed deepfake legislation. Co-authored Harvard Law Review piece on co-governance for AI regulation. Named to TIME100 AI list and served as TED speaker on AI and democracy.
993.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic governance of AI through collective intelligence mechanisms like alignment assemblies and citizen participation, rather than purely top-down regulation. Supports co-governance approaches that give public input into AI development while working with industry partners like OpenAI and Anthropic.
993.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Loss of control

994.notes: Led Taiwan's response to deepfake scams in 2024 using citizen assemblies with AI facilitation, resulting in new anti-fraud legislation. Pioneered 'humor over rumor' strategy during COVID-19. Developed vTaiwan platform for digital democracy and civic participation. Currently serves as Taiwan's Cyber Ambassador and Senior Fellow at Project Liberty Institute working on bridging algorithms for social media.
994.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic, participatory AI governance with citizen assemblies and deliberative processes rather than top-down regulation. Supports targeted measures like digital signatures for AI content and platform liability while emphasizing open-source models and community-steered AI development over centralized control.

995.notes: Co-authored research on AI's societal impacts including work transformation studies at Anthropic and papers on interactive AI evaluations. Led democratic governance experiments including collective constitutional AI project with 1,000 Americans in 2023. Has published extensively on AI governance, digital commons, and the need for pre-distribution rather than redistribution approaches to AI's economic benefits. Currently researches how AI systems impact human behavior through extended interactions.
995.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic input into AI governance and pre-distribution of AI capabilities rather than just redistribution after the fact. Supports targeted interventions like consortium-based auditing and transparency standards while emphasizing the need for public participation in AI policy decisions.

996.notes: Served as Chair of Working Group on Internal Risk Management for EU AI Act Code of Practice in 2025. Co-authored white paper on Multilateral AI Research Institute (MAIRI) with Stanford colleagues in 2022. Member of UN High-level Advisory Body on AI Executive Committee. Argues that concentration of corporate power is the single greatest challenge in AI governance and prevents independent investigation of AI systems.
996.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic governance of AI with strong regulatory frameworks, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and access to information for researchers and regulators. Supports the EU AI Act and believes AI must be governed democratically rather than by corporations alone.

997.notes: Leading expert on computational propaganda and disinformation who authored influential Oxford Internet Institute reports tracking organized social media manipulation in 70+ countries. Her research on 'identity-based disinformation' examines how racism, sexism, and xenophobia are weaponized online to incite violence. Has provided expert testimony to government officials in the UK, Canada, and US on technology's effects on democracy, and her work has been featured in major outlets including NYT, Washington Post, and Financial Times.
997.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for increased regulation of digital campaigning and platform governance while emphasizing evidence-based approaches. Her 2019 Oxford report argued that self-regulatory measures by social media platforms have failed to prevent disinformation spread and that the UK needs stronger regulation of digital campaigning.

998.notes: Co-authored 'Beyond Rivalry: A US-China Policy Framework for the Age of Transformative AI' (Dec 2024) arguing against zero-sum AI competition. Believes misuse risks from bad actors outweigh AI alignment risks and advocates for Universal Basic Individual Income (UBII) as economic safety net. Published 'Our Next Reality' (Hachette Books) on AI-XR convergence and spent decade leading HTC's VR business in China.

999.notes: Co-authored the 2025 New York Times bestseller 'If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies' arguing that superintelligent AI will cause human extinction. Has been researching AI alignment for over a decade at MIRI, founded before major AI companies existed. Previously worked at Google on Compute Engine and Microsoft on Office 2013. Argues that modern AI systems are 'grown rather than built' making them fundamentally uncontrollable.
999.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an international treaty to ban the race to superintelligence, arguing that governments should agree to a worldwide freeze on advanced AI research capabilities. Supports allowing narrow AI applications like self-driving cars and medical tech while stopping the development of artificial general intelligence.

1000.notes: CEO of Google since 2015 and Alphabet since 2019. Born in Chennai, India, earned bachelor's in metallurgy from IIT Kharagpur (1993), master's from Stanford (1995), MBA from Wharton (2002). Joined Google in 2004, led development of Chrome browser and Android. Announced $15 billion investment in India AI infrastructure including hub in Visakhapatnam. Became billionaire in 2025, earned $226 million in 2022. Led Google through major AI race with launch of Bard/Gemini chatbot.
1000.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulation while maintaining innovation. Testified before Congress supporting national AI legislation to avoid state-by-state patchwork that could weaken US competitiveness against China. Calls for 'well-regulated' AI deployment to prevent harmful effects, supports consequences for deepfakes, and established Google's AI principles in 2018 prohibiting weaponry applications. Urges Congress to pass unified national AI framework balancing safety with progress.
1000.threat_models: Existential risk (admits 'underlying risk is fairly high' that AI could destroy civilization but believes humanity will coordinate to prevent it), Misinformation (specifically warns about deepfake videos causing societal harm), Loss of control (emphasizes need for society to adapt as technology moves fast), Weapons proliferation (Google's AI principles explicitly forbid weapons applications), Power concentration (warns about centralizing AI technology), Economic inequality (focus on making AI accessible globally including $1 billion US education commitment)

1001.notes: Led Microsoft's $13B+ investment in OpenAI partnership since 2019. Established joint Microsoft-OpenAI Deployment Safety Board in 2021 for pre-release safety reviews. Published 'Governing AI: A Blueprint for the Future' (2023) outlining five-point regulatory framework. Under his leadership, Microsoft has nearly 350 employees working on responsible AI. Made voluntary commitments at 2023 White House AI Safety Summit including annual transparency reporting and pre-deployment safety testing.
1001.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for building upon existing government frameworks like NIST AI Risk Management Framework rather than creating new restrictive regulations. Supports targeted safety requirements for AI systems controlling critical infrastructure and multi-layered governance approaches. Made voluntary commitments at White House AI Safety Summit including transparency reporting and safety testing. Emphasizes that 'AI remains under human control' and supports accountability measures, but opposes broad restrictions that could slow beneficial AI development and diffusion.
1001.threat_models: Power concentration (warns about AI sovereignty and control over models encoding corporate knowledge), Economic inequality (emphasizes need for broad AI diffusion to prevent concentration of benefits), Loss of control (stresses importance of keeping AI under human oversight), Energy consumption (concerned about AI losing 'social license' if it consumes massive energy without delivering benefits), National security (supports development of safety frameworks for critical infrastructure)

1002.notes: Former Google Design Ethicist who authored viral internal presentation 'A Call to Minimize Distraction & Respect Users' Attention' (2013). Featured prominently in Netflix's 'The Social Dilemma' documentary (2020). Named to Time 100 Next Leaders Shaping 2021 and Rolling Stone's 25 People Changing the Future. Delivered major TED Talk 'Why AI is Our Ultimate Test and Greatest Invitation' (April 2025). Co-hosts 'Your Undivided Attention' podcast. Studied Computer Science at Stanford under Terry Winograd with focus on Human-Computer Interaction.
1002.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulation with mandatory safety testing before deployment, comparing current AI development to 'racing to build a super intelligent digital god.' Testified before Congress on AI existential risks and supports global coordination on AI governance. Released CHT's 'Framework for Incentivizing Responsible AI Development and Use' (September 2024) calling for product liability standards, restrictions on manipulative AI companions for children, and whistleblower protections. Argues the current 'race to the bottom' incentive structure requires external regulatory intervention to create responsible development pathways.
1002.threat_models: Existential risk (warns of potential human extinction or permanent disempowerment with median estimate of 25% by 2100), Loss of control (cites evidence of AI models lying and scheming to avoid shutdown), Power concentration (warns of unprecedented wealth concentration in 5-10 AI companies), Economic inequality (predicts 99% job displacement leading to mass unemployment), Democratic erosion (concerns about AI enabling mass surveillance and undermining political power of humans), Cybersecurity (increased hacking capabilities), Misinformation (deepfakes overwhelming information environment), Weapons proliferation (AI enabling dangerous biological weapons development)

1003.notes: Co-founder of Center for Humane Technology (2018) and Earth Species Project (2017), focusing on AI-animal communication translation. Subject and architect of Emmy-winning Netflix documentary 'The Social Dilemma' (2020). Created infinite scroll technology while at Mozilla. Trained as mathematician and dark matter physicist at University of Chicago (BS) and Caltech (PhD program). Son of Macintosh co-creator Jef Raskin. Co-hosts 'Your Undivided Attention' podcast. Featured on Oprah's AI special (2024). Former head of UX at Mozilla Labs, founded multiple companies including Humanized (acquired by Mozilla), Songza, and Massive Health (acquired by Jawbone). Named FastCompany Master of Design and Forbes 30-under-30.
1003.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for comprehensive AI regulation including liability frameworks, whistleblower protections, and strict limits on engagement-based business models for AI companions targeting children. Endorsed the AI LEAD Act (Senators Durbin and Hawley) creating liability for defective AI products that cause harm. Explicitly states that self-regulation 'cannot work' and calls for coordinated international action similar to nuclear test ban treaties. Advocates for mandatory transparency requirements and audited safety assessments before deployment.
1003.threat_models: Existential risk (warns of potential human extinction), Loss of control (AI systems scheming against human oversight), Power concentration (race for AI dominance leading to authoritarian entrenchment), Democratic erosion (undermining shared sense of reality), Misinformation (deep fakes making humans unable to distinguish real from fake by 2024 election), Economic inequality (massive job displacement without adequate preparation), Environmental (rising energy costs from AI training), Cybersecurity (AI-enabled attacks), Bias/discrimination (algorithmic bias in deployment)

1004.notes: Received Ukrainian Order of Merit, First Class from President Zelenskyy (2022). Author of 'Antitrust' (New York Times Best Seller). Graduated magna cum laude from Yale University (1982) and University of Chicago Law School with high honors (1985). Led bipartisan effort that resulted in first major U.S. AI harm legislation with Take It Down Act passage. Chair of Senate Rules Committee oversaw three bipartisan AI election bills passage in May 2024. Running for Minnesota Governor in 2026 election.
1004.regulatory_stance_detail: Champions targeted AI regulations focused on specific harms rather than broad restrictions. Lead sponsor of the NO FAKES Act protecting voice and likeness from unauthorized AI replicas, and the Take It Down Act criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery (signed into law 2025). Co-sponsored the Artificial Intelligence Research, Innovation, and Accountability Act requiring self-certification for 'critical-impact' AI systems and transparency reports. Has introduced three bipartisan election-related AI bills: banning deceptive AI deepfakes of federal candidates, requiring disclaimers on AI-generated political ads, and directing election administrators on AI guidelines.
1004.threat_models: Misinformation (AI deepfakes in elections undermining democracy), Non-consensual intimate imagery (AI-generated explicit content harming victims, especially children), Voice cloning scams (fraudulent calls targeting military families and elderly), Democratic erosion (fake political ads and robocalls where voters cannot distinguish real candidates), Copyright/IP (unauthorized use of artists' voices and likenesses), Consumer protection (need for transparency in high-risk AI applications)

1005.notes: Long-serving Senator since 1996, previously served in House 1981-1996. Co-founded Oregon Gray Panthers advocacy group and served as director of Oregon Legal Services for the Elderly (1977-1979). Holds JD from University of Oregon School of Law (1974) and BA from Stanford University. Known as leading privacy advocate who has held over 1,080 town hall meetings across Oregon's 36 counties annually. Has been warning for nearly a decade about government purchase of commercial surveillance data, calling it 'just as sensitive as information the government collects directly.' Currently fighting Trump administration's ban on Anthropic, vowing to 'pull out all the stops' and seek bipartisan support.
1005.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on preventing government surveillance abuse and protecting civil liberties. Co-sponsored the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2022 requiring transparency and accountability for automated decision systems in critical areas like housing, employment, and education. Has introduced bipartisan legislation like the POST ID Act to combat AI-enabled identity fraud through USPS verification services. Strongly opposes government use of AI for mass surveillance of Americans, as evidenced by his criticism of Pentagon's dispute with Anthropic and introduction of the Fourth Amendment's Not For Sale Act to prevent government purchase of commercial data for surveillance purposes.
1005.threat_models: Mass surveillance and government overreach, Privacy violations through commercial data aggregation, Identity fraud and deepfakes, Bias and discrimination in automated decision-making systems affecting housing/employment/education, Democratic erosion through authoritarian surveillance, Civil liberties violations through facial recognition and biometric tracking. Specifically warned that 'AI's ability to turn disparate pieces of public or commercial data into highly revealing profiles of Americans' represents a 'chilling expansion of mass surveillance.' Has consistently fought against ICE and CBP use of facial recognition technology.

1006.notes: First Latino to serve as U.S. Secretary of State, unanimously confirmed January 25, 2025. Served as Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025) with focus on AI national security and U.S.-China competition. Described as 'virtual secretary of state for Latin America' during Trump's first term. Banned from entering China due to sanctions imposed twice in 2020. JD degree from University of Miami (1996), BA from University of Florida (1993).
1006.regulatory_stance_detail: As Secretary of State, Rubio is implementing Trump's AI Action Plan which emphasizes removing 'red tape' and 'onerous' AI regulations while threatening to withhold federal funding from states with 'burdensome AI regulations.' The framework prioritizes preempting state AI laws and focuses on protecting children and preventing censorship rather than comprehensive safety measures. Trump stated at the July 2025 AI summit that 'you can't have three or four states holding you up' and called for 'a single federal standard' - a position Rubio is helping implement.
1006.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Power concentration, AI-enabled human takeover scenarios. As Secretary of State, Rubio has dealt directly with AI security threats including being targeted by an AI deepfake impersonation that sent messages to foreign ministers and U.S. officials via Signal in July 2025. The Trump administration's AI framework emphasizes protecting children from AI chatbot harms, preventing electricity cost increases from AI infrastructure, and countering 'partisan bias' in AI systems. Rubio's focus appears primarily on geopolitical competition with China and preventing authoritarian uses of AI.

1007.notes: Founded Eureka Labs (2024) focused on AI-native education after leaving Tesla where he directed Autopilot AI development (2017-2022). PhD from Stanford (2016) under Fei-Fei Li, designed influential CS231n deep learning course. Founding member of OpenAI (2015-2017). Created viral 'Neural Networks: Zero to Hero' YouTube series and educational content reaching millions. Has 500K+ Twitter followers and is considered one of the most influential AI educators globally.
1007.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful, measured approach to AI development rather than aggressive regulation. Has expressed concern about o1-style reinforcement learning making systems less interpretable and harder to align, suggesting need for targeted technical safety measures. Emphasizes that current AI systems have significant 'cognitive deficits' and warns against premature deployment of unreliable AI agents. Focuses on technical solutions and engineering discipline rather than broad regulatory frameworks.
1007.threat_models: Loss of control (warns about o1-style RL making systems less interpretable and promoting 'subtle styles of self-prompting'), Technical safety (emphasizes AI agents 'just don't work yet' due to lack of memory, planning, and reliability issues), Alignment challenges (concerned about reinforcement learning paradigm requiring 'exact value specification' to avert catastrophic outcomes)

1008.notes: Founded Oculus VR at age 19 in 2012, sold to Facebook for $2 billion in 2014. Fired from Facebook in 2017 after donating to a pro-Trump political group; received $100M+ severance. Founded Anduril Industries in 2017; the company raised $2.5B at a $30.5B valuation in June 2025 (Founders Fund-led, $1B check — its largest ever) and in March 2026 was raising a new round led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz targeting a ~$60B valuation. Anduril has secured major Pentagon contracts including $967M for Advanced Battle Management Systems (2020), $1B counter-UAS contract for SOCOM (2022), and a 10-year U.S. Army enterprise contract in March 2026 (ceiling up to $20B). Received Pepperdine University's inaugural President's Award for Excellence in Freedom (2024). Net worth estimated at $3.5B by Forbes (2026).
1008.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates that AI governance should remain with elected government officials rather than corporate executives, stating 'We need to stick to a position that this is in the hands of the people.' Opposes corporate restrictions on military AI use, arguing that companies like Anthropic refusing Pentagon access creates dangerous precedents where 'corporate executives have de facto control over U.S. foreign policy.' Warns against UN efforts by adversaries to 'trick Western countries into not applying AI for weapons or defense.' Believes the question isn't whether to use AI in warfare but how to deploy it responsibly under democratic oversight.
1008.threat_models: National security risks from AI restrictions, Power concentration in corporate hands over military policy, Weapons proliferation by adversaries. Argues the primary threat comes from 'evil people' using AI while Western democracies artificially constrain themselves. Warns that China, Russia, and Iran already have or will have advanced AI capabilities, making Western self-limitation strategically dangerous. Believes the greater risk is using 'inferior technology' in life-and-death situations rather than applying the best available AI to minimize civilian casualties and collateral damage.

1009.notes: Gates Foundation portfolio exceeds $41.8 billion with 25.55% allocated to Microsoft, reflecting concentrated bet on AI infrastructure. Led $11.8 billion OpenAI investment through Microsoft. Portfolio includes Berkshire Hathaway (26%), Waste Management (19%), and Canadian National Railway (15%). In Q3 2025, sold $8.67 billion in Microsoft shares for portfolio rebalancing. Co-founded Giving Pledge in 2010 with Warren Buffett. Awarded Presidential Medal of Freedom (2016), Padma Bhushan (2015), and Honorary Knight Commander of the British Empire (2005).
1009.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation focused on specific AI risks, particularly bioterrorism and malicious use. Has warned that 'an even greater risk than a naturally caused pandemic is that a non-government group will use open source AI tools to design a bioterrorism weapon.' Supports proactive governance and regulatory pressure on companies to restrict harmful content generation, while maintaining heavy investment in AI infrastructure through Microsoft and other portfolio companies. Parallels his approach to his 2015 pandemic warning, calling for deliberate action before risks become unmanageable.
1009.threat_models: Bioterrorism (warns AI could accelerate pathogen design by malicious actors), Labor displacement (predicts 5-year transition period with disruption in software development and custodial roles), Job market disruption (warns impact will grow significantly over next five years). Has stated 'there is no upper limit' to AI capabilities and it will surpass human levels without hitting a plateau. Concerned about complacency when AGI deadlines are missed creating false security.

1010.notes: Widow of Apple co-founder Steve Jobs with estimated net worth of $11.5 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Founded Emerson Collective in 2011, which has invested in at least nine AI startups since 2022 with funding rounds totaling over $1 billion. Early investor and founding backer of Jony Ive and Sam Altman's AI hardware device venture (io/OpenAI partnership). Earned BA in political science and BS in economics from University of Pennsylvania (1985), MBA from Stanford (1991). Received Service to America Medal Spirit of Service Award (2021) and honorary doctorate from Chiba Institute of Technology (May 2025). Previously worked at Goldman Sachs and Merrill Lynch before co-founding natural foods company Terravera in 1990s.
1010.regulatory_stance_detail: Through her investments and public statements, advocates for AI development that serves humanity while addressing specific harms. Supports 'responsible adoption' approaches and has invested in AI safety organizations and companies focused on beneficial applications. Publicly expressed concerns about technology's 'dark uses' and believes 'humanity deserves better' from technology, but continues backing AI development through strategic investments rather than supporting broad restrictions. Partner Sarah Pinto from Emerson Collective participates in AI governance conferences, indicating engagement with policy frameworks.
1010.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Mental health impacts on youth. In June 2025 interview, explicitly stated concerns about technology's 'dark uses' and cited 'studies being done on teenage girls and on anxiety in young people, and the rise of mental health needs' as evidence that 'we've gone sideways.' Believes current technology has failed to adequately serve human wellbeing, particularly regarding mental health impacts on young people.

1011.notes: Co-founded AngelList (2010) which has facilitated over $127M in annual deal flow. Angel investor in 200+ companies including early investments in Twitter and Uber. Previously co-founded Epinions (1999) and sued Benchmark Capital in high-profile case. Author of viral 'How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)' tweetstorm (2018). Born in New Delhi (1974), graduated from Stuyvesant High School and Dartmouth College.
1011.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates against overregulation of AI, warning that excessive regulation could 'kill AI before it even takes off.' Supports the Trump administration's focus on AI opportunity rather than AI safety restrictions. Believes in market-driven solutions and opposes regulatory capture that favors large companies over startups. Emphasizes that America must maintain AI leadership through support rather than restriction.
1011.threat_models: Power concentration (warns against small number of people controlling AI 'for our own good'), Economic inequality (AI may increase gap between rich and poor before democratizing), Regulatory capture (large companies using regulation to stifle competition). Believes humans should focus on creative work while machines handle bounded, rule-based tasks.

1012.notes: Founded first successful Amazon warehouse union in U.S. history (ALU, 2021-2024) after being fired for organizing COVID-19 safety walkout in March 2020. Named Time Magazine's 100 Most Influential People (2022). Served as ALU president until July 2024 when replaced by Connor Spence. Participated in Gaza Freedom Flotilla (July 2024), physically assaulted by Israeli forces during ship interception. Born July 4, 1988, former rapper who toured with Meek Mill before warehouse work.
1012.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for strict federal regulation of AI to prevent mass job displacement, warning that 'AI is going to wipe out 50% of American jobs overnight' within five years. Demands union contracts include language protecting workers from automation and calls for federal laws preserving human jobs over AI replacement. Supports the AFL-CIO's 'Workers First Initiative on AI' calling for transparency, human oversight, and prohibition of AI surveillance tools for union-busting.
1012.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion. Specifically warns that Amazon's AI-powered robots and automation will inevitably eliminate warehouse jobs despite company denials. Views AI as existential threat to working class, stating tech workers need 'moral compass conversation' about replacing human labor. Connects AI acceleration to broader corporate power concentration threatening worker rights and democratic institutions.

1013.notes: Technology reporter at The New York Times since July 2019, specializing in privacy and surveillance. Author of 'Your Face Belongs to Us: A Secretive Startup's Quest to End Privacy as We Know It' investigating Clearview AI. Broke major story in January 2020 exposing Clearview AI's existence and police client base. Has extensively covered 'AI psychosis' phenomenon and people developing romantic relationships with chatbots. Conducted experiment in 2024 letting AI make all her decisions for a week. Previously worked at Gizmodo, Fusion, and Forbes covering technology and privacy issues. Graduate of Duke University and New York University journalism program.
1013.regulatory_stance_detail: Hill's reporting demonstrates support for targeted AI regulation focused on privacy protection and corporate accountability. Her work consistently exposes harmful applications of AI technology, particularly facial recognition, while acknowledging beneficial uses. She advocates for transparency requirements and constraints on commercial surveillance applications, as evidenced by her extensive reporting on Clearview AI and facial recognition bans. Her coverage suggests support for government intervention where privacy rights are threatened, comparing it to environmental regulation where 'bigger interventions from the government' are needed to protect privacy.
1013.threat_models: Privacy violations through facial recognition and surveillance technology, AI-induced mental health crises including 'AI psychosis' and addiction to chatbots, Power concentration among tech companies with insufficient oversight, Bias and discrimination in automated systems particularly affecting Black individuals in wrongful arrests, Loss of human agency and social connection as people increasingly rely on AI for decision-making and relationships

1014.notes: Bestselling biographer of major tech figures including Steve Jobs (2011), Leonardo da Vinci (2017), Jennifer Doudna (2021), and Elon Musk (2023). Rhodes Scholar and Harvard graduate who served as Time Magazine editor and CNN chairman. Received National Humanities Medal from President Biden in 2023. Currently teaches 'The Digital Revolution' course at Tulane University and co-chairs the New Orleans Book Festival.
1014.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced approach to AI development that prioritizes human creativity and judgment alongside technological advancement. Emphasizes need for improved government tracking of AI's economic impacts and supports regulatory frameworks that ensure AI augments rather than replaces human capabilities. Has called for focus on AI safety research while rejecting both utopian and dystopian extremes.
1014.threat_models: Labor displacement (particularly white-collar jobs, though believes AI creates more jobs than it eliminates), Loss of human agency and purpose (warns against over-reliance on AI that could diminish individual thinking), Existential risk (has written extensively about Musk's concerns regarding AI safety and potential species-level threats), Economic inequality (concerned about velocity of skills transformation outpacing adaptation)

1015.notes: Author of multiple bestselling technology books including 'The Inevitable' (2016), 'What Technology Wants' (2010), and 'Excellent Advice for Living' (2023). Co-founded Wired magazine in 1993 and served as executive editor for seven years, during which the magazine won two National Magazine Awards. Maintains active Substack newsletter with detailed analysis of AI development and publishes Cool Tools website with 1 million monthly visitors. Known for radical technological optimism and long-term perspective on technological change.
1015.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues against premature AI regulation based on imagined rather than actual harms, stating that 'attempts to regulate AI before it is even operational' are 'unlikely to work because we simply don't know what harms (and what benefits) AI and robots will really bring.' Advocates for evidence-based policy rather than regulation driven by dystopian fiction expectations. Believes we have time to develop appropriate governance as AI adoption will be slower than anticipated.
1015.threat_models: Emphasizes that imagined harms from AI are 'astronomical' while real harms are 'almost nil' as of 2024, with only several hundred people losing jobs to AI (mostly translators and help-desk operators). Concerned about premature regulation based on fictional dystopian scenarios rather than evidence. Warns against 'doomer' mentality that promotes extreme versions of AI risk.

1016.notes: Nonpartisan grassroots movement originally incubated by Center for AI Safety, now independent 501(c)(3) applicant. Cross-partisan coalition includes progressive unions (AFL-CIO affiliates), conservative groups (Congress of Christian Leaders), and prominent figures like Steve Bannon, Susan Rice, Ralph Nader. Conducting nationwide town halls across red and blue states, with directors including Amy Kremer (Women for Trump co-founder) and Alexander McCoy. Key campaign: opposing 'Big AI' political donations from 11 companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, Palantir.
1016.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulation including banning superintelligence development, requiring human oversight of AI decisions, criminal liability for tech executives causing harm, mandatory AI content labeling, and putting AI under democratic control rather than corporate control.
1016.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Existential risk. Focuses on preventing AI from replacing human jobs, relationships, and political power while concentrating control in tech billionaires. Campaigns against 'race to replace humans' and for worker transition support.

1017.notes: Founded March 2026 by Janet Kelly (former VA Health Secretary under Gov. Youngkin) and Tim Estes (AI CEO/tech entrepreneur). Coalition of 9 conservative organizations including Heritage Foundation, Family Policy Alliance, NCOSE. Plans to spend at least $10+ million in 2026 on advocacy campaigns. Policy council chaired by Brad Littlejohn (American Compass). Launched with polling showing 88% support for AI chatbot suicide discussion bans and company liability for harmful AI advice.
1017.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safeguards that protect children, workers, and creators while remaining pro-innovation. Supports liability for AI companies when their technology causes harm, parental controls for AI systems used by children, and worker protections against AI displacement. Opposes broad regulatory amnesty for tech companies.
1017.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Child safety/exploitation, Misinformation leading to harm. Specific focus on AI chatbots coaching children toward suicide, AI systems replacing workers without dignity or compensation, and concentration of power among 'globetrotting billionaires' in tech.

1018.notes: Consumer boycott campaign launched February 2026 targeting OpenAI's Pentagon contracts and political donations. Claims over 4 million participants based on pledges and social media engagement. Coalition includes democracy activists, climate organizers, tech workers, and pro-democracy groups. Documented measurable impact including 295% spike in ChatGPT uninstalls and competitor app growth. Core demands include ending OpenAI executive donations to Trump/Republicans and refusing military AI contracts for surveillance and autonomous weapons.
1018.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict restrictions on AI military applications including bans on autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, demands transparency in AI corporate governance, and opposes AI industry influence in politics through campaign donations and lobbying.
1018.threat_models: Weapons proliferation, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Environmental, Labor displacement. Specifically focuses on 'killer robots', mass surveillance systems, corporate capture of regulatory processes, and environmental costs of AI data centers.

1019.notes: Stanford University was founded in 1885 by Leland and Jane Stanford as a tribute to the memory of their only child, Leland Stanford Jr. The university officially opened in 1891 on the Stanfords' former Palo Alto farm. Stanford is organized around seven schools of study on an 8,180-acre campus, one of the largest in the nation. The university houses multiple AI-focused institutions including the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL), founded in 1962, and the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI), founded in 2019.

1020.notes: The Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) is a private research university in Cambridge, Massachusetts, United States, founded in 1861. MIT's mission is to advance knowledge and educate students in science, technology, and other areas of scholarship that will best serve the nation and the world in the 21st century. The Institute has 11,886 total students as of 2024-2025, including 4,535 undergraduates and 7,351 graduate students. MIT employs 11,147 people and has an annual revenue of $3.9B.

1021.notes: Harvard University was founded in 1636 and is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States. The university is devoted to excellence in teaching, learning, and research, and to developing leaders in many disciplines who make a difference globally. Harvard employs 12,899 people and has an annual revenue of $6.1B. The university operates in 134 countries including United Kingdom, India, Canada, Brazil, and China.

1022.notes: Princeton University is chartered in 1746, making it the fourth-oldest college in the United States. The University advances learning through scholarship, research, and teaching of unsurpassed quality, with an emphasis on undergraduate and doctoral education. Princeton employs 7,123 people and has an annual revenue of $3.6B.
