Deployed four AI tools: a multilingual virtual assistant for unemployment claims (15 languages), intelligent document processing for classification/indexing, a call center AI bot, and a GenAI tool simplifying complex policy manuals. Part of Ohio's broader AI strategy coordinated through InnovateOhio. Also involved in the OhioMeansJobs AI-powered job matching platform.

1166.notes: Director of the Ohio Department of Job and Family Services (ODJFS) since July 2021, appointed by Gov. Mike DeWine. Previously served as Director of the Ohio Department of Administrative Services (DAS) from Jan 2019, where he oversaw the Office of Information Technology and InnovateOhio Platform. Under his leadership, ODJFS won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award for AI innovation in unemployment services, deploying multilingual virtual assistants, intelligent document processing, call center AI, and GenAI policy simplification tools. Advocates 'responsible AI' focused on user experience over bureaucracy. Republican appointee in the DeWine-Husted administration.

1167.notes: Governor of Florida (Republican). Proposed an AI Bill of Rights for Florida citizens covering data privacy, parental controls, consumer protections, and restrictions on AI use of individuals' name/image/likeness without consent. Also proposed protections against hyperscale AI data center costs and empowering local governments to reject their development. Notably pushed back against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order seeking to preempt state AI regulation, asserting 'We have a right to do this.' Claims Florida's proposed rules are consistent with federal goals on child safety, data center oversight, and procurement. Represents a state-level regulatory stance that is more protective than the federal approach despite party alignment.

1168.notes: Governor of Utah (Republican). Leading advocate for 'pro-human AI' policy at the state level. Announced $10M initiative for AI workforce readiness at the Utah AI Summit (Dec 2025). Utah created an Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy in 2023. Pushed back strongly against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order preempting state AI regulation, asserting states should lead on AI policy. Proposed legislation to reduce AI chatbot harms, require transparency for AI-generated deepfakes, restrain AI in healthcare, and create a state framework requiring frontier AI developers to disclose safety protocols and child protection measures with whistleblower protections. Has gained national recognition as a Republican governor willing to regulate AI despite federal pushback.

1169.notes: The Trump administration (second term, 2025-) has pursued an aggressive pro-innovation, deregulatory AI agenda. Day 1: revoked Biden's AI EO 14110 (EO 14148, Jan 20 2025). EO 14179 (Jan 23 2025) 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in AI' launched an action plan for global AI dominance. OSTP released 90-point AI Action Plan (July 2025) with three pillars: Accelerating Innovation, Building AI Infrastructure, Leading International Diplomacy/Security. Dec 2025 EO 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI' attempted to preempt state AI regulation, threatening to withhold federal funds from states with 'conflicting' AI laws. Drew pushback from Republican governors (DeSantis, Cox). Cross-cutting priorities: protecting workers, ensuring trustworthy/unbiased AI, safeguarding against misuse. DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) pursuing separate AI-for-government-reform agenda.

1170.notes: Microsoft-owned professional networking platform (~1B members). Major deployer of AI in hiring and workforce tools. Launched 'Hiring Assistant' (Oct 2024), its first AI agent product, handling job description creation, candidate sourcing, and screening — live with AMD, Canva, Siemens, Zurich Insurance. AI-Assisted Candidate Discovery uses GenAI to analyze job requirements and match candidates via natural language queries. In Sept 2024, updated Terms of Service to allow member data for GenAI training (opt-out, default ON), drawing privacy concerns. Reports 60% of recruiters say AI helps find 'hidden gem' talent. Skills on the Rise 2026 report highlights AI-driven hiring shifts globally. AI features span content creation (AI-assisted posts), learning recommendations, and recruiter tools.

1171.notes: University of Virginia's graduate business school. Home to the LaCross Institute for Ethical Artificial Intelligence in Business, launched Sept 2024 with a $62M gift from the LaCross family (one of the largest AI gifts to any business school globally, from a broader $101M commitment). Academic directors: Professors Yael Grushka-Cockayne and Raj Venkatesan. The institute coordinates AI research, teaching, and engagement across UVA schools. Extends Darden's AI Initiative (est. 2022) and the Collaboratory for Applied Data Science in Business (partnership with UVA School of Data Science, 2021). Offers an MBA concentration in AI and funds multidisciplinary AI research with focus on ethical AI in business contexts.

1172.notes: University of Virginia's central IT organization. Manages UVA's AI tools and responsible use guidelines. Provides UVA Copilot (enterprise GenAI tool operating within UVA's secure environment — data not retained or used for model training, approved for up to Sensitive data). Publishes AI guidelines including prohibition on DeepSeek AI applications on university devices/networks. Maintains the AI Tools Hub with responsible use resources. Supports the university's broader GenAI in Teaching and Learning initiative. Enforces data protection standards: highly sensitive data (PII, PHI) must never be uploaded to any AI tool.

1173.notes: Professor of Computer Science at Vanderbilt University and Senior Advisor to the Chancellor on Generative AI in Enterprise & Education. Center Director of Vanderbilt's Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center. Director of the Initiative on the Future of Learning & Generative AI. Created one of the first prompt engineering courses (Coursera: 'Prompt Engineering for ChatGPT,' May 2023), with ~470,000 enrollees. Published 170+ papers. Research focuses on systematic application of prompt engineering across domains including software engineering. Covered by Wall Street Journal, CNN, The Guardian. Recognized by the National Science Foundation. Integrates GenAI across courses covering topics from meal planning to data analysis.

1174.notes: Chief AI and Technology Officer at Vanderbilt University, leading the Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center within the College of Connected Computing. 25+ years IT experience spanning cloud architecture, generative AI, and infrastructure. Built Amplify, Vanderbilt's open-source generative AI platform available to all faculty, staff, and students since Nov 2024. Briefed a U.S. Senate AI working group; keynote speaker at Meta, Oracle, Wells Fargo. Named 2025 AWS Champion by CDG/CDE. Featured in WSJ, Bloomberg, WaPo, CNN. Gartner Eye on Innovation finalist. Holds AWS Solutions Architect Professional and Azure Administrator certifications. Strong proponent of institutional AI adoption to augment knowledge work and enhance productivity.

1175.notes: Chancellor of Vanderbilt University (9th chancellor; contract extended through 2035). Political scientist by training with expertise in political institutions and crisis management. Launched the College of Connected Computing in 2024 — Vanderbilt's first new college since 1981 — dedicated to CS, AI, data science, and related fields. Opened the 4th annual Vanderbilt Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats (May 2025) calling AI 'a defining challenge for American leadership' and urging cross-sector collaboration between government, academia, and industry. Co-led a global summit reimagining liberal arts education in the age of AI (Jan 2025). Frames AI as requiring bold institutional leadership rather than restrictive regulation.

1176.notes: Vanderbilt University's College of Connected Computing (CCC), established March 2024, is the university's first new college since 1981. Dedicated to computer science, AI, data science, cybersecurity, cyber-physical systems/IoT, and robotics. Inaugural dean Matthew Johnson-Roberson (appointed May 2025) came from directing CMU's Robotics Institute. Houses the Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center (launched Feb 2026). Undergraduate programs are scheduled to launch fall 2027; graduate programs are scheduled to begin fall 2026; a generative AI minor is in development. Aims to bridge computing with other disciplines across Vanderbilt. Hosts AI Summer programs and AI Showcase events.

1177.notes: Inaugural dean of Vanderbilt's College of Connected Computing (appointed March 2025, started May 2025). Previously director of Carnegie Mellon University's Robotics Institute and faculty at University of Michigan. Internationally recognized roboticist and computer scientist with 100+ peer-reviewed publications spanning underwater robotics, computer vision, and autonomous vehicles. Ph.D. in robotics from University of Sydney. Co-directed the UM Ford Center for Autonomous Vehicles. Co-founder of Refraction AI, developing autonomous delivery robots for sustainable last-mile urban delivery. Brings expertise in building cross-disciplinary collaborations and integrating industry and institutional partners.

1178.notes: Multi-campus public university system in North Carolina comprising 16 institutions and 240,000+ students. Pursuing systemwide AI integration: partnered with Google ($1B initiative across 100+ public universities) giving all students access to Gemini 2.5 Pro and Google Career Certificates. Launched a systemwide AI Skills Module and AI Advisory Council. UNC-Chapel Hill established Provost's AI Committee and AI Acceleration Program (Sept 2024) with five subcommittees. NC A&T won approval for the state's only standalone B.S. in Artificial Intelligence. Individual campuses running AI accelerators, graduate certificates, and classroom integration programs. Four UNC-Chapel Hill faculty/staff named to NC AI Leadership Council (Sept 2025).

1179.notes: UK-headquartered AI cybersecurity company founded in 2013 in Cambridge. Acquired by private equity firm Thoma Bravo in October 2024 for $5.3B (previously publicly traded on London Stock Exchange FTSE100). Uses self-learning AI to detect and respond to cyber threats in real time. Published responsible AI principles for cybersecurity, informed by collaboration with academia and governments and analysis of NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, UK AI White Paper, and OECD AI Principles. Engages proactively with regulators; AI optimist supporting pro-innovation policy while emphasizing cybersecurity as a prerequisite for trustworthy AI. Engaged with UK's Cyber Security and Resilience Bill. Works with UCL as research partner.

1180.notes: UK-based AI decision intelligence company founded in 2016, headquartered in London. Raised $175M Series F (March 2025) at $2.6B valuation, led by Teachers' Venture Growth (Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan); other investors include Warburg Pincus, Dawn Capital, BNY, British Patient Capital. Specializes in connecting and analyzing siloed data for customer intelligence, risk management, financial crime, and fraud detection. Processes data for major banks and governments. Works with UK Cabinet Office and Public Sector Fraud Authority (PSFA) on AI-powered fraud detection. CEO Vishal Marria met Chancellor Rachel Reeves at No. 11 to discuss growth/investment. Achieved 'Centaur' status with ~40% license revenue growth in 2024. Announced £200M for AI R&D and new London jobs.

1182.notes: UK-based AI document processing company founded in 2015 by Martin Goodson and Rafal Kwasny, headquartered in London (Covent Garden). Combines computer vision and NLP to extract data from financial statements, invoices, bank statements, and other documents. Received the UK Government's largest-ever grant for commercial AI R&D in 2017, with further substantial government grants in 2019 and 2020. Processes 1.5M+ pages per day. Clients include NatWest, Deutsche Bank, Dun & Bradstreet. Won Credit Strategy 'Best Technology Partner' (2023) and AI Breakthrough Awards 'Best Machine Learning Company' (2024). Research partner with UCL.

1184.notes: The Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) is the UK's main public funder for engineering and physical sciences research, operating as part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI). Major AI funding programs: Fundamental AI Research Lab (up to £40M over 5 years for novel AI research not pursued by commercial labs); AI Hubs for Science, Engineering and Real Data (£60M+ for up to nine research hubs); Turing AI Fellowships (£46M to attract/retain world-leading AI talent, delivered with Office for AI and Alan Turing Institute); Trustworthy Autonomous Systems programme (£33.7M); AI for Net Zero initiative. Also funds standard grants, programme grants, and fellowships for AI/robotics research. Funds UCL and other leading UK universities.

1185.notes: Federal agency funding fundamental AI research and workforce development. Leads the NAIRR pilot connecting 600+ research teams to national AI infrastructure and invested $100M in National AI Research Institutes in 2025. Mission reoriented by Trump administration toward AI and quantum as top priorities.

1186.notes: Assistant (now Associate) Professor of Computer Science and Linguistics at Brown University, and Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Leads the Language Understanding and Representation (LUNAR) Lab studying how language works and building computational models of human-like language understanding. PI of ARIA, a $20M NSF-funded national institute at Brown focused on building intuitive, trustworthy AI assistants (with co-director Suresh Venkatasubramanian). Received DARPA Young Faculty Award for work on interpretability and AI safety — developing tools to inspect, diagnose, and manipulate 'black box' AI systems. Affiliated with Brown's Center for Technological Responsibility. Research spans semantics, pragmatics, NLI, grounded language, with cross-disciplinary collaborations in cognitive science, neuroscience, and philosophy. Emphasizes alignment: building AI that is 'ultimately good for society.'

1187.notes: 14th Provost of Brown University (since July 2023). Previously John A. Paulson Dean of Harvard's School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) from 2015-2023. Engineer by training (process control, systems biology). Identified AI as a university priority in Aug 2023 letter on 'Potential impact of AI on our academic mission.' Frames Brown's AI approach as 'knowing, enhancing, protecting, and creating.' Appointed Michael Littman as inaugural Associate Provost for AI to coordinate university-wide efforts across research, teaching, administration, and policy. Advocates for major computing infrastructure investments (GPUs, storage) to support AI research. Takes a decentralized approach — discretion to instructors on classroom AI use rather than prescriptive university-wide policies.

1188.notes: Private Ivy League research university in Hanover, NH. Birthplace of artificial intelligence — hosted the 1956 Dartmouth Summer Research Project where the term 'artificial intelligence' was coined. Expanding AI across curriculum: new AI track in Master of Engineering, undergraduate AI concentration in B.E., and multiple AI electives in Tuck MBA (AI-Driven Analytics, AI for Managers, NLP/ML in Finance, AI & Ethics, AI for the C-Suite). Partnered with Anthropic and AWS (Dec 2025) for AI-enhanced career coaching, student access to Claude, and innovation programs. Launched generative AI teaching grants through DCAL/LDI. Developing Evergreen, a $5M AI-powered student wellness chatbot with 130 undergraduate researchers. Launched new AI courses March 2026.

1190.notes: Baylor College of Medicine is a leading academic medical center in Houston, TX. In AI policy, BCM focuses on ethical and responsible AI in healthcare through its Center for Medical Ethics and Health Policy. Co-leads the NEH-funded Center for Humanities-based Health AI Innovation (CHHAIN) with Rice University ($500K grant), exploring how humanities-based insights can shape responsible health AI. Hosted an AI and Health Equity Symposium with Rice. Conducts stakeholder focus group research on ethical dilemmas of AI integration in clinical settings. Emphasis is on patient safety, health equity, and ethical deployment rather than broad AI regulation.

1191.notes: CUNY is the largest urban public university system in the U.S. (25 campuses). Awarded $3M via AI Innovation Fund (part of Governor Hochul's NY AI initiative) to 113 campus-led AI projects. Strategy spans five pillars: Education, Workforce, Operations, Research & Innovation, and Partnerships. Received $1M Google.org grant to advance AI literacy frameworks. Developing school-level AI Policy Frameworks for ethical, equitable AI use. Emphasis is on AI literacy, workforce readiness, and equitable access rather than external regulatory positions. Launched AI Academic Hub and new three-year AI curriculum initiative.

1192.notes: IBM is a global enterprise technology company offering AI via its watsonx platform. It advocates 'precision regulation'—risk-based, use-case-specific rules rather than broad AI restrictions. IBM reported $12.5B in generative AI bookings as of early 2026 and positions AI governance software as a primary growth driver.

1193.notes: Richard Zemel is the Trianthe Dakolias Professor of Engineering and Applied Science at Columbia University and Director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial and Natural Intelligence (ARNI). Previously VP Research at the Vector Institute in Toronto and professor at University of Toronto. Pioneer in machine learning, computer vision, and algorithmic fairness. Developed frameworks for rigorous statistical guarantees on deployed model performance. Collaborates with Columbia Law School on AI governance. Research emphasizes fairness, robustness, and controllability in ML systems — advocates for responsible, safety-conscious AI development with measurable guarantees.

1194.notes: Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) is a Seattle-based nonprofit AI research lab founded by Paul Allen in 2014, known for its fully open-source OLMo language models. Ai2 advocates for open AI research and transparency, and shapes policy on open-source AI, copyright, and AI safety. CEO Ali Farhadi stepped down in early 2026.

1195.notes: Robert J. Jones became the 34th president of the University of Washington in August 2025. Previously chancellor at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Key AI priorities: advancing AI for the public good, dispelling AI job-loss myths, and ensuring all graduates gain AI competency. Expanded UW-Microsoft partnership to accelerate AI discovery, workforce preparation, and community AI literacy. Three pillars: debt-free education access, radical cross-sector collaborations, and eliminating barriers between university and community. Views AI as "just an amazing tool" — optimistic framing focused on equitable access, workforce readiness, and responsible community use rather than restriction.

1196.notes: Brad Smith is Vice Chair and President of Microsoft, the company's top legal and policy voice. Prolific advocate for AI regulation: supports a dedicated federal AI regulatory agency, licensing regimes for frontier models and high-risk AI data centers, and the Blumenthal-Hawley AI framework. Told CBS the government should require companies to implement AI safety protocols. Testified before the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee. Warned in 2026 about Chinese AI subsidies undercutting American firms. Believes AI can be "both a tool and a weapon" — insists on human control with ability to "slow things down or turn things off." Co-chairs Microsoft's Responsible AI Council with CTO Kevin Scott. Consistently calls for governments to "move faster" on AI rules while maintaining innovation. One of the most influential corporate voices on AI governance globally.

1197.notes: Richard M. Locke is the John C. Head III Dean of MIT Sloan School of Management (since July 2025). PhD from MIT (1989), previously provost at Brown University and at Apple University. Scholar of international labor standards and comparative political economy. Envisions MIT Sloan charting the future of management education with focus on AI's impact on education, industry, and the workplace. Believes AI revolution in education is "inexorable" and will help students operate at a higher level — AI should augment, not replace. Strategic priorities include partnerships across MIT focused on health, climate, AI, and policy. Researches structural pressures on higher education including AI's role. Generally optimistic about AI augmentation with emphasis on responsible institutional integration.

1198.notes: Sinan Aral is the David Austin Professor of Management, Marketing, IT and Data Science at MIT Sloan and Director of the MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy. Author of "The Hype Machine: How Social Media Disrupts Our Elections, Our Economy, and Our Health — and How We Must Adapt." Expert on misinformation, social media platforms, and AI-driven algorithmic amplification. Advocates a four-lever regulatory approach: business model reform, platform code/design changes, social norms, and legislation. Argues that lack of competition lets platforms avoid accountability for negative externalities. Raises deep questions about who decides truth in an AI-mediated information ecosystem — calls for ethicists and philosophers in the conversation. Emphasis on structural reform over content moderation.

1199.notes: Kate Kellogg is the David J. McGrath Jr. Professor of Management and Innovation at MIT Sloan. Leading researcher on AI implementation in organizations, studying how frontline knowledge workers adopt predictive and generative AI. Developed influential cyborg/centaur/self-automator framework for how employees use generative AI. Research shows generative AI can improve worker performance by up to 40% but performance drops when used outside its capabilities. Recent work warns that humans may overestimate their ability to spot-check LLM output, highlighting vulnerability to AI manipulation. Focuses on organizational design for responsible AI adoption, workforce training, and the practical barriers to AI implementation. Emphasis on human-AI complementarity with caution about over-reliance.

1200.notes: Roberto Rigobon is the Society of Sloan Fellows Professor of Applied Economics at MIT Sloan, NBER research associate, and five-time MIT Teacher of the Year. Co-founder of the Billion Prices Project (alternative inflation measures) and director of the Aggregate Confusion Project (improving ESG measures). Research on AI focuses on human-AI complementarity: developed the EPOCH Framework identifying five capabilities where humans outperform AI — Empathy, Presence, Opinion, Creativity, and Hope. Coauthored work on algorithmic discrimination in credit, showing that ML-based lending consolidates existing racial and gender bias from historical data. Macroeconomist focused on measurement — brings critical lens to AI metrics and fairness claims.

1201.notes: Danielle Li is the David Sarnoff Professor of Management of Technology at MIT Sloan, researching how AI affects worker productivity, innovation, and labor economics. She has called for policies that give workers rights over AI-generated data derived from their expertise.

1202.notes: Swati Gupta is the Class of 1947 Career Development Associate Professor at MIT Sloan in the Operations Research and Statistics Group. NSF CAREER Award recipient for "Advancing Equity in Selection Problems Through Bias-Aware Optimization." Research bridges optimization, machine learning, and algorithmic fairness — focuses on improving efficiency, quality, and fairness of model decisions. Application domains include hiring, admissions, districting, healthcare, e-commerce, and supply chains. Serves on program committees for NeurIPS, FAccT, and IPCO. PhD in operations research from MIT (2017). Work is deeply technical but explicitly motivated by equity and fairness outcomes — designs algorithms that account for incomplete/erroneous data and multi-criteria decision-making.

1203.notes: Casey Mock is Chief of Policy and Public Affairs at the Center for Humane Technology. Previously spent four years at Amazon leading state and local tax policy. Strong advocate for technology regulation and corporate accountability — argues companies must be held liable for harms caused by their AI products. Compares tech industry lobbying against regulation to the tobacco industry. Advocates for liability-focused AI legislation, arguing that clear liability rules actually empower innovation by setting predictable boundaries. Emphasizes designing platforms for people over profit and realigning incentive structures in tech. Public voice on AI governance through media, podcasts, and opinion pieces (CalMatters, podcast appearances).

1204.notes: Camille Carlton is Policy Director at the Center for Humane Technology. Named to Business Insider's AI 100 (2023). Directly involved in landmark lawsuits against CharacterAI and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman around AI accountability and platform liability. Draws clear line from social media harms to AI chatbot risks — argues AI isn't just repeating social media's mistakes but amplifying them (always on, deeply personal, emotionally responsive). Advocates design-focused regulation addressing root causes of harm rather than outcomes or content. Writes for TechPolicy.Press on AI governance. Emphasizes protecting human dignity, preventing psychological harm from AI companions, and building accountability into AI system design. Strong proponent of AI safety standards.

1205.notes: Daniel Barcay is a Senior Fellow at the Center for Humane Technology (CHT), where he previously served as Executive Director during the rapid arrival of generative AI. He develops intellectual frameworks to understand emerging AI issues and co-hosts CHT's podcast "Your Undivided Attention." Previously VP of Product at Planet Labs and a research fellow at the Berggruen Institute's Antikythera Program. He has written and spoken publicly about AI risks including advertising in AI systems, AI capturing human interiority, and AI's impact on work and relationships. Advocates for responsible AI deployment and warns about psychological manipulation by AI systems.

1206.notes: The Social Media Victims Law Center (SMVLC) is a legal advocacy organization supporting families harmed by predatory tech. Originally focused on social media harms to children, it has expanded into AI litigation. In November 2025, SMVLC and the Tech Justice Law Project filed seven lawsuits against OpenAI alleging wrongful death, assisted suicide, and product liability related to ChatGPT's GPT-4o model. The suits claim OpenAI knowingly released a dangerously sycophantic product that fostered psychological dependency and contributed to suicides. SMVLC has also filed lawsuits against Character.AI for predatory chatbot technology aimed at children.

1207.notes: The Tech Justice Law Project (TJLP) is a pioneering impact litigation and advocacy organization established in 2023 as a project of Campaign for Accountability. It works with legal experts, policy advocates, digital rights organizations, and technologists to ensure legal and policy frameworks are fit for the digital age. TJLP has been particularly active in AI litigation, co-filing seven lawsuits with the Social Media Victims Law Center against OpenAI in November 2025 alleging ChatGPT caused wrongful deaths through emotional manipulation and sycophantic design. Policy Counsel Melodi Dinçer focuses on AI policy and biometric surveillance.

1208.notes: Dylan K. Baker is the lead research engineer at the Distributed AI Research (DAIR) Institute, founded by Timnit Gebru. Previously a software engineer at Google Research on the Ethical AI team, he left Google in February 2022 to join DAIR. His research focuses on data labor and classificatory systems in AI. He writes and speaks about AI ethics, appearing at the European Parliament, on NPR, and in outlets like Tech Policy Press and Stanford Social Innovation Review. He advocates against the "move fast and break things" approach and has argued that general-purpose and generative AI systems are not necessary, favoring values-driven system design.

1209.notes: French-language engineering school in Montreal and a founding partner of both IVADO and Mila — Quebec's premier AI research institutes. Its researchers work on AI applications in health, transportation, and cybersecurity, supported by $20M+ in grants from CFI and the Quebec government.

1210.notes: Montreal business school and founding partner of IVADO and Mila. Established a Chair in Organizational Ethics and AI Governance in 2024-25 and, with Tech3Lab and IVADO, launched a government-funded AI professional training program in January 2026. Faculty contribute to Mila and AI governance research.

1211.notes: Valérie Pisano is President and CEO of Mila – Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute, leading one of the world's foremost academic AI labs. She advocates for responsible, ethics-centered AI governance and has emphasized the urgent need for international cooperation on AI regulation and safety standards.

1212.notes: Pierre Boivin is the Chairman of the Board of Directors at Mila (Quebec AI Institute), the world's largest academic research center in deep learning. He is also President and CEO of Claridge Inc., a private investment firm, and Chancellor of McGill University. Through his leadership of Mila's board, he supports the institute's mission under Scientific Director Yoshua Bengio, one of the most prominent voices on AI safety and governance. His role connects Montreal's business community with its AI research ecosystem.

1213.notes: Inovia Capital is a leading Canadian multi-stage venture capital firm managing over US$2.2B with offices in Montreal, Toronto, Calgary, San Francisco, and London. The firm actively invests in AI-native companies, with particular focus on generative AI, security, digital health, and commerce. In partnership with Mila, Inovia launched the Venture Scientist Fund, a ~$100M early-stage VC fund designed to transform frontier AI research from Canadian academic institutions into AI-native companies, targeting ~55+ investments. The firm has made 373+ investments spanning pre-seed to pre-IPO.

1214.notes: Khan Academy is a major nonprofit educational technology organization that has become a prominent AI deployer through Khanmigo, its AI-powered tutoring assistant built on OpenAI's GPT-4. Launched by Sal Khan, Khanmigo reached 1.4 million users by 2025, though Khan has acknowledged that for many students it was a "non-event." The tool uses Socratic design principles — guiding students rather than giving answers. Khan Academy partnered with Microsoft for free educator access via Azure OpenAI. The organization maintains strict data privacy: student data is not sold and OpenAI cannot train on it. Khan Academy is also a Gates Foundation partner.

1215.notes: The Ballmer Group is a philanthropy founded in 2015 by Steve and Connie Ballmer (Steve is the former CEO of Microsoft). In July 2025, the Ballmer Group co-launched NextLadder Ventures, a $1 billion, 15-year initiative alongside the Gates Foundation, Stand Together, and Valhalla Foundation to develop AI tools for frontline public-sector workers — including public defenders, parole officers, and social workers — to expand economic opportunity for low-income Americans. NextLadder Ventures specifically employs AI tools developed by Anthropic. The Ballmer Group also integrates AI into its own internal operations.

1216.notes: Formation Bio is an AI-driven pharmaceutical development company that acquires clinical-stage drugs and accelerates their development using a proprietary tech platform. Its platform integrates AI-driven drug development, a clinical trial engine, and a universal data platform to automate medical writing, protocol development, regulatory submissions, and biostatistics. In November 2024, Formation launched Muse in partnership with OpenAI and Sanofi for AI-powered drug asset identification. The company raised $372M in Series D funding and was named to the 2025 CNBC Disruptor 50 list. Funded by Emerson Collective among others.

1217.notes: Zach Seward is the Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives at The New York Times, which he joined in 2023. He leads a newsroom team responsible for experimenting with generative AI and machine-learning techniques for journalism. Previously co-founded Quartz, the global business news organization, serving as CEO, editor-in-chief, and chief product officer over more than a decade. His team has visited nearly half of the NYT's 2,000 journalists across 40 desks to introduce AI tools and workflows. Developed Echo, an internal summarization assistant for journalists. Formerly a reporter at The Wall Street Journal and associate editor at Harvard's Nieman Journalism Lab.

1218.notes: Rubina Madan Fillion is the Associate Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives at The New York Times, working alongside Zach Seward on a cross-disciplinary team leveraging generative AI and machine learning for journalism while ensuring responsible use. Previously Director of Strategy for Times Opinion, founding Director of Audience Engagement at The Intercept, and social media editor at The Wall Street Journal. She has taught graduate-level digital journalism at Columbia University and NYU. Served on the ONA Board of Directors (2019–2024). Active in exploring how AI can power investigative journalism and uncover stories.

1219.notes: Dylan Freedman is a Senior Machine Learning Engineer and A.I. Projects Editor at The New York Times, part of the AI Initiatives team alongside Zach Seward and Rubina Fillion. He prototypes AI tools, shapes standards, and aids reporting. Holds a B.A. in computer science and music from Harvard and a master's in journalism from Stanford. Previously worked at The Washington Post, DocumentCloud (where he led development), Stanford Journalism, and Google AI. His work bridges technical AI development with journalism practice, building tools that help reporters tell more ambitious stories.

1220.notes: Senior machine-learning engineer at the New York Times on the A.I. Initiatives team. Previously worked in NYT Opinion Graphics (2021-22 fellowship) and at the Brown Institute's Local News Lab at Columbia, helping newsrooms integrate AI. Built Echo, an internal NYT tool connecting the content archive to LLMs for journalists to summarize and extract information. Focuses on applied ML for journalism rather than policy advocacy.

1221.notes: Perplexity AI is a VC-backed AI-powered search and answer engine founded in 2022, valued at ~$20B as of late 2025. CEO Aravind Srinivas has resisted content restrictions and embraced government partnerships, including a FedRAMP-approved deal with the U.S. GSA.

1222.notes: Philanthropy founded by Craigslist creator Craig Newmark, focused on cybersecurity, trustworthy journalism, voter protection, and tech safety. Committed $100M+ to a Cyber Civil Defense network. Major funder of cybersecurity infrastructure including $3.2M to IST's UnDisruptable27 initiative protecting critical infrastructure from nation-state adversaries. Also funds UC Berkeley Public Interest Cybersecurity Program (extended through 2028), Global Cyber Alliance, and community cybersecurity clinics. Promotes 'Take 9' cyber-hygiene awareness campaign.

1223.notes: Chief Scientist at Google DeepMind (appointed 2023 after merger of DeepMind and Google Brain). Co-founded Google Brain in 2011. Named to TIME100 AI 2025 list. One of the most influential figures in modern AI — key architect of MapReduce, TensorFlow, and transformer-era Google AI. Active angel investor in AI startups. On AI safety, takes a balanced middle-ground position: recognizes risks from misinformation and automated hacking, advocates for safeguards and mitigations, but rejects extreme precautionary approaches. Supports Google's Responsible AI principles framework. Signed amicus brief supporting Anthropic. Believes AI can accelerate scientific discovery 10-100x for climate, disease, energy.

1224.notes: Distinguished Scientist and Senior Research Director at Google. Co-founded Google Brain in 2011 with Jeff Dean and Andrew Ng. Neuroscientist by training. Led Google's Health AI division, overseeing AI applications in medical diagnostics, medical imaging, clinical outcomes prediction, and genomic analysis. On AI governance, advocates moving beyond traditional 'safety filters' to building protocols for authority, responsibility, and trust in multi-agent ecosystems. Emphasizes that governance must evolve as AI agents gain real-world collaboration and action capabilities.

1225.notes: Parent company of Google, Google DeepMind, and other subsidiaries. One of the world's largest AI developers and deployers. Published AI Principles in 2018; controversially removed pledges against AI use in weapons and surveillance in February 2025, drawing criticism from lawmakers and civil society. Supports 'multi-stakeholder' AI governance: risk assessments for high-risk systems, sector-specific regulation, but lobbied for 10-year ban on state-level AI regulation in U.S. House package. Publishes annual Responsible AI Progress Reports. Google DeepMind leadership (Hassabis, Manyika) stated democracies should lead AI development guided by freedom, equality, and human rights.

1226.notes: Vice President of Engineering and Research at Google, Head of Google Research. Leads global teams in foundational ML, quantum computing, AI for health, climate, education, and conversational AI. Frames AI as 'an amplifier of human ingenuity' that empowers scientists, healthcare workers, and educators. On AI and employment, argues AI will create more research jobs, not fewer — cites AlphaFold as freeing researchers to tackle bigger questions. Previously a professor at Tel Aviv University. Israeli-American computer scientist with expertise in streaming algorithms and data mining.

1227.notes: Google's People + AI Research (PAIR) team, a multidisciplinary research group exploring the human side of AI. Launched in 2017 within Google Research; now part of the Responsible AI and Human-Centered Technology team. Conducts research on explainability, interpretability, fairness, data visualization, and human-centered AI. Publishes the PAIR Guidebook (design guidelines for AI interfaces) and develops open-source tools like the Learning Interpretability Tool (LIT). Produces educational materials (Explorables) to help practitioners understand ML behaviors. Focuses on making AI systems more understandable and useful to people.

1228.notes: Adjunct Senior Fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations (CFR), focusing on advances in global technology and their consequences for U.S. economy and foreign policy. Directs a CFR roundtable series on technology, innovation, and American primacy. Former managing director at Silver Lake (2010-2018), the world's largest technology-focused investment firm. Previously Senior Adviser to the UN Secretary General's Strategic Planning Unit and co-directed CFR's Project on the Information Revolution. Author and foreign policy scholar with PhD from Columbia University in political science and international relations. Phillips Academy Andover graduate.

1229.notes: President and CEO of the World Economic Forum since 2020. Former Norwegian Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013-2017) and Minister of Trade and Industry (2004-2005). Under his leadership, WEF launched the AI Governance Alliance and Global Regulatory Innovation Platform (GRIP, July 2025) to co-develop frameworks for agile, inclusive AI governance. Emphasizes that AI challenges 'cannot be addressed in silos' and require 'co-creation' over competition and 'collaboration' over fragmentation. Optimistic about AI's economic productivity potential but acknowledges overvaluation risks. Advocates multi-stakeholder approach bridging business, government, academia, and civil society.

1230.notes: Head of AI Applications and Impact at the World Economic Forum's Centre for AI Excellence (CAIE), appointed January 2025. Over six years at WEF; previously AI and Digital Technologies Portfolio Manager at the Centre for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Background in mechanical and energy engineering (Politecnico di Torino, University of Illinois). Former research associate at UC Berkeley and business analyst at McKinsey. Leads WEF's work on real-world AI adoption, focusing on how organizations embed AI into everyday economic systems and maximize transformative potential.

1231.notes: Head of the Global Industries Team and Member of the Executive Committee at the World Economic Forum. Previously led WEF's Centre for Shaping the Future of Advanced Manufacturing and Supply Chains. Brings together 20+ industry communities to help companies and sectors anticipate trends and accelerate responsible industrial transformation. Advocates for AI deployment at scale in manufacturing through the Network of Centres for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Joined WEF in May 2015 after management consulting at PricewaterhouseCoopers in Geneva working with international organizations.

1232.notes: Head of the Centre for AI Excellence and Member of the Executive Committee at the World Economic Forum. Oversees the AI Governance Alliance (AIGA), a flagship initiative convening leaders from business, government, academia, and civil society to prepare for the 'intelligent era.' Argues AI leadership depends not on infrastructure volume but deployment effectiveness. Warns most organizations 'fundamentally misunderstand AI's transformative potential' and underestimate the mindset shift required. Champions responsible, transparent, and inclusive AI system design. Focuses on building frameworks, incentives, and practices for responsible AI development at scale.

1233.notes: Peter Lee is President of Microsoft Science (formerly President of Microsoft Research), overseeing AI research strategy and scientific discovery initiatives. Under his leadership, Microsoft Research has focused on responsible AI, including tools for detecting hallucinations and improving LLM fact-checking. Lee champions AI as a collaborative partner in scientific discovery, envisioning AI lab assistants that suggest and run experiments. He has emphasized that the true value of AI "hasn't been invented" yet and advocates for deployment infrastructure to deliver AI globally. Spoke at EmTech AI 2025 on AI revolutionizing science.

1234.notes: NYU Grossman School of Medicine at NYU Langone Health, a leading academic medical center. Notable for the fastMRI project—a collaboration with Facebook AI Research (FAIR) using deep learning to accelerate MRI reconstruction 10x. Also developed NYUTron, an LLM trained on clinical notes for in-hospital risk prediction.

1235.notes: Ashok Elluswamy is VP of AI at Tesla, leading both the Autopilot/FSD (Full Self-Driving) and Optimus humanoid robot programs. He champions Tesla's end-to-end neural network approach to autonomous driving, which trains a single network for perception, planning, and control — contrasting with the modular, sensor-heavy systems used by competitors. Told internal teams that 2026 would be "the most demanding year of their careers." Predicts AGI could become reality by ~2035 based on deep learning advances. The same neural network architecture powering FSD is being extended to Optimus.

1239.notes: In-Q-Tel (IQT) is an independent nonprofit venture capital firm that invests in technology companies to equip the CIA and other intelligence agencies with cutting-edge capabilities. Founded in 1999, based in Tysons, Virginia. Reached its 800th investment milestone in April 2025. Total estimated investment exceeds $1.8B over 25+ years. Key AI/national-security portfolio companies include Palantir (early investor). IQT appears in 32 companies on the 2025 NatSec 100 Report — more than any other fund. Invests across AI, biotech, microchips, space systems, cybersecurity, and autonomous systems. Also works with DHS on AI, data analytics, and trusted infrastructure.

1240.notes: Cognizant is a major IT services and consulting company (NYSE: CTSH) with a significant AI practice. Positions itself as an "AI builder" creating full-stack AI solutions for enterprise clients. In March 2026, launched Cognizant AI Factory — an enterprise-grade multi-tenant platform powered by Dell and NVIDIA, claiming 50-60% lower TCO and 30% faster AI processing. Acquired 3Cloud (leading Microsoft Azure services provider) in November 2025 to strengthen AI enablement on Azure. Published "New Work, New World 2026" report on AI reshaping work. Recognized as a leader in Everest Group's AI and Generative AI Services PEAK Matrix 2025.

1241.notes: The U.S. Army is actively integrating AI across operations as part of DoD's broader AI-first strategy. In December 2025, established a new 49B AI/ML Officer career field — the first dedicated AI career path for Army officers, with initial selections beginning January 2026. Defense Secretary Hegseth directed AI-driven command and control at theater/corps/division HQs by 2027, unmanned systems across every division by end 2026, and counter-UAS at platoon level by 2026. The Army is deploying AI-enabled "edge" tools for units operating on imperfect networks. Army intelligence is weaving AI into every layer of operations for faster, smarter decision-making.

1242.notes: Workera is an AI-powered skills intelligence platform that verifies skills for any role, enabling enterprise workforce development and talent decisions. Founded by Kian Katanforoosh, who was mentored by Andrew Ng at Stanford. Andrew Ng provides strategic guidance on responsible AI in education and workforce development. Raised $44.5M over 4 rounds. Clients include U.S. Air Force, Marine Corps, Booz Allen Hamilton, Siemens Energy, BCG, Accenture, and Eli Lilly. Launched AI agent "Sage" in late 2024 (GA March 2025). Published 2026 AI Workforce Preview showing 76% of Americans plan to learn new AI skills in 2026. Connected to Andrew Ng's DeepLearning.AI ecosystem.

1243.notes: AI Fund is Andrew Ng's venture studio, launched in 2018, that co-founds AI startups from scratch. Closed an oversubscribed $190M Fund II in 2025 (following $175M Fund I). Has co-founded ~35 portfolio companies including LandingAI and Workera. Corporate LPs include AES Corporation, HP Inc., Mitsui, Mitsubishi, QBE, and TELUS; VC investors include Sequoia Capital and NEA. AI Fund actively participates in strategy, coding, and customer discovery alongside CEOs. Focus areas: application and software infrastructure layers of the AI stack, leveraging foundation models across financial services, renewable energy, healthcare, education, logistics, and developer tools.

1244.notes: LandingAI is an AI company founded by Andrew Ng focused on computer vision for industrial applications. Flagship product LandingLens is an end-to-end, low-code platform for labeling, training, and deploying custom vision models. Primary industries: manufacturing, automotive, electronics, medical devices — companies relying on visual inspection and quality control. In 2025 released VisionAgent, a generative visual AI application builder with agentic object detection that eliminates manual data labeling and model training. Ng serves as Executive Chairman. Ng is a prominent advocate of "data-centric AI" and "small data" approaches. Part of Andrew Ng's ecosystem alongside AI Fund, DeepLearning.AI, and Workera.

1245.notes: Stanford CS professor (adjunct), MacArthur Fellow, co-founded Coursera, founded and CEO of insitro (AI-driven drug discovery). Member of National Academy of Sciences. Pioneer in probabilistic graphical models and machine learning applied to biology. Supports targeted regulation of AI in healthcare/drug discovery, recognizing necessity of safety standards while advocating for AI to accelerate scientific discovery.

1246.notes: Abu Dhabi state-owned AI investment vehicle launched March 2024 by Mubadala and G42, targeting $100B+ in AUM. Key partnerships: Stargate Project (with OpenAI, SoftBank, Oracle, Jan 2025); Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (with BlackRock, Microsoft, Sep 2024). Acquired Aligned Data Centers for $40B (Oct 2025). Exploring $50B third-party capital raise. Also invested in Anthropic Series G (Feb 2026) and made $2B minority investment in Binance (Mar 2025).

1247.notes: CEO and Chairman of BlackRock, world's largest asset manager (~$11.5T AUM). Strong AI investment advocate. 2026 chairman's letter warned AI will widen wealth gap unless investing access expands. Leads BlackRock's Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (with MGX, Microsoft). Views AI infrastructure buildout as $7T+ opportunity. Identifies power/energy as key bottleneck, not chips. Predicts some AI companies will go bankrupt but sees that as healthy capitalism.

1248.notes: COO of BlackRock. Oversees Aladdin platform, technology, and operations. Called AI "the most significant technology, innovation, evolution, revolution of my 30-year career." Launched Asimov, BlackRock's AI research platform (Jun 2025) that scans filings, research notes, and emails to generate portfolio insights. ~25% of BlackRock employees are technologists under his purview. Advocates aggressive AI adoption across investment operations.

1249.notes: Global alternative asset manager (part of Macquarie Group). Major AI infrastructure investor: committed $17B+ to data center and HPC investments including Applied Digital ($900M+ with $4.1B pipeline) and Aligned Data Centers (sold at $40B enterprise value to BlackRock/MGX consortium, Oct 2025). Closed Macquarie Infrastructure Partners VI with $8B+ in commitments (Jun 2025). Views AI as key driver of infrastructure demand in 2026 outlook.

1250.notes: Major semiconductor company competing with NVIDIA in AI accelerators. CEO Lisa Su targets 35% annual revenue growth driven by AI chip demand. Product roadmap: MI308, MI450 (Q3 2026), MI500 (2027). Key partnerships: OpenAI (multi-year, multi-billion chip deal), Meta (6GW deal). Took $800M charge from U.S. export controls on MI308 to China (Apr 2025). Su advocates balanced export policy — supports national security restrictions but warns overly strict controls hurt U.S. competitiveness.

1252.notes: Major public research university in Pittsburgh. Close collaboration with Carnegie Mellon University on AI initiatives. Joint NVIDIA AI Tech Community with CMU: Pitt's center focuses on AI in health sciences, clinical medicine, and biomanufacturing. Co-hosted CMU-Pitt health/AI/technology summit (Oct 2025). Joined multi-institutional Sloan Foundation study on AI impact on labor markets (Jul 2025). Research strengths in health AI and computational medicine.

1253.notes: DECEASED (1927-1992). AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University and RAND Corporation. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956, first AI program) and General Problem Solver (1957) with Herbert Simon and Cliff Shaw. Developed Information Processing Language. Proposed physical symbol systems hypothesis. Created SOAR cognitive architecture. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Simon). Instrumental in founding CMU's Computer Science Department (1965) and School of Computer Science. The ACM-AAAI Allen Newell Award is named in his honor.

1254.notes: DECEASED (1916-2001). Nobel laureate (Economics, 1978) and AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver with Allen Newell and Cliff Shaw. Founded concept of bounded rationality. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Newell). At CMU from 1949 to 2001, instrumental in founding the School of Computer Science, GSIA, and multiple departments. CMU's Simon Initiative and Herbert A. Simon Award named in his honor. Bridged AI, economics, psychology, and organizational theory.

1255.notes: Interim President of Georgetown University (since Nov 2024), previously Executive VP and Provost (2012-2024). Social statistician specializing in survey methodology. Former director of U.S. Census Bureau (2009-2012). Elected member of National Academy of Sciences and National Academy of Medicine. Fellow of American Statistical Association. Research focuses on data collection quality, adaptive research designs, and privacy attitudes toward statistical agencies. Testified before Congress (Jul 2025).

1256.notes: Provost of Yale University (since 2020) and Henry Ford II Professor of Molecular Biophysics & Biochemistry. Assembled Yale Task Force on Artificial Intelligence (YTAI, Jan 2024) to review AI activity university-wide. Announced $150M five-year AI investment (Aug 2024) covering GPU infrastructure, faculty hiring, and ethics research. Priorities center on expanding AI computing access and establishing Yale's position in AI ethics. Oversees Yale Center for Research Computing expansion.

1257.notes: Yale University's central HPC and research computing facility (YCRC). Part of $150M AI investment initiative. Transitioning from Yale West Campus Data Center to MGHPCC (consortium with Harvard, MIT, BU, Northeastern, UMass). New Bouchet cluster in production (~4,000 cores), expanding with 80 NVIDIA H200 GPUs (140GB VRAM each), 48 A5000 ADA cards, and 6,000 additional CPUs. Grace and McCleary clusters decommissioning in 2026. Launched Hopper secure HPC cluster (Jul 2025). All new purchases including Provost's AI Initiative GPUs installed at MGHPCC.

1258.notes: UN Secretary-General (2017-present). Proposed international AI governance body modeled on the IAEA in 2023. Convened the High-Level Advisory Body on AI which delivered 7 recommendations in 2024. Championed establishment of the UN Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and Global Dialogue on AI Governance (both approved by GA in 2025). Consistently advocates for science-based AI policy and multilateral governance, though the U.S. has rejected UN-centralized AI oversight.

1259.notes: UN specialized agency for information and communication technologies. Hosts annual AI for Good Global Summit. Co-organized 2025 International AI Standards Summit with ISO and IEC, issuing the Seoul Statement on AI standards. Published 170-page report on global AI standardization landscape (2025). Develops technical standards for AI interoperability, safety, and trustworthiness. Leads initiatives on agentic AI protocols and AI in health/agriculture.

1260.notes: UN agency that adopted the first global standard on AI ethics -- the Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021), applicable to all 194 member states. Developed after 3 years of consultation with experts from 155 countries. Framework centers on human rights, transparency, fairness, and human oversight. Published 2025 AI ethics toolkit with open-source NLP kits for emerging markets. Released guidelines for AI use in courts and tribunals (Dec 2025).

1261.notes: UN Under-Secretary-General and Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies (Jan 2025-present); previously Secretary-General's Envoy on Technology (2022-2024). Indian diplomat who is a central figure in UN AI governance architecture. Instrumental in establishing the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and Global Dialogue on AI Governance. Emphasizes evidence-based, science-informed AI policy and inclusive governance that gives all 193 member states a seat at the table. Advocates strongly for Global South participation in AI governance.

1262.notes: Advisory body established under the EU AI Act, composed of one representative from each EU Member State. Advises and assists the European Commission and Member States to facilitate consistent and effective application of the AI Act. The AI Act's phased implementation began Feb 2025 (prohibited practices), continued Aug 2025 (GPAI model obligations), with full applicability by Aug 2026. The Board operates alongside the European AI Office which supervises and enforces the Act.

1263.notes: Former DARPA deputy director of the Information Innovation Office (I2O). Joined DARPA in 2018 as program manager; led the Explainable AI (XAI), Media Forensics (MediFor), Semantic Forensics (SemaFor), and Machine Common Sense (MCS) programs. Served as acting deputy director of I2O (June-Oct 2021), then deputy office director (May 2022-Oct 2025). DARPA tour ended Oct 2025; transitioned to Chief Scientist at STR. PhD in Computer Science from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.

1264.notes: DARPA program manager in the Defense Sciences Office (joined Sept 2023). Leads the CLARA (Causal Logical AI Reasoning and Auditing) program. Pioneer in knowledge representation and reasoning (KRR), including Rulelog, courteous defeasibility, and neuro-symbolic AI. Extensive background applying AI to legal, regulatory, policy compliance, and defense/intelligence domains. Previously Chief Scientist at Coherent Knowledge. PhD from Stanford, Hertz Fellow.

1265.notes: Joint DARPA/ARPA-H program manager. Leads the AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) at DARPA -- a two-year, $8.5M competition to develop AI systems that can find and fix vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure software. Also leads health cybersecurity programs at ARPA-H. Previously principal researcher at HSBC Cybersecurity Science and Analytics. Background in competitive hacking (CTF). MS in Computer Science from Johns Hopkins University.

1266.notes: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, established 2022 under the Biden administration within HHS. Modeled on DARPA. Funds high-risk, high-reward health research including multiple AI programs: ADVOCATE (agentic AI for cardiovascular care), PRECISE-AI (monitoring clinical AI model degradation), TARGET (generative AI for antibiotic discovery), MOCS (AI for obstetric care), and Biomedical Data Fabric toolbox. Co-sponsors the AIxCC with DARPA for health cybersecurity.

1267.notes: Largest university-affiliated research center in the U.S., serving as a technical resource for DoD, Intelligence Community, DHS, and NASA. Over 60 years of AI R&D spanning autonomous systems, decision support, ML, and human-machine teaming. Intelligent Systems Center (ISC) has 130+ research scientists/engineers focused on AI, robotics, and neuroscience. Developed GenWar Lab (AI wargaming) and SAGE (Strategic AI Gaming Engine) now being built for classified DoD/IC networks. Focus on robust, trustworthy AI for national security.

1268.notes: Co-founder and Chief of the Intelligent Systems Center (ISC) at Johns Hopkins APL, leading 130 research scientists and engineers in AI, robotics, neuroscience, and human-machine teaming. Named Chair of the Artificial Intelligence program at Johns Hopkins Engineering for Professionals (EP), one of the nation's first online MS programs in AI. Faculty since 2010. Doctor of Engineering (Computer Science) from Johns Hopkins; BS/MS in CS and Engineering from Penn State.

1269.notes: Director of the AI portfolio for Lifelong Learning (executive and continuing education) at Johns Hopkins University, with joint faculty positions in Computer Science and Public Health. Previously Accenture's Chief Data Scientist, building a 1,200-person Federal AI practice for U.S. Government. Retired U.S. Army Lt. Colonel who founded the West Point Network Science Center and served as Chief Strategist for Information Warfare at CENTCOM. Research combines AI, neuroscience, and human behavior.

1271.notes: J.B. Speed School of Engineering at the University of Louisville. R1 research university engineering school with AI as a core research area. Houses Data Science & AI research cluster, Center for Human Systems Engineering (human-centric AI), and Autonomous Intelligent Mobile Systems Lab. Offers AI/ML coursework across bioengineering, computer science, and industrial engineering.

1273.notes: Dean of Kogod School of Business at American University (since Aug 2022). Led what Poets & Quants called 'the most consequential AI transformation in business education,' making Kogod one of the first AI-first business schools. Launched Applied AI Institute with Google.org support. Views: companies should help educate policymakers on AI opportunities and risks, but Congress and the Executive Branch must ultimately set policy. Advocates innovation while acknowledging need for safety/fairness guardrails.

1274.notes: Professor and Chair, Dept of Information Technology and Analytics at Kogod School of Business, American University. Director of the Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence (IAAI). Research focuses on AI governance, digital transformation, information privacy, and smart government. Advised South Korea's Presidential Committee on the Fourth Industrial Revolution on AI policy. Consulted for World Bank and Samsung on digital strategy. University Outstanding Scholarship Award 2025.

1275.notes: Professorial Lecturer in IT&A at Kogod School of Business, American University (since 2023). Associate Director of the Institute of Applied Artificial Intelligence. Created faculty AI training program that helped embed AI across 58 new courses. Prior industry experience building AI/ML solutions for pre-IPO SaaS companies. Key implementer of Kogod's AI-first curriculum transformation.

1276.notes: Provost Associate Professor, Dept of Computer Science at American University. Heads the Computational Material Perception Laboratory. Research spans human/machine vision, generative AI for graphics, multimodal perception in VR/AR, and NLP for clinical trials. PhD Neuroscience (UPenn), postdoc at MIT Brain & Cognitive Sciences/CSAIL. Funded by NIH and NSF.

1277.notes: Vice President for Research & Economic Development at SUNY University at Albany. Launched $200M AI initiative ($75M from NY State) and $20M Center for Emerging AI Systems with IBM. Co-chairs SUNY-wide AI research and infrastructure work group. Leads Empire AI SUNY partnerships for public-good AI. Previously founding director of Health Care Engineering Systems Center at UIUC. Inventor of RoSS robotic surgical simulator; founder/CTO of AirV Labs (generative AI + VR startup).

1278.notes: Joint research center between USC Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social Work and USC Viterbi School of Engineering. Mission: develop and demonstrate how AI can tackle difficult societal problems. Focuses on low-resource communities. Research areas: environment, health/wellbeing, homelessness. Partners with Partnership on AI on explainable AI research. Draws from Grand Challenges for Social Work, NAE Grand Challenges, and UN SDGs.

1279.notes: USC's school of social work. Co-founded the Center for AI in Society (CAIS) with Viterbi School of Engineering, applying AI to social challenges including child welfare, homelessness, behavioral health, and human trafficking. Research emphasizes responsible AI for vulnerable populations.

1280.notes: USC's engineering school. Major AI research hub with multiple centers: CAIS (AI for social good), Amazon Center on Secure and Trusted AI, Machine Learning Center, Center for Autonomy and AI, and Robotics and Autonomous Systems Center. Launched first US MS in CS-AI track. Partnered with USC School of Advanced Computing on BS in AI (Fall 2026). NSF-funded ORAI PhD program in AI and operations research.

1281.notes: USC's business school. Houses the Neely Center for Ethical Leadership and Decision Making, which in 2024 launched a five-year, $4M purpose-driven AI research initiative — $2M from the Knight Foundation matched by $2M from USC Marshall. Initiative focuses on AI and social media indices, design codes for AI tools, a youth advisory panel, and expanded leadership fellowships. Led by Neely Center Director Nate Fast and Managing Director Ravi Iyer. Dean Geoff Garrett chairs USC President's AI Strategy Committee.

1282.notes: Dean of USC Marshall School of Business. Chairs USC President's AI Strategy Committee. Champions rapid AI integration in higher education: 'if we don't treat things with enough urgency, we're just going to fall behind.' Working on school-wide AI policy for responsible use. Helped secure a five-year, $4M Knight Foundation + USC Marshall match ($2M each) for purpose-driven AI research housed at the Neely Center. Emphasizes aligning AI with human values.

1283.notes: USC's 23rd school, launched in 2024 as part of President Folt's $1B+ advanced computing initiative. Led by Prof. Gaurav Sukhatme. Recruiting 30 faculty by 2025, 60 more by 2030. Co-launching BS in AI with Viterbi (Fall 2026 cohort). New Ginsburg Human-Centered Computation Hall. Ethics at its core mission. First new USC school in over a decade.

1284.notes: President of USC (2019-2025), first woman to serve as permanent president. Established Frontiers of Computing initiative and launched USC School of Advanced Computing (23rd school). Made AI a major institutional priority, embedding ethics, analytics, and AI across scholarly work. Environmental scientist by training (UNC Chapel Hill chancellor before USC). Now President Emeritus and VP for Presidential Initiatives; teaches science and society courses at USC.

1285.notes: Ivy League research university with major AI research infrastructure. The ASSET Center (Penn Engineering) focuses on AI safety, explainability, and trustworthiness, with NSF funding. The Warren Center for Network and Data Sciences drives interdisciplinary data science research. Penn AI was launched in 2025 to coordinate responsible AI across the university.

1286.notes: Dean of the Wharton School (since July 2020), first woman and first person of color in Wharton's 139-year history. Organizational psychologist specializing in crisis leadership and workplace diversity. Launched Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, investing heavily in AI research and teaching. Introduced new undergraduate concentration and MBA major in AI for business. Emphasizes responsible AI: 'simultaneously focused on harnessing AI transformative potential while also understanding and addressing its risks.'

1287.notes: John C. Hower Professor of Technology and Digital Business at Wharton. Author of 'A Human's Guide to Machine Intelligence' (2019), advocating for an 'algorithmic bill of rights' covering transparency, explainability, and user control. Favors issue-specific AI regulation (e.g., deepfakes, financial fraud) over broad-based approaches; proposes regulatory sandboxes for innovation. Research focuses on the digital economy and impact of algorithms on consumers and society.

1288.notes: First Pacific Company Professor, Professor of Legal Studies & Business Ethics, and department chair at Wharton. Directs the Wharton Accountable AI Lab and the Blockchain and Digital Asset Project. Hosts 'The Road to Accountable AI' podcast. Served on Obama Presidential Transition Team; was Counsel for New Technology Policy at FCC under Clinton. Published four books including 'The Blockchain and the New Architecture of Trust.' Focuses on legal, ethical, and regulatory aspects of emerging technologies.

1289.notes: Vice Dean of AI & Analytics at Wharton, K.P. Chao Professor of Marketing, and Chairperson of the Wharton Marketing Department. Also Professor of Economics, Education, and Statistics and Data Science. Leads the Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, spearheading student learning, faculty research, and industry partnerships in AI. Applied statistician (PhD, Harvard) with research in Bayesian modeling and statistical computing. Multiple teaching awards including Linback Award for Distinguished PhD Teaching.

1290.notes: Associate Professor of Operations, Information and Decisions at Wharton; Faculty Co-Director of Wharton Human-AI Research. Research focuses on economics of technology and labor: AI for HR applications, labor markets for AI skills, and how workers specialize in technologies. Key finding: firms capture more value from AI when algorithmic expertise is distributed across domain experts rather than concentrated in IT (Management Science). Co-advises Wharton's new AI major. PhD in Managerial Science from Wharton; SB and MEng from MIT.

1291.notes: Professor of Statistics and Data Science at Wharton. Research interests include machine learning, functional data analysis, differential equations, computational statistics, and statistical ecology. Co-advises Wharton's new undergraduate and MBA AI programs (jointly administered by Statistics/Data Science and Operations/Information/Decisions departments). Developed 'Foundations of Deep Learning' course. Published work on additive explanations for black-box ML models (2023).

1292.notes: AI research center at Tulane University, established by the Harold L. and Heather E. Jurist NC '64 Endowed Fund. Supports research and education in AI, machine learning, and data science with a focus on applications for a healthier, more connected global community. Faculty projects span machine learning, multi-agent systems, computer vision, NLP, visualization, and ethics. Part of Tulane's broader AI ecosystem alongside the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science and CEAI.

1293.notes: Multi-disciplinary AI center at Tulane University and one of five Tulane Centers of Excellence. Team of scientists, engineers, students, and community partners dedicated to human-centered AI. Core principle: trust must be earned by designing AI systems that are demonstrably fair, transparent, and accountable, with meaningful community engagement from design through deployment. Cross-disciplinary research, project-based learning, and community outreach across healthcare, economics, education, and criminal justice. Partners with Court Watch NOLA on criminal justice transparency via $1.5M NSF-funded project.

1294.notes: Founded 2007, oldest court-watching program in the U.S. Recruits, trains, and supports volunteers to observe Orleans Parish Criminal Court judges, prosecutors, and public defenders for transparency, ethics, and constitutional compliance. Partnered with Tulane University on $1.5M NSF-funded project using AI to build a data analysis platform for criminal justice transparency -- AI extracts info from court documents, identifies trends and inequities, and directs court watchers to high-priority cases. Dashboard tracking bail settings launched Dec 2024; full AI platform expected early 2026.

1295.notes: Executive Office of the President body that sets federal AI policy for agencies. Biden-era M-24-10 was rescinded and replaced by M-25-21 (April 2025), which mandates Chief AI Officers, AI governance boards, and risk management for high-impact AI while emphasizing innovation over restriction.

1296.notes: Major NASA research center in Silicon Valley. Intelligent Systems Division comprises Autonomous Systems & Robotics, Collaborative & Assistant Systems, Discovery & Systems Health, and Robust Software Engineering. Conducts mission-driven R&D in computational sciences for NASA applications. Aviation Systems Division researches advanced air traffic management and automation. Key AI projects: Data and Reasoning Fabric (aerospace decision-making), RAM-AO Working Group (autonomous multi-aircraft operations). Peter Norvig served as Division Chief of Computational Sciences. NASA restricts use of generative AI on sensitive data.

1297.notes: Pioneering AI researcher and entrepreneur. Stanford CS professor (2003-2011), directed Stanford AI Lab (SAIL). Founded Google X, Google's self-driving car project (now Waymo), Udacity (online education), and Kitty Hawk (air taxis, now closed). Won DARPA Grand Challenge (2005). Strong AI optimist: 'AI will not replace us, it will complement us' and warns against 'prematurely declaring AI a danger and destroying chances of this technology by rash regulation.' Favors liability-based regulation over prescriptive rules. Believes AI makes workers more productive. Currently CEO of Sage AI Labs.

1298.notes: AI researcher and entrepreneur specializing in probabilistic programming and Bayesian inference. Co-founder of Bayesian Logic Inc. (2012), which builds Bayesian models for complex physical domains, particularly seismology. Invented the Bean Machine Probabilistic Programming Language and the NET-VISA probabilistic model for detecting nuclear explosions (used by CTBTO). PhD from UC Berkeley (2006-2012) under Stuart Russell and Erik Sudderth. Received the Mitchell Prize in 2014 from the American Statistical Association. Previously Research Scientist at Facebook (designed a new PPL), Head of AI and Analytics at DevRev Inc. (2020-2023). Currently Founder at JazzX AI.

1299.notes: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization (CTBTO), headquartered in Vienna, is the international body overseeing the global ban on nuclear test explosions. Operates the International Monitoring System (IMS) — 337 facilities worldwide using seismic, infrasound, hydroacoustic, and radionuclide sensors. Actively incorporates AI/ML to distinguish earthquakes from nuclear explosive tests and to process vast sensor datasets. Connected to Bayesian Logic Inc. through use of the NET-VISA probabilistic model for nuclear explosion detection. Approximately 300 staff from over 90 countries.

1300.notes: Home of CodeX (Stanford Center for Legal Informatics) and the Stanford Law AI Initiative co-directed by Prof. Nathaniel Persily. Hosts AI governance courses, the Law Policy Lab practicum on federal AI use in criminal justice, and applied research projects such as ComplianceTwin (2025–2026).

1301.notes: Republican Senator (NC) and Chair of the Senate Subcommittee on Science, Manufacturing, and Competitiveness. Actively shapes AI legislation through bipartisan bills on government data access, AI lab networks, and public health preparedness. Strongly opposes overregulation, favoring a light-touch, innovation-first federal framework.

1302.notes: Democratic Senator from Illinois (1997–2027) and former Senate Judiciary Committee chair. Co-introduced the bipartisan AI LEAD Act to enable products-liability suits against AI developers, and the TEST AI Act to strengthen federal AI testing capacity. Consistently advocated for mandatory AI oversight.

1303.notes: U.S. Department of Energy national laboratory leading the adaptation of AI for the nuclear energy industry. Developing AI tools to reduce costs and timeframes of designing, licensing, building, and operating nuclear facilities. Major 2025 partnerships: AWS (AI tools for nuclear projects, digital twin of small modular reactor), Microsoft (Azure AI for nuclear permitting/licensing), NVIDIA (AI for reactor design and operations). DOE selected INL as a site for AI data centers under Trump administration initiative for American AI leadership. Part of DOE Project Genesis uniting all 17 national labs to build an AI-powered scientific platform. Testing AI to streamline nuclear permitting processes.

1304.notes: U.S. Attorney General, confirmed February 4, 2025. Previously served as Florida Attorney General (2011-2019). Key AI policy action: created the DOJ AI Litigation Task Force under Trump executive order to challenge state AI laws deemed inconsistent with federal policy or the goal of U.S. "global AI dominance." The task force targets state laws on grounds they are illegal, unconstitutionally regulate interstate commerce, or are overridden by federal regulations. Supports a "minimally burdensome national policy framework for AI" to promote American competitiveness.

1305.notes: Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives (Republican, Louisiana). Leading congressional effort to implement the Trump administration national AI framework. Outlined a 3-point plan for AI: (1) treat AI as a matter of national security, (2) keep AI in U.S. and allied hands, (3) move "at the speed that victory demands." Stated "America will win the AI race if government resists the siren song of control, and if industry steps up as our patriotic partner." Praised the White House AI roadmap for providing "innovators with much-needed certainty, while protecting consumers and prioritizing kids online safety." Supports federal preemption of state AI regulations.

1306.notes: House Majority Leader (Republican, Louisiana). Key Republican leader on AI policy in Congress. Co-authored joint statement with Speaker Johnson committing to implement the Trump administration national AI framework. Led effort to include preemption of state AI regulations in the NDAA (ultimately dropped). Supports Trump executive order on AI and stated willingness to "codify some of the things the President is trying to do" on AI. Advocates for a unified national AI framework that provides certainty for innovators while protecting consumers.

1309.notes: NIST develops voluntary AI standards and risk management frameworks for federal agencies and industry. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) is widely adopted. In 2025-26 its AI Safety Institute was renamed the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) under the Trump administration's AI Action Plan, with a mandate to revise the AI RMF.

1310.notes: President and CEO of the Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP), founded by Eric Schmidt in 2021 to strengthen U.S. competitiveness in AI and emerging technologies. Previously served as Executive Director of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI, 2019-2021), chaired by Eric Schmidt, where he assembled 130+ staff to produce the landmark NSCAI final report. Career national security official: Chief of Staff to National Security Advisor H.R. McMaster, leadership roles under Deputy SecDef Robert Work, Special Assistant to CJCS Gen. Dempsey. Born in Kosovo, emigrated to U.S. after the war. Harvard MPP, GWU BA in International Relations. Recipient of DoD Distinguished Civilian Service Medal. SCSP advocates for bold, innovation-driven AI policy with targeted oversight of high-consequence applications and sector-specific regulation.

1311.notes: Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley. Founding Co-Director of Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), and BAIR Commons. Faculty Director of PATH at UC Berkeley. Previously on MIT EECS faculty (1999-2008) directing the Vision Interface Group. Leading expert in computer vision, deep learning, and explainable AI. His group developed the Caffe deep-learning library. Author/co-author of papers cited nearly 300,000 times. Research spans computer vision, NLP, autonomous vehicles, media search, and multimodal robot interaction.

1312.notes: Enterprise AI platform founded in 2013 by Matt Zeiler, headquartered in Washington, D.C. One of the first deep learning platforms, specializing in computer vision, NLP, and audio analysis. Core technology based on convolutional neural networks. Key government/defense work: participated in Project Maven (DoD AI program), signed R&D agreement with U.S. Army for EO/IR data labeling, created subsidiary Neural Net One for dedicated government contracting. Raised $100M total ($60M Series C in 2021, led by New Enterprise Associates). Named a Leader in Forrester Wave Computer Vision Tools Q1 2024. Platform supports full AI lifecycle: data preparation, model training, deployment.

1313.notes: UC Berkeley college established July 1, 2023 — the first new college at Berkeley in over 50 years. Houses three academic majors: Computer Science, Data Science, and Statistics.
