Includes the Berkeley Institute for Data Science, Center for Computational Biology, Bakar Institute of Digital Materials for the Planet, and shares EECS with the College of Engineering and the D-Lab with Social Sciences. Parent organization of BAIR-affiliated units. Mission: develop ethics-oriented, accessible curricula in data science, computing, and statistics. The Gateway collaboration hub expected to open spring 2026 as CDSS home.

1314.notes: University of Utah hosts the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, a $100M transdisciplinary program led by the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. A $50M AI supercomputer partnership with NVIDIA and HPE is slated to come online in summer 2026. Research focuses on healthcare, societal wellness, and public services.

1315.notes: Private research university in Atlanta, Georgia. Major AI initiative: AI.Humanity, which brings together full university resources to shape the AI revolution for better human health, economic value, and responsible applications. Key AI centers: Center for Artificial Intelligence Learning (AI literacy across campus), Empathetic AI for Health Institute (AI.Health — ML and big data for healthcare transformation), and multiple departmental AI labs. Research spans NLP, computer vision, geospatial AI, health informatics, and AI ethics. Interdisciplinary approach connects AI research with humanities, social sciences, and public health.

1316.notes: Research collective within Emory University encompassing multiple AI-focused labs and faculty across the Department of Computer Science and School of Medicine. Includes the Emory NLP group, AI and Simulation Lab (geospatial AI, computer vision, foundation models), MAIX Lab (health AI), and Cardiothoracic Imaging and AI Lab. Faculty research spans natural language processing, large language models, conversational AI, information retrieval, AI interpretability, and medical imaging AI. Part of the broader AI.Humanity initiative at Emory.

1317.notes: The Block Center for Technology and Society at Carnegie Mellon University was established in 2019 to study how emerging technologies alter the future of work, how AI and analytics can be harnessed for social good, and how innovation can reduce inequality. Houses the Responsible AI Initiative (RAI), which translates AI research into actionable policy insights promoting fairness, accountability, and transparency. Runs community education programs like 'AI 101' for labor unions, media professionals, and government representatives. Collaborates with MIT FutureTech on labor implications of AI research. Engages directly with policymakers in Washington, D.C., sharing AI expertise. Research focuses on future of work, responsible AI deployment, and data-driven policy solutions.

1318.notes: Presidential Professor at the University of Utah's Kahlert School of Computing and Director of the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. Appointed as the university's inaugural Chief AI Officer. Led the launch of the One-U Responsible AI Initiative with $100 million investment in AI research across environment, healthcare, and education. Previously served at the National Science Foundation as Office Director of Advanced Cyberinfrastructure, co-chaired the National Science and Technology Council's Subcommittee on Future Advanced Computing Ecosystem and the National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) Task Force. Won the 2023 IEEE Sidney Fernbach Award and 2024 CRA Distinguished Service Award. Research spans parallel/distributed computing and computational science.

1319.notes: The Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute (Amii), one of Canada's three national AI institutes alongside Mila and Vector Institute, funded through the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy. Founded over 20 years ago, headquartered in Edmonton. Mandate to build technical/scientific capacity in machine learning and translate it into commercial applications across Alberta's economic sectors. Key partner in the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) alongside CIFAR, NRC, Mila, and Vector. Received $5M Google.org grant to establish a national AI literacy consortium across 25 post-secondary institutions reaching 125,000 students. Also received $9M federal grant to train ~5,000 mid-career energy sector workers in AI skills. Strong focus on AI trust and safety, workforce readiness, and responsible AI deployment.

1320.notes: Co-founder and former CEO of Element AI, a Montreal-based applied AI research lab co-founded with Yoshua Bengio and Nicolas Chapados. Element AI was acquired by ServiceNow in 2020 for ~$500M. Subsequently served as Head of AI Product Management and Strategy at ServiceNow. As of 2025-2026, working on a stealth AI startup based in Montreal. Recognized as one of Fast Company's Most Creative People in Business (2019). Active voice in Canada's AI ecosystem, advocating for AI commercialization and startup development. Element AI was a notable early attempt to bridge academic AI research and enterprise applications.

1321.notes: Public enterprise software company (NYSE: NOW) specializing in workflow automation. Heavily invested in AI via 'Now Assist' and autonomous AI agents; targeting $1B AI ACV by 2026. Advocates responsible AI governance and compliance with EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF.

1322.notes: Canada was the first country globally to adopt a national AI strategy, launching the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy in 2017. Phase 2 announced in 2022 with $443M+ in funding, supporting the three national AI institutes (Mila, Amii, Vector). Tabled the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) in June 2022 as part of Bill C-27, one of the first proposed AI-specific laws globally. AIDA focuses on responsible design, development, and use of AI in the private sector, with emphasis on health, safety, and human rights impacts. Requires businesses to implement governance mechanisms, assess AI risks, and provide transparency to users. Established the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI). Also investing in AI workforce development, compute infrastructure, and commercialization support across provinces.

1323.notes: DCVC (formerly Data Collective) is a deep tech venture capital firm founded by Matt Ocko and Zachary Bogue, investing in entrepreneurs using computational approaches—especially AI—to solve large-scale real-world problems. Manages billions in AUM across 13 funds including six flagship funds. Invests at seed, Series A, and growth stages across AI, exascale computing, climate, robotics, materials science, biology, defense, and security. Backed by a network of 50+ technology executives and experts (CTOs, chief scientists, professors at Stanford and Berkeley). Was a lead investor in Element AI. Focus on AI-enabled solutions for industries that haven't seen material progress in decades.

1324.notes: Canada's leading early-stage venture capital firm, established in 2007 and headquartered in Montreal. Manages $325M across five funds with an active portfolio of 100+ companies valued at ~$10B. Has invested in 214 companies total, with portfolio unicorns including Xanadu, Blockstream, and Tenstorrent. Deeply embedded in Montreal's AI and deep tech ecosystem. Was an early investor/backer of Element AI. Focuses on nurturing entrepreneurial communities and supporting founders from the earliest stages. Portfolio spans AI, healthcare, fintech, and other technology verticals. Sonder (portfolio company) went public on NASDAQ in 2021.

1325.notes: Major international human rights organization with strong positions on AI governance and digital rights. Calls for outright ban on facial recognition technology for mass surveillance by police and state agencies, citing racial discrimination (higher false positive rates for women and people of color) and chilling effects on peaceful assembly. Runs the 'Ban the Scan' campaign documenting FRT harms in New York, Israel/OPT, and Hyderabad. Advocates for legally binding AI regulation centered on documented harms, not voluntary industry commitments. Criticized the EU AI Act as insufficient to protect marginalized communities. Launched an Algorithmic Accountability Toolkit (2025) for investigators and rights defenders. Calls for meaningful involvement of impacted communities in AI regulatory decisions and warns against regulatory gaps that allow export of banned AI systems to other countries.

1326.notes: Singapore Management University (SMU) hosts the Centre for AI and Data Governance, established in 2019 at its School of Law. Received a S$4.5M grant from the National Research Foundation and Infocomm Media Development Authority for a five-year Research Programme on the Governance of AI and Data Use, structured around three streams: AI and Society, AI and Industry, and AI and Commercialisation. Publishes research on Singapore's evolving AI governance framework through SSRN. The Centre establishes expert panels drawing from academia, technology industry, and legal professions, with international institutional partnerships. Also offers AI-led business strategy programs. Contributing to Singapore's national approach to responsible AI and data governance.

1329.notes: Consumer technology company deploying AI via Apple Intelligence across its device ecosystem. Pursues an on-device, privacy-first AI strategy using Private Cloud Compute for sensitive tasks. Partnered with OpenAI (ChatGPT integration) and Google (Gemini for Siri). Avoids public regulatory advocacy but leverages privacy standards as a competitive differentiator.

1330.notes: Full Professor at Université de Montréal and founding core member of Mila. Co-authored the seminal Deep Learning textbook with Bengio and Goodfellow. Holds a CIFAR AI Chair. Incoming scientific director of IVADO as of 2025.

1331.notes: Established in 1985 as an inter-departmental, inter-faculty research center at McGill University spanning Electrical and Computer Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Computer Science. One of the longest-running AI research centers in the world. Comprises ~20 professors and 150 graduate students. Research organized around five themes: Computer Vision, Robotics, Systems and Control, Human-Computer Interaction, and Graphics. Half of CIM professors engaged in AI research including robotic vision and medical image analysis. Notable projects include the Aqua amphibious robot for environmental monitoring and the SmartWheeler assistive technology. Draws researchers from around the world to McGill.

1332.notes: The McGill Collaborative for AI and Society (McCAIS) is a strategic initiative of the Computational and Data Systems Institute (CDSI) at McGill University. Interdisciplinary, university-wide effort bringing all faculties together to understand AI's societal impact and develop evidence-based best practices for responsible AI use. Work organized around four pillars: Thought Leadership, Research to Action, Experiential Learning Opportunities, and Lifelong Learning. Partners with IVADO (Quebec AI research consortium) on courses in algorithmic fairness, privacy in AI, creativity in AI, and AI in health sciences. Runs the BMO Responsible AI Awards Program. Draws expertise from Philosophy, Engineering, Computer Science, and the Laidley Centre for Business Ethics and Equity. Co-directed by Prof. Samira A. Rahimi.

1333.notes: Umbrella term for McGill University's AI research labs, most prominently the Reasoning and Learning Lab co-directed by Prakash Panangaden, Doina Precup, Joelle Pineau, Jackie Chi Kit Cheung, Siva Reddy, Reihaneh Rabbany, Siamak Ravanbakhsh, and David Rolnick. McGill is a world leader in machine learning with particular strengths in reinforcement learning and natural language processing. Connected to Mila (Quebec AI Institute) through shared faculty. The iSMART Lab (Prof. Narges Armanfard) focuses on computer vision, time series analysis, and physics-informed modeling. Also participates in the International Laboratory on Learning Systems (ILLS) with ETS, CNRS, Universite Paris-Saclay, and CentraleSupelec. Closely linked to CIM (Centre for Intelligent Machines) and McCAIS within McGill's AI ecosystem.

1334.notes: Executive Director of the Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) at UC Berkeley since 2016, and Interim Executive Director of IASEAI. Also head of strategic outreach for Berkeley AI Research (BAIR). Former CEO of The Blindsight Corporation (acquired by Amazon A9).

1335.notes: Chief Responsible AI Officer at Cognizant. Former GM of Innovation at XPRIZE, led IBM Watson AI XPRIZE. Co-founded AI Commons collective. Co-created AI for Good Global Summit with ITU/UN. Elected to GPAI Multistakeholder Expert Group, co-chairs Responsible AI working group. 25+ years in emerging tech, prior roles at European Space Agency, Airbus, European Commission. Managed two venture capital funds.

1336.notes: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (38 member countries). Published OECD AI Principles in 2019 (first intergovernmental AI standard), updated 2024 — now endorsed by 47 jurisdictions. Operates AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai) tracking AI strategies, policies, and incidents globally. Integrated GPAI partnership under OECD umbrella (44 countries). Partners with UN Office of Tech Envoy. Published common AI incident reporting framework (Feb 2025). Member-funded intergovernmental org.

1337.notes: Communications Director at National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) since Jan 2025. Championed NRSC use of AI in political campaigns, defended AI-generated campaign ads including deepfake-style ad targeting Chuck Schumer (Oct 2025). Stated "AI is here and not going anywhere. Adapt & win or pearl clutch & lose." Previously worked in office of Sen. Pete Ricketts.

1338.notes: Saudi Arabia sovereign wealth fund (~$930B AUM). Launched Humain (May 2025) as full-stack AI company — data centers, cloud, models (ALLAM Arabic LLM), applications. $40B AI investment fund. Strategic partnership with NVIDIA (500MW GPU capacity over 5 years). $10B joint investment with Google Cloud for AI hub. Aramco acquired minority stake in Humain. Central to Vision 2030 economic diversification. Chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.

1339.notes: Crown Prince and Prime Minister of Saudi Arabia. Architect of Vision 2030, which places AI as central pillar of economic diversification. Chairs Humain (PIF-backed AI company). Established Saudi Data & AI Authority (SDAIA) in 2019. National Strategy for Data & AI targets 20K AI specialists, 300+ AI startups, $20B investment by 2030. Designated 2026 as Year of Artificial Intelligence. Oversaw $40B+ in AI infrastructure commitments.

1340.notes: Saudi Arabian infrastructure financing entity operating under the National Development Fund. Signed $1.2B financing framework with Humain (announced at Davos, Jan 2026) to develop 250MW of AI data center capacity. Exploring creation of AI data center investment platform to attract global/local institutional investors for Humain AI strategy. Supports Vision 2030 tech infrastructure goals.

1341.notes: Professor at Princeton University, Department of Molecular Biology and Princeton Neuroscience Institute. Runs the Berry Lab studying neural coding in the retina. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) during doctoral research on large-scale electrophysiology of neural circuits. Published extensively on neural population coding and information processing in biological systems.

1342.notes: Theoretical biophysicist, professor at Princeton University (Physics) and CUNY Graduate Center. Pioneer in applying information theory to neural coding and biological systems. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) on PhD dissertation "Network-Scale Electrophysiology: Measuring and Understanding the Collective Behavior of Neural Circuits." Co-authored PNAS paper on thermodynamics and criticality in neural networks with Amodei.

1343.notes: Office held by Adrian Fontes (D). Formed AI & Election Security Advisory Committee (June 2024), co-chaired by Chris Cummiskey and Gowri Ramachandran. Released final report (Feb 2025) proposing AI Elections Lab for developing guidelines and pilot programs. Deployed AI tools in 2024 election: chatbot handled 655K+ interactions, SMS system reached 281K voters. Advocates both beneficial AI use in elections and safeguards against AI-driven threats. Pushes for state and federal AI election policy.

1344.notes: Chief Research Officer at OpenAI. Joined 2018 after working as quantitative trader at Jane Street Capital. Led development of Codex (adapting GPT-3 for code generation), co-led vision team for GPT-4 (image recognition integration), contributed to DALL-E. Advocates focusing on "main quest" of AGI over short-term product competition. Views math/coding proficiency as "bedrock for a far more general form of intelligence." Background in competitive programming.

1345.notes: Convened by Governor Jared Polis (Oct 2025) to revise Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205), the nation's first comprehensive AI anti-discrimination law. Diverse membership: consumer groups, hospitals, school districts, tech companies large and small. Delivered unanimous support for revised policy framework (March 2026) emphasizing transparency over bias audits: up-front notice when AI is used in consequential decisions, access to information on adverse decisions, right to correction, human review option. Recommendations to be codified in legislation.

1346.notes: Co-founder and President of OpenAI. Took a sabbatical Aug–Nov 2024, then returned. In 2025 led OpenAI's Stargate infrastructure buildout. Major individual backer of the pro-AI super PAC 'Leading the Future,' launched August 2025 by AI-industry figures (including Andreessen Horowitz partners and Joe Lonsdale) to oppose state-level AI regulation and shape the 2026 midterms; the PAC raised $125M+ in 2025, with Brockman and his wife each contributing $12.5M.

1347.notes: Private nuclear fusion company building the Orion plant in Chelan County, WA, targeting power delivery to Microsoft data centers by 2028 under the world's first fusion power purchase agreement. Backed by Sam Altman ($350M+) and SoftBank; raised $425M Series F in Jan 2025 at a $5.4B valuation. Directly tied to AI compute energy demands.

1349.notes: Co-founder of Anthropic. Previously architect of GPT-3 at OpenAI. Left OpenAI in 2020 with seven colleagues to found Anthropic because safety was not the top priority. Advocates layered safety approach ("Swiss Cheese Model of Defense"). Key contributor to Constitutional AI methodology. Estimates 50th percentile chance of superintelligence by ~2028. Describes alignment challenge as "how do we keep God in a box."

1350.notes: Co-founder of Sierra AI (with Bret Taylor), an enterprise AI agent platform valued at $10B. Former Google VP (18 years), led Google Labs, AR/VR, Project Starline, Google Lens. Sierra hit $100M ARR in 7 quarters. AI optimist focused on applied/enterprise AI.

1351.notes: Nonprofit research org focused on AI welfare, consciousness, and moral patienthood. Co-founded by Kyle Fish, Robert Long, Kathleen Finlinson. Published landmark report "Taking AI Welfare Seriously." Commended Anthropic's model welfare efforts. Five research priorities: welfare interventions, human-AI cooperation, leveraging AI for welfare research, standardized evaluations, credible communication.

1352.notes: University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science at NYU. Co-director of Center for Mind, Brain, and Consciousness. Formulated the "hard problem of consciousness." Leading voice on AI consciousness and moral status. Author of "Reality+" on virtual worlds. Warns against corporate control of virtual/AI worlds.

1353.notes: Executive Director of Eleos AI. PhD Philosophy from NYU. Former research fellow at Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford) and CAIS Philosophy Fellow (2023). Leading researcher on AI consciousness, sentience, and moral status. Co-author of "Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence" with Butlin, Bengio, et al. NOTE: Same person as Rob Long (entity 1354) — "Rob" is informal name.

1355.notes: Head of Strategy and co-founder of Eleos AI. Background in applied mathematics, data science, and AI forecasting. Researches strategic considerations for AI welfare action. Advocates balancing AI control with cooperative strategies that respect AI welfare.

1356.notes: Centrist/center-right think tank founded 2015 by Jerry Taylor (ex-Cato). Named "Most Interesting Think Tank" by TIME (2023). AI policy stance: regulatory restraint, intervene only when harm is realistic. Has 501(c)(3) research arm and 501(c)(4) lobbying arm. Major funders include Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Carnegie Corp.

1357.notes: DC-based science and technology policy nonprofit founded in 1945. FAS runs the Day One Project to translate scientific expertise into federal policy; active in AI governance, advocating for responsible AI innovation, NIST AISI funding, and federal AI adoption. Submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House in 2025.

1358.notes: Housed within NIST. Develops science-based metrics, tools, and guidelines for AI safety evaluation. Established by Biden EO 14110 (Oct 2023). Paul Christiano served as Head of AI Safety. Runs AISIC consortium (290+ members). Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in 2025. Budget ~$10M (2024). NOTE: Related entities — 205 (U.S. AI Safety Institute at NIST, likely same entity), 1629 (AISIC consortium, its membership body), 1309 (NIST, parent agency).

1359.notes: Nonprofit AI safety eval org (formerly ARC Evals, spun off Dec 2023). Founded by Beth Barnes (ex-OpenAI). Evaluates frontier model capabilities for catastrophic risk. Worked with OpenAI (o3, GPT-4.5) and Anthropic (Claude). Does NOT accept lab funding to avoid conflicts of interest. Budget ~$13-15M/year. Funded via Audacious Project (TED) and donations. Paul Christiano was originally involved but stepped back for AISI role.

1360.notes: UK government body established April 2023 with £100m mandate to evaluate frontier AI safety risks ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Chaired by Ian Hogarth, it transitioned in November 2023 into the AI Safety Institute (later renamed AI Security Institute in 2025), which is a separate entity in this database.

1361.notes: Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, founded 2018 as a joint MIT–Community Jameel initiative. Conducts translational AI research across clinical AI, drug discovery, and epidemiology. Developed clinical AI tools MIRAI and SYBIL, and discovered antibiotics halicin and abaucin using AI-driven methods.

1363.notes: Major research university with broad AI activities spanning Cornell Tech (NYC), the AI Initiative, and the MacArthur-funded AI, Policy, and Practice (AIPP) initiative focused on AI's societal impacts. In 2025 NSF awarded Cornell a $20M AI Materials Institute, and a new Vice Provost for AI Strategy was named in January 2026.

1364.notes: Co-founder and Chief AGI Scientist at Google DeepMind, leading its Technical AGI Safety team. Has predicted a 50% chance of human-level AGI by 2028 since 2009, reaffirmed publicly in 2025. Advocates for deliberative, human-auditable AI safety mechanisms to address alignment and misuse risks.

1365.notes: Mustafa Suleyman co-founded DeepMind (2010) and Inflection AI (2022), and became CEO of Microsoft AI in 2024. He argues AI is 'already superhuman' and predicts human-level task performance within 5-7 years. He advocates for humanist superintelligence with strong human control, global regulations, and has pledged to halt development if AI poses existential risk.

1366.notes: Director at Google DeepMind. Led development of AlphaFold (protein structure prediction). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (shared with Demis Hassabis and David Baker). PhD theoretical chemistry (U of Chicago). AI optimist — believes AI has "unparalleled potential to improve the lives of billions."

1367.notes: Nonprofit founded 2017 by Timnit Gebru and Rediet Abebe. Increases presence and inclusion of Black people in AI. Four pillars: education pathways, financial barriers, visibility, and community support. Received MacArthur Foundation funding. Hosts annual workshop at NeurIPS. Aims to broaden voices in AI development, deployment, and regulation.

1368.notes: Chief Ethics Scientist at Hugging Face. Previously founded and co-led Google Ethical AI group (fired 2021 alongside Timnit Gebru). Pioneered "Model Cards" for ML model reporting. Created "Seeing AI" for blind/low-vision users. ~100 papers on NLG, assistive tech, CV, AI ethics. Strong advocate for diversity in AI and regulation that centers those most harmed.

1369.notes: President of WGA West (2021-2025). TV showrunner (The Rookie, Code Black, Cold Case, NYPD Blue). Named TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI 2024. Led WGA during historic 148-day strike in 2023 that secured precedent-setting AI protections for writers. Called on studios to sue AI companies for using copyrighted work to train models. Strong advocate that AI cannot replace human creative experience.

1370.notes: President of WGA East (elected Sept 2023). First person of color to hold the position. TV writer/producer (Empire, Five Days at Memorial). Participated in April 2024 White House roundtable on AI and Workers Rights with Acting Secretary of Labor Julie Su. Launched WGA East AI Task Force to track AI's effect on writing professions. Strong enforcement of 2023 MBA AI protections for film/TV/streaming writers.

1371.notes: Conservative super PAC affiliated with Club for Growth. Supports limited-government, free-market candidates. Major donors include Peter Thiel and Richard Uihlein. Focuses on tax cuts, deregulation, free trade, school choice. No specific AI policy position — general deregulatory stance would extend to opposing AI regulation. Founded 2010 as independent expenditure arm.

1372.notes: Private Catholic research university in Indiana. Major AI initiatives: AI@ND hub, Lucy Family Institute for Data & Society, Scientific AI Initiative. Received $50.8M Lilly Endowment grant for DELTA Network expanding faith-based AI ethics framework. Won $1M Bloomberg Mayors Challenge Award for human-centered AI in public service with City of South Bend. Pete Buttigieg is an alumnus.

1373.notes: Senior Fellow at Brookings Institution, leading multi-year project on generative AI's impact on work and workers. Nationally recognized expert on AI, economic inequality, and future of work. Advocates for worker voice in AI implementation, enhanced union protections, and public investment in workforce training. Argues U.S. lags Europe in institutional mechanisms to help workers navigate AI transition. Work cited by Sen. Chris Murphy.

1374.notes: Premier annual international security conference held in Munich, Germany (since 1963). Increasingly prominent on AI governance. Convened 'Tech Accord to Combat Deceptive Use of AI in 2024 Elections' signed by 27 tech companies. Hosts Frontier AI Safety Commitments discussions. Technology program frames AI as 'decisive factor' in systemic competition. 2025 conference marked shift from AI safety to AI security framing.

1376.notes: 46th U.S. President (2021-2025). Issued Executive Order 14110 in October 2023, the most comprehensive U.S. AI governance action to that point, requiring safety reporting from frontier AI developers and coordinating federal agency standards. Also released the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights in 2022.

1377.notes: Aerospace and space launch company that merged with xAI in February 2026 to create a $1.25T 'Orbital Intelligence' entity. Plans space-based AI data centers using Starlink satellite infrastructure. Founder Elon Musk favors light-touch AI regulation while pursuing aggressive AI deployment through xAI's Grok models.

1378.notes: X (formerly Twitter), owned by Elon Musk, integrates the Grok AI chatbot developed by xAI. In March 2025, xAI formally acquired X in an all-stock deal. X has faced EU and UK regulatory investigations over Grok-generated deepfake images and has deployed content restrictions in response.

1379.notes: Brain-computer interface company co-founded by Elon Musk (2016). Developing implantable BCI (N1 chip) for paralysis patients. FDA IDE approval May 2023; first human implant Jan 2024 (PRIME trial). FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for speech restoration tech (May 2025). Raised over $700M. Regulatory gap: neural data privacy laws lag behind technology. Competitor Precision Neuroscience also received FDA clearance.

1380.notes: High-Flyer (Zhejiang High-Flyer Asset Management) is a Chinese quantitative hedge fund founded by Liang Wenfeng. In 2023 it incubated DeepSeek as a spin-off AI lab, funding it internally. High-Flyer posted 56.6% average returns in 2025 and manages over 70 billion yuan (~$10B USD).

1381.notes: Professor of Law at UC Berkeley School of Law. Faculty co-director of Berkeley Center for Law & Technology. Former clerk for Justice Breyer (SCOTUS) and Judge Diane Wood (7th Circuit). Former FCC special counsel (2012-2013). Published 'An Antimonopoly Approach to Governing AI' (Yale Law & Policy Review, 2024) with Ganesh Sitaraman — argues AI infrastructure warrants regulation via structural separation, nondiscrimination, and public cloud options. Work cited in 2024 Economic Report of the President. Participated in White House AI competition policy meeting.

1383.notes: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab (SVL), co-directed by Fei-Fei Li, focuses on computer vision, embodied AI, and robotics. Key projects include BEHAVIOR benchmark for household AI agents and the PAIR group for generalizable robot perception. Li has called for science-based AI governance.

1384.notes: Nonprofit co-founded in 2017 by Fei-Fei Li, Olga Russakovsky, and Rick Sommer to increase diversity in AI. Grew from 2015 Stanford summer program for high school girls. 2022 strategic pivot to College Pathways — accelerating underrepresented college students into AI internships and research. Partnerships with 16+ universities (Texas A&M, UIUC, UMBC). Early funders include Melinda French Gates/Pivotal Ventures and Jensen Huang.

1385.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Christoph Lassner, Ben Mildenhall). Assistant Professor of CS at University of Michigan. PhD from Stanford (advised by Fei-Fei Li). Key contributor to early PyTorch development. Created CLEVR visual reasoning benchmark. NSF CAREER Award recipient. World Labs raised $1B+ at $5B valuation, building Large World Models for 3D perception.

1386.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall). PhD from University of Tubingen. Previously led research teams at Epic Games and Meta Reality Labs. Created Pulsar differentiable renderer (precursor to Gaussian Splatting). Built human pose estimation for Amazon Halo (via Bodylabs acquisition). Specialist in 3D computer vision and graphics.

1387.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Christoph Lassner). Co-creator of NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields), ECCV 2020 honorable mention for best paper. PhD from UC Berkeley (advised by Ren Ng), Hertz Fellow. Former Google Research scientist (2021-2023). Research spans computational photography, 3D reconstruction, and text-to-3D generation.

1388.notes: Stanford professor of philosophy and former provost (2000-2017). Co-founded and co-directs Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI) with Fei-Fei Li since 2019. Leading advocate for national AI research resource to counter industry concentration of AI compute and data. Argues 'the science has outgrown the university model of research.' HAI promotes human-centered approach: AI evaluation and accountability over hype. Expert in logic and philosophy of language.

1389.notes: The primary academic division of Harvard University housing most arts, humanities, and sciences departments. Home to the Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence (launched 2022), a university-wide initiative with strong FAS ties bridging neuroscience and AI. FAS Research Computing supports the Kempner AI cluster.

1390.notes: Major US philanthropy (est. 1936) with significant AI accountability and governance funding. Part of $200M+ coalition (with MacArthur, Omidyar, Mozilla, Open Society Foundations) for public-interest AI. Co-launched Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative (Oct 2025) with ten major foundations. Funds AI Now Institute. Supports efforts on AI racial/social bias accountability, labor rights in AI deployment, and global AI fairness standards through Current AI collaboration. Focus areas: transparency, algorithmic accountability, worker protections.

1391.notes: Premier Indian technical university (est. 1959). Established Wadhwani School of Advanced AI and Intelligent Systems with Department of Intelligent Systems (2025), admissions starting 2026. Hosts Sustainable Cities Centre of Excellence in AI under Ministry of Education initiative. Research partnerships with TCS (urban planning), IBM (air quality monitoring via AIRAWAT Foundation). Active in AI for sustainable cities, robotics, cybersecurity. Growing AI policy and outreach center.

1392.notes: MIT-based consortium (Trust::Data Consortium) founded by Alex 'Sandy' Pentland. Builds open-source tools for secure, privacy-preserving data sharing using blockchain and AI. Mission: create trusted internet-based network for safe data exchange. Focus areas: data cooperatives, privacy-preserving identity systems, safe distributed computation. Advocates collective data governance model over individualized data control. Part of MIT Connection Science ecosystem.

1393.notes: Blogger and writer behind Astral Codex Ten (formerly Slate Star Codex). Psychiatrist by training. Arguably the most influential AI forecasting voice in the rationalist/EA community. Co-authored 'AI 2027' scenario analysis (April 2025) with Daniel Kokotajlo, modeling fast intelligence explosion by 2027-2028. Supports AI safety research funding (ACX Grants). Nuanced on regulation: supports safety standards but worries about US-China race dynamics leading to worst-case policy tradeoffs. Personal AGI median later in 2020s/early 2030s.

1394.notes: Researcher at the AI Futures Project focused on dangerous capability evaluations, AI safety measurement, and compute governance. Co-authored the AI 2027 scenario forecast with Daniel Kokotajlo and Eli Lifland. Previously founded the Center for AI Policy; also a MATS mentor.

1395.notes: Founding Researcher at AI Futures Project and leading AI forecaster; co-author of the AI 2027 scenario (April 2025) alongside Daniel Kokotajlo, Thomas Larsen, and Romeo Dean. Previously forecasted at Samotsvety and worked at Ought and SaferAI. Co-founded Sage (a forecasting platform) and has served as guest fund manager at the Long Term Future Fund. Publicly argues that short AGI timelines are underweighted and that existential risk from AI is not taken seriously enough.

1396.notes: Researcher at AI Futures Project and co-author of the AI 2027 scenario forecast (April 2025). Graduated cum laude from Harvard with a concurrent AB/SM in Computer Science; previously an AI Policy Fellow at the Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) and an Astra Fellow at Constellation. Specializes in forecasting AI chip production, government intervention, and international coordination dynamics. Also a mentor in the MATS (Machine Learning Alignment Theory Scholars) program.

1397.notes: German research university and major European AI hub. Home to Tübingen AI Center (co-directed by Philipp Hennig and Matthias Bethge since July 2025) and Cluster of Excellence 'Machine Learning: New Perspectives for Science' (renewed 2025). Partners with Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, ELLIS Institute Tübingen, and Cyber Valley ecosystem. ML Cloud serves 430+ active researchers. Focus areas: machine learning, computer vision, robotics, probabilistic methods.

1398.notes: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning at University of Tübingen. Co-director of Tübingen AI Center since July 2025. Pioneer of probabilistic numerics — treating numerical computation as machine learning inference. Author of 'Probabilistic Numerics: Computation as Machine Learning' (Cambridge University Press, 2022). Previously at Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems. Organizer of ProbNum conference series since 2012. Over 11,000 citations.

1400.notes: British AI music startup (founded 2012 by Ed Newton-Rex) that built a platform for generating royalty-free music using AI. Raised $2.5M from Cambridge Innovation Capital, Parkwalk Advisors, Backed VC, Playfair Capital. Acquired by ByteDance/TikTok in July 2019; site went offline post-acquisition. Technology integrated into ByteDance's AI music capabilities. Newton-Rex became director of ByteDance AI Lab, then VP Audio at Stability AI (later resigned over copyright stance).

1401.notes: Chinese tech giant (est. 2012), parent company of TikTok. Major AI capabilities in recommendation algorithms, content moderation, generative AI. Spent $5.4M on US lobbying in H1 2025 (41 lobbyists). Acquired AI music startup Jukedeck (2019). Subject to PAFACA law requiring divestiture of US TikTok operations; deal closed Jan 2026 creating TikTok USDS (with Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX). Developing large language models and AI tools internally. One of the most heavily scrutinized tech companies in US-China AI competition.

1402.notes: Open-source generative AI company (est. 2019). Creator of Stable Diffusion. Founded by Emad Mostaque, who stepped down as CEO in March 2024 citing desire to 'fix concentration of power in AI.' Current CEO: Prem Akkaraju (since June 2024). Raised $80M from Eric Schmidt, Coatue, Lightspeed. Strong open-source advocate: released model weights under permissive licenses. Wrote to US Senate urging open-source AI preservation. B2B2C model: open-source distribution + commercial API. Expanding into video, audio, 3D. Growing at triple-digit rates as of late 2024.

1403.notes: Short-form video platform owned by ByteDance, central to US-China tech tensions. Subject to PAFACA ban law (signed April 2024, effective Jan 2025). After multiple Trump executive order delays, US operations divested to TikTok USDS (Jan 2026) consortium including Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX. Uses AI extensively: recommendation algorithm, content moderation (85%+ AI removal rate), creator tools. Requires AI-generated content labeling. Revised Community Guidelines (Sept 2025). ~150M US users. Acquired Jukedeck (2019) for AI music tech.

1404.notes: AI-focused holding company launched April 2025 by Joshua Kushner's Thrive Capital. Buys, owns, and runs companies positioned to benefit from AI — starting with accounting (Crete Professionals Alliance) and IT services (Shield Technology Partners). OpenAI took ownership stake in Thrive Holdings (Dec 2025) and embeds engineering/product teams within portfolio companies. Parent firm Thrive Capital manages $25B+, raised $10B in 2026. Named TIME100 AI 2025 (Kushner). Early OpenAI investor ($130M in 2022, $1B at $285B valuation). Also invested in Databricks, Anduril, Cursor/Anysphere.

1405.notes: German research institute for interdisciplinary digitalization research, formally 'Weizenbaum Institute for the Networked Society.' Founded 2017, funded by German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (~€50M over 5 years). Consortium led by Berlin Social Science Center (WZB), includes TU Berlin, FU Berlin, HU Berlin, UdK Berlin, Uni Potsdam, Fraunhofer FOKUS. Studies social impact of digitalization — AI ethics, data governance, platform economies. Named after Joseph Weizenbaum, German-American computer scientist who critiqued uncritical tech adoption.

1406.notes: Major German technical university (TU9 member). ~50 academic chairs in AI and computer science. Hosts BIFOLD (Berlin Institute for Foundations of Learning and Data), one of Germany's six national AI competence centers with permanent federal funding. Co-founded Einstein Center Digital Future (ECDF) for digitalization research. Strong socio-technical AI focus — ethics, fairness, explainability. Key partner in Weizenbaum Institute consortium.

1407.notes: Argentina's largest public university, accounts for ~40% of national research output. Has AI-focused labs: IALAB (Law Faculty — AI governance, judicial AI guidelines) and LIAA (Applied AI Lab in Computer Science). IALAB published guide on responsible use of generative AI for judiciary, modeled on MIT Task Force approach. Key institution in Latin American AI policy discourse.

1408.notes: Bipartisan U.S. Senate caucus on artificial intelligence, launched March 2019 by Sens. Martin Heinrich (D-NM) and Rob Portman (R-OH). Original members included Schatz (D-HI), Gardner (R-CO), Peters (D-MI), Ernst (R-IA). After Portman's retirement (2023), Mike Rounds (R-SD) became co-chair. Heinrich named one of TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI. The caucus works toward responsible AI policy balancing innovation with ethical standards.

1409.notes: Major U.S. business newspaper owned by News Corp (Rupert Murdoch). Covers AI extensively through its tech and business desks and has employed prominent AI journalists including Karen Hao (China tech, 2022–2023). Has adopted AI-generated tools internally (Narrativa for market summaries, AI article summaries) and its editor-in-chief Emma Tucker has been openly pro-AI in newsrooms. Editorial coverage is business-focused and generally frames AI as an economic and competitive opportunity.

1410.notes: Quarterly magazine of public affairs, literature, science, history, and culture published by Phi Beta Kappa Society since 1932. Named after Ralph Waldo Emerson's 1837 oration. Multiple National Magazine Award winner. Publishes essays, criticism, poetry, and (since 2006) fiction. Also maintains daily website content and bimonthly e-newsletter. Relevant to AI discourse through long-form cultural and intellectual essays on technology and society.

1411.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (sworn in Jan 2025). Former CEO of Bridgewater Associates (2020-2022), world's largest hedge fund. West Point graduate, Bronze Star recipient (82nd Airborne, Gulf War). Under Secretary of Treasury for International Affairs under George W. Bush (2007-2009). Strong AI-and-energy nexus advocate — hosted inaugural Pennsylvania Energy and Innovation Summit (July 2025) at CMU with President Trump. Champions Pittsburgh as AI hub ('AI Avenue'). Pro-innovation stance on AI with focus on national competitiveness and energy infrastructure.

1412.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Mississippi (since 2007). Chairman of Senate Armed Services Committee (119th Congress). Key defense AI champion — secured AI funding in FY2025 and FY2026 NDAAs including $10M for autonomous AI data fusion C2 capability and $10M for AI-enabled Army multi-domain operations (drone swarm defense). Authored 'Restoring Freedom's Forge' plan to reform Pentagon procurement and embrace commercial AI innovation. Pushed $925B defense policy bill (FY2026). Focus: AI for military modernization to counter China.

1413.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (2009–January 2025) who retired after choosing not to seek re-election in 2024. Took targeted AI positions: secured $47.7M for NIST's AI Safety Institute, pushed to restrict advanced AI chip exports to China, and urged DOJ/CISA to address AI-generated deepfakes threatening elections. Consistent with a moderate, national-security-forward approach to AI governance rather than broad regulation.

1414.notes: Rob Portman (R-OH) served as a U.S. Senator from 2011 until his retirement in January 2023, having chosen not to seek re-election in 2022. While in office he co-founded the bipartisan Senate Artificial Intelligence Caucus with Martin Heinrich (D-NM) in 2019 and co-introduced the Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act, authorizing a $2.2 billion federal investment in AI R&D and workforce development. He also co-passed the AI for Armed Forces Act and National AI Research Resource Task Force Act via successive NDAAs. His AI stance emphasized national competitiveness, workforce readiness, and national security rather than heavy-handed regulation.

1415.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from Vermont (since 2023, formerly House rep since 2007). Most active Senate voice on AI copyright/consent legislation. Introduced ~6 AI bills including: TRAIN Act (subpoena power for copyright holders to discover AI training data use), AI CONSENT Act (require consent before using personal data for AI training), Digital Platform Commission Act (new federal agency for platform regulation). Serves on Judiciary Committee (copyright jurisdiction) and Finance Committee. Champions Vermont creative economy and artist protections from AI.

1417.notes: Federal agency overseeing U.S. energy systems and nuclear infrastructure. Plays a central AI role through its 17 National Laboratories, its membership in the TRAINS Taskforce for national security AI testing, and its initiative to co-locate AI data centers with federal energy infrastructure across 16 identified sites.

1418.notes: Catherine Cortez Masto (D-NV) is a U.S. Senator who narrowly won re-election in 2022 and serves until January 2029. She focuses on consumer protection in AI: her IOGAN Act (signed 2020) directed NSF and NIST to research deepfake detection, and she has backed the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed 2025) criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate images. She co-sponsored legislation establishing the National Security Commission on AI with Joni Ernst, and urged the FTC to prioritize consumer protection from AI misuse. Her approach is bipartisan and targets harmful AI applications rather than broad regulation.

1419.notes: U.S. federal agency (est. 1953) supporting small businesses and entrepreneurs. Increasingly relevant to AI policy as small business AI adoption surges (58% using generative AI per 2025 Chamber survey, up from 40%). Provides AI guidance for small businesses (sba.gov/ai). Subject of the bipartisan Small Business AI Training Act (2026, Cantwell/Moran) directing Commerce Dept + SBA to create AI training resources. Under Senate oversight scrutiny regarding AI practices (Sen. Ernst). Trump admin's 2026 AI policy framework includes 'AI infrastructure and small business support' pillar.

1420.notes: The Department of Defense is the largest U.S. federal agency and a major driver of AI policy and procurement. In January 2026, Secretary Hegseth released an "AI-first" strategy directing the department to adopt AI at wartime speed, including deploying frontier models to warfighters within 30 days of public release. The Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) was realigned under the Under Secretary for R&E/CTO Emil Michael with a Barrier Removal Board to accelerate AI delivery. DoD AI work spans autonomous systems, logistics, intelligence, and enterprise operations across seven pace-setting projects.

1421.notes: The Joint Economic Committee (JEC) is a bicameral congressional committee that studies economic policy. In the 119th Congress (2025–2026), it is chaired by Rep. David Schweikert (R-AZ) with Sen. Maggie Hassan (D-NH) as Ranking Member. The JEC held a hearing in June 2024 on "Artificial Intelligence and Its Potential to Fuel Economic Growth and Improve Governance," examining AI's productivity impacts and regulatory approaches. The committee's AI work focuses primarily on economic competitiveness and workforce implications.

1422.notes: Republican U.S. Senator (MO). Introduced the Decoupling America's AI Capabilities from China Act (2025) and the bipartisan GUARD Act banning AI chatbots for minors (2026). Opposes preempting state AI laws and advocates targeted consumer and child-safety regulation.

1423.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Indiana (since Jan 2025, formerly House rep 2017-2025). Serves on Senate Armed Services Committee (subcommittees: Personnel, Seapower, Strategic Forces) and Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee. Previously ranking member of House Cyber, Innovative Technologies, and Information Systems subcommittee (117th Congress). Also served on House Select Committee on U.S.-China Strategic Competition. Conservative defense hawk with national security focus. No significant public AI-specific policy positions identified.

1424.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from West Virginia (since 2015). Key AI legislation author: VET AI Act (with Hickenlooper) directs NIST to develop voluntary guidelines for AI assurance and verification; AI Scam Prevention Act prohibits AI-generated deepfakes for fraud. Serves on Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee. Pragmatic, bipartisan approach to AI — favors voluntary industry guidelines over heavy regulation. Also focused on broadband/rural connectivity relevant to AI access.

1425.notes: Senate Majority Leader (Jan 2025-present), senior U.S. Senator from South Dakota. Key AI legislation gatekeeper — co-authoring bipartisan 'light-touch' AI bill with Sen. Klobuchar requiring risk/impact assessments and self-certification for critical-impact AI systems. Opposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation authority. Supported GAIN AI Act in FY2026 NDAA to keep advanced AI chips available to U.S. innovators over adversaries. Previously chaired Senate Commerce Committee where he led early AI policy work.

1426.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Louisiana, Chair of Senate HELP Committee (2025-present). Released major AI white paper favoring flexible, sector-specific regulation over one-size-fits-all approach. Proposed novel concept of using AI to regulate AI — government-utilized AI as industry watchdog. Co-sponsored RAISE Act empowering states to include AI/emerging tech in K-12 curriculum. Introduced Future of Artificial Intelligence Innovation Act of 2026 (S.3952). Focus areas: AI in healthcare, education, workforce.

1427.notes: Senate's primary oversight committee for government operations and DHS. Active AI oversight role: held hearings on 'AI Risks and Opportunities' and 'AI in Government.' Key legislation includes AI LEAD Act (Chief AI Officer at every federal agency), No Adversarial AI Act (S.2177, protecting government from foreign adversary AI), and AI procurement standards bill (Peters-Tillis). Focus on federal AI use management, security, and procurement guardrails.

1428.notes: U.S. Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI, technology, and telecommunications. Chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025, who introduced the SANDBOX Act and a five-pillar light-touch AI framework. Oversees the Senate AI Working Group and has held hearings on U.S. AI competitiveness vs. China.

1429.notes: Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy, and Environment (confirmed Oct 2025). Former Senior Advisor to Palantir CEO. Retained investments in OpenAI, SpaceX, and dozens of AI/defense/fintech companies. Launched Pax Silica (Dec 2025) — State Dept's flagship AI and supply chain security initiative. Former U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission member. Founded Hill and Valley Forum connecting Silicon Valley to Capitol Hill. Major China hawk advocating hardline tech competition stance.

1430.notes: Chairman of FDD's China Program. Former Deputy National Security Advisor (2019-2021) and NSC Senior Director for Asia under Trump first term. Led shift in U.S. China policy. Now prominent voice on AI national security — advocates restricting China's access to U.S. AI labs and technology. Former journalist and Marine intelligence officer in East Asia. Co-author in Foreign Affairs on China strategy. Warns Beijing actively exploits U.S. tech openness for military AI buildup.

1431.notes: Youth-led DC-based nonprofit founded by Sneha Revanur advocating for responsible AI governance. Sponsored California SB 53 and New York's RAISE Act; challenged OpenAI's for-profit conversion. Funded by FLI, Heising-Simons, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and others; explicitly rejects corporate and AI-executive funding.

1432.notes: Full legal name: Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI Labs). AI startup co-founded by Yann LeCun (exec chairman) and Alex LeBrun (CEO) after LeCun left Meta (Nov 2025). Raised $1.03B seed at $3.5B pre-money valuation (Mar 2026) — one of largest seed rounds ever. Building 'world models' using JEPA architecture as alternative to LLMs. Backed by Nvidia, Samsung, Bezos Expeditions, Eric Schmidt. Key team: Saining Xie (CSO), Pascale Fung (CRIO), Michael Rabbat (VP World Models), Laurent Solly (COO). Target: industrial control, robotics, healthcare, wearables. NOTE: Entity 1433 (AMI Labs) is the SAME organization — short name variant.

1434.notes: Legendary corporate research lab (est. 1925). Pioneering AI/ML contributions: Claude Shannon's learning machines (1952), neural network research (1986-1996) including handwritten digit recognition with USPS data, early deep learning work by Yann LeCun on convolutional neural networks. 11 Nobel Prizes, 5 Turing Awards for work done there. In the 1990s, AT&T Labs' AI/ML group was arguably the strongest in the world. LeCun worked as researcher there before moving to NYU and later Facebook/Meta. Now AT&T holds 6th most AI patents among U.S. firms (2025).

1435.notes: Prestigious French academic institution (est. 1530) in Paris. AI relevance: Yann LeCun held the Annual Chair for Computer Science and Digital Technologies (Chaire Annuelle Informatique et Sciences Numériques) in 2016, delivering inaugural lecture on deep learning. The institution hosts visiting professorships connecting top researchers to French academia. Not an AI-focused institution per se, but its rotating chairs have featured leading AI scientists.

1436.notes: CEO of AMI Labs (Advanced Machine Intelligence), co-founded with Yann LeCun (2025). Serial French AI entrepreneur: founded VirtuOz (acquired by Nuance), Wit.ai (acquired by Facebook 2015 after 21 months), and Nabla (healthcare AI, remains chairman). Worked under LeCun at Meta FAIR after Wit.ai acquisition. 24 years building AI products spanning chatbots, NLU, virtual assistants, healthcare AI. Graduate of École Polytechnique.

1437.notes: Federal advisory committee created by National AI Initiative Act of 2020. Advises the President and National AI Initiative Office (within OSTP). Housed at NIST. Members drawn from academia, nonprofits, civil society, private sector. Covers AI competitiveness, workforce, R&D, standards, ethics, governance. Finalized transition recommendations for Trump admin (Mar 2025) with 10 priority recommendations. NOTE: Formally called NAIAC (National AI Advisory Committee) — 'Commission' in entity name may be a slight misnomer.

1438.notes: Federal cabinet department overseeing labor standards, workforce, and employment. Key AI role: published national AI Literacy Framework (Feb 2026) with 5 content areas and 7 delivery principles. Partnered with NSF on AI-ready workforce initiative (MOU for AI literacy, training pathways, labor market research). Hosts AI page at dol.gov/ai. Focus on workforce preparation for AI disruption through guidance and skills development rather than heavy regulation. Daniel Ho serves as Senior Advisor on Responsible AI.

1439.notes: Bipartisan 501(c)(4) nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy, co-founded in 2025 by former Congressmen Brad Carson (D) and Chris Stewart (R). Received $20M from Anthropic in Feb 2026 — its largest single donation — to counter 'Leading the Future,' a $125M super PAC backed by tech billionaires opposing AI regulation. Policy priorities: AI model transparency safeguards, federal AI governance framework, smart export controls on AI chips, targeted regulation of near-term high risks (bioweapons, cyberattacks). Plans to back 30-50 candidates from both parties in 2026 state and federal races.

1440.notes: Former Special Assistant to the President and Senior Advisor in the Biden White House Chief of Staff office, where she drove implementation of the Inflation Reduction Act, Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, and CHIPS and Science Act. Previously Senior Policy Advisor at the White House National Economic Council (2021-2023) focusing on industrial strategy and energy market resilience. Now Director at Foundry Logic, scaling businesses addressing structural supply-demand imbalances. Co-authored AI energy policy proposals with Brian Deese (MIT) on 'National AI Additionality Framework' for managing AI data center electricity demands. Stanford SIEPR affiliated.

1441.notes: Postdoctoral fellow at the Stanford Digital Economy Lab directed by Erik Brynjolfsson. Macroeconomist studying AI's economic impacts. Co-authored 'The Macroeconomics of Artificial Intelligence' (IMF Finance & Development, Dec 2023) with Brynjolfsson, examining whether transformative AI can fulfill its revolutionary economic potential. Research addresses three fundamental questions: shared vision for AI's economic role, implications for growth theory, and redesigning education and social connections in the age of transformative AI.

1442.notes: Principal Research Scientist at MIT Sloan School of Management. Co-founder and co-director of MIT's Initiative on the Digital Economy (IDE). Co-authored 'The Second Machine Age' (2014) with Erik Brynjolfsson — influential book arguing technology races ahead of skills, organizations, and economic institutions. Also authored 'More from Less' (2019). Inaugural Visiting Fellow at Google's Technology and Society organization. Research focuses on how digital technologies reshape work, firms, and the economy. Prominent voice on AI's labor market disruption potential.

1443.notes: Economist and AI researcher. Postdoctoral fellow in Economics and Computation at Microsoft Research. Will join MIT as Assistant Professor (joint Economics and Computer Science) in summer 2026. PhD from MIT (2024), BA from Yale. Former staffer at White House Council of Economic Advisers (2021-2022). Key research: 'Generative AI at Work' (with Brynjolfsson and Li), studying 5,179 customer support agents — found AI tools increased productivity 14% on average, 35% for novice workers. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow. Research focus: AI's effects on labor markets, firms, and global organization of work.

1444.notes: Ivy League law school at Columbia University, New York. Active in AI governance research and education. Faculty-led task force (professors Gillis, Liebman, Talley, Wexler) developing AI curriculum. Key AI faculty: Tim Wu (Julius Silver Professor — competition and tech policy, former Biden White House advisor), Rebecca Wexler (AI and criminal justice/due process). Offers seminar on 'Emerging Law of AI' comparing regulatory approaches across US, EU, and China. Hosted 2023 symposium on new legal frameworks for generative AI revolution. Adopted formal generative AI policy for academic integrity (Aug 2025).

1447.notes: SUNY flagship research university on Long Island, New York. Home to the AI Innovation Institute (AI3) directed by Lav Varshney. Participates in Empire AI — SUNY's multi-campus partnership for AI research and public-good applications. Three faculty received SUNY-IBM AI Research Alliance grants (Zadok, Balasubramanian, Ferdman) for cloud computing, sustainable energy, and supercomputer optimization. Computer science department has active AI/ML, NLP, and computer vision research groups. Governor Hochul proposed Empire AI Beta (2026 State of the State) to scale to world's most advanced academic supercomputer. Yejin Choi was previously an assistant professor of CS here.

1448.notes: South Korea's top research university, located in Seoul. Major AI research hub with 100+ AI professors, 200+ AI application labs, and 2,000+ AI researchers. Houses the Artificial Intelligence Institute (AIIS), selected as core part of Korea's National Strategy AI Development Project. Established Interdisciplinary Program in Artificial Intelligence (IPAI). Key labs: Biointelligence Lab (Prof. Byoung-Tak Zhang — biological/artificial intelligence), Data Science & AI Lab, AI and Biomedical Informatics Lab. Yejin Choi is affiliated with SNU (edge 1609). Strong in NLP, computer vision, and biomedical AI.

1449.notes: John C. Ford Professor of Engineering in Computer Science and Information Science at Cornell University. Leading NLP researcher specializing in coreference resolution, information extraction, and opinion mining. Founding chair of Cornell's Department of Information Science and led development of its academic programs. AAAI Fellow (2024), ACM Fellow (2019, 'for contributions to NLP including coreference resolution, information and opinion extraction'), AAAS Fellow (2021). Doctoral advisor to Yejin Choi (edge 1611). Research group develops ML/neural network techniques for large-scale NLP tasks.

1450.notes: Harry and Norman Chandler Professor of Communication at Stanford University. Founding director of the Stanford Social Media Lab. Research focuses on AI's effects on trust, deception, and human communication — from AI-generated text detection to social media well-being impacts. His TED talk 'The Future of Lying' (2012) has 1M+ views. Author of 80+ journal articles. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1612). Notable controversy: in 2024, as expert witness defending a state deepfake law, he submitted a court filing relying on ChatGPT that contained fabricated citations; a federal judge barred his testimony.

1451.notes: Greek-Australian moral and legal philosopher. Was inaugural Director of the Institute for Ethics in AI at the University of Oxford (resigned Sep 2025 during harassment investigation). Professor of Ethics and Legal Philosophy, Senior Research Fellow at Balliol College Oxford. Rhodes Scholar. Previously held chairs at King's College London, UCL, and University of Glasgow. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow — his project takes a 'humanistic approach' to AI ethics centered on distinctive value and capabilities of human beings. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1613). Published widely on moral philosophy, legal philosophy, and philosophy of technology.

1452.notes: Matthew J. Salganik is the Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University. Leading figure in computational social science — co-founded the Summer Institute in Computational Social Science (SICSS) with Chris Bail in 2017, now a global annual program training researchers in data science methods for social research. Author of 'Bit by Bit: Social Research in the Digital Age' (2017). Affiliated with Princeton AI Lab, Princeton Precision Health, and Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP). Research focuses on social networks, digital traces, and novel survey methods. Won Outstanding Article Award from ASA Mathematical Sociology Section (2005) and Outstanding Statistical Application Award from ASA (2008). Published in Science, PNAS, Sociological Methodology.

1453.notes: Leading cybersecurity research, engineering, and consulting firm founded in 2012. Pioneered AI security assurance practice combining safety and security methodologies for ML/AI risk assessment — Engineering Director of AI Assurance team is Heidy Khlaaf. Won $3M second-place prize in DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) with their 'Buttercup' system. Clients include Facebook/Meta, DARPA, and major cryptocurrency protocols. Strong open-source ethos — builds and maintains public security tools across static/dynamic analysis, AI/ML security, and fuzzing. Member of OpenSSF. As of 2026, has 94 plugins, 201 skills, 84 specialized agents for AI-augmented security auditing.

1454.notes: UN Secretary-General's High-Level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence, established October 2023. Comprised 39 preeminent AI leaders from 33 countries — geographically diverse, gender-balanced group from government, private sector, civil society, and academia. Published final report 'Governing AI for Humanity' in September 2024, proposing seven recommendations for global AI governance cooperation including foundations for the first globally inclusive and distributed architecture for AI governance. Process included 18 deep-dive discussions, 50+ consultation sessions, 50+ written submissions from 150+ organizations. Recommendations inspired the Global Digital Compact adopted at the Summit of the Future (September 2024). Heidy Khlaaf served as Network of Experts member.

1455.notes: The British Standards Institution (BSI) is the UK's national standards body, playing a central role in shaping international AI governance standards. First certification body accredited by UKAS to certify ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System standard, published end of 2023) — also accredited by RvA (Netherlands) and ANAB (US). Actively involved in developing ISO/IEC 42001 and published ISO 42006 (July 2025) setting requirements for bodies auditing AI management systems. ISO/IEC 42001 addresses AI-specific aspects: unwanted bias, fairness, safety, security, privacy, accountability, explainability, and transparency. BSI performs AI management system audits globally. Heidy Khlaaf is an ISO SC 42 Committee Member through BSI.

1456.notes: Former and current First Lady of the United States. Emerged as unexpected AI policy voice in 2025-2026 through education and child safety lens. Launched Presidential AI Challenge — nationwide contest inviting K-12 students to develop AI projects addressing community challenges. Convened White House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence Education (September 2025) with tech CEOs and Cabinet members. Advocated 'watchful guidance' approach to AI growth. Championed the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025) criminalizing non-consensual sharing of explicit images including AI-generated deepfakes — grew out of her BE BEST initiative on child online safety. Hosted deepfake victim Elliston Berry at White House to amplify awareness.

1457.notes: President of North America's Building Trades Unions (NABTU), an alliance of 14 national/international unions representing 3+ million skilled craft professionals in US and Canada. Glazier by trade — began career in 1981 with Glaziers Local Union 252 in Philadelphia, worked up through IUPAT leadership. Key figure at AI-labor intersection: partnered with Sam Altman/OpenAI (March 2026) to ensure AI data center infrastructure construction supports union careers and registered apprenticeship programs. NABTU invests $2.5B annually in workforce training. Positions unions as essential builders of AI infrastructure rather than victims of automation. Named to U.S. Commerce Department Workforce Policy Advisory Board. Collaborator with Randi Weingarten on labor issues.

1458.notes: AI-native cybersecurity startup founded by serial entrepreneurs Brian Long (CEO) and Andrew Jones. Builds platform simulating AI-powered attacks — deepfake voice/video phishing, smishing, and GenAI social engineering — to train corporate defenders. Raised $136M+ total: $43M round co-led by OpenAI and Andreessen Horowitz (OpenAI's first cybersecurity investment), plus $55M Series A (April 2025) led by a16z and OpenAI Startup Fund. Platform includes real-time threat triage, personalized training, and AI-driven risk scoring. Also partnered with Elliston Berry and Pathos Consulting Group to create free deepfake safety training course for US schools. Brian Long previously co-founded Attentive ($500M+ ARR, 8000+ customers) and TapCommerce (acquired by Twitter).

1459.notes: AI ethics consulting firm focused on K-12 education, founded by Evan J. Harris (President). Specializes in helping independent schools integrate AI tools responsibly — staff department automation, student AI empowerment, and ethical professional development. Harris holds master's in private school leadership from Teachers College and studied tech ethics at Stanford's Human-Centered AI Institute (HAI). National expert on emerging AI risks in schools, particularly deepfake abuse and digital safety. Employs holistic systems approach addressing data privacy, bias, authentic communication. Partnered with Elliston Berry and Adaptive Security to create free deepfake safety training course for US schools (17-minute online course for students, teachers, parents).

1460.notes: CEO and Co-Founder of Adaptive Security, an AI-native cybersecurity startup simulating deepfake and GenAI attacks for corporate defense training. Serial entrepreneur: previously co-founded Attentive (SMS/email marketing platform, grew to $500M+ ARR, 8,000+ customers, 1,000+ employees) and TapCommerce (acquired by Twitter, where he served as Senior Director leading mobile teams for 19 months). Raised ~$900M+ in venture capital across ventures from Sequoia Capital and other top-tier firms. Under his leadership Adaptive Security raised $136M+ from OpenAI and Andreessen Horowitz. University of Pennsylvania graduate; on permanent leave from NYU Stern School of Business.

1461.notes: Mother of Elliston Berry and co-advocate for deepfake child safety legislation. After her 14-year-old daughter was targeted by a classmate who used AI to create non-consensual deepfake nude images (Aledo, Texas, fall 2023), McAdams gave emotional testimony before the Texas Senate Committee on Criminal Justice and took the story public nationally. Met with lawmakers in Washington including Sen. Ted Cruz and First Lady Melania Trump to push for federal legislation. Their advocacy was instrumental in shaping the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025), which criminalizes sharing non-consensual explicit images including AI-generated deepfakes. Continues to advocate for stronger tech company accountability and child digital safety protections.

1462.notes: Digitally native business news outlet founded in 2012 by Atlantic Media, now owned by Redbrick (Canadian software firm). Organized around 'obsessions' rather than traditional beats. Notable for AI integration controversy: in January 2025, drew significant attention for quietly publishing AI-generated news articles — described as 'purely experimental' AI newsroom intended to free editorial staff for deeper reporting. Publishes 'Quartz AI & Tech' newsletter covering business of AI and technology. Uses in-house Chartbuilder tool and Atlas data visualization platform. Previously employed Karen Hao as tech reporter and data scientist. Covers global business, economics, and technology with focus on the 'new global economy.'

1463.notes: Nonprofit journalism organization (Pulitzer Center on Crisis Reporting) supporting independent journalists on underreported global issues. Major AI accountability journalism hub: runs AI Accountability Network with dedicated fellowships, reporting grants, ML reporting grants, and AI Spotlight Series (2,000+ journalists trained since 2024). AI Accountability Fellowship launched 2022, supporting 27 journalists from five continents. Awards $2M+ annually in direct grants. Stories by grantees have been cited by courts, sparked official investigations. Funded by MacArthur Foundation, Omidyar Network, Howard Hughes Medical Institute, Humanity United, and Facebook Journalism Project endowment gift. Karen Hao is a collaborator/co-creator. Focus areas include AI, environment, health, conflict, and human rights.

1464.notes: Public graduate journalism school at CUNY (est. 2006), the only public graduate journalism school in the northeastern U.S. Named after Craig Newmark (craigslist founder) who donated $20M (2018) and an additional $10M endowment for ethics/security center. AI relevance: runs AI Journalism Labs (5th and 6th cohorts in 2026 with Microsoft support and Nordic AI Journalism partnership) training experienced journalists on generative AI tools. Community Media Training program helps niche outlets report on how AI systems affect immigrants, communities of color, and vulnerable populations. Working toward tuition-free model with $60M fundraising goal. Key funders include Ford Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, Knight Foundation.

1465.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Missouri (sworn in Jan 2023). Former Missouri AG. Key AI legislation: co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Warren (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) to prevent big tech monopolies in DoD AI/cloud procurement. Co-introduced AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act with Sen. Peters to build America's AI workforce pipeline. Secured $250M for AI in U.S. Cyber Command (One Big Beautiful Bill, Jul 2025). Secured DoD AI cybersecurity requirements provision in FY26 NDAA. Serves on Armed Services and Commerce committees. Frames AI policy around national security competition with China and ensuring small/mid-size startups can compete with big tech.

1466.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from North Carolina (2015-present). Key AI legislation: co-sponsored Secure Artificial Intelligence Act with Sen. Warner (2024) creating voluntary AI security incident database and NSA AI Security Center. Co-introduced federal AI procurement guardrails bill with Sen. Peters requiring agencies to assess AI risks before deployment. Co-sponsored NO FAKES Act (2024) with Sens. Coons, Blackburn, Klobuchar protecting voice/visual likenesses from AI-generated replicas. Notably was sole senator to vote against removing 10-year state AI regulation moratorium from reconciliation bill (Jul 2025), supporting federal preemption of state AI laws. Serves on Judiciary, Banking, and Armed Services committees.

1469.notes: House Democratic caucus body launched Dec 2025 by Leader Hakeem Jeffries. Co-chaired by Rep. Ted Lieu (CA-36), Rep. Josh Gottheimer (NJ-05), and Rep. Valerie Foushee (NC-04). Ex officio co-chairs: Rep. Zoe Lofgren (Ranking Member, Science, Space & Tech Committee) and Rep. Frank Pallone Jr. (Ranking Member, Energy & Commerce Committee). Will convene throughout 2026 to develop AI policy expertise on guardrails, economic impacts, safety, and health. Successor to Democrats' participation in the now-expired Bipartisan AI Task Force. All House Democratic Caucus members invited to participate.

1471.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from New Jersey's 5th district (2017-present). Centrist Democrat on House Intelligence Committee, Ranking Member of NSA & Cyber Subcommittee. Co-Chair of House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy (Dec 2025). Key AI legislation: Unleashing AI Innovation in Financial Services Act (Innovation Labs for AI testing at financial regulators), Advanced AI Security Readiness Act (Jun 2025, directing NSA to develop AI security playbook with Rep. LaHood), AI Workforce Training Act. Critical of White House's Mar 2026 AI framework as blocking state laws without sufficient consumer protections. Advocates 'Smart AI' regulation balancing innovation with state regulatory carveouts.

1472.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from New York's 9th district (2007-present). Chair of Congressional Black Caucus. Early AI regulation champion — introduced Algorithmic Accountability Act (first version 2019 with Sens. Wyden and Booker, updated 2022 and 2025) requiring companies to audit high-impact AI systems. Introduced DEEPFAKES Accountability Act (2021, 2023 with Rep. Ivey) requiring digital watermarking and criminalizing malicious deepfakes. Introduced REAL Political Ads Act requiring AI disclosure on political advertising. Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Lee, Pressley) banning algorithmic discrimination. Focuses on protecting marginalized communities, election integrity, and AI transparency.

1473.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Washington's 7th district (2017-present). Chair of Congressional Progressive Caucus (2021-2024). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (introduced Sep 2024, reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Clarke, Lee, Pressley) — comprehensive legislation banning algorithmic discrimination, requiring pre- and post-deployment testing, mandating transparency of algorithms used in critical decisions (housing, employment, credit, education, law enforcement). Bill would establish civil rights protections when personal data is processed by algorithms. Frames AI regulation through civil rights and equity lens, emphasizing protection for marginalized communities.

1474.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 12th district (2023-present). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Pressley) banning algorithmic discrimination in critical decisions. Co-introduced Eliminating BIAS Act with Sen. Markey mandating dedicated Office of Civil Rights in every federal agency that uses, funds, or oversees AI to combat AI-driven bias and discrimination. Stated: 'Discrimination is illegal when it's done by a person and it should be just as illegal when it's done by an algorithm.' Focuses on racial, gender, and disability-based inequities replicated by AI systems in jobs, housing, healthcare, loans, and law enforcement.

1475.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Massachusetts's 7th district (2019-present). Member of 'The Squad.' Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Lee) banning algorithmic discrimination. Called on Congress (Mar 2024) to address AI risks to marginalized communities. Co-introduced No Biometric Barriers Housing Act prohibiting facial/biometric recognition in federally funded public housing. Emphasizes AI's disparate impact on communities of color — concerns include facial recognition criminalizing people of color, deepfake pornography targeting women, and biased algorithms denying access to critical resources. Frames AI oversight through equity and civil rights.

1476.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (2013-present). Major voice on AI antitrust and competition policy. Co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Schmitt (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) preventing big tech monopolies in DoD AI procurement. Co-sponsored GAIN AI Act ensuring U.S. customers get priority access to advanced AI chips over adversaries. Launched investigation (2024) into Google-Anthropic and Microsoft-OpenAI partnerships with Sen. Wyden. Wrote DOJ supporting Nvidia antitrust probe (Sep 2024). Pressed OpenAI on whistleblower protections with Rep. Trahan (Aug 2024). Co-sponsor of AI Civil Rights Act (Dec 2025). Led opposition (with Sen. Markey, Nov 2025) to big tech's attempt to insert 10-year AI regulation moratorium into NDAA.

1477.notes: Nonpartisan civil rights legal organization founded in 1963 at the request of President Kennedy. Champions the AI Civil Rights Act (co-endorsed with Rep. Pressley, Sen. Markey, Rep. Clarke) requiring transparency, accountability, and anti-discrimination safeguards in AI systems used in housing, lending, healthcare, employment, education, and law enforcement. Applauded Biden's AI Executive Order and White House AI Bill of Rights. Opposed Trump-era executive order seeking to bar states from addressing AI harms. Leverages the nation's largest pro bono legal network (62,000+ hours/year). Headquarters: Washington, D.C.

1478.notes: America's oldest and largest civil rights coalition (240+ member organizations including ACLU, NAACP LDF, Sierra Club). Launched the Center for Civil Rights and Technology in 2023 focused on AI policy impacts on civil and human rights. Published an Innovation Framework guiding responsible AI investment, creation, and use. Led a 50+ organization letter to the Senate opposing a 10-year ban on state/local AI laws.
