Named in TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2023. PhD in Behavioural Science from University of Warwick; first-class degree in Mathematics and Philosophy from Oxford. Previously Senior Research Fellow and Deputy Director of the AI: Futures and Responsibility Programme at the University of Cambridge (CSER). Has published widely on AI policy and provided expert advice to UK government bodies including the Centre for Data Ethics and Innovation and Office for AI. Also affiliated with the Centre for Emerging Technology and Security (CETaS) at the Alan Turing Institute, GovAI, and CNAS.

1777.notes: Interim Director of AI Policy at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR), leading the AI policy unit's work on mitigating extreme risks from AI. Over 10 years of experience in technological risks policy. Previously at the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), where he led a team delivering a £10m programme to develop AI techniques to analyse disinformation impacting UK national security and conducted risk assessments of frontier AI. PhD candidate at King's College London researching AI incidents monitoring. Authored key reports on AI security incident preparedness (2025) recommending DSIT implement a preparedness framework, and the Loss of Control Observatory tracking real-world AI scheming behavior.

1778.notes: Senior Policy Analyst for Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Previously at the Future of Privacy Forum. Develops forward-looking policies on challenges from emerging technologies. Has authored and co-authored multiple CAP reports on AI policy, including work opposing federal moratoriums and preemption of state AI laws. Argues that moratoriums offer no federal protections in return and that preemption undermines state regulatory authority while entrenching industry power. Covers AI regulation, generative AI safeguards, federal privacy, and the intersection of AI with employment, healthcare, housing, and elections.

1779.notes: Director of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Focuses on AI governance, digital policy, and societal impacts of emerging technologies. Previously worked at Meta, Instagram, and X (formerly Twitter) on privacy, platform policy, and product strategies to protect users from harm. Earlier career in government: U.S. Department of Treasury (Office of International Affairs, Office of Domestic Finance) and the White House Domestic Policy Council. Siegel Research Fellow. Has contributed to CAP work on responsible AI deployment, NIST generative AI risk frameworks, and protecting democracy online. Also a Georgetown TPP visiting fellow (spring 2026).

1780.notes: Vice President of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP), leading the inaugural Technology Policy team. Builds progressive technology policy platform covering AI regulation, competition, disinformation, and content policy. Previously spent seven years on the Facebook Privacy and Public Policy team as their first D.C. employee. Vocal advocate for AI safety regulation; has argued against federal preemption of state AI laws, criticized the Trump administration's rollback of AI safety restrictions, and warned about AI-enabled disinformation. Co-authored CAP pieces including "4 Reasons the Senate's AI Pause Should Be Opposed" and work on regulating high-risk AI under existing U.S. law.

1783.notes: Researcher at METR focused on loss-of-control risk assessment. Host of AXRP (AI X-risk Research Podcast), interviewing safety researchers since 2020. Previously a senior researcher at MATS. Active contributor to the AI safety research community.

1784.notes: Project Manager on the events team at FAR.AI, delivering events that bring together AI safety researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders. Honours Integrated Sciences degree (concentration in Mathematics and Statistics) from McMaster University. Three years of experience running large-scale conferences on pandemic preparedness, global health, animal welfare, and AI safety. Previously worked at AI Safety Support. Involved in organizing FAR.AI's London Alignment Workshop featuring researchers from leading labs, academia, and government.

1785.notes: Cari Tuna is an American philanthropist and former Wall Street Journal reporter who co-founded Good Ventures and Open Philanthropy (now Coefficient Giving) with her husband Dustin Moskovitz. Open Philanthropy was an early backer of AI safety, giving more than $580 million toward building a field of researchers studying the potential catastrophic risks of AI. She was included in TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2024.

1849.threat_models: Bio/chem weapon uplift, automated cyberattacks, loss of control, concentration of power / stable totalitarianism, great-power AI race, systemic labor and autonomy risks

1850.threat_models: Loss of control / meaningful human oversight, erosion of human autonomy, democratic erosion via bureaucratic AI decision-making, labor displacement, plus systemic risks (cyber, bio, misinformation) covered in IASR co-lead role

2036.notes: AMP PBC is a newly formed venture firm founded by Anjney Midha, a former general partner at Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). Midha secured $1.3 billion for AMP's first fund, making it a notable debut in the venture capital landscape. AMP PBC is listed as a significant investor in Anthropic's $30 billion Series G funding round (announced February 2026), which valued Anthropic at $380 billion post-money, confirming a reported $300 million investment in Anthropic as part of that round. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem directly through large-scale capital allocation to frontier AI labs. Midha's background at a16z, one of the most influential technology venture firms, brings deep networks and expertise to AMP. The 'PBC' designation (Public Benefit Corporation) may signal an intent to balance profit with social mission, though details are limited in available sources. AMP represents a new generation of AI-focused venture capital emerging from established firm alumni.
2036.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation from AMP PBC or Anjney Midha are present in the available sources. As a major investor in Anthropic — a company that publicly advocates for targeted AI safety regulation — AMP's investment choices may suggest alignment with moderate or targeted regulatory stances, but this is inferred from association rather than explicit statements.

2037.notes: Accel is a prominent global venture capital firm with a strong focus on technology and AI investments. The firm has been an active investor across the AI ecosystem, participating in high-profile rounds including Thinking Machines Lab's $2 billion seed round led by a16z (July 2025, valuation $12 billion), Nebius's $700 million private placement alongside Nvidia and Orbis (December 2024), and Dovetail's $63 million Series A led by Accel partners Rich Wong and Arun Mathew. Accel has also led investments in AI-adjacent infrastructure and developer tools, including the Series C in n8n, an AI-native workflow automation platform, with portfolio companies including Cursor, Linear, Lovable, Supabase, and Vercel. The firm's partner Matt Weigand joined Nebius's board of directors as an observer following the $700M investment. Accel's investment thesis centers on backing foundational AI tooling, infrastructure, and frontier AI labs, positioning it as a major capital allocator shaping the AI ecosystem. The breadth of its AI portfolio—spanning frontier model startups, cloud infrastructure, and developer tooling—underscores its systemic influence on AI development trajectories.
2037.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation are found in the sources. However, Accel's consistent pattern of investing in frontier AI labs (Thinking Machines Lab), AI infrastructure (Nebius), and AI developer tools without any stated advocacy for regulatory constraints suggests a preference for industry-led, light-touch governance that prioritizes rapid capital deployment and innovation.
2037.threat_models: Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Power concentration

2039.notes: Athena 2.0 is the second iteration of the Athena research mentorship program, led by Claire Short, focused on fostering diversity of ideas in AI safety research by supporting women and marginalized genders in entering technical and governance AI safety research roles. The first iteration (Athena 1.0) ran in early 2024 as a 2-month hybrid mentorship program and was fully funded at $20,000, with partial support from a Lightspeed grant and Manifund donations. Athena 2.0 was planned to begin in January/February 2025, also as a 2-month hybrid program, with a $80,000 funding goal, of which only $8,000 had been raised via Manifund at the time of discovery. Funding is used for participant travel fees, retreat venue and food, operations staff payment, and compute and research-related needs for participants. The program shapes the AI ecosystem by expanding the pipeline of researchers working on technical and governance AI safety, with a specific focus on inclusion and diversity. The program includes a retreat component and aims to strengthen participants' research skills and professional networks in the AI safety field.
2039.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is stated by the program. Its focus on AI safety research and governance suggests awareness of AI risks, but no specific regulatory positions are articulated in available sources.

2040.notes: The German Federal Government ('Bund') is the central government of the Federal Republic of Germany, acting as a major funder and policy actor in science, research, and digitalization. As confirmed in Source [4], the Bund co-funds the Weizenbaum-Institut für die vernetzte Gesellschaft—Germany's premier internet and digitalization research institute—alongside the states of Berlin and Brandenburg, with a new institutional funding agreement effective from October 2027. The Weizenbaum Institute, operating under the mandate of the Federal Ministry for Research (Bundesforschungsministerium), investigates societal impacts of digitalization and AI. The Bund also participates in numerous Bund-Länder agreements through the Gemeinsame Wissenschaftskonferenz (GWK), coordinating large-scale research funding including for AI-adjacent institutions. The federal government's involvement in funding AI and digitalization research positions it as a key shaper of Germany's AI policy ecosystem. The Wissenschaftsrat's 2024 evaluation of the Weizenbaum Institute praised its high scientific quality and recommended permanent institutional funding, which the Bund has moved to implement. Germany's federal government also sets overarching AI strategy and participates in EU-level AI regulation deliberations.
2040.regulatory_stance_detail: The German Federal Government supports structured, institutionalized oversight of AI and digitalization through funding of research bodies like the Weizenbaum Institute, which advises policymakers on AI impacts. Germany has been an active participant in EU AI Act negotiations, generally supporting a risk-based regulatory framework—consistent with a moderate, balanced stance. The Bund's co-funding of interdisciplinary research on societal AI impacts signals a preference for evidence-based governance rather than either laissez-faire or precautionary approaches.

2041.notes: Captions (Captions.AI) is an AI-powered creative suite for video content creators, founded by former Snap employees Gaurav Misra and Dwight Churchill. The company was first invested in at the seed stage (February 2021, $4M seed round) by Jacob Andreou as an angel investor and others including Antoine Martin. Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) has been a repeated investor, backing the company at seed, Series A, and Series B stages. The company's first feature automated the generation of captions for videos, and it has since expanded to include AI Eye Contact (correcting eye contact in post-production), AI Music (matching music to video content), and broader scripting, recording, and editing tools aimed at short-form video creators. Jacob Andreou, then an executive at Snap and later a General Partner at Greylock, made an angel investment in the company at the Series A stage (January 2022, $12.5M round per CBInsights). Captions shapes the AI ecosystem by deploying applied AI tools directly to consumers and creators, representing the growing wave of generative AI-powered media production platforms targeting the creator economy.
2041.regulatory_stance_detail: No direct public statements from Captions as a company on AI regulatory stance have been identified in the sources. As an AI-powered consumer product company backed by a16z and angel investors from Snap, the company is focused on product growth rather than policy advocacy, making its regulatory stance unclear.

2042.notes: Caroline Mehl is the Co-Founder and Executive Director of the Constructive Dialogue Institute (CDI), a nonprofit that translates behavioral science research into scalable educational tools designed to help Americans communicate across differences; CDI's tools have been used by over 60,000 students and professionals. She is listed as an individual donor to Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a 501c4 nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy, alongside Eric Gastfriend, ARI's Executive Director, suggesting a personal relationship with that organization's leadership. She began her career at Blackstone in its Tactical Opportunities private equity fund and has experience in venture capital, social entrepreneurship, and venture philanthropy. Mehl was previously an Associate Research Scholar and Visiting Scholar at NYU's Stern School of Business. She holds a bachelor's degree from Yale and a master's from Oxford as a Blavatnik Foundation Scholar. Her writing has appeared in TIME, the Harvard Business Review, the Chronicle of Higher Education, and Inside Higher Ed. She is on the Advisory Board of the Disagree Better and Builders initiatives focused on reducing societal polarization.
2042.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from her financial support of ARI, which advocates for thoughtful, bipartisan AI governance frameworks that protect the public from harm while fostering innovation — a moderate, balanced regulatory stance. ARI explicitly opposes both under-regulation and sweeping moratoriums on state AI laws.

2045.notes: Composite is a San Francisco-based AI startup founded in 2024 by Yang Fan Yun (CEO, New Zealand national valedictorian and highest GPA in Stanford CS) and Charlie Deane, with co-founder Shine Wu also listed in some sources. The company builds AI-powered browser automation technology that transforms users' existing browsers into intelligent assistants that predict and automate repetitive digital tasks. On October 1, 2025, Composite announced a $5.6 million seed round led by Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross of NFDG, with participation from Menlo Ventures and Anthropic's Anthology Fund. Its core product is a browser extension and desktop app that operates locally on-device, monitors user workflows, and automates repetitive tasks across any website without requiring users to switch browsers or surrender credentials. The product is available on Mac and Windows, works with Chrome, Edge, and Brave, and is used by professionals at companies including Google, Uber, DoorDash, Tesla, Salesforce, and Reddit. Composite differentiates itself from AI browser competitors like Perplexity Comet and OpenAI's ChatGPT Agent by targeting professional knowledge-worker workflows rather than consumer errands, and by running actions locally with a zero data retention policy and SOC-2 Type 2 compliance. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by building agentic AI infrastructure that enables real-world task automation at scale inside the browser layer.
2045.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy positions stated, but Composite's product design philosophy emphasizes local execution, user control, and privacy-by-design (zero data retention, SOC-2 Type 2 compliance, explicit action confirmations) rather than advocating for external regulation. Their approach suggests a preference for industry self-governance and technical safeguards over mandatory regulatory frameworks.

2046.notes: DeepTechXL Fund I is a Dutch deep-tech venture capital fund launched in May 2022 and based in Eindhoven, Netherlands, targeting a minimum fund size of €100 million (ultimately raising ~€110 million by early 2024). Strategic investors and co-initiators include ASML, Philips, Brabantse Ontwikkelings Maatschappij (BOM), and research institute TNO, with additional investors including PME Pensioenfonds, Invest-NL (€15M), Oost-NL, Technical University of Twente, the Netherlands Enterprise Agency (RVO, which provided a €9M loan for seed investments), and various Dutch family offices. The fund focuses on early-stage deep-tech startups and scale-ups in the Netherlands, with particular emphasis on complex technologies such as photonics, semiconductors, quantum computing, and complex mechanical engineering — areas directly relevant to AI hardware and compute infrastructure. By 2024, the fund had invested in at least eight companies, including SuperLight Photonics. The fund is managed by partners Guus Frericks, Teska van Vuren, Ron Maurer, and Ronald Meersschaert, and works closely with venture builder HighTechXL (founded 2013), an existing deep-tech innovation initiative of ASML, Philips, TNO, and BOM. Beyond capital, the fund provides startups access to knowledge, networks, technology licenses, supply chain partners, and business development support, aiming to build the Dutch deep-tech manufacturing ecosystem.
2046.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI or deep-tech regulation have been identified in available sources. The fund's focus on ecosystem-building and collaboration with government-backed entities like Invest-NL and RVO suggests openness to public-private partnership models, but no specific regulatory stance on AI policy is articulated.

2048.notes: Draper Laboratory is a nonprofit research, development, and manufacturing company headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts, with more than 2,500 employees across 12 locations. The organization focuses on four critical mission areas: Strategic Systems, Space Systems, Electronic Systems, and Biotechnology Systems. In February 2026, Draper received an up to $26.7 million Other Transaction Award (OTA) from ARPA-H to support the CATALYST program, developing in silico models of human physiology using AI/ML for drug development, including its PREDICT96 microphysiological system. Draper also holds a $26 million Pentagon OTA contract awarded in January 2024 to develop autonomous UAS software for CBRN reconnaissance missions using AI-driven sensor-fusion and autonomous teaming algorithms. In March 2026, Draper joined the ARPA-H BREATHE-funded SCALE team to develop advanced biosensors and AI-driven respiratory risk assessment software for building air quality. Draper shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying AI/ML systems for defense autonomy, drug development modeling, and public health, primarily as a federally funded R&D contractor. The organization also runs the Draper Scholars program and DraperSPARX to engage startups and the next generation of innovators.
2048.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulatory stance were found in the sources. Draper operates primarily as a government contractor aligned with federal R&D objectives; its work on AI/ML for drug development explicitly supports FDA IND filing pathways and ARPA-H programmatic goals, suggesting acceptance of regulatory frameworks rather than opposition or advocacy for specific stances.

2049.notes: Emergent Ventures is a fellowship and grant program housed at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, launched in 2018 with a $1 million seed grant from the Thiel Foundation and administered by economist and Mercatus faculty director Tyler Cowen. The program funds 'zero to one' ideas from entrepreneurs, researchers, and thinkers worldwide, awarding grants ranging from a few hundred dollars to tens of thousands. It has supported numerous individuals relevant to the AI policy and safety ecosystem, including Leopold Aschenbrenner (AI safety/governance researcher), Marius Hobbhahn (AI safety researcher), Dwarkesh Patel (AI-focused journalist/podcaster), and Sam Bowman (now on Mercatus board). The program has expanded to include geographic verticals for India, Ukraine, and Africa & the Caribbean, with the latter supported by Schmidt Futures. A COVID-focused spin-off, Fast Grants, raised over $50 million and awarded 260 grants for COVID-19 research starting in April 2020. Emergent Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a talent-discovery and early-stage funding mechanism for researchers and communicators working on AI safety, progress studies, and adjacent intellectual fields.
2049.regulatory_stance_detail: Emergent Ventures is housed at the Mercatus Center, a libertarian-leaning think tank with a strong preference for market-based solutions and limited government intervention. The program's mission emphasizes entrepreneurialism, decentralized innovation, and 'classical liberal' insights, and its founding was supported by the Thiel Foundation. Grantees span AI safety research to charter cities, reflecting a disposition toward bottom-up innovation over regulatory mandates.
2049.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2050.notes: Grok Ventures is the single-family office of Mike and Annie Cannon-Brookes, where Mike is the co-founder and CEO of Atlassian. Founded in 2008 and headquartered in Sydney, Australia, it operates as a private investment firm deploying permanent capital primarily into technology-enabled opportunities. In 2021, the Cannon-Brookes pledged $1.5 billion over 10 years — $1 billion for climate tech investments and $500 million for philanthropy — making it Australia's first $1 billion+ VC fund focused on climate tech. In 2024, Grok announced it would focus exclusively on climate-related investments, viewing decarbonisation as one of the greatest economic opportunities of our time, and appointed Tan Kueh as new CEO. As of 2025, Grok has made over 56 investments across stages including seed through late VC, with portfolio companies including Airwallex, Culture Amp, Canva, SpaceX, Sun Cable, and tech/AI-adjacent startups. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through early-stage funding of technology companies, including past investments in AI-adjacent tools like Culture Amp (people analytics) and Dovetail (user research AI platform), as well as broader tech infrastructure plays. Grok co-invests frequently with Blackbird Ventures and has backed companies across Australia, the US, and globally.
2050.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit AI regulatory stance is documented for Grok Ventures. Their focus is on climate tech and energy transition investing. Mike Cannon-Brookes has been publicly outspoken on climate regulation (e.g., pressuring AGL Energy on emissions), but no specific AI regulation positions are on record from Grok Ventures or Cannon-Brookes.

2051.notes: HSBC is one of the world's largest banking and financial services organizations, headquartered in London, UK. Its corporate venture capital arm, HSBC Strategic Innovation Investments, has been active since 2014, making minority investments in early-stage technology companies with strategic relevance to HSBC's core banking business. As of 2024, HSBC has invested in 21 companies across Enterprise Applications, FinTech, and High Tech sectors, with a portfolio that includes 4 unicorns and 7 acquisitions. Notable AI-relevant investments include Quantexa (contextual decision intelligence/AI, Series C 2020 and Series E 2023), Contextual AI (Series A 2024), Elliptic (blockchain analytics/AI, 2025), Axoni (distributed ledger, Series B), and OpenFin (financial OS, Series C 2019). HSBC is simultaneously a customer of several AI companies it invests in, including Quantexa and Elliptic, deepening strategic alignment. The firm's investment thesis centers on financial crime detection, data intelligence, cybersecurity, and financial infrastructure — areas where AI is central. Richard May, HSBC's Group Head of Financial Crime, joined Elliptic's board following their 2025 investment, reflecting active engagement beyond passive capital.
2051.regulatory_stance_detail: HSBC's investment in Elliptic — described explicitly as helping 'meet rising regulatory expectations and industry standards' around digital assets and financial crime — signals support for targeted compliance-oriented AI regulation. As a globally systemically important bank, HSBC operates under extensive financial regulation and appears to favor AI governance frameworks that support risk management and compliance rather than opposing regulation.

2052.notes: Horizons Ventures is a Hong Kong-based venture capital firm co-founded in 2002 by Solina Chau and Debbie Chang, closely associated with Hong Kong billionaire Li Ka-shing and his Li Ka Shing Foundation. The firm focuses on deep science and disruptive technology investments globally, with a portfolio of over 369 investments as of 2026. It is renowned for pioneering early-stage investments in transformative AI and tech companies, including DeepMind, Siri, Skype, Facebook, Spotify, Zoom, Slack, and Inflection AI. In AI specifically, Horizons Ventures has backed speech and NLP technologies from early on, investing in Siri, Viv, Fano Labs, and Inflection AI, making it a significant early-stage funder of foundational AI capabilities. The firm operates as a single-family office for Solina Chau with a global investment strategy spanning the US, Europe, Southeast Asia, and Japan. Its investment thesis centers on backing innovations that address fundamental global challenges including health, inequality, and climate adaptation with long-term perspective and multi-round support.
2052.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public policy statements found, but Horizons Ventures' investment pattern—backing early-stage, frontier AI companies like DeepMind, Siri, and Inflection AI without apparent advocacy for regulatory constraints—suggests a preference for enabling rapid technology development with minimal regulatory friction. This is inferred from actions rather than explicit statements.

2053.notes: Hugging Face is a New York-based AI company founded as a chatbot app that evolved into the leading open-source AI model hosting and collaboration platform, often described as 'GitHub for AI.' The platform hosts over 500,000 AI models, 250,000 datasets, and serves more than 10,000 paying enterprise customers including Intel, Qualcomm, Bloomberg, and Pfizer. In May 2022, Hugging Face raised $100 million in a Series C round led by Lux Capital with Sequoia and Coatue participating, valuing the company at $2 billion. In August 2023, it raised a $235 million Series D at a $4.5 billion valuation, with backers including Google, Amazon, Nvidia, Salesforce, AMD, Intel, IBM, and Qualcomm. Hugging Face's primary infrastructure enables developers to share, test, and deploy AI models openly, supporting transformer architectures and large language models as a core part of the ML development stack. Its open-source ethos and BLOOM platform have made it a central node in the global AI ecosystem, especially for startups, researchers, and enterprises building production AI systems. CEO Clement Delangue has stated funds are focused on talent acquisition to remain competitive in AI.
2053.regulatory_stance_detail: Hugging Face's core business model is built on open-source AI model sharing and democratizing access to AI tools, which reflects a light-touch regulatory philosophy favoring openness and developer access over restrictive controls. The company's platform explicitly facilitates broad distribution of AI models including powerful generative ones, suggesting preference for industry self-governance and open access rather than heavy regulatory oversight. No explicit public statements supporting restrictive regulation are evident from available sources.

2054.notes: ICONIQ Capital is a secretive San Francisco-based wealth management and venture capital firm co-founded in 2011 by Divesh Makan, which manages personal fortunes of tech billionaires including Mark Zuckerberg, Satya Nadella, Dustin Moskovitz, Sheryl Sandberg, and Reid Hoffman, as well as senior figures in Middle Eastern and Asian governments. The firm has approximately $100 billion in total AUM, with $26 billion specifically dedicated to VC investing, making it one of the largest startup investors in the United States. ICONIQ emerged as a major force in AI investing in 2025, putting more than $3 billion into AI startups that year alone and over $5.2 billion into AI investments in total. Its most significant AI bet is Anthropic, in which it has invested approximately $4 billion — first leading the Series F round in 2025 at a $183 billion valuation, then co-leading a $30 billion investment round in early 2026 at a $380 billion post-money valuation; ICONIQ CEO Divesh Makan has joined Anthropic's board as an observer. ICONIQ also holds a stake of over $150 million in OpenAI via direct investment and through its earlier investment in Statsig, which OpenAI acquired. Beyond capital deployment, ICONIQ serves as a global connector and convener, having organized a Middle East roadshow for Anthropic's leadership that resulted in investment from Gulf sovereign wealth funds including Qatar Investment Authority and MGX. The firm raised $5.75 billion for its most recent VC fund and has filed securities disclosures indicating plans to raise billions more for a new fund.
2054.regulatory_stance_detail: ICONIQ's actions strongly suggest a light-touch regulatory stance: the firm has aggressively poured billions into frontier AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, facilitated access to global capital including Gulf sovereign wealth funds, and helped Anthropic navigate geopolitical concerns about international funding. There are no public statements from ICONIQ supporting significant AI regulation; their investment thesis frames the AI boom as an enduring opportunity rather than a risk requiring governance.

2055.notes: The Institute for New Economic Thinking (INET) is a nonprofit think tank and grant-making organization founded to challenge conventional economic orthodoxy and advance heterodox economic research. INET has funded influential AI-related research, notably supporting Joseph Stiglitz (Columbia University) and Anton Korinek (University of Virginia) on the economic implications of artificial intelligence, including their 2017 NBER working paper 'Artificial Intelligence and Its Implications for Income Distribution and Unemployment' and subsequent papers through 2025. INET's research in this area focuses heavily on how AI-driven labor displacement, technological unemployment, and winner-takes-all dynamics could exacerbate inequality and poverty, particularly in developing economies. The organization runs a Center for Innovation, Growth and Society (INET-CIGS), which specifically funds AI economics research, and has co-hosted events such as the INET/IMF Conference on Macroeconomics in the Age of AI. INET publishes working papers through its own working paper series (e.g., WP No. 146, 232) and disseminates research through partnerships with NBER and SSRN. It also supports the Young Scholars Initiative (YSI) and the Global Commission on Economic Transformation, which engages Stiglitz and others on structural economic reform including technology policy.
2055.regulatory_stance_detail: INET-funded research by Stiglitz and Korinek explicitly argues that without 'radical reform of tax and redistribution politics,' AI will produce widespread technological unemployment and poverty (NY Times citation, 2021). Their research calls for substantial government intervention, redistribution reform, and reforms to global economic governance to counteract AI's adverse effects on labor and developing economies.
2055.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

2057.notes: Imbue (formerly known as Generally Intelligent) is a San Francisco-based AI startup focused on building practical AI systems that can reason and accomplish large-scale, real-world tasks, with a particular emphasis on autonomous AI agents and coding capabilities. The company rebranded from 'Generally Intelligent' to 'Imbue' in September 2023 alongside its $200M Series B announcement, valuing the company at over $1 billion. Its Series B was led by the Astera Institute with participation from NVIDIA, Cruise CEO Kyle Vogt, Notion co-founder Simon Last, GPT-3 author Tom Brown, and Dropbox CEO Drew Houston. In October 2023, the company raised an additional $12 million from Amazon's Alexa Fund and former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, bringing total Series B funding to over $210 million. Total funding across all rounds stands at approximately $232 million. CEO Kanjun Qiu has articulated the company's vision as 'reinventing the personal computer so that it's truly personal' by building AI agents that work reliably and have strong reasoning capabilities. Imbue sits in a select group of well-capitalized AI labs capable of developing foundation models, and its work on reasoning and autonomous agents directly shapes frontier AI development.
2057.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy statements found in sources. Imbue's focus on building 'trustworthy' and 'robust' agents suggests some self-governance orientation. CEO Kanjun Qiu's public statements emphasize reliability and practicality of AI agents rather than regulatory frameworks, suggesting a builder-first, light-touch stance inferred from company positioning.

2058.notes: Inclusive Abundance Action is a 501(c)(4) advocacy and lobbying affiliate of the Inclusive Abundance Initiative, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. It was launched in 2024 as the advocacy arm to house lobbying activities for the broader Inclusive Abundance movement, which promotes an 'abundance agenda' focused on energy, housing, science/technology, and government reform. The parent organization, Inclusive Abundance Initiative, received $120,000 grant to Inclusive Abundance Action in December 2024. The organization is a registered lobbying client covering issue areas including Energy, Housing, Science/Technology, and Government Issues, with lobbying activity touching AI policy. It is a funder of Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a 501(c)(4) AI policy advocacy group, placing it in the AI governance funding ecosystem. Inclusive Abundance Action helped support the launch of the bipartisan Congressional Build America Caucus in partnership with Congressman Josh Harder. The organization is nonpartisan and coalition-oriented, convening policy experts, think tanks, and advocacy groups to advance abundance-focused policy proposals.
2058.regulatory_stance_detail: Inclusive Abundance Action promotes an 'abundance agenda' that explicitly frames scarcity-oriented regulation as an obstacle to progress. Its lobbying covers AI under a science/technology issue area alongside energy permitting and housing deregulation, suggesting a preference for enabling innovation over restrictive governance. Its funding of ARI, which advocates for 'thoughtful governance' that 'fosters innovation,' is consistent with a light-touch or moderate stance rather than a restrictive one.

2059.notes: Index Ventures is a multi-stage venture capital firm with headquarters in London and San Francisco, founded to support ambitious entrepreneurs globally. The firm has been an active investor in AI and enterprise technology companies. Index Ventures led Cohere's $40 million Series A round in September 2021, with partner Mike Volpi joining Cohere's board as part of the investment. Index Ventures continued as an existing investor in Cohere's $270 million Series C round in June 2023, led by Inovia Capital. The firm also co-led Ideogram's $16.5 million seed financing round alongside Andreessen Horowitz, backing the Toronto-based generative AI image startup. Mike Volpi, co-founder and partner at Index Ventures, has been a key figure in the firm's AI investments and has publicly championed democratizing access to NLP and other AI technologies. Through its investments in enterprise AI infrastructure and generative AI startups, Index Ventures plays a meaningful role in shaping the development and scaling of foundational AI technologies.
2059.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy statements found in sources. Inferred from investment behavior: Index Ventures has backed companies focused on broad AI accessibility and democratization (Cohere, Ideogram), suggesting a preference for enabling rapid AI development with minimal regulatory friction. Mike Volpi's framing of Cohere's mission as 'democratizing access to one of the most important technologies of our time' implies an accelerationist, light-touch regulatory disposition.

2060.notes: Ineffable Intelligence is a London-based AI research lab founded in late 2025 by David Silver, former lead of the reinforcement learning team at Google DeepMind and UCL professor, known for his central role in creating AlphaGo, AlphaZero, and AlphaStar. The company emerged from stealth in April 2026 with a record $1.1 billion seed round — the largest ever in Europe — at a $5.1 billion valuation. The round was co-led by Sequoia Capital and Lightspeed Venture Partners, with participation from Nvidia, Google/Alphabet, DST Global, Index Ventures, BOND Capital, EQT Ventures, UK Wellcome Trust, Flying Fish Ventures, Evantic Capital, the British Business Bank, and the UK's Sovereign AI Fund. Ineffable's core mission is to build a 'superlearner' — an AI system capable of autonomously discovering all knowledge purely from its own experience via reinforcement learning, without relying on human-generated data. The company positions its ambition as potentially the most transformative scientific breakthrough since Darwin, aiming for 'first contact with superintelligence.' The UK government, through Science and Technology Secretary Liz Kendall, publicly endorsed the investment as part of making the UK an 'AI maker.' David Silver has publicly committed via Founders Pledge to donate 100% of his personal equity proceeds to high-impact charities.
2060.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance has been articulated by Ineffable Intelligence or David Silver in available sources. The company has received backing from the UK government's Sovereign AI Fund and British Business Bank, suggesting alignment with UK national AI strategy, but no specific policy positions on regulation have been stated.
2060.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2061.notes: Infisical is a San Francisco-based open-source secrets, identity, and access management company founded by CEO Vlad Matsiiako, Tony Dang, and Maidul Islam. The company provides an end-to-end encrypted platform that helps development teams manage API keys, database credentials, certificates, SSH keys, and other sensitive data across teams, devices, and infrastructure. In June 2025, Infisical raised a $16 million Series A led by Elad Gil, with participation from Y Combinator, Gradient Ventures (Google), Dynamic Fund, and angel investors including CEOs of Datadog, Samsara, and Valor. The company has reached over 100,000 developers and 40 million software downloads globally, securing over 10 billion secrets per month, and counts customers such as Hugging Face and LG. Infisical grew revenue over 20x in the past year and became cash flow positive. The company shapes the AI ecosystem directly by developing security infrastructure for AI agents and workloads, which is explicitly listed as a strategic focus for the Series A funding. Its open-source approach to secrets management is positioned as foundational security infrastructure for the rapidly growing AI and cloud-native application landscape.
2061.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation were found in available sources. The company's focus on enterprise security and developer-first tooling suggests awareness of security and compliance needs, but no formal regulatory stance is articulated.

2062.notes: The Infosys Foundation is the philanthropic arm of Infosys Technologies Ltd. (now Infosys Ltd.), established in 1996 with the objective of fulfilling the company's social responsibility by creating opportunities and working toward a more equitable society. The Foundation has made contributions in healthcare, education, social rehabilitation, and the arts, with Infosys contributing up to one percent of its profits annually. In the AI ecosystem, its most notable contribution is the endowment of the ACM-Infosys Foundation Award in the Computing Sciences, announced in August 2007, which provides a $150,000 (later $250,000) annual prize recognizing young scientists making breakthrough contributions to computing. The inaugural 2007 award went to Daphne Koller of Stanford University for her groundbreaking work combining relational logic and probability in AI. The award was later renamed the ACM Prize in Computing (from 2016 onward), with funding transitioning to Infosys Ltd. directly; past recipients include AI/ML luminaries such as David Blei (2013) and Dan Boneh (2014). Through this award, Infosys Foundation has played a meaningful role in recognizing and incentivizing foundational AI and computing research over nearly two decades.
2062.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance on AI policy is evident from available sources. The Foundation's activities are philanthropic and prize-oriented, with no public statements on AI regulation found in the search results.

2063.notes: Innovate UK is the UK government's national innovation agency, operating as part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI), tasked with supporting UK businesses in developing and commercializing new products, processes, and services. It administers the Smart Grant program, a highly competitive bi-annual scheme offering shares of up to £25 million in government R&D funding; only 2% of applicants across multiple sectors receive awards, with roughly 46 companies selected per cycle from nearly 2,000 applications. Innovate UK actively funds AI-driven technology companies, as evidenced by its £500,000 Smart Grant to Electric Sheep in 2024 for the development of Spotlight V3, an AI-powered rotoscoping tool for the VFX industry. The agency's mandate is to identify and back 'game changing and world leading ideas,' explicitly including AI applications in creative industries, making it a direct funder and enabler of the UK AI ecosystem. Its Smart Grants program is administered through Innovate UK Business Connect. By channeling government funds into AI startups and R&D projects, Innovate UK plays a significant role in shaping the UK's competitive position in AI development and deployment.
2063.regulatory_stance_detail: As a UK government innovation agency, Innovate UK operates within the UK's broader AI regulatory framework, which favors a pro-innovation, context-specific approach rather than blanket regulation. Its grant-making activity reflects a belief that government should actively support AI development while ensuring it benefits the economy and society, consistent with the UK government's AI policy stance of enabling innovation with targeted oversight.

2065.notes: Intel Corporation is one of the world's largest semiconductor manufacturers, headquartered in Santa Clara, California, and a major force in AI infrastructure through chips, research, and strategic investments. Intel shapes the AI ecosystem through its hardware (CPUs, GPUs, and AI accelerators), research partnerships, and significant investments in AI-adjacent companies and academic institutions. Intel acquired Mobileye, an autonomous driving technology company, for approximately $15.3 billion, and later took it public; Mobileye uses cameras and algorithms for self-driving systems. Intel received an $8.5 billion award from the U.S. Department of Commerce under the CHIPS and Science Act to fund semiconductor factories in Arizona, Ohio, New Mexico, and Oregon. Intel Labs has been a long-term partner with NSF across multiple joint programs, including the MLWiNS machine learning and wireless networking program, the NSF/Intel Future of Semiconductors (FuSe and FuSe2) programs totaling tens of millions of dollars, a $10 million semiconductor workforce development initiative under the CHIPS Act, and a 10-year $100 million collaboration on semiconductor design and manufacturing. Intel also funded AI research hubs including Element AI (a $135M investment) and university research at MIT CSAIL and Cornell University, including co-funding the TILOS institute for learning-enabled optimization. Intel Labs actively collaborates with universities on AI, heterogeneous integration for AI compute, and wireless ML edge research, and is directly engaged with NSF's 2025 ASCENT program on wafer- and panel-scale integration to address AI compute demands.
2065.regulatory_stance_detail: Intel's public posture emphasizes public-private partnerships and workforce development as the preferred policy mechanism, as evidenced by its active collaboration with NSF and support for the CHIPS and Science Act. Intel leadership (e.g., Dr. Richard Uhlig) has framed semiconductor policy in terms of industry-academia collaboration and filling skills gaps rather than advocating for restrictive AI regulation. No evidence from sources of Intel supporting broad mandatory AI regulation.

2066.notes: IvySys Technologies, LLC is a small, privately held defense-focused AI company founded in 2005 by Dr. James A. DeBardelaben, headquartered in Arlington, Virginia (with offices also in Silver Spring, MD). The company specializes in synthetic data generation, predictive analytics, and AI/ML solutions for national security and defense missions. Its flagship product, DataFaucet™, uses agentic AI and large language models to generate high-fidelity synthetic datasets for training, testing, and validating AI systems in classified or data-scarce environments. IvySys has received multiple DARPA contracts, including a $4.6M contract to combat weapons of mass terror, a SocialSim contract, a disinformation detection contract, and a 2026 Embedded Entrepreneur Initiative (EEI) award worth approximately $1.1M to commercialize DataFaucet™. The company was also awarded the DoD Joint Artificial Intelligence Center's (JAIC) Data Readiness for Artificial Intelligence Development (DRAID) Basic Ordering Agreement (valued at $241M ceiling), and a $2.1M SBIR Phase III contract for synthetic data generation in support of JWAC. IvySys is classified as a Socially and Economically Disadvantaged small business and reports approximately $20.5M in annual revenue despite a small headcount (~4-8 employees), reflecting a contract-heavy revenue model typical of defense tech firms.
2066.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy positions stated. IvySys's focus on transitioning AI from R&D into operational defense deployment — and its emphasis on accelerating commercialization via DARPA's EEI program — suggests a preference for enabling faster AI deployment with minimal friction. No advocacy for restrictive AI regulation has been identified.

2067.notes: JPMorgan Chase & Co. is the largest U.S. bank by assets and a major global financial institution that has emerged as a significant capital facilitator in the AI ecosystem. In March 2026, JPMorgan Chase was one of four lead underwriters of SoftBank Group's record $40 billion bridge loan—the largest dollar-denominated loan in SoftBank's history—arranged to fund SoftBank's $30 billion follow-on investment in OpenAI through Vision Fund 2. This loan, maturing in March 2027, also included Goldman Sachs, Mizuho Bank, Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corp, and MUFG Bank as co-lenders. JPMorgan Chase also participated in OpenAI's direct fundraising round, contributing approximately $4.7 billion alongside Citi, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley, channeling billions in institutional capital to the leading generative AI company. Through these two transactions alone, JPMorgan Chase has helped direct over $44 billion toward the OpenAI ecosystem, making it one of the most consequential financial intermediaries in the current AI investment wave. The bank's role reflects Wall Street's deep integration with the AI infrastructure buildout, including the broader Stargate Project initiative targeting $500 billion in U.S. AI infrastructure investment. JPMorgan Chase's active participation as a lead arranger and lender positions it as a critical enabler of the capital flows shaping the frontier AI landscape.
2067.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public stance on AI regulation is evident from the available sources. JPMorgan Chase's actions—facilitating massive capital flows to OpenAI and SoftBank—suggest a posture that implicitly supports continued rapid AI development, but no formal regulatory position is stated in these sources.

2068.notes: Jakob N. Foerster is an Associate Professor in the Department of Engineering Science at the University of Oxford, where he joined in fall 2021, and simultaneously holds a Research Scientist position at Meta AI's Fundamental AI Research (FAIR) lab, splitting time 50/50 between the two. He is the director of the Foerster Lab for AI Research (FLAIR) at Oxford. During his PhD at Oxford, he helped pioneer deep multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), with internships at Google Brain, OpenAI, and DeepMind. He was the lead organizer of the first Emergent Communication workshop at NeurIPS in 2017 and was awarded a prestigious CIFAR AI Chair in 2019. He received a Cooperative AI Foundation grant (GBP 166,370, 2021–2023) alongside Christian Schroeder de Witt to support the FLAIR lab, and was awarded a Horizon Europe Guarantee grant (£1,905,276, Jan 2024–Dec 2028) for 'Scalable Human-AI Coordination from First Principles.' In 2024, his lab received the 2023 Amazon Research Award ($70,000 cash + $50,000 AWS credit) for work on compute-only scaling of large language models. His research spans multi-agent RL, zero-shot coordination, cooperative AI, and emergent communication, making him one of the most prominent academic researchers in cooperative and multi-agent AI.
2068.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. His research focus on cooperative AI and human-AI coordination suggests awareness of alignment and safety concerns, but no direct policy positions are documented.

2069.notes: Jane Street is a global technology-driven quantitative trading and market-making firm founded in 2000, with over 3,500 employees across offices in New York, London, Hong Kong, Singapore, and Amsterdam. The firm generated $20.5 billion in net trading revenue and approximately $13 billion in net income in 2024, reflecting its scale as one of the most profitable proprietary trading firms in the world. Jane Street has become a significant investor in AI infrastructure and AI companies: it holds a major stake in Anthropic (whose surging valuation boosted Jane Street's Q3 2024 trading revenue by ~$830 million), participated in the Thinking Machines Lab fundraise, co-invested in Nscale's $2 billion Series C alongside Nvidia and Citadel in March 2026, and led Antithesis's $105 million Series A in December 2025. Most prominently, in April 2026, Jane Street committed approximately $6 billion to CoreWeave's AI cloud platform and made a $1 billion equity investment at $109/share, making it CoreWeave's fifth-largest shareholder. The firm applies machine learning extensively across its trading operations and uses large-scale compute as a core business input, making its AI infrastructure investments both strategic and operational. Jane Street's capital deployments and investment activity make it an increasingly consequential funder and customer in the AI ecosystem.
2069.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public policy positions found, but Jane Street's pattern of aggressive capital deployment into AI infrastructure, frontier AI labs, and compute without apparent concern for regulatory risk signals a preference for minimal regulatory friction. Its investments in Anthropic, CoreWeave, Nscale, and Thinking Machines Lab reflect an accelerationist capital posture consistent with light-touch regulatory views.

2070.notes: Jed McCaleb (born 1975, Fayetteville, Arkansas) is an American programmer, entrepreneur, and philanthropist with an estimated net worth of approximately $2.9–3.2 billion as of 2025, derived largely from XRP and XLM holdings. He is the creator of eDonkey2000 (2000), Mt. Gox (2010), co-founder of Ripple (2012), and co-founder and CTO of the Stellar Development Foundation (2014), a nonprofit blockchain network designed to improve global financial access. In 2020, he founded the Astera Institute, an AI-focused nonprofit dedicated to improving the scientific research process. In 2021, he founded aerospace company Vast Space, into which he has invested approximately $1 billion of his own funds to develop commercial space stations. McCaleb shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through large-scale philanthropy: in late 2023, he and partner Dr. Seemay Chou established the Navigation Fund with a $1.3 billion endowment focused on AI-enabled open science, farm animal welfare, criminal justice, climate change, and 'digital sentience.' The Navigation Fund notably purchased roughly $500 million in NVIDIA GPUs in 2023 to establish Voltage Park, a for-profit compute-leasing operation for AI projects, valued at $883 million in its 2023 IRS filing. He has also donated to MIRI (including $500,000 in XRP plus additional grants totaling over $773,000 as of 2021) and was announced as a donor to OpenAI in February 2018.
2070.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation found, but McCaleb's investment pattern—funding open science compute infrastructure (Voltage Park), supporting MIRI, donating to OpenAI, and endowing the Navigation Fund to accelerate 'bold scientific bets' that bypass 'protracted bureaucratic processes like multi-stage peer reviews'—strongly suggests a preference for minimizing bureaucratic friction and enabling rapid AI development with light regulatory oversight.

2071.notes: Jessica Rumbelow (also known as Jessica Cooper) is the CEO and co-founder of Leap Laboratories, an AI interpretability startup she founded in February 2023. Leap Labs builds a 'Discovery Engine' that uses deep learning and interpretability methods to accelerate scientific discovery by finding patterns in complex datasets — the company has co-published novel scientific findings in plant biology, immunology, and meteorology as of 2025. Rumbelow holds a PhD in model agnostic interpretability from the University of St Andrews and previously worked as a Research Scientist there applying deep learning to digital pathology. She is a MATS Autumn 2022 alumna, where she co-authored the influential 'SolidGoldMagikarp' paper on glitch tokens in LLMs with Matthew Watkins, which received significant media coverage including Vice News. Leap Laboratories received $195,000 in seed funding from the AI Risk Mitigation Fund and has positioned itself at the intersection of AI safety and scientific acceleration. Rumbelow has contributed to the 'Open Problems in Mechanistic Interpretability' literature and articulates a dual mission: slowing potentially dangerous broad-domain AI systems through interpretability-based red-teaming, while speeding up narrow-domain AI for scientific progress.
2071.regulatory_stance_detail: Rumbelow has explicitly stated that 'regulation is coming — let's use it,' predicting governments will regulate powerful models and advocating that such regulations be grounded in state-of-the-art interpretability work. She supports audit and bias-prevention regulation but frames it instrumentally — as an opportunity to ensure safety measures are technically rigorous rather than as a broad restrictive agenda.
2071.threat_models: Loss of control, Bias/discrimination, Existential risk

2072.notes: James Joseph Pallotta (born March 13, 1958) is an American billionaire investor and founder of Raptor Group, a private family office investment firm he established in 2009 after spinning out from Tudor Investment Corporation, where he had managed over $11 billion in assets as Vice Chairman. In January 2017, Pallotta committed $1 million to the Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence Fund, a $27 million initiative launched alongside Reid Hoffman, Omidyar Network, the Knight Foundation, and the Hewlett Foundation, channeled through MIT Media Lab and Harvard's Berkman Klein Center to apply humanities and social sciences to AI development. He serves on the Advisory Council of the MIT Media Lab and the External Advisory Committee for the Center for Brains, Minds and Machines at MIT, giving him ongoing institutional ties to AI research. Raptor Group invests across technology, fintech, healthcare, and consumer sectors, with notable holdings including Airbnb, Coupang, and Uber. Pallotta also served as President and Chairman of Italian football club AS Roma (2012–2020) and co-owner of the Boston Celtics (2002–2020). His AI ecosystem influence is primarily as a philanthropic funder of responsible AI governance research rather than a direct builder or regulator.
2072.regulatory_stance_detail: Pallotta's $1M contribution to the Ethics and Governance of AI Fund in 2017 signals support for structured, multidisciplinary governance of AI—applying humanities and social sciences to AI development—suggesting a moderate, governance-oriented stance rather than pure acceleration or heavy restriction. No explicit regulatory position statements found.

2073.notes: John Arnold (BA '95, Vanderbilt) is a billionaire philanthropist and co-founder of Arnold Ventures, a major philanthropic organization he leads with his wife Laura Arnold (Harvard BA, Yale JD). He is a former natural gas trader at Enron who became one of the most successful hedge fund managers before retiring to focus on philanthropy. In March 2025, Arnold and Laura committed $25 million to Vanderbilt University's newly established College of Connected Computing—the university's first new college in 40 years—to fund endowed chairs including the inaugural dean's chair and three faculty chairs, as well as computing education and research initiatives in AI, data science, and interdisciplinary computing. Arnold and Laura are Giving Pledge signatories and have given over $34.5 million to Vanderbilt overall, including the Arnold Family Scholarship, Arnold Scholars Program, and the Laura and John Arnold Fund supporting public policy and quantitative analytics programs. Arnold served on Vanderbilt's Board of Trust from 2010–2014 and was reelected in 2023. His funding of a computing college explicitly focused on AI and data science positions him as a meaningful funder in the AI education and research ecosystem.
2073.regulatory_stance_detail: No direct public statements on AI regulation are found in the available sources. Arnold Ventures broadly supports evidence-based policy reform across many domains, but no specific AI regulatory stance is documented in these sources.

2074.notes: The John S. and James L. Knight Foundation (Knight Foundation) is a major American philanthropic organization originally established to support communities where the Knight brothers published newspapers. It has evolved into a significant funder of journalism, free expression, arts, and technology policy research. In the AI ecosystem, Knight Foundation has made substantial grants including a $2 million contribution (matched by USC Marshall for a $4 million total) to USC Marshall's Neely Center for Ethical Leadership and Decision Making for purpose-driven AI research. It co-funded a $30 million initiative with Georgetown University to establish a nonpartisan institute helping policymakers navigate technology issues. It was also a founding funder of the MIT Media Lab's ethics/accountability research alongside Reid Hoffman and the Omidyar Network, contributing approximately $27 million initially. Knight Foundation has funded Fiona Scott Morton's economics research, the UW Tech Policy Lab, and numerous other AI governance and information ecosystem projects. The Foundation's AI-related grantmaking is guided internally by its Information & Society program, led by director A.J. D'Amico, focusing on how AI and social media affect democratic discourse and information sharing.
2074.regulatory_stance_detail: Knight Foundation consistently funds research on ethical, human-centered AI governance and supports nonpartisan policy institutions to help policymakers make informed decisions about AI. A.J. D'Amico has emphasized the importance of transparency in AI development and governance. The Foundation's funding pattern — supporting academic research, policy institutes, and ethics centers — reflects a moderate stance favoring evidence-based, informed regulation rather than either unfettered acceleration or heavy-handed restriction.

2075.notes: The John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation, commonly known as the Guggenheim Foundation, is a prestigious American philanthropic organization that has awarded fellowships annually since 1925 to artists, scholars, and scientists selected on the basis of distinguished past achievement and exceptional promise for future accomplishment. Fellows are selected from a highly competitive applicant pool — in 2001, for example, 183 fellows were chosen from over 2,700 applicants, with awards totaling more than $6.5 million. The foundation operates two fellowship competitions: one for US and Canadian applicants, and one for Latin American and Caribbean applicants. Fellowships average over $34,000 and provide recipients 6–12 months of freedom to pursue their work. The foundation shapes the AI ecosystem indirectly by funding foundational researchers whose work underpins cognitive science and AI-relevant disciplines — notable fellows include John A. Bargh (social psychology, 2001) and Alison Gopnik (developmental psychology and AI/cognition research, 2020), whose work on children's learning has directly influenced machine learning and AI research. The foundation's broad support for social scientists, cognitive scientists, and interdisciplinary researchers means it has historically seeded intellectual groundwork relevant to AI development, safety, and ethics.
2075.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public positions on AI regulation have been identified in the available sources. As a philanthropy focused on individual fellowship awards across disciplines, the foundation does not publicly advocate for specific AI regulatory frameworks.

2077.notes: Joshua Clymer is an independent researcher working on AI safety and alignment. He previously worked on AI safety field-building and communications before transitioning to technical AI safety research. In 2023-2024, he received a $1,500 grant from the Long-Term Future Fund to rent A100 GPUs for experiments on instruction-following generalization, representing his first technical AI safety project. His research introduced a benchmark examining whether models trained within a specific distribution can generalize correctly out of distribution, using a 'sandwiching' methodology where a non-expert trains the model but an expert evaluates it. The published paper and benchmark found that reward models do not inherently learn to evaluate 'instruction-following' and instead favor personas resembling internet text — meaning models trained on easy tasks do not naturally generalize instruction-following to hard tasks. He also writes on Substack about extreme AI risks, including topics like planning for scenarios involving advanced AI systems. His work is discussed on the Alignment Forum and LessWrong.
2077.regulatory_stance_detail: Clymer writes about 'Planning for Extreme AI Risks' on Substack and his research focuses on alignment failures and out-of-distribution generalization problems, suggesting concern that AI development needs safety infrastructure before scaling. His work implies support for slowing or carefully managing AI development until alignment is better understood.

2079.notes: Julie Carson is identified as an individual donor to Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a DC-based 501c4 nonprofit AI policy organization founded in 2023. She is listed alongside her spouse Brad Carson, the former U.S. Congressman and President of ARI, as a named individual supporter of the organization. ARI focuses on bipartisan AI safety legislation, catastrophic risk prevention, compute governance, and responsible AI development standards. The organization has achieved notable policy wins including supporting passage of the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025) and leading the campaign that resulted in a 99-1 Senate vote to strip a 10-year moratorium on state AI laws from the reconciliation bill. ARI is also supported by institutional funders including Inclusive Abundance Action, Omidyar Network, Open Philanthropy, and Coefficient Giving. Julie Carson's influence on the AI ecosystem is primarily through philanthropic support of ARI rather than through direct policy or research roles. Very limited public information is available about her independently of her association with Brad Carson and ARI.
2079.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from funding ARI, which advocates for a thoughtful governance framework for AI that protects the public from harm while fostering innovation, takes a bipartisan approach, and focuses on catastrophic risk prevention and compute governance without opposing AI development broadly.

2080.notes: Jump Capital is a thesis-led, sector-focused, and operating-centric venture capital firm specializing in Seed and Series A investments. The firm focuses on data-driven technology companies in sectors including FinTech, B2B SaaS, IT/Data Infrastructure, and Media. Jump Capital shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its investment and funding activities, including leading Workera's $23.5 million Series B round in March 2023, a skills intelligence platform that uses AI-powered assessments and personalized learning paths for enterprise talent development. The firm was founded by Mike McMahon, who serves as co-founder and managing partner and was quoted directly in Workera's funding announcement emphasizing the importance of high-fidelity skills data over traditional inference-based approaches. Jump Capital maintains offices in both Chicago and New York and positions itself as providing institutional-level resources and advocacy for the entrepreneurs it backs. The firm's investment in Workera signals interest in AI-powered workforce and upskilling technology as a sector thesis. Its portfolio approach to data-driven technology companies places it as an active participant in shaping how AI tools are deployed in enterprise and workforce contexts.
2080.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy positions are stated, but Jump Capital's investment thesis focuses on accelerating data-driven and AI-powered technology companies with no stated regulatory concerns. Their backing of AI-enabled workforce platforms suggests a preference for market-driven innovation over regulatory intervention. Stance is inferred from investment actions rather than explicit statements.

2081.notes: Jump Trading Group is a leading data and research-driven quantitative trading firm founded in 1999, headquartered in Chicago with 13 offices across 8 countries and approximately 1,396 employees. The firm uses AI and machine learning as core components of its trading operations, building advanced models, low-latency infrastructure, custom foundation models, and LLM agents integrated across its tools and data. In 2025, Jump Trading became one of the first financial services firms to deploy the NVIDIA Vera Rubin Platform in a deep learning financial research data center expansion, signaling significant investment in AI compute infrastructure. Jump Trading actively funds external AI and neuroscience research: in 2025 it established Jump Trading Fellowships at the University of Chicago, offering PhD candidates full tuition, fees, and ~$50,000 stipends to advance scientific research across six fields. In 2026, it partnered with UCL's Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit to fund four fully supported PhD scholarships beginning autumn 2026, covering tuition, stipend, travel, and visa costs, to support research at the intersection of neuroscience, machine learning, and AI. The firm's CTO Alex Davies has publicly articulated Jump Trading's commitment to advancing transformative research at the neuroscience-AI interface. Jump Trading shapes the AI ecosystem through both its internal deployment of frontier AI/ML systems in high-stakes trading environments and its philanthropic funding of academic AI and computational neuroscience research.
2081.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in available sources. Jump Trading's actions — deploying cutting-edge AI infrastructure and funding academic AI research — suggest a pro-development orientation, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

2082.notes: Justin Dollman is Co-Director of the Cambridge AI Safety Hub and a community organizer focused on AI safety and model evaluations. He received a $3,000 rapid grant from BlueDot Impact to lead a weekly AI Safety Evals reading group, which grew to approximately 120 attendees per week with ambitions to scale to 300. He organizes the AI Safety Evals Paper Reading Club under BlueDot Impact's events calendar alongside co-organizers Joshua Landes, Mark Keavney, and Matthew Broerman. The reading group has included an author series format, hosting researchers to present papers such as 'Evidence for Limited Metacognition in LLMs.' His GitHub profile describes him as someone who 'tries to do political science' and often ends up doing data analysis, and he maintains a personal site featuring a NYT AI tracker and CV. Through his organizing work, he plays a connector and community-building role in the AI safety talent pipeline, helping people learn about and work on model evaluations.
2082.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found in sources. His work focuses on AI safety community-building and model evaluations rather than policy advocacy, making his regulatory stance unclear.

2083.notes: KAIST (Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology) is South Korea's premier public research university, specializing in science, engineering, and technology. The institution has a Graduate School of AI, reflecting its central role in AI research and education in South Korea. KAIST alumni have made significant contributions to global AI research, including Professor Kyunghyun Cho (BS 2009, School of Computing), who became a prominent AI researcher at NYU known for foundational work on neural machine translation and attention mechanisms. KAIST received scholarship endowments from Cho in 2019 ($3,000 to support female AI PhD students attending ICLR) and again in 2021 (100 million KRW Lim Mi-Sook Scholarship for female computer scientists, funded from his Samsung Ho-Am Prize money). The university has actively engaged with diversity and inclusion in AI/STEM fields. As a leading Asian academic institution producing influential AI researchers, KAIST shapes the global AI ecosystem through research output, talent development, and international collaborations.
2083.regulatory_stance_detail: No evidence in the available sources about KAIST's institutional stance on AI regulation.

2084.notes: Capital K is a ~$200 million (SAR 750 million) deep tech venture capital fund launched in August 2023 by Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman as part of KAUST's 'Accelerating Impact' strategy. It is based in Silicon Valley and operates independently of KAUST, though it is funded by the university. The fund aims to invest $2–5 million into 20–25 early-stage deep tech projects over five to ten years, targeting companies from KAUST, Saudi Arabia, and internationally that contribute to the Kingdom's economic development and job creation goals under Vision 2030. Capital K is one of three major initiatives in KAUST's new strategy, alongside the National Transformation Institute for Applied Research (NTI) and restructured research centers. It prioritizes sectors including health and wellness, sustainable environment, energy, and 'economies of the future,' which explicitly encompass AI and advanced technology. The fund is designed to help KAUST-developed IP reach the market and to position Saudi Arabia as a hub for foreign investment and deep tech, including AI-related startups. Its Silicon Valley base and focus on global deep tech investment place it directly within the AI funding ecosystem.
2084.regulatory_stance_detail: Capital K and the broader KAUST strategy are oriented toward accelerating commercialization and deep tech investment with no stated regulatory constraints. The fund is designed to operate independently and flexibly, focused on growth and economic diversification rather than imposing governance requirements on AI development.
2084.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

2086.notes: The Kavli Foundation is a philanthropic organization dedicated to advancing scientific research, particularly in neuroscience, nanoscience, astrophysics, and theoretical physics. In the AI ecosystem, it funds the 2026 AI-for-Science Postdoctoral Fellowship in partnership with FutureHouse, supporting independent, AI-enabled research projects in neuroscience. The foundation has also funded exploration of how AI is integrated into basic research through its Science and Society Program, and has established Kavli Centers for Ethics, Science, and the Public at UC Berkeley and Cambridge University to address ethical and societal implications of scientific discovery, including AI. It co-sponsors the Kavli-Grass Fellowship with the Grass Foundation for early-career neuroscience researchers at the Marine Biological Laboratory at Woods Hole. The foundation funds NeuroData Discovery Awards (up to $100k for group projects) supporting innovative computational analyses of neuroscience datasets, including AI-driven approaches. Its Science and Society Program Director has publicly articulated the foundation's nuanced approach to AI philanthropy—supporting proactive engagement with AI's societal implications rather than reactive mitigation alone. The Kavli Foundation operates a network of Kavli Institutes at leading research universities globally.
2086.regulatory_stance_detail: The Kavli Foundation's Science and Society Program explicitly supports proactive engagement with ethical and societal implications of AI, investing in ethics centers and civic science fellows rather than purely reactive regulation. Their stated philosophy is to bring scientists, social scientists, philosophers, and civic leaders together early in the discovery process to consider implications—suggesting support for thoughtful governance structures rather than either acceleration or heavy restriction.

2088.notes: Kearny Jackson is an early-stage venture capital firm founded in 2018 by Sunil Chhaya and Sriram Krishnan, named after the San Francisco intersection where the two co-founders often met. The firm focuses on pre-seed and seed investments in B2B SaaS, developer tools, and fintech infrastructure, writing $1.5M checks for 6-10% ownership stakes. The firm raised a $14M second fund in 2023 and a $65M third fund in July 2024, attracting high-profile limited partners including Sequoia Capital (with a 5x increased commitment in Fund III), Marc Andreessen personally, Bain Capital Ventures, Menlo Ventures, Stepstone, and multiple endowments and pension funds. Sriram Krishnan brings operator experience from companies including Spotify and Tinder, while Sunil Chhaya brings institutional VC experience from backing companies such as Gong, New Relic, and Wavefront from their earliest stages. As of early 2026, Kearny Jackson has made 33 investments, with portfolio companies including notable names like Figma, Calm, and Sorare, and co-investors including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, SV Angel, and others. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem by funding early-stage technology companies, including those building AI-adjacent infrastructure, developer tools, and SaaS platforms, and by connecting founders to tier-one follow-on capital through its LP network.
2088.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found. Inferred from the firm's close alignment with Marc Andreessen and Sequoia Capital—both known advocates of minimal AI regulation and accelerationist positions—and its focus on funding early-stage technology companies without publicly expressed regulatory concerns.

2089.notes: Kim Jungsik (1929–2019) was the founder and chairman of Daeduck Electronics, one of Korea's pioneering printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturers, which grew to roughly 2,000 employees and 96 billion won in annual revenue. He founded the company in 1965 (some sources say 1972) and built it into a key supplier to Samsung Electronics. In February 2019, while hospitalized due to advanced age, he made a landmark 50 billion won (~$37.5M USD) donation to his alma mater Seoul National University to establish an interdisciplinary AI research center modeled on MIT's new College of Computing — reportedly inspired by a New Year's Day news article about Stephen Schwarzman's 300 billion won donation to MIT. His total cumulative donations to SNU reached 65.7 billion won, the largest ever from a single individual to that institution. In 1991, he founded the Haedong Science and Culture Foundation, which funded 282 science and engineering researchers, provided scholarships to approximately 280 students, built libraries at over 20 engineering colleges nationwide, and co-published 70 academic books with the National Academy of Engineering of Korea. He passed away on April 11, 2019, at age 90, and was posthumously recognized as a science and technology meritorious person by the Korean government.
2089.regulatory_stance_detail: No direct statements on AI regulation were found in sources. His actions suggest strong support for accelerating AI research and infrastructure investment in Korea, driven by competitive concern about falling behind the U.S. and Japan, but he expressed no explicit regulatory position.

2090.notes: Kitware, Inc. is a research and software development company founded in 1998, widely recognized for its contributions to open-source software including the Visualization Toolkit (VTK), Insight Segmentation and Registration Toolkit (ITK), CMake, and ParaView. The company has a strong focus on computer vision, medical imaging, scientific visualization, and AI/ML research, and has been a major recipient of DARPA and AFRL funding. In 2010, Kitware received an $11 million DARPA contract to lead Phase II of the VIRAT (Video and Image Retrieval and Analysis Tool) program, assembling a large team including Honeywell, Raytheon BBN, BAE Systems, General Dynamics, Lockheed Martin, and numerous top research universities.
