The company also received DARPA funding for the PerSEAS program for wide-area motion imagery analysis, with total DARPA computer vision contract wins exceeding $30 million by around 2011. Kitware's computer vision team, which grew to over 30 members, has worked on large-scale behavior recognition, object detection and tracking, activity recognition, and anomaly detection. Notable alumni include Matt Turek, who led Kitware's computer vision team before joining DARPA in 2018 as a Program Manager in the Information Innovation Office (I2O), where he managed programs including Explainable AI (XAI), Media Forensics (MediFor), Semantic Forensics (SemaFor), and Machine Common Sense (MCS). Kitware's partnerships extend to NIH, U.S. National Labs, Harvard, MIT, and Stanford, as well as commercial sectors including medical, pharmaceutical, and engineering simulation.
2090.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. Kitware's work primarily involves building government-contracted AI and computer vision systems for DARPA and AFRL, suggesting alignment with U.S. national security priorities, but no formal regulatory stance is articulated.

2093.notes: Laidir Foundation is a private, non-operating philanthropic foundation based in Seattle, WA (EIN: 47-2519767), tax-exempt since April 2015 and directed by Brian Lonergan (President & Treasurer) and Matthew McBay (Vice President). With approximately $81M in assets as of 2024, it distributed roughly $4M annually across 22+ grantees. The foundation is a significant funder in the effective altruism and AI safety ecosystem: in 2023 it gave $500,000 to the AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund (via Vanguard Charitable), $300,000 to The Future Society to improve AI governance, $540,000 to Effective Ventures Foundation USA (including Longview Philanthropy and 80,000 Hours support), and $150,000 to the Effective Institutions Project. In 2024, it gave $390,000 to the Effective Institutions Project, $500,000 to Princeton University for the Craig Samuel Falls AI Policy Fellowship, and continued multi-year support for 80,000 Hours (totaling £400,000–£1M across years including 2024, 2022, 2019, 2018, 2017, and pre-2017). Beyond AI, it also funds housing policy (Sightline Institute), global poverty (Innovations for Poverty Action), and congressional reform (Rebuild Congress Initiative/Issue One). The foundation only makes contributions to preselected organizations and does not accept unsolicited applications.
2093.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from grant portfolio: Laidir funds AI governance work at The Future Society ('improve AI governance'), AI safety research (AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund), AI policy fellowships (Princeton/Craig Samuel Falls AI Policy Fellowship), and institutions focused on effective policy (Effective Institutions Project, Rebuild Congress Initiative). This pattern suggests support for thoughtful AI governance and institutional capacity-building rather than either acceleration or restrictive precautionary stances.
2093.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2094.notes: Lambda (also known as Lambda Labs) is an AI cloud infrastructure company headquartered in San Jose, California, founded by CEO Stephen Balaban. The company provides high-performance GPU cloud computing services — including Nvidia GPU clusters — to AI developers, researchers, and Fortune 500 companies such as Apple, Intel, Microsoft, and Tencent. In February 2025, Lambda raised a $480 million Series D round co-led by Andra Capital and SGW (the family office of early Google investor Scott Hassan), bringing total equity raised to $863 million, with a reported post-money valuation of $2.5 billion. The round included notable investors such as Nvidia, ARK Invest, In-Q-Tel (the CIA's investment arm), Andrej Karpathy, G Squared, Pegatron, Supermicro, Wistron, and Wiwynn. Lambda operates GPU cloud infrastructure out of colocation data centers in San Francisco, CA and Allen, TX, and has also partnered with Cologix to launch Nvidia HGX B200 clusters in Columbus, Ohio. The company positions itself as a hyperscaler-alternative cloud platform specifically tailored to AI developers for training, fine-tuning, and deploying models, and participates in Nvidia's Lepton AI platform connecting GPU cloud providers.
2094.regulatory_stance_detail: Lambda's public messaging emphasizes rapid expansion of AI compute infrastructure and CEO Stephen Balaban has articulated an ambitious vision of 'one person, one GPU' as a future goal, reflecting a strong pro-acceleration posture. No public statements supporting AI regulation have been found; the company's strategic focus is on scaling infrastructure as fast as possible.

2095.notes: Land Berlin (the State of Berlin) is one of Germany's 16 federal states (Bundesländer), functioning as both a city-state and a full state government with its own Senate. It is a significant funder of the Weizenbaum Institute for the Networked Society, a major German research institute focused on the societal implications of digitalization and AI, co-funded with the federal government and other Berlin-area universities. The Berlin Senate, led by the Governing Mayor and various senators, allocates substantial public funds to universities and research institutions through multi-year Hochschulverträge (university contracts), with approximately 6.8 billion euros committed to Berlin universities for 2025–2028. The Senator for Science, Health and Care, Dr. Ina Czyborra, oversees these funding decisions and negotiations. Berlin's investment in research infrastructure, including the Weizenbaum Institute, directly shapes Germany's AI policy research ecosystem by funding critical independent scholarship on AI governance, digital society, and technology regulation. The state also participates in Bund-Länder (federal-state) joint science governance through bodies like the Gemeinsame Wissenschaftskonferenz (GWK). As a co-funder of the Weizenbaum Institute, Land Berlin plays a meaningful role in enabling independent AI and digital policy research in the EU context.
2095.regulatory_stance_detail: Land Berlin's funding of the Weizenbaum Institute — an institute explicitly focused on researching the societal and regulatory dimensions of digitalization and AI — signals support for evidence-based, research-informed AI governance rather than either laissez-faire or precautionary extremes. The state's science policy emphasizes strengthening research capacity and applying independent academic analysis to emerging technology challenges, consistent with a moderate, balanced regulatory approach.

2096.notes: Land Brandenburg is one of Germany's 16 federal states (Länder), governed by a state government (Landesregierung) and parliament (Landtag Brandenburg), currently led by Minister-President Dietmar Woidke (SPD). As a co-funder of the Weizenbaum Institute for the Networked Society — Germany's leading interdisciplinary internet and AI research institute — Brandenburg shapes the AI policy ecosystem through public research funding. The state has been active in higher education reform, passing legislation in 2025-2026 to strengthen the competitiveness of Brandenburg's universities, including fast-tracking professorial appointments and supporting the Hasso-Plattner-Institut (HPI) in Potsdam as a leading digital sciences institute in Europe, with a Letter of Intent signed in June 2025 between the state, the University of Potsdam, and the Hasso Plattner Foundation. In December 2025, the Landtag passed the 'Zukunftspaket Brandenburg' special fund law providing approximately 3 billion euros for infrastructure investment. The state's Committee on Science, Research, and Culture (Ausschuss für Wissenschaft, Forschung und Kultur) regularly convenes on topics related to research resilience, digital universities, and higher education. Brandenburg's co-funding of the Weizenbaum Institute directly supports critical AI and internet policy research that influences national and EU-level discourse.
2096.regulatory_stance_detail: Brandenburg's actions suggest a balanced approach: it funds independent research institutions like the Weizenbaum Institute that critically examine AI and digital governance, while simultaneously deregulating higher education to accelerate research capacity (e.g., removing bureaucratic hurdles in professor appointments). It invests in digital sciences infrastructure without explicit calls for either heavy AI regulation or laissez-faire approaches.

2097.notes: Langsikt - Centre for Long-Term Policy is an independent, non-profit Norwegian think tank founded to promote more long-term policymaking on major global challenges including artificial intelligence, pandemics, and extreme poverty. The organization bridges research and policy development to help ensure societal resources are used where they create the most benefit. Langsikt receives philanthropic funding from organizations such as the Astralis Foundation, which specifically supported their work producing evidence-based recommendations on beneficial AI for Norwegian policymakers. Their AI-related policy work includes engaging with Norwegian government processes on AI governance, and they actively contribute to public debate through notes, consultation inputs, opinion pieces, and podcasts. Beyond AI, Langsikt works on long-term institutional reform (e.g., proposals for a 'Future Committee' in Parliament, a future ombudsperson, and an independent carbon pricing committee), Norwegian foreign aid policy, and pandemic preparedness. General Manager Eirik Mofoss and senior advisors including Sondre Hansmark and Aksel Braanen Sterri are among their key personnel engaging with policymakers and the public. Langsikt has co-organized events such as the Future Panel (Framtidspanelet), a citizens' assembly on Norway's long-term priorities, in partnership with Save the Children, WWF, and others.
2097.regulatory_stance_detail: Langsikt advocates for evidence-based, long-term policy on AI, recommending that Norwegian policymakers develop governance frameworks for beneficial AI. Their approach is balanced—neither calling for acceleration nor broad moratoriums—but emphasizing systematic institutional responses to emerging technology risks, consistent with a moderate, targeted regulatory posture.
2097.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2098.notes: Leidos (NYSE: LDOS) is a large defense, intelligence, and technology contractor headquartered in Reston, Virginia, with approximately 48,000 global employees and reported annual revenues of approximately $16.7 billion for fiscal year ended January 3, 2025. The company serves both government and commercial customers across defense, intelligence, civil, and health sectors, describing itself as 'an industry and technology leader serving government and commercial customers with smarter, more efficient digital and mission innovations.' In April 2025, Leidos committed $10 million to a five-year partnership with the University of Pittsburgh's Computational Pathology and AI Center of Excellence (CPACE) to develop AI-powered tools for disease detection, including cancer and heart disease, with a focus on underserved communities including veterans. This builds on a 17-year relationship between Leidos and Pitt spanning 355 projects across 40 academic departments totaling over $37 million in collaborative work prior to this latest investment. The company's Health and Civil Sector, led by President Liz Porter, is actively developing and commercializing AI models for healthcare diagnostics, citing that their AI models can predict cardiovascular disease with nearly 90% accuracy. Leidos shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a major technology builder and funder, deploying AI solutions across government and health sectors and investing in academic AI research infrastructure.
2098.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulatory stance are present in the available sources. Leidos' actions suggest a commercially oriented approach focused on developing and deploying AI solutions in partnership with government and academia, but no formal policy positions on regulation are discernible from the evidence.

2099.notes: Lemni Technologies (trading as Lemni) is an Amsterdam-based AI startup founded in 2024 by Alex Buis and Krijn Rijshouwer. The company builds a plug-and-play, no-code environment for deploying custom AI agents designed to deliver personalized customer interactions at scale for businesses. In March 2025, Lemni raised $3.5M in pre-seed/seed funding led by Sequoia Capital (via partner Julien Bek), with participation from Designer Fund, Interface Capital, Festina VC, and notable angel investors including Frank Slootman, Scott Belsky, Arthur Mensch (CEO of Mistral AI), Christian Reber, Koen Bok, Jorn van Dijk, Niklas Jansen, and Mario Götze. The platform provides tools, guardrails, and business intelligence to help companies deliver AI-driven customer engagement across multiple channels. Its primary competitors include HubSpot, Dialpad, and LivePerson. Lemni shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying AI agent infrastructure for business customer interaction at scale.
2099.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation have been identified from Lemni Technologies. The company's focus on providing 'guardrails' within its platform suggests some awareness of responsible deployment, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

2100.notes: The Leverhulme Trust is a major UK philanthropic foundation established in 1925 under the will of William Hesketh Lever, providing grants for research, scholarships, and education across all academic disciplines. In the AI ecosystem, the Trust made a landmark £10 million grant to the University of Cambridge in 2015 to establish the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (LCFI), one of the most prominent academic AI safety and governance research centers in the world. The LCFI, launched officially in October 2016, brings together computer scientists, philosophers, and social scientists from Cambridge, Oxford, Imperial College London, and UC Berkeley to study the long-term implications of artificial intelligence. The Trust's Leverhulme Research Centres scheme awards up to £10 million over 10 years to UK universities for bold, interdisciplinary, blue-skies research, explicitly calling for 'bold, disruptive thinking capable of creating a step-change in understanding.' As of 2024, the Trust has invested approximately £100 million across ten Leverhulme Research Centres, with further £30 million announced for three new centres including the Leverhulme Centre for Humanity and Space at the University of Leicester. The Trust's director Anna Vignoles has noted the scheme's focus on arts, humanities and social sciences as drivers of transformative interdisciplinary research. By funding LCFI, the Trust has played a foundational role in institutionalizing AI safety and ethics research in academia globally.
2100.regulatory_stance_detail: The Leverhulme Trust funded the LCFI explicitly in response to concerns about risks posed by advanced AI, supporting research into 'responsible development of technology,' autonomous weapons, and existential risk. The Trust's call emphasized 'bold, disruptive thinking' to address challenges before they materialize, reflecting a precautionary orientation toward ensuring AI governance frameworks are developed proactively.
2100.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons

2102.notes: Lightspeed Venture Partners is a major global venture capital firm with approximately 25 years of history that announced a record $9 billion fundraise in December 2024, the largest in firm history. The firm is a significant backer of AI companies across the risk spectrum, having led Mistral AI's €105 million seed round in June 2023 (Europe's largest seed round at the time), invested in Safe Superintelligence Inc.'s $1 billion funding round in September 2024 (at a $5 billion valuation), and participated in Goodfire's $7 million seed round for AI interpretability research. The firm also made an early investment in Midjourney, reportedly leading a $150 million Series B at a $10 billion valuation. Lightspeed's AI portfolio spans foundational model labs (Mistral, SSI), AI safety/interpretability (Goodfire), and AI applications, as well as defense-adjacent autonomous systems companies like Orqa, Helsing, and Anduril. The breadth of investments—from frontier labs to safety research to defense AI—makes Lightspeed one of the most influential capital allocators shaping the global AI ecosystem.
2102.regulatory_stance_detail: Lightspeed's portfolio reflects mixed regulatory signals: they fund frontier AI labs like Mistral and SSI that push rapid capability development, but also fund AI safety/interpretability work at Goodfire. An individual partner-linked contribution to PauseAI hints at internal heterogeneity. No firm-level public regulatory stance has been stated.
2102.threat_models: Existential risk, National security, Loss of control

2103.notes: Likith Govindaiah is a quantitative trader at Jane Street (since 2017), where he previously interned in 2016, holding a Bachelor's degree in Mathematics from Princeton University. He is an EA-aligned philanthropist who made a $400,000 DAF donation to Epoch AI in January 2025, providing general support to the AI research and forecasting organization. He has been active on the EA Forum since December 2021, engaging with questions about effective career paths, grant meta-analyses, and pandemic risk topics. He writes a personal Substack ('To First Order') where he discusses topics including philanthropic opportunities, news consumption strategy, and travel; in one post he explicitly frames his time as being split between improving financial market effectiveness and 'seeking out investment/philanthropic opportunities in AI capabilities/safety + global health/poverty.' He has also promoted an Indian NGO (Pratibha Poshak) focused on identifying and nurturing elite talent in Karnataka. His significant donation to Epoch AI places him as a meaningful individual funder in the AI safety/research ecosystem.
2103.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance found. His philanthropic focus on AI capabilities and safety research via Epoch AI suggests he cares about AI governance, but no specific regulatory positions have been publicly stated.

2104.notes: Liquid Intelligent Technologies is Africa's largest independent digital infrastructure provider, operating as a subsidiary of Cassava Technologies and providing fiber, cloud, cybersecurity, and AI-enabled services across more than 25 African countries. The company has built a network of 115,000 kilometers of fiber cable and is positioning itself at the intersection of connectivity and artificial intelligence. In February 2026, Liquid completed an $855 million recapitalization and debt refinancing package anchored by a $195 million equity injection from parent Cassava Technologies, along with $410 million in new credit facilities from commercial and development finance institutions. In April 2026, Liquid closed a further $660 million refinancing including a $300 million Eurobond listed on Euronext Dublin that was oversubscribed 2.5 times, signaling strong institutional confidence in African digital infrastructure. The company is part of Cassava Technologies' 'OneCassava' integrated ecosystem which explicitly includes AI, cloud, data centres, payments, and broadband connectivity. Cassava's strategy includes building an 'AI factory in Africa' to give African businesses local AI infrastructure, with Liquid serving as the foundational connectivity and compute layer. The company's services span 94 countries and it works alongside Cassava business units including Cassava AI, Liquid C2, Africa Data Centres, and Sasai Fintech.
2104.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. The company's focus appears growth-oriented and commercially driven, emphasizing accelerating Africa's digital transformation and AI adoption, which may suggest a light-touch or accelerationist preference, but this is not explicitly stated.

2105.notes: Lisa Thiergart is a computer scientist with an ML research specialization who has become a significant figure in AI technical governance and security. She co-founded the SL5 Task Force at the Institute for Security and Technology (IST), which aims to develop Security Level 5 standards for frontier AI and create the optionality for US labs to deploy SL5 within 1–3 months of choosing to. Prior to the SL5 Task Force, she spent approximately two years at the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI), first joining as Research Manager in summer 2023 to help execute MIRI's strategic pivot, then founding and leading MIRI's Technical Governance Team in February 2024, which she led until mid-2025. At MIRI, her team researched verification mechanisms, international AI coordination, limitations of model evaluations, and AI forecasting, while advising policymakers and producing technical reports with a focus on governance challenges near ASI. She is also a Manifund regrantor for 2025 with a $100,000 budget, funding neglected work in AI security (SL5-relevant research, hardware security) and technical AI governance (flexHEGs, compute governance, export controls). She holds academic affiliations with Georgia Tech, TUM, CDTM, and UCL, and is a Brains Fellow.
2105.regulatory_stance_detail: Thiergart's work is centered on developing and implementing stringent security standards (Security Level 5) for frontier AI labs, research into verification mechanisms, and input into regulatory bodies such as NIST/US AISI, EU, and UN. Her focus on 'long-term solutions' that remain functional as AI approaches ASI, and her explicit concern with limitations of existing proposals like Responsible Scaling Policies, indicates a view that current governance frameworks are insufficient and more robust mandatory requirements are needed.
2105.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Cybersecurity

2106.notes: Look AI Ventures (LAIV) is the first venture capital fund in the Czech Republic dedicated exclusively to early-stage AI startups, founded in 2022 in Prague and built on the foundation of AI Startup Incubator (AISI), which has operated since 2017. The fund targets pre-seed and seed stage investments with ticket sizes up to €250K with follow-on potential up to €1M, and aims to raise a total of €20M for its first fund. LAIV is industry-agnostic but primarily focuses on EU-based AI startups, particularly in Czech Republic and the UK, while also maintaining a geography-agnostic global mandate. As of early 2026, the fund has made approximately 17-19 investments, including Electric Sheep (AI VFX startup, August 2023 pre-seed round of $500K), and Secjur (AI-driven compliance technology, November 2024), and Surveily (Series A, March 2026). The team comprises approximately 11 people including 6 partners, with offices in Prague and presence in Ukraine. LAIV provides not just capital but also technical support and governance, assigning tech and business resources to portfolio founding teams. The fund co-invests frequently with firms such as Spatial Capital (US), Dasein Capital (US), and Jet Investment.
2106.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory position statements found in sources. As an early-stage AI-focused VC actively investing in and promoting AI startups globally, LAIV's posture is inferred as favoring a light-touch regulatory environment that enables AI innovation and startup growth. Their mission to 'nurture the AI startup ecosystem' and focus on 'disruptive AI solutions' suggests preference for enabling rather than restricting frameworks.

2107.notes: The Lord Foundation of California is a 501(c)(3) California nonprofit public benefit corporation established in 1977 as a supporting organization of the University of Southern California (USC). Its charitable purpose is to support USC through grants to research and teaching programs. The foundation was endowed through a unique estate plan created by entrepreneur Thomas Lord, whose father Hugh Lord founded the LORD Corporation in 1924. In 2019, the sale of LORD Corporation to Parker Hannifin Corporation for approximately $3.675 billion triggered a distribution of over $1 billion to four Lord foundations; the California foundation received approximately $260 million, which it distributed to USC beginning in 2020. This transformative gift, one of the largest in higher education history, has directly shaped USC's computing and AI ecosystem: it funded the Thomas Lord Department of Computer Science naming gift, the creation of the USC School of Advanced Computing (launched 2024), and the USC Institute on Ethics & Trust in Computing. The foundation also provided a 50% match for a $5 million endowed chair gift at the School of Advanced Computing as recently as December 2025, indicating continued (if winding down) philanthropic activity. Following its landmark distribution to USC, the foundation made the decision to sunset (spend-down), consistent with Thomas Lord's philosophy that private institutions — rather than government — should solve social and economic problems. Since 1980, it had also funded programs including Girls Who Code, the Space Engineering Research Center at USC Viterbi, and USC Marshall business programs.
2107.regulatory_stance_detail: The foundation's funding of the USC Institute on Ethics & Trust in Computing suggests implicit support for ethical governance frameworks around AI, but no explicit regulatory stance has been publicly stated. Thomas Lord's founding philosophy emphasized private institutions over government as problem-solvers, which could suggest a preference for industry/academic self-governance over government regulation.

2108.notes: Los Alamos National Laboratory (LANL) is one of 17 U.S. Department of Energy national laboratories, overseen specifically by the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA). Founded during the Manhattan Project, it is one of the world's foremost research institutions with an expected budget approaching $5 billion as of FY2025, more than double its 2019 level. LANL actively funds and conducts advanced AI and computing research: in 2024 it awarded the University of Michigan a five-year, $15 million grant to develop AI, sophisticated modeling, bioinformatics, genomics, and quantum sciences. In 2022, the DOE selected LANL to lead a $9.25 million SciDAC collaborative project on nuclear energy materials modeling. LANL plays a principal role in the DOE's Quantum Science Center, which received renewed funding of $125 million over five years for quantum-accelerated high-performance computing. The lab applies AI and advanced computing to challenges including turbulent plasma modeling for fusion energy, nuclear warhead pit production, and legacy waste cleanup. Its dual mission—national security (nuclear weapons modernization) and open science—makes it a uniquely influential node in the U.S. AI and scientific computing ecosystem.
2108.regulatory_stance_detail: As a federal national laboratory operating under DOE/NNSA, LANL does not articulate independent regulatory stances on AI policy. Its research posture—funding AI development across universities and leading large-scale computing programs—suggests an orientation toward accelerating AI capabilities in the national interest, but formal policy positions are set by DOE and NNSA leadership rather than the laboratory itself.

2109.notes: Loti AI is a Seattle-based technology company founded in 2022 by Luke Arrigoni (CEO), Rebekah Arrigoni, and Hirak Chhatbar, specializing in likeness protection technology using facial recognition, voice recognition, and deepfake detection. The company builds AI-powered tools to scan the internet for unauthorized use of individuals' likenesses and issue takedowns, serving clients ranging from celebrities and influencers to everyday consumers. In April 2025, Loti AI closed a $16.2M Series A funding round led by Khosla Ventures with participation from FUSE, Bling Capital, and Ensemble VC, following a $6.65M seed round in 2024 that included Alpha Edison and K5 Tokyo Black. The company has established strategic partnerships with major talent agencies WME and CAA, as well as Dolphin Entertainment and its subsidiaries, positioning itself as infrastructure for identity protection in the generative AI era. Loti recently launched a free consumer product to expand likeness protection beyond high-profile clients to the general public. Ranked No. 156 on GeekWire's top Pacific Northwest startups list, the company operates at the intersection of AI safety, digital rights, and trust infrastructure. Its platform shapes the AI ecosystem by providing critical guardrails against deepfake misuse and unauthorized synthetic likeness generation.
2109.regulatory_stance_detail: Loti AI's CEO Luke Arrigoni has stated a vision of 'personal autonomy and technological progress existing in harmony,' and the company frames itself as building safety infrastructure for the generative AI economy. Their business model implicitly supports targeted regulation around likeness rights and deepfake misuse, positioning themselves as solving 'likeness and licensing challenges online,' suggesting support for structured digital identity protections without opposing AI development broadly.

2110.notes: Lucidworks (formerly Lucid Imagination) is a San Francisco-based SaaS enterprise search company founded in 2007 and launched in 2009 as the first commercial company dedicated to Apache Lucene/Solr open source search technology. The company was renamed Lucidworks in 2012 and has since evolved into an AI-powered enterprise search and digital experience platform. It is notable for being among the first companies to integrate large language models (LLMs) with enterprise search, deploying its first LLM in 2018 and deep learning recommenders by 2019. Its flagship Lucidworks Platform includes Commerce Studio, Analytics Studio, Neural Hybrid Search, and data integration components. The company received a strategic investment from In-Q-Tel, the CIA's venture arm, alongside Shasta Ventures, Granite Ventures, and Walden International, indicating relevance to intelligence and government use cases. Lucidworks has served major enterprise and government-adjacent customers including AT&T, Raytheon, Sears, Ford, Verizon, Cisco, The Smithsonian Institution, and Reddit. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying AI-powered search infrastructure used across commercial and potentially government sectors.
2110.regulatory_stance_detail: No public statements or policy positions on AI regulation have been identified in the available sources.

2111.notes: Luke Ding (full name Luke Hsi Lin Ding, born December 1964) is a British-based financier, philanthropist, and effective altruism funder who studied medicine at Oxford University before switching to finance. He held senior management positions at NatWest and Merrill Lynch, and became a senior partner at hedge fund Brevan Howard Asset Management. He now focuses full-time on philanthropic activities alongside business interests. Ding committed over £1 million to found the Global Priorities Institute (GPI) at Oxford University, a research centre investigating how to do the most good, which secured posts for Professor Hilary Greaves, Associate Professor William MacAskill, and Associate Professor Andreas Mogensen — researchers whose work directly informs AI safety and existential risk priorities. He is also a documented funder of 80,000 Hours (2017, 2018, 2019 and earlier), an organization that directs talent toward AI safety and other high-impact careers, and is active on the EA Forum discussing funding flows within the EA ecosystem. He serves as a Trustee and Director at Founders Pledge and has supported projects in global health, animal welfare, and 'safeguarding future generations' — EA shorthand for existential and catastrophic risk including AI risk. His philanthropic portfolio places him as a significant behind-the-scenes funder of the intellectual infrastructure underpinning the AI safety movement.
2111.regulatory_stance_detail: No direct statements on AI regulation found, but Ding's sustained major funding of GPI, 80,000 Hours, and EA infrastructure — organizations centered on existential risk mitigation and careful prioritization of long-term harms — strongly suggests a precautionary orientation toward transformative technologies including AI. His own stated motivation is ensuring resources go toward preventing large-scale harm.
2111.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2112.notes: Luma AI (also known as Luma Labs) is a generative AI startup focused on building multimodal foundation models, with a particular emphasis on 3D and visual AI. The company is best known for its text-to-3D generative model called Genie, which launched as a research preview in November 2023 and became generally available in January 2024, attracting tens of thousands of users on its first day. On January 9, 2024, Luma announced a $43 million Series B round led by Andreessen Horowitz (with partner Anjney Midha), bringing total funding to over $70 million and valuing the company at between $200 million and $300 million. Other investors include Amplify Partners, Matrix, NVIDIA (NVentures), South Park Commons, and angel investors. Luma's founding vision is that multimodality and visual understanding are critical for the next step-change in AI capability, moving beyond language models toward systems that can 'see, understand, show, explain, and interact with the world.' The company is also active in research and development of 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis technologies. As of late 2024, Crunchbase records an additional funding round closed December 6, 2024, with Factorial Funds and Hanwha among newer investors.
2112.regulatory_stance_detail: Luma AI has not made explicit public statements on AI regulation, but their messaging emphasizes rapid scaling of foundation models, fast deployment (Genie went live same day as Series B announcement), and the transformative potential of multimodal AI. Their focus is on building and deploying quickly, inferred from actions and investor communications.

2113.notes: M12 is Microsoft's corporate venture fund, investing in enterprise technology startups across cybersecurity, AI, cloud, and related sectors. In March 2020, M12 led a $22 million Series B round for Arkose Labs, an online fraud and abuse prevention platform, alongside existing investors PayPal and USVP. Nagraj Kashyap served as Microsoft Corporate Vice President and Global Head of M12 at the time of the investment. M12's portfolio spans companies whose technologies are directly integrated into Microsoft's own product ecosystem, as evidenced by multiple Microsoft business units adopting Arkose Labs' anti-fraud platform. As a corporate venture arm of one of the world's largest AI developers, M12 shapes the AI ecosystem by directing capital toward AI-adjacent security, fraud detection, and enterprise software companies. The fund's investments signal Microsoft's strategic priorities around AI safety, trust, and enterprise deployment infrastructure. M12 operates at the intersection of AI development and cybersecurity, funding companies that address challenges like bot abuse, account fraud, and adversarial automation that are increasingly relevant as AI capabilities grow.
2113.regulatory_stance_detail: As Microsoft's venture arm, M12's investment thesis is inferred from Microsoft's broader stance, which generally favors industry-led governance and targeted regulation over broad mandates. No explicit regulatory positions are stated in the sources; stance is inferred from corporate alignment with Microsoft's AI policy approach and investment focus on enterprise technology enablement.

2115.notes: MATS Research Inc. (ML Alignment & Theory Scholars) is an independent 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization (EIN: 99-0648563) founded in 2024, headquartered at 2036 Bancroft Way, Berkeley, CA. It operates a 12-week research and educational seminar program connecting talented researchers with mentors in AI alignment, interpretability, transparency, and governance, with cohorts in both Berkeley and London. The organization was previously a fiscally-sponsored project of the Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative (BERI) before incorporating independently; MATS staff transitioned formal employment to MATS Research Inc. in March 2024. The Summer 2024 cohort supported approximately 90 scholars with 40 mentors—the broadest in MATS history—and produced notable research outputs including a best paper award at ICML 2024 and NeurIPS workshop acceptances. Fellows receive a $15,000 stipend (via AI Safety Support), $12,000 in compute resources, and housing/office space in Berkeley and London, with top performers eligible for a 6–12 month extension program. Major funders include Good Ventures Foundation ($4,558,450 across two grants in May 2025), Coefficient Giving via Silicon Valley Community Foundation ($1,042,029 in late 2024), and Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative ($1,500,000 across four grants in 2024). MATS explicitly frames its mission around reducing existential and catastrophic risks from unaligned AI, making it a key talent pipeline and research convener in the AI safety ecosystem.
2115.regulatory_stance_detail: MATS frames AI alignment as 'the world's most urgent and talent-constrained problem' and explicitly aims to reduce risks from unaligned AI, suggesting a precautionary stance prioritizing safety development before broader deployment. The program's expansion into governance and policy research tracks further reflects a proactive approach to AI risk management rather than reliance on market self-governance.

2116.notes: The MIT Quest for Intelligence (now MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence) is a campus-wide initiative launched on February 1, 2018, at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, dedicated to understanding the foundations of human and machine intelligence and developing AI technologies that can benefit society. The initiative has two linked entities: 'The Core,' focused on the science and engineering of intelligence, and 'The Bridge,' dedicated to applying MIT discoveries in AI across all disciplines. Antonio Torralba was named inaugural director in June 2018; Jim DiCarlo later became director when the Quest joined the MIT Stephen A. Schwarzman College of Computing in January 2020. The initiative received early philanthropic support from Eric Schmidt and his wife Wendy, as well as from Google, and was more recently re-endowed through a major gift from the Siegel Family Endowment. Its roots trace to the NSF-funded Center for Brains, Minds and Machines (CBMM), established in 2013. The Quest brings together neuroscience, cognitive science, and computer science researchers and has significant industry and philanthropic backing, positioning MIT as a major hub for AI research and policy influence.
2116.regulatory_stance_detail: The Quest's launch materials emphasize 'thoughtful' development of AI and the importance of human values in AI systems, as expressed by advisors at the 2018 kickoff event. The initiative focuses on research and tools to ensure AI positively influences society, suggesting a balanced, responsible-development stance rather than either accelerationist or restrictive positions.

2117.notes: MIT Lincoln Laboratory is a federally funded research and development center (FFRDC) operated by MIT and sponsored by the U.S. Department of Defense, focused on advanced technology research including AI, computing, cybersecurity, and national security systems. It appears in the US AI policy ecosystem as a funder of MIT FutureTech, the interdisciplinary research project led by Neil Thompson at MIT CSAIL that studies the economic and technical foundations of progress in computing and AI. FutureTech's about page and multiple job postings explicitly list MIT Lincoln Laboratory alongside Open Philanthropy, NSF, Accenture, IBM, and the MIT-Air Force AI accelerator as funders of the project. MIT Lincoln Laboratory's support for FutureTech connects it to AI governance and global catastrophic risk research, as FutureTech is described as one of Open Philanthropy's 10 largest Global Catastrophic Risk and AI Governance grantees. As a DoD-affiliated FFRDC, MIT Lincoln Laboratory has a long history of bridging academic research and national security applications, making its investment in AI trend analysis and policy-relevant computing research consistent with its broader mission. Its funding of FutureTech research that has been cited in the 2024 Economic Report of the President underscores its indirect influence on U.S. AI policy discourse.
2117.regulatory_stance_detail: MIT Lincoln Laboratory's regulatory stance on AI is not explicitly stated in the available sources. As a DoD-affiliated FFRDC, it operates within U.S. government frameworks and its funding of AI trend research at FutureTech suggests interest in informed, evidence-based AI governance, but no direct policy positions are attributable from these sources.

2118.notes: The MIT Task Force on the Work of the Future was commissioned by MIT President L. Rafael Reif in spring 2018 as an institute-wide initiative to understand how emerging technologies—particularly AI and robotics—are transforming the nature of human work and what policy reforms are needed to ensure broadly shared prosperity. The task force was co-chaired by David Autor (Ford Professor of Economics), David Mindell (Dibner Professor of Engineering History), and executive director Elisabeth Reynolds (MIT Industrial Performance Center). It brought together more than 20 MIT faculty members from 12 departments across all five Institute schools, plus the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing, along with over 20 graduate students. The task force assembled a 22-person advisory board including leaders from Alphabet, Amazon, Ford Motor Company, IBM, PepsiCo, and the Ford Foundation, as well as a 14-person research board of leading scholars. In November 2020, it released its final report, 'The Work of the Future: Building Better Jobs in an Age of Intelligent Machines,' which provided six major conclusions and policy recommendations on aligning automation with durable, broadly accessible careers. The task force was supported by funders including the Ford Foundation and engaged policymakers, corporate leaders, labor representatives, and community college leaders in disseminating its findings. Its work directly informed public discourse on AI, labor displacement, and institutional reform.
2118.regulatory_stance_detail: The task force's 2020 final report explicitly calls for institutional innovations to complement technological change, arguing that deploying AI and robotics into existing labor institutions 'designed for the last century' will worsen inequality. It advocates targeted policy reforms—including workforce training, labor market institutions, and social protections—rather than restricting AI development, reflecting a balanced, reform-oriented regulatory stance.

2120.notes: MITRE Corporation is a nonprofit, federally funded research and development organization (FFRDC) that works across national security, cybersecurity, and emerging technology domains. In December 2025, NIST announced a $20 million partnership with MITRE to establish two AI-focused research centers: the AI Economic Security Center for U.S. Manufacturing Productivity and the AI Economic Security Center to Secure U.S. Critical Infrastructure from Cyberthreats. MITRE has operated NIST's National Cybersecurity FFRDC (NCF) since 2014 and brings deep government-industry-academia collaboration capabilities to AI governance and applied research. The organization maintains several publicly available AI and cybersecurity tools including the ATT&CK, ATLAS, and CALDERA frameworks, as well as a state-of-the-art AI Lab and Federal AI Sandbox. The two new centers are designed to implement the White House's America's AI Action Plan and support NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI, formerly the U.S. AI Safety Institute). CEO Mark Peters emphasized MITRE's goal of producing 'transformational research to accelerate U.S. development and adoption of reliable, secure, and trustworthy AI.' MITRE's role as a trusted government partner positions it as a key institutional node in the U.S. federal AI policy and R&D ecosystem.
2120.regulatory_stance_detail: MITRE's work emphasizes developing AI safety standards, technical evaluations, and best practices rather than opposing or aggressively restricting AI. Its partnership with NIST focuses on 'reliable, secure, and trustworthy AI' and aligns with the America's AI Action Plan, suggesting support for targeted standards-based governance rather than heavy-handed regulation. Statements from CEO Mark Peters and NIST collaboration activities reflect a balanced approach that accelerates AI adoption while embedding assurance and security frameworks.

2121.notes: Manas AI is a full-stack, AI-native biopharmaceutical company launched on January 27, 2025, co-founded by oncologist and Pulitzer Prize-winning author Dr. Siddhartha Mukherjee, LinkedIn co-founder and venture capitalist Reid Hoffman, and (later added) Ujjwal Singh as Co-Founder and CTO. The company uses proprietary neuro-symbolic, science-based foundational AI models to discover and develop novel medicines, initially targeting aggressive cancers including triple-negative breast cancer, prostate cancer, and lymphoma, with plans to expand to autoimmune and rare diseases. Manas AI raised $24.6 million in seed funding led by General Catalyst with participation from Greylock and strategic investors, followed by a $26 million seed extension. The company has a strategic partnership with Microsoft, leveraging Azure cloud infrastructure to perform molecular docking at speeds 100x faster than traditional methods. Its flagship initiative, Project Cosmos, aims to map the fundamental rules of drug binding to accelerate discovery of novel chemical entities. The team includes five members of the National Academy of Sciences, four members of the National Academy of Medicine, and two recipients of the Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences. Manas AI shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier AI models for drug discovery, directly advancing the application of generative AI and computational chemistry in biomedical research.
2121.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy statements found from the organization. Co-founder Reid Hoffman has historically favored AI acceleration and innovation over heavy regulation, and the company's framing emphasizes speed, disruption, and 'abandoning things quickly that aren't working,' suggesting a light-touch preference. The company's close partnership with Microsoft and positioning as a disruptive startup further implies an innovation-first orientation.

2122.notes: Marcus Abramovitch is a Managing Partner at AltX, an EA-aligned crypto trading hedge fund, and a prominent earn-to-give effective altruist who has donated approximately $1.5 million representing over 60% of his lifetime earnings. He serves as a regrantor through Manifund, where he made AI safety grants from a $100k budget in 2023, funding early-stage researchers including Marius Hobbhahn (Apollo Research), Jesse Hoogland (Timaeus), Joseph Bloom (who led Whitebox Interp at UK AISI), and Lisa Thiergart (MIRI technical governance team). In 2025, he co-launched Manifund's Falcon Fund, an animal welfare grant fund targeting a $2M raise, focused on the intersection of animal welfare and transformative AI, making rapid grants of $25k–$150k. He has personally funded projects including Apollo Research's interpretability and behavioral model evals, sparse autoencoder research in transformer LLMs, activation vector steering, and AI governance forecasting. His background includes professional poker, a chemistry PhD program, and being a top trader on Manifold Markets. He is also developing a 'GiveWell for AI safety' cost-effectiveness framework to evaluate AI safety interventions including YouTube communications, podcasts, and policy work. He has funded CaML (Compassion in Machine Learning) as noted in the edge context, consistent with his focus on animal welfare and AI intersection.
2122.regulatory_stance_detail: Abramovitch explicitly states he views AI as posing the most significant existential risk and funds direct technical safety work such as deception detection in AI systems and interpretability research. His grant priorities favor concrete near-term safety interventions over governance meta-orgs, suggesting he sees precautionary technical work as more urgent than regulatory frameworks, though he does fund AI governance forecasting.
2122.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons

2123.notes: R. Martin 'Marty' Chavez, Ph.D., is a Partner and Vice Chairman at Sixth Street Partners and one of the most prominent figures at the intersection of finance, technology, and AI. He holds an A.B. magna cum laude in Biochemical Sciences and S.M. in Computer Science from Harvard (1985) and a Ph.D. in Medical Information Sciences from Stanford (1990). During his ~20-year career at Goldman Sachs, he served as Chief Information Officer (overseeing 9,000 engineers), Chief Financial Officer, and global co-head of the Securities Division, and is credited with transforming Wall Street trading into a software business through his work on SecDB. Since leaving Goldman Sachs in 2019, he has been an active investor and advisor in AI-adjacent companies, including Abacus.AI, Recursion Pharmaceuticals, and Samaya AI, and sits on the Board of Directors of Alphabet Inc. (Google's parent company). He created and taught 'How Software Ate Finance' at the Stanford Graduate School of Business in 2020, and has spoken extensively on AI's role in financial services alongside prominent AI figures including Eric Schmidt and Yann LeCun. He co-founded Quorum Software Systems and was CEO and co-founder of Kiodex (acquired by SunGard in 2004), demonstrating a long entrepreneurial track record. He has publicly discussed AI regulation, calling for 'stress tests' for Big Tech modeled on banking regulation, and has commented on the implications of AI developments including DeepSeek.
2123.regulatory_stance_detail: Chavez has publicly called for Big Tech stress tests modeled on banking-style regulation, and has discussed regulatory pitfalls of AI in interviews (e.g., a16z podcast). He supports regulated entities performing core banking and AI functions within a regulatory boundary, suggesting a balanced approach that neither rejects regulation nor calls for heavy restriction. His framing is that regulation should be targeted and structural rather than broad or stifling.

2124.notes: MatX is an AI chip startup founded by two former Google semiconductor engineers, including CEO Reiner Pope, focused on building dedicated hardware for large language model (LLM) inference. The company's flagship product, the MatX One, is a purpose-built LLM inference chip designed with a novel architecture that prioritizes memory bandwidth efficiency to deliver faster and cheaper inference at scale, in direct competition with Nvidia. In early 2024, MatX raised a Series A of approximately $100 million led by Spark Capital, valuing the company at over $300 million. On February 24, 2026, the company announced a $500 million Series B led by Jane Street and Situational Awareness LP (founded by Leopold Aschenbrenner), with participation from Marvell Technology, Spark Capital, NFDG, Stripe co-founders Patrick and John Collison, Andrej Karpathy, Dwarkesh Patel, Triatomic Capital, Harpoon Ventures, and Alchip. The new funding will be used to finalize chip design (with tape-out expected within a year), scale manufacturing through TSMC, and begin shipping chips in 2027. MatX is valued at several billion dollars as of the Series B. The company represents a significant effort to challenge Nvidia's dominance in AI compute infrastructure.
2124.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance has been stated by MatX or its leadership in the available sources. However, the company's investor base includes figures associated with accelerationist and light-touch regulatory views (e.g., Leopold Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness LP, Andrej Karpathy), which may suggest alignment with minimal regulatory friction for AI hardware development.

2125.notes: Matt McIlwain is a Managing Director at Madrona Venture Group, a Seattle-based VC firm he joined in 2000, where he has spent over 25 years investing across the internet era, SaaS, cloud computing, and AI/machine learning. In January 2025, Madrona announced its largest-ever fundraise of $770 million across two funds — an early-stage fund (~$490M for pre-seed to Series A) and an acceleration-stage fund for Series B/C — with McIlwain as a key spokesperson and decision-maker. His portfolio spans notable AI and tech companies including Amperity, Read AI, Gradial, Edgerunner AI, Synthesize Bio, A-Alpha Bio, and Ozette, and he led Madrona's investments in OctoAI (acquired by Nvidia for ~$250M) and Lexion (acquired by DocuSign for $165M). He has championed the 'Intelligent Applications 40' (IA40) annual list recognizing leading applied AI companies and trends. McIlwain writes extensively on AI topics, including a 2025 essay on the 'Reasoning Revolution' arguing that the next wave of AI value comes from systems that combine models, memory, tools, and feedback to compound value over time. Prior to Madrona, he held roles at McKinsey and as VP of Business Process at Genuine Parts Company, and he has a Dartmouth educational background.
2125.regulatory_stance_detail: McIlwain's August 2025 essay titled 'Embrace the Risk On Mindset in America 2025 — The Year to Bet Big on Innovation' signals strong pro-innovation, low-regulation sentiment. His framing around AI investment and growth consistently emphasizes opportunity and entrepreneurship over regulatory caution, and his focus on deploying $770M into AI startups reflects an 'accelerate and build' philosophy rather than advocacy for regulatory guardrails.

2126.notes: Matthew J. Salganik is the Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University, widely recognized as a leading figure in computational social science and social network research. He is the author of 'Bit by Bit: Social Research in the Digital Age,' a foundational text in computational social science, and co-founder (with Chris Bail) of the Summer Institute in Computational Social Science (SICSS), which trains graduate students and faculty in computational methods. His research has been funded by NSF, NIH, UNAIDS, Russell Sage Foundation, Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, Facebook, and Google, with grants ranging from methodology improvements (network scale-up, respondent-driven sampling) to wiki surveys and algorithmic fairness. He is affiliated with Princeton's AI Lab and the Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP), connecting his sociological research directly to AI and technology policy concerns. He has served on the Board of Directors of Mathematica Policy Research since July 2018, and his work has been featured in Science, PNAS, Nature, the New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Economist, and New Yorker. His research areas include social networks, computational social science, algorithmic risk assessments, and methods for studying hard-to-reach populations, all of which have direct relevance to AI fairness, bias, and societal impact debates.
2126.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance on AI policy is documented in the sources. His affiliation with CITP and research on algorithmic pretrial risk assessments suggest awareness of AI's societal risks, but no direct policy positions on AI regulation have been stated in available sources.

2127.notes: Max Chiswick (1986–January 7, 2025) was a co-founder of the AI Safety Awareness Foundation (AISAP) and co-founder of Poker Camp, a nonprofit using poker to teach probability, AI, and decision-making under uncertainty. He held a B.S. in Electrical Engineering from Northwestern University (2008) and an M.Sc. in Electrical Engineering from Technion Israel Institute of Technology (2017), where his thesis focused on Counterfactual Regret Minimization and abstraction analysis in computer poker. He played online poker professionally for five years, logging over 10 million hands. In 2019, he spent 18 weeks at the Recurse Center focused on AI and reinforcement learning, and subsequently received a grant from MIRI to upskill in machine learning. He participated in AI Safety Camp in 2020 and served as an organizer in 2021, where he co-authored research on 'Assessing Generalization in Reward Learning.' He co-authored published research papers on poker AI and Bayesian prediction with Sam Ganzfried, and built the AI Poker Tutorial website. He passed away on January 7, 2025, while traveling.
2127.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. His work in AI safety awareness and alignment research suggests concern about AI risks, but no specific regulatory positions are documented.

2128.notes: The Max Planck Society (Max-Planck-Gesellschaft) is Germany's premier independent research organization, operating a network of over 80 institutes spanning natural sciences, life sciences, and social sciences. It is one of the world's most prestigious research funders and hosts, known for supporting fundamental, long-term scientific inquiry. The Society awards the Max Planck Research Prize annually to outstanding scientists, including John A. Bargh and Peter Gollwitzer in 1990. It has direct relevance to AI through institutes such as the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems and support for researchers like Philipp Hennig, a leading figure in probabilistic machine learning and Bayesian deep learning. Hennig led an independent research group supported by Max Planck Society funding, conducting foundational AI/ML research. The Society's Emmy Noether-adjacent and independent group funding mechanisms support early-career AI researchers in Germany. As a major funder and host of frontier ML and AI research in Europe, it plays a structural role in shaping the global AI research ecosystem.
2128.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public stance on AI regulation is attributable to the Max Planck Society as an institution from the available sources. As a research organization, it tends to advocate for scientific freedom and robust public funding of basic research, but no specific AI regulatory position is documented here.

2129.notes: Melinda French Gates (formerly Melinda Gates) is a prominent philanthropist and founder of Pivotal, a group of organizations she founded in 2015 to accelerate social progress for women and young people in the U.S. and globally. She shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through philanthropic funding focused on diversity, equity, and inclusion in AI. In 2017, she provided a gift to AI4ALL through Pivotal Ventures, co-funding the nonprofit alongside Jensen and Lori Huang, to expand access to AI education for underrepresented groups including women and people of color. She has publicly articulated the risk of AI bias, stating: 'If we don't get women and people of color at the table — real technologists doing the real work — we will bias systems.' In 2024, she announced a $1 billion commitment through Pivotal Philanthropies Foundation to advance women's power globally through 2026, with focus areas including workplace barriers and women's health. Her AI-related philanthropy reflects a belief that diversity in AI development is a systemic imperative, not merely aspirational. Pivotal Ventures also functions as an investment and incubation arm, using capital to drive social outcomes in tech and adjacent fields.
2129.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation or policy frameworks found in sources. Her funding and advocacy focus on inclusive AI development and bias prevention, suggesting concern about harms from unrepresentative AI systems, but she has not publicly staked out a position on regulatory frameworks.

2130.notes: Mentee Robotics is an Israeli humanoid robotics startup founded approximately 2021-2022 by Prof. Amnon Shashua (Mobileye CEO), Prof. Shai Shalev-Shwartz (Mobileye CTO), and Prof. Lior Wolf (serving as CEO). The company develops humanoid robots capable of picking up objects, classifying them, packaging, and assembling products — targeting industrial labor applications. Mentee raised roughly $37.7–$40 million over its lifetime across a seed round (~$2.15M) and a Series A ($15M, December 2022) and a later early-stage VC round (March 2025), reaching a valuation of $162 million before its acquisition. In January 2026, Mobileye announced the acquisition of Mentee Robotics for approximately $900 million ($612 million cash plus ~26.2 million Mobileye shares), announced at CES in Las Vegas. The deal was approved by Intel's board (which controls ~85% of Mobileye) and was a significant related-party transaction given Shashua's dual role as Mobileye CEO and Mentee chairman/largest shareholder (37.8%, worth ~$341 million). Investors included Israeli VC fund 10D, Ahren Innovation Capital (14.5%), Cisco Ventures, and Samsung's venture arm. The acquisition signals Mobileye's strategic expansion from autonomous driving into humanoid robotics, with commercial sales projected no earlier than 2028.
2130.regulatory_stance_detail: No public statements from Mentee Robotics as an organization regarding AI or robotics regulation were found in the available sources.

2131.notes: Merge Labs is a brain-computer interface (BCI) research lab co-founded by Sam Altman (CEO of OpenAI), Alex Blania, Sandro Herbig, Mikhail Shapiro, Tyson Aflalo, and Sumner Norman, emerging from stealth on January 15, 2026. The company describes itself as dedicated to 'bridging biological and artificial intelligence to maximize human ability, agency, and experience.' It raised a $252 million seed round at an $850 million valuation, with OpenAI writing the largest single check; other investors include Bain Capital, Interface Fund, Fifty Years, and Gabe Newell (co-founder of Valve). Merge Labs is pursuing non-invasive BCI technology using ultrasound and molecular approaches rather than surgical implants, aiming to connect human neurons to AI without requiring brain surgery. The company grew out of earlier work at Forest Neurotech, a nonprofit BCI research group that developed the Forest-1 ultrasound brain-imaging device. OpenAI has also committed to helping Merge Labs build 'scientific foundation models and other frontier tools,' making the relationship both financial and technical. The company is positioned as a long-term R&D organization with no near-term product, competing in a BCI landscape that includes Neuralink, and is seen as a key player in the emerging human-AI augmentation frontier.
2131.regulatory_stance_detail: Merge Labs and its backers frame BCIs as 'an important new frontier' that will create 'a natural, human-centered way for anyone to seamlessly interact with AI,' with no stated support for regulatory constraints. The company's positioning as a long-term research lab focused on expanding human capabilities, backed by OpenAI and Sam Altman, strongly implies an accelerationist stance prioritizing innovation over precautionary governance.

2132.notes: Michael Bloomberg (born February 14, 1942, in Boston) is the founder of Bloomberg L.P., a global financial technology, data, and media company he launched in 1981 with $4 million of a $10 million severance from Salomon Brothers. He owns approximately 88% of Bloomberg L.P., which generates over $15 billion in annual revenue (2024) and serves 350,000+ terminal subscribers worldwide. Bloomberg served as Mayor of New York City from 2002 to 2013, stepping down as CEO to pursue that role and returning as CEO from 2014 to 2023. Bloomberg Philanthropies, funded by the vast majority of Bloomberg L.P.'s profits, has distributed approximately $17.4 billion to nonprofits in public health, environment, education, government innovation, and the arts. Bloomberg shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through philanthropy and capital: Bloomberg Philanthropies funds initiatives related to technology governance and government innovation, and Bloomberg L.P. itself increasingly incorporates AI and data analytics into its financial intelligence products. His philanthropic giving is a major source of funding for policy and research organizations that engage on AI governance issues.
2132.regulatory_stance_detail: Bloomberg's philanthropic focus on 'government innovation' and public health regulation suggests a pragmatic, evidence-based regulatory approach. No direct statements on AI regulation were found in sources, but his record as NYC mayor emphasized data-driven governance and targeted regulation rather than laissez-faire or heavy-handed approaches.

2133.notes: Micron Technology is the only U.S.-based manufacturer of memory semiconductors (DRAM), making it a uniquely critical player in the domestic chip supply chain. In December 2024, the U.S. Department of Commerce finalized a CHIPS and Science Act award of up to $6.165 billion for Micron to construct leading-edge DRAM fabrication facilities in Clay, New York and Boise, Idaho, as part of a two-decade vision to invest approximately $125 billion and create ~20,000 jobs. Simultaneously, the Department signed a Preliminary Memorandum of Terms for up to $275 million to expand and modernize Micron's existing manufacturing facility in Manassas, Virginia, onshoring its 1-alpha DRAM technology for automotive, industrial, and defense markets. These investments are projected to grow the U.S. share of advanced memory manufacturing from less than 2% to approximately 10% by 2035. Memory chips produced by Micron are foundational to AI data centers, advanced defense systems, autonomous vehicles, and consumer electronics. Micron's CEO Sanjay Mehrotra has explicitly framed the company's memory products as essential to meeting growing AI demand, positioning Micron as a key infrastructure provider for the AI ecosystem. The scale of federal investment and Micron's monopoly position as the sole U.S. memory manufacturer make it a highly strategic asset for U.S. national security and AI competitiveness.
2133.regulatory_stance_detail: Micron has actively supported and welcomed the CHIPS and Science Act framework, which provides incentives rather than restrictions. CEO Sanjay Mehrotra praised the bipartisan CHIPS Act and the public-private partnership model, suggesting preference for government investment and supply-chain policy over regulatory mandates on AI or semiconductors specifically.

2134.notes: Minderoo Foundation is an Australian philanthropic organization founded in 2001 by Andrew and Nicola Forrest, backed by the Forrest family's private investment group Tattarang. In 2013, the Forrests became the first Australians to sign the Giving Pledge. The foundation shapes the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its Frontier Technology initiative, which in 2022-2023 launched a AU$20 million (~$14 million USD) 'Tech Impact Network' — a global network of five academic centers focused on the societal implications of technology and AI, located at Cambridge, UCLA, NYU, Oxford, and the University of Western Australia. Key centers include the Minderoo Centre for Technology and Democracy at Cambridge, the Minderoo Initiative on Technology and Power at UCLA, and the Minderoo-Oxford Challenge Fund in AI Governance. The foundation also funded the Minderoo Research Professorship at NYU, held by Meredith Whittaker (co-founder of the AI Now Institute), directly seeding critical AI policy research. The foundation explicitly cited concerns about 'industry capture' in AI research as motivation for its Tech Impact Network. In April 2024, a restructure axed about 100 jobs and consolidated international offices, with CEO John Hartman steering a more Australia-first, collaborative strategy.
2134.regulatory_stance_detail: Minderoo Foundation explicitly cited 'industry capture' in AI research as a major problem driving its $14 million Tech Impact Network, funding independent academic centers to produce reform-oriented analysis of AI and digital technology. The foundation's research chair Fiona David stated they sought to amplify 'unflinching' analysis of an industry with 'unmitigated power' and to be 'an engine room of ideas for reform,' signaling a strong pro-regulatory, anti-industry-concentration stance.

2135.notes: The Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT, 과학기술정보통신부) is the South Korean cabinet-level government ministry responsible for national science, technology, and information and communications technology (ICT) policy. MSIT oversees and funds IITP (Institute for Information and Communications Technology Planning and Evaluation), which serves as its primary operational engine for ICT and AI R&D management, with a budget of approximately $1.8 billion. MSIT is a key funder of major South Korean AI initiatives, including the Global AI Frontier Lab with a $42.3 million investment over five years (45 billion won) beginning in the current fiscal year. The ministry sets strategic direction for Korea's AI development, including through the K-Moonshot program, which encompasses 12 national mission objectives for AI and ICT competitiveness. MSIT coordinates a three-agency funding model with IITP and the National Research Foundation (NRF) and Korea Institute for Advancement of Technology (KIAT) to manage AI research across technology readiness levels. The ministry has invested heavily in GPU infrastructure deployment, AI talent development programs (including partnerships with Oxford University for cybersecurity training), and domestic AI chip development to build sovereign AI capabilities. MSIT represents South Korea's primary government vehicle for competing in the global AI race and shaping Korea's position as a leading AI nation.
2135.regulatory_stance_detail: MSIT primarily acts as a promoter and funder of AI development rather than a regulator, channeling billions into R&D programs like K-Moonshot and the Global AI Frontier Lab. However, as a government ministry it also sets ICT policy frameworks and oversees compliance and evaluation through IITP. Its stance appears to balance aggressive investment in AI acceleration with government-directed strategic oversight, suggesting a 'developmental state' model rather than either pure acceleration or restrictive regulation.

2136.notes: Morgan Stanley is a global financial services firm headquartered at 1585 Broadway, New York, incorporated in Delaware, with operations spanning Institutional Securities, Wealth Management, and Investment Management (SEC CIK 0000895421, SIC 6211). It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a major capital allocator and underwriter for AI-relevant companies. As noted in the edge context, Morgan Stanley participated in a ~$1.3 billion conventional debt round for ByteDance (April 2019) and was among the bank-channel investors in OpenAI's approximately $7.7 billion fundraising round alongside JPMorgan Chase, Citi, and Goldman Sachs. For Q1 2019, the firm reported net revenues of $10.286 billion and net income of $2.429 billion, reflecting its substantial scale and capacity to deploy capital into technology and AI ventures. Morgan Stanley also regularly issues large-scale debt offerings (e.g., a $10 billion medium-term note offering with tranches through 2037) that fund its ongoing investment and lending activities, including into the tech sector. Through its institutional securities and wealth management arms, Morgan Stanley influences which AI companies receive growth capital, making it a key financial infrastructure actor in the AI ecosystem.
2136.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit AI policy statements appear in the available sources. As a major Wall Street financial institution that funds and underwrites AI companies including ByteDance and OpenAI, Morgan Stanley's actions suggest preference for minimal regulatory friction on AI investment and capital deployment. Large financial institutions historically favor light-touch regulation that preserves deal-making flexibility.

2137.notes: MosaicML (legal name: Mosaic ML, Inc.) was an AI startup founded in 2021 by Naveen Rao, Hanlin Tang, and Jonathan Frankle, focused on making large language model training and deployment more efficient, accessible, and cost-effective. The company built an open-source deep learning library called Composer and a multi-cloud platform enabling organizations to train and deploy LLMs on their own proprietary data in secure environments. MosaicML released notable open models including MPT-30B, positioning itself as an open-source-oriented alternative to proprietary AI labs like OpenAI. The company raised approximately $37–64 million from investors including DCVC (first invested 2020), Lux Capital (invested 2021), AME Cloud Ventures, Frontline, Atlas, Playground Global, Samsung Next, Inspired Capital, and Maverick Ventures. In June 2023, Databricks acquired MosaicML for $1.3 billion, integrating its generative AI tooling into the Databricks Lakehouse Platform. Its customers included the Allen Institute for AI, Replit, Hippocratic AI, and Generally Intelligent. MosaicML shaped the AI ecosystem by democratizing access to LLM training infrastructure and championing open-source, data-private AI development.
2137.regulatory_stance_detail: MosaicML's public statements and mission emphasized democratizing AI and empowering every organization to build and train their own models with their own data, framing this as opposition to centralized AI control. CEO Naveen Rao stated: 'We believe in a world where everyone is empowered to build and train their own models, imbued with their own opinions and viewpoints.' This ethos favors broad access and minimal gatekeeping over heavy regulation.

2138.notes: The Mozilla Technology Fund (MTF) is a grant program operated by the Mozilla Foundation that makes awards to open-source technologists working on pressing internet health issues, with a particular focus on AI transparency, bias, and accountability. The MTF has run three cohorts: 2022 (Bias and Transparency in AI), 2023 (Auditing Tools for AI Systems), and 2024 (AI and Environmental Justice), making awards of up to $50,000 per project. By 2024, the fund had disbursed more than $1 million in awards across three years. The 2023 cohort funded eight projects — including Reward Reports (by GEESE, involving Nathan Lambert), AI Forensics, Zeno (Carnegie Mellon), and Gigbox — each focused on building open-source auditing tools for AI systems. The 2023 cohort was shaped in collaboration with leading AI accountability researchers including Mozilla Fellow Deb Raji (UC Berkeley) and Abeba Birhane (Trinity College Dublin). The MTF explicitly aims to grow and coordinate the community of AI auditors, linking funded projects to the Open Source Audit Tooling (OAT) Initiative. The fund represents a philanthropic/nonprofit mechanism for seeding civil-society-led AI accountability infrastructure.
2138.regulatory_stance_detail: The MTF's grant focus on AI auditing tools and accountability mechanisms reflects a belief that AI systems require robust external oversight and accountability structures. By funding tooling to hold AI systems accountable for harms, bias, and discrimination — areas where the MTF notes 'few mechanisms' currently exist — the fund implicitly advocates for stronger, mandatory accountability regimes rather than voluntary self-governance.

2139.notes: Mozilla Ventures is the venture capital arm of the Mozilla Foundation/Corporation, focused on funding companies that advance responsible, ethical, and trustworthy technology including AI. The fund invests in AI governance, safety, and infrastructure companies with an emphasis on aligning commercial AI development with human values and ethical standards. Notable investments include Credo AI (led $21M Series A1 round in July 2024 and participated in earlier $15M Series A in May 2022), Fiddler AI, Holistic AI, Armilla AI, Themis AI, Vera, TruePic, and Trail. As described in Mozilla's 2024 Annual Report, Mozilla Ventures has invested specifically in 'AI governance leaders' and companies building technical infrastructure for responsible AI/ML training, deployment, and management. The fund serves as a key capital allocator in the responsible AI ecosystem, channeling funding toward governance tools, bias/fairness monitoring, and AI accountability infrastructure rather than raw AI capability development.
2139.regulatory_stance_detail: Mozilla Ventures' investment thesis explicitly targets AI governance, ethics, and accountability companies, signaling support for meaningful oversight mechanisms. By backing firms like Credo AI (AI governance software) and Holistic AI, the fund implicitly endorses structured compliance and governance frameworks rather than pure self-regulation, but focuses on market-based governance solutions rather than government mandates.

2140.notes: The Musk Foundation is the personal philanthropic organization of Elon Musk, established in December 2001 with his brother Kimbal Musk and initially registered in Los Angeles before relocating to Austin, Texas. It funds renewable energy research and advocacy, human space exploration, pediatric research, science and engineering education, and the 'development of safe artificial intelligence to benefit humanity.' The foundation has been a notable funder of AI safety and policy organizations, including a $10 million grant to the Future of Life Institute in 2015 and early support for OpenAI (when it was a nonprofit), and it funds CSET (Center for Security and Emerging Technology) at Georgetown. The foundation disbursed a record $474 million in grants in 2024, its largest year of giving, after failing to meet the IRS's mandatory 5% payout requirement for several consecutive prior years. Grantmaking is managed by an unpaid board including Elon Musk, Jared Birchall, and Matilda Simon, with no full-time employees; a significant portion of grants flow through donor-advised funds at Vanguard and Fidelity, and to The Foundation, another Musk-affiliated entity. The foundation does not accept unsolicited proposals and maintains a famously minimal web presence. Tax filings indicate grantmaking closely aligns with Musk's personal business interests and objectives rather than a formal philanthropic strategy.
2140.regulatory_stance_detail: The Musk Foundation's stated mission includes 'development of safe artificial intelligence to benefit humanity,' suggesting some concern for responsible AI development. However, Elon Musk's broader public stance on AI regulation has been inconsistent — he co-founded OpenAI and funded AI safety organizations, yet has also publicly opposed heavy-handed regulation and has launched his own AI company (xAI). Grants to organizations like CSET and Future of Life Institute suggest support for AI governance research, but overall posture leans away from restrictive regulation.
