2140.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security

2141.notes: NASA Innovative Advanced Concepts (NIAC) is a program within NASA's Space Technology Mission Directorate (STMD) that funds early-stage, visionary technology concepts with the potential to transform future aerospace missions. The program operates via Phase I and Phase II grants, with Phase II awards of approximately $500,000 over two years to develop promising Phase I concepts further. NIAC has funded research directly relevant to AI and autonomous systems, including the 2015 selection of 'Swarm Flyby Gravimetry' by Justin Atchison of Johns Hopkins University, which explored autonomous swarms of nanosatellites for asteroid exploration. The program emphasizes transformative concepts typically requiring 10 or more years of development before operational use. NIAC is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem because it seeds early research into autonomous multi-agent systems, machine learning for space applications, and swarm robotics — all foundational AI capabilities. The edge context links NIAC to researcher Matthew Johnson-Roberson via satellite swarm research, consistent with NIAC's documented funding of swarm satellite technology demonstrations such as the PY4 mission led by Carnegie Mellon University and the Starling mission. Jason Derleth has served as NIAC Program Executive at NASA Headquarters.
2141.regulatory_stance_detail: As a U.S. federal government program, NIAC operates within NASA's institutional framework and does not take explicit public positions on AI regulation. Its funding of advanced autonomous systems and swarm AI suggests an accelerationist posture toward technology development, but it operates under existing federal oversight structures without advocating for or against specific AI regulatory frameworks.

2142.notes: The National Defense Science and Engineering Graduate (NDSEG) Fellowship Program is a U.S. Department of Defense graduate fellowship program established by Congress in 1989 to increase the number of U.S. citizens receiving doctoral degrees in science and engineering disciplines of military importance. The program is jointly sponsored by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research (AFOSR), the Army Research Office (ARO), and the Office of Naval Research (ONR) under the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense for Research & Engineering. Since inception, it has awarded nearly 4,700 fellowships from over 70,000 applications. The program awards approximately 150–350 three-year fellowships annually, covering full tuition, mandatory fees, a monthly stipend (~$3,200), and medical insurance. Supported disciplines include computer and computational sciences, cognitive/neural/behavioral sciences, electrical engineering, and mathematics — all directly relevant to AI research. The program shapes the AI ecosystem by funding early-career doctoral researchers, including AI/ML researchers like Nathan Lambert (Top 200, 2018), who later became influential in AI alignment and RLHF research. Fellows may also conduct summer research at Navy laboratories.
2142.regulatory_stance_detail: As a DoD fellowship program, NDSEG operates under congressional direction and DoD policy. Its mandate is to increase U.S. scientific and engineering talent for national defense — implying support for government-directed investment in STEM but no explicit public stance on civilian AI regulation.

2143.notes: NFDG Ventures (also referred to simply as NFDG) is an investment partnership co-run by Nat Friedman (former CEO of GitHub) and Daniel Gross (CEO of Safe Superintelligence Inc.). The name 'NFDG' appears to be derived from the initials of Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross. NFDG served as the lead investor in SSI's landmark $1 billion Series A funding round announced September 4, 2024, which valued the three-month-old company at $5 billion. SSI was co-founded by former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, Daniel Gross, and former OpenAI researcher Daniel Levy, with a mission to develop safe superintelligence. Other investors in the round included Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital, DST Global, and SV Angel. NFDG's investment is notable given that Daniel Gross is simultaneously SSI's CEO, making NFDG a uniquely insider-positioned investor. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem through direct capital allocation to frontier AI safety research.
2143.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public regulatory stance has been articulated by NFDG as an entity. However, their lead investment in SSI — a company explicitly focused on safe superintelligence — suggests some alignment with safety-conscious AI development rather than pure acceleration.

2145.notes: NVentures is the corporate venture capital arm of NVIDIA, officially launched in May 2022 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California. Led by Mohamed 'Sid' Siddeek, NVIDIA's Corporate Vice President and Head of NVentures, the fund focuses on investing in early- to growth-stage startups advancing artificial intelligence, accelerated computing, robotics, generative AI, digital twins, healthcare AI, and quantum computing. NVentures typically writes checks ranging from approximately $1 million (seed) to $20 million (Series A/B), and has built a portfolio of over 40 companies, having led approximately 16 rounds since inception. Notable portfolio companies include World Labs, Samaya AI, Radical AI, Genesis Therapeutics, QuEra Computing, Odyssey, Freeform, Neocis, and PhysicsX, spanning sectors from life sciences and manufacturing to quantum computing and world simulation. As a strategic investor, NVentures aims to cultivate an ecosystem around NVIDIA's core platforms—including CUDA, Omniverse, and NVIDIA Inception—ensuring portfolio companies integrate with and accelerate NVIDIA's accelerated computing stack. NVentures' investments are both financial and strategic, offering portfolio companies access to NVIDIA engineering expertise, infrastructure, and technology partnerships, amplifying NVIDIA's influence across the AI startup landscape.
2145.regulatory_stance_detail: NVentures has made no explicit public statements on AI regulation; however, its investment strategy—broadly funding AI acceleration across sectors with emphasis on speed and scale—strongly implies a preference for light-touch or minimal regulatory interference. The fund's mission to accelerate AI-native industrial revolution and its focus on enabling rapid deployment of AI across defense, healthcare, robotics, and manufacturing reflects an accelerationist posture consistent with NVIDIA's broader corporate stance favoring market-led innovation.

2146.notes: The NYU Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy (CMEP) is an endowed academic research center at New York University's College of Arts & Science, formally launched in Fall 2024 after transitioning from the Mind, Ethics, and Policy Program (MEP), which was founded in 2022. The center is directed by Jeff Sebo, Associate Professor of Environmental Studies at NYU, who also directs the Center for Environmental and Animal Protection (CEAP) and co-directs the Wild Animal Welfare Program. CMEP's mission is to advance understanding of the consciousness, sentience, sapience, moral status, legal status, and political status of nonhuman beings—both biological and artificial—through research, teaching, outreach, and field building across science, philosophy, and policy. The $6 million endowment established in May 2024 comprises a $5 million gift from The Navigation Fund and $1 million from Polaris Ventures (also referred to as Macroscopic Ventures in some sources). CMEP is described as the first center to integrate science, ethics, and policy research on animal and AI minds together, with philosopher Kristin Andrews calling it 'a game changer for research in animal mind and cognition.' The center directly shapes the AI policy ecosystem by researching the moral and legal status of AI systems and advocating for policy frameworks that account for AI sentience and moral significance. It is affiliated with the effective altruism and longtermist philanthropic community through its funders.
2146.regulatory_stance_detail: CMEP advocates for policy frameworks that recognize the moral and legal status of AI systems and other nonhumans, implying a targeted regulatory approach requiring legal and political protections for AI entities. The center engages in field building across science, philosophy, and policy, suggesting support for evidence-based, measured governance rather than either deregulation or blanket restrictions.
2146.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Loss of control

2147.notes: Natcast, formally the National Center for the Advancement of Semiconductor Technology, is a private nonprofit corporation established during the Biden Administration to operate the National Semiconductor Technology Center (NSTC) under the CHIPS and Science Act (signed August 2022). In January 2025, NIST awarded Natcast up to $6.3 billion over 10 years to operate the NSTC, following a prior $1.1 billion award from the CHIPS National Advanced Packaging Manufacturing Program (NAPMP) for advanced packaging activities. Natcast's mandate includes semiconductor R&D, workforce development, an investment fund, the Design Enablement Gateway, silicon aggregation services, and operating CHIPS for America R&D flagship facilities. CEO Deirdre Hanford led the organization, which attracted over 100 NSTC members since fall 2024. In August 2025, the Trump Administration's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick declared Natcast illegally established under the Government Corporation Control Act, voided the funding agreement, and transferred NSTC operational responsibility back to NIST. The organization is directly relevant to AI and advanced computing ecosystems as semiconductor supply chain, R&D infrastructure, and workforce development underpin AI hardware capabilities. Natcast also funded a $1.72 million workforce curriculum grant to the American Federation of Teachers in Michigan and Minnesota.
2147.regulatory_stance_detail: Natcast operated as a quasi-governmental nonprofit implementing CHIPS Act mandates, favoring structured public-private partnership governance for semiconductor R&D. Its stance on broader AI or semiconductor regulation is not explicitly articulated in available sources; its approach was defined by the statutory framework of the CHIPS and Science Act rather than an independent policy position.
2147.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

2148.notes: The National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) Pilot is a U.S. federal initiative launched in January 2024, led by the National Science Foundation (NSF) in partnership with 10+ other federal agencies (including DOE and NIH) and 25+ private sector, nonprofit, and philanthropic organizations. Its mission is to democratize access to computational resources, datasets, models, software, training, and user support for U.S.-based researchers and educators working on responsible AI discovery and innovation. The pilot was directly mandated by President Biden's Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence (October 2023). As of its operational phase, the NAIRR has supported more than 600 research projects and 6,000 students across all 50 states, Washington D.C., and Puerto Rico. Non-governmental partners include Microsoft (contributing $20M in Azure compute credits), Google, Amazon Web Services, NVIDIA, Meta, OpenAI, Anthropic, and others. The pilot is explicitly designed as a proof-of-concept to inform the design and funding of a full-scale, permanent NAIRR infrastructure. In 2025, NSF announced a new solicitation to establish a NAIRR Operations Center (NAIRR-OC), marking the transition from pilot to a sustainable national program.
2148.regulatory_stance_detail: The NAIRR Pilot was established under the Biden Executive Order on AI, which called for balanced, responsible AI development. The initiative explicitly focuses on 'responsible AI discovery and innovation' and prioritizes research into AI safety, fairness, accuracy, reliability, transparency, privacy, and security — reflecting a moderate, targeted regulatory and governance posture rather than either laissez-faire or restrictive approaches.

2149.notes: The National Artificial Intelligence Research Institutes (NAI Research Institutes) is a flagship NSF-led program launched in 2020 to build a nationwide network of multidisciplinary AI research hubs. As of the latest round (2025), the program encompasses 29 institutes connecting over 500 funded and collaborative institutions across the U.S. and internationally, representing approximately half a billion dollars in cumulative public-private investment. The program operates through multi-sector partnerships including NSF, NIST, DoD, USDA, DHS, Department of Education, and private partners such as Capital One, Intel, Google, Amazon, IBM, Accenture, and the Simons Foundation. In May 2025, NSF announced a $100 million investment round in partnership with Capital One and Intel to support five institutes and a central community hub (NSF AIVO, led by UC Davis) focusing on mental health, materials discovery, STEM education, human-AI collaboration, and drug development. Individual institutes include NSF IFML (UT Austin, generative AI foundations), NSF MMLI (UIUC, molecule discovery), NSF iSAT (CU Boulder, AI in STEM education), NSF AI-MI (Cornell, materials science), and NSF ARIA (Brown University, AI assistants). The program directly shapes the U.S. AI ecosystem by funding long-term foundational research, workforce development, and open innovation infrastructure, and is explicitly cited in the 2023 National AI R&D Strategic Plan and endorsed by the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence as a key component of U.S. AI technical leadership.
2149.regulatory_stance_detail: The program is aligned with the White House AI Action Plan and Executive Order 14277, emphasizing responsible AI, trustworthy AI systems, and ethical AI development (e.g., the TRAILS institute focuses on AI ethics and human rights). The program supports open innovation and public benefit while explicitly funding institutes addressing AI risk, bias, and governance, suggesting a balanced, moderate stance rather than either accelerationist or restrictive positions.

2150.notes: National Bank of Canada is the sixth largest commercial bank in Canada, founded in 1859 and headquartered in Montreal, Quebec, with approximately 30,000-31,000 employees and over CAD $418 billion in assets as of 2023. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through NAventures, its corporate venture capital arm, which invests in early-to-mid-stage technology and fintech companies; in June 2017, NAventures co-invested in Element AI's landmark $100M Series A round alongside DCVC, BDC Venture Capital, Microsoft's M12, Fidelity Investments Canada, and others. The bank has a dedicated Technology and Innovation Banking Group and in August 2023 acquired Silicon Valley Bank's Canadian commercial loan portfolio (approximately CAD $1 billion in commitments) in the Technology, Life Science, and Global Fund Banking sectors, further deepening its exposure to AI and tech-adjacent companies. Through its investment arm (National Bank of Canada /fi/), the bank holds significant equity positions in major AI-relevant technology companies including Nvidia ($5B+), Microsoft ($4.5B+), Amazon, and Apple as of mid-2025. NAventures has made dozens of fintech and technology investments including MindBridge (AI-powered audit analytics, 2018 Series A), d1g1t, Sensibill, and others. The bank has been active in supporting Canadian innovation for over 25 years and positions itself as a strategic partner—not just a capital provider—for technology startups.
2150.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public positions on AI regulation have been identified from the available sources. The bank's active investment in AI companies and its acquisition of SVB Canada's tech portfolio suggest a generally favorable view of AI development, but no formal regulatory stance has been stated.

2151.notes: The National Development Fund (NDF) is a government-managed investment vehicle under Taiwan's National Development Council (NDC), part of the Executive Yuan. It serves as the primary public capital arm for strategic industrial policy investment in Taiwan. In 2024–2025, the NDF allocated NT$10 billion (approximately US$304–313 million) to the Ministry of Digital Affairs (MODA) to fund the 'Enhanced AI Startup Investment Program,' a 10-year initiative aimed at cultivating domestic AI startups and digital economy industries. The program operates on a 1:1 public-private co-investment basis, with the government committing NT$10 billion and seeking to match that with private sector capital from venture capital firms, angel investors, and strategic corporate partners such as ASUS-affiliated Taiwan AI Cloud, TaiAn Technologies, and Abico Asia Capital. The Cabinet approved the AI startup plan on August 9, 2024, and the program was officially launched on March 31, 2025. The NDC's minister Liu Chin-ching described the NT$10 billion as 'just the foundation,' signaling intent to expand further. The fund's goal is to generate NT$1 trillion in production value, help at least 20 startups list on capital markets, and position Taiwan as a global AI hub with multiple AI unicorns.
2151.regulatory_stance_detail: The NDF's approach is to use public capital to accelerate private AI investment rather than impose regulatory constraints. The NDC is simultaneously revising the Statute for Industrial Innovation to stimulate AI investment and adoption, suggesting a pro-growth, enabling regulatory posture rather than a restrictive one. The 1:1 public-private co-investment model reflects a preference for market-led development with government catalysis.

2152.notes: The National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA) is an agency within the U.S. Department of Commerce and serves as the principal executive branch advisor to the President on telecommunications and information policy. NTIA's mission is largely focused on expanding broadband internet access and adoption, expanding spectrum use, and promoting digital equity across the United States. Under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021), NTIA administers billions in broadband and digital equity programs, including the $42.45 billion BEAD (Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment) Program and the $2.75 billion Digital Equity Act programs. As of 2024, NTIA approved Colorado's Final BEAD Proposal for $826.5 million and a separate $420.6 million allocation, and awarded California a $70.2 million Digital Equity Capacity Grant. NTIA shapes the AI ecosystem through its jurisdiction over internet infrastructure, data policy, spectrum management, and its role advising on AI governance and telecommunications policy. Administrator Alan Davidson has been a prominent voice on broadband access and digital inclusion as of 2024. NTIA's work on broadband deployment and digital equity directly underpins the infrastructure upon which AI services and digital economy applications depend.
2152.regulatory_stance_detail: NTIA has historically supported balanced, targeted regulation of internet and communications technologies, advising on AI accountability and transparency. The agency has issued reports and guidance recommending targeted oversight for AI systems, particularly around accountability and transparency, consistent with a moderate regulatory approach that focuses on high-risk uses without broadly restricting innovation.

2153.notes: The Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) is Canada's primary federal funding agency for natural sciences and engineering research, investing over $1.2 billion annually to support more than 11,000 professors and their research programs. NSERC shapes the AI ecosystem directly through grants to leading AI researchers, including Canada Research Chairs awarded to figures like Aaron Courville (Université de Montréal, 2022) and NSERC Industrial Research Chairs such as Richard Zemel's Chair in Machine Learning (since 2018). NSERC awarded Joelle Pineau the prestigious E.W.R. Steacie Memorial Fellowship (valued at $250,000), recognizing her foundational contributions to reinforcement learning and AI for health. The agency also funded HEC Montréal's Tech3Lab with $18 million over five years for user experience research with AI applications. NSERC prizes, awarded annually at Rideau Hall with the Governor General and relevant ministers, have explicitly recognized breakthroughs in artificial intelligence for mobile robots and other AI subfields (2022 ceremony). The current President is Professor Alejandro Adem, who leads annual prize announcements celebrating Canadian research excellence including AI. NSERC's funding programs—including Discovery Grants, Alliance Grants, and Industrial Research Chairs—are key instruments for sustaining Canada's internationally recognized AI research ecosystem, particularly in Montréal and Toronto.
2153.regulatory_stance_detail: NSERC is a research funding agency, not a regulatory body, and takes no explicit public stance on AI regulation. Its posture is implicitly pro-research and pro-innovation, channeling over $1.2 billion annually into science and engineering including AI, suggesting a disposition toward enabling and accelerating research rather than restricting it. No formal policy position on AI regulation has been stated in available sources.

2154.notes: Naver Corporation is South Korea's largest internet portal and technology company, operating as a major AI developer with significant research infrastructure including supercomputing facilities introduced in October 2020. In May 2021, Naver signed a landmark industry-academia partnership with Seoul National University (SNU) to establish the SNU-NAVER Hyperscale AI Research Center, committing over 20 billion KRW (tens of billions of won) over three years—the largest AI industry-academia collaboration in South Korean history. The joint center deployed approximately 100 AI researchers from both institutions to develop Korean-language hyperscale AI models capable of simultaneously understanding text, images, and voice, with the explicit ambition of surpassing OpenAI's GPT-3 in Korean-language performance. Naver provided its supercomputing infrastructure and proprietary data to the joint center, and its researchers served as adjunct professors at SNU, co-supervising graduate students to develop the next generation of Korean AI talent. The partnership included mutual personnel exchange, biannual technology-sharing workshops, and SNU student internship pipelines at Naver. Naver's AI Lab and its CLOVA CIC division (led by heads such as Ha Jung-woo and Jeong Seok-geun) were central participants in this collaboration. Through this partnership and its broader AI R&D investments, Naver is a key shaper of South Korea's national AI competitiveness strategy and the Korean-language AI ecosystem.
2154.regulatory_stance_detail: Naver's public statements and actions emphasize competitive, market-driven AI development aimed at global competitiveness rather than regulatory frameworks. COO Choi In-hyuk framed the SNU partnership as a means to 'compete with global rivals' and lead hyperscale AI research, suggesting a pro-development, industry-led stance. No explicit advocacy for regulation is evident in the sources.

2155.notes: Navitas Capital is an early-stage venture capital firm founded in 2008 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California, focused on transformative technology and innovation, particularly in real estate technology (proptech) and construction technology. The firm has invested in approximately 60 companies and closed its third fund at $160 million in 2024, which was 60% above its initial commitment target. Navitas has been a consistent investor in EliseAI across multiple rounds, including the Series C ($35M), Series D ($75M), and Series E ($250M led by a16z in 2025), making EliseAI one of its marquee AI portfolio companies. Other notable AI-related investments include Document Crunch (AI-powered construction contract compliance), Field Materials (AI for construction procurement), Firmus (preconstruction AI design review and risk analysis), and Paces (GIS/data platform for clean energy). The firm has produced 4 unicorns, 2 IPOs, and 13 acquisitions in its portfolio, including Procore, Matterport, and View. Travis Putnam serves as Managing Partner and co-founder. Navitas shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its investment activity in AI-driven proptech, construction tech, and healthcare automation companies.
2155.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions are stated by Navitas Capital in available sources. As an early-stage VC actively deploying capital into AI-driven startups across construction, real estate, and healthcare, their investment pattern strongly implies a preference for minimal regulatory friction to allow portfolio companies to scale. No evidence of advocacy for AI regulation.

2156.notes: Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ: NBIS) is an Amsterdam-based AI infrastructure company and 'neocloud' provider that emerged from the restructuring of Yandex N.V. in 2024, led by CEO Arkady Volozh. The company builds and operates GPU-intensive AI data centers designed from the ground up for AI workloads, with facilities in Finland, France, and the United States. In September 2025, Nebius signed a $17.4 billion multi-year AI infrastructure supply agreement with Microsoft, including a dedicated 300 MW campus in Vineland, New Jersey. In November 2025, Nebius signed an initial $3 billion deal with Meta, which was dramatically expanded in March 2026 to a total of up to $27 billion over five years, making it one of the largest AI compute contracts ever announced; the deal includes one of the first large-scale deployments of Nvidia's Vera Rubin chip platform. In March 2026, Nvidia made a $2 billion strategic investment in Nebius, granting the company preferred partner status and priority access to next-generation GPU architectures. Nebius has outlined a $16–20 billion capital expenditure plan for 2026, targeting 2.5+ gigawatts of contracted power capacity by late 2026, including a flagship 1.2 GW AI factory campus in Independence, Missouri, and raised $4.3375 billion in convertible senior notes in March 2026 to fund expansion.
2156.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions are stated in available sources. Nebius's strategy — aggressively expanding GPU-as-a-Service capacity, locking in multi-decade contracts with hyperscalers, and racing to deploy compute at gigawatt scale — is strongly consistent with an accelerationist or light-touch regulatory posture that prioritizes speed of deployment over regulatory friction.

2157.notes: Neel Nanda is a Senior Research Scientist and Mechanistic Interpretability Team Lead at Google DeepMind, leading efforts to reverse-engineer neural networks and understand their internal algorithms. He studied Mathematics at Trinity College, Cambridge (graduating 2020), interned at the Future of Humanity Institute, DeepMind, and the Center for Human-Compatible AI, before working as a language model interpretability researcher under Chris Olah at Anthropic (2021–2022), then joining DeepMind's mechanistic interpretability team in 2023. He is the creator of TransformerLens, an open-source library widely used in the interpretability research community (~3,100 GitHub stars, 112+ contributors), and co-author of 'A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits' (2021), a foundational paper in the field. His team produced Gemma Scope (2024), a publicly released collection of over 400 sparse autoencoders trained on Google's Gemma 2 models, enabling external researchers to probe model internals. He received an ICLR 2023 Spotlight for his grokking paper and has accumulated over 13,000 citations with an h-index of 31 as of 2025. Nanda is a mentor in the MATS program and has mentored approximately 50 junior researchers, with 7 placed at major AI companies, and has produced extensive educational content (blog posts, YouTube walkthroughs) to popularize mechanistic interpretability. He was named to MIT Technology Review's Innovators Under 35 list in 2025 and received a $250,000 grant from Manifund in recognition of his leadership of the mech interp team.
2157.regulatory_stance_detail: Nanda explicitly states 'I see the main goal of my work as reducing existential risk from AI' and identifies as part of the Effective Altruism and rationality communities. His MIT Technology Review profile quotes him: 'I see my job as: Do research such that by the time we make human-level AI, it is safe and good for the world.' He frames mech interp as a tool to detect deception, understand concerning behaviours, and monitor deployed systems for harmful behaviour.

2159.notes: New Enterprise Associates (NEA) is a global venture capital firm founded in 1977, making it one of the longest-standing VC firms in the industry. As of December 31, 2024, NEA manages more than $27 billion in assets under management and invests across technology and healthcare sectors at all stages from seed through IPO. The firm has an extensive track record with more than 280 portfolio company IPOs and more than 470 mergers and acquisitions. NEA is deeply embedded in the AI ecosystem through a series of high-profile investments, including co-leading Samaya AI's $43.5M round (2025), leading Veza's $108M Series D (2025), co-leading Twelve Labs' $50M Series A with NVIDIA NVentures (2024), co-leading Orby AI's $30M Series A (2024), and participating in World Labs, Workera ($23.5M Series B, 2023), AI Fund, and Sakana AI. NEA partners active in AI include Scott Sandell, Tiffany Luck, Aaron Jacobson, and Vanessa Larco, who serve on boards of AI portfolio companies. The firm's AI investment strategy spans foundation models, enterprise AI platforms, multimodal AI, identity security, and AI infrastructure, making it a significant capital allocator shaping the direction of AI development.
2159.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found in sources, but NEA's investment pattern — aggressively funding AI startups across foundation models, enterprise AI, and agentic AI without evident advocacy for regulation — strongly suggests a light-touch, pro-innovation stance typical of major Silicon Valley VCs. The firm's consistent deployment of capital into frontier AI companies implies a preference for market-driven development over regulatory constraints.

2160.notes: New Priorities Foundation is a private non-operating foundation headquartered in Berkeley, California, incorporated and granted IRS tax-exempt status in 1997. It holds total assets of approximately $12.3M as of 2023, with annual income of $7.3M. The foundation has made 62 grants totaling $1.8M across its recorded history, with a median grant size of $25K and individual grants ranging from $5K to $100K. It primarily funds organizations in California, Washington, and Montana. The foundation's connection to the AI policy ecosystem is established through a $25,000 grant it made to the Effective Institutions Project (EIP) for an AI Governance Research Project in December 2024, as identified in the database edge context. EIP is a prominent AI governance organization focused on influencing institutions to address transformative AI risks. The foundation's grantmaking peaked in 2022 at $940K across 32 grants and has since decreased to $340K in 2023, suggesting modest but targeted philanthropic activity. Its support for AI governance work through EIP aligns it with the broader ecosystem of funders supporting catastrophic AI risk mitigation.
2160.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from the foundation's grant to the Effective Institutions Project for AI governance research, an organization that advocates for proactive but balanced governance frameworks to address transformative AI risks. No explicit public statements on regulatory stance are available.
2160.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, National security

2161.notes: New Science is a 501(c)(3) research nonprofit incorporated in Massachusetts, founded by Alexey Guzey, a writer and metascience researcher, with Adam Marblestone (Schmidt Futures Innovation Fellow, Federation of American Scientists) on the board. Its core mission is to build new institutions of basic science by funding and supporting independent researchers outside traditional academic structures. The organization runs fellowship programs placing exceptional scientists in labs at Harvard, MIT, UC Berkeley, and the Broad Institute, providing stipends of ~$6,000/month plus project costs and compute credits. Among its 2024 fellows is Theia Vogel, working on multi-agent AI and ML interpretability, as well as fellows in ML/proteomics (Samarth Jajoo, Seyone Chithrananda) and other life sciences. Past grantees include Nora Belrose (featured in Vox Future Perfect 50) and Kevin Liu (joined OpenAI AGI Preparedness team), demonstrating meaningful connections to the AI ecosystem. New Science explicitly targets 'brilliant outliers' doing unconventional research and has funded work at the intersection of ML and biology, including protein folding and interpretability. The organization is donor-funded and led by Alexey Guzey (alexey@newscience.org).
2161.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation found in available sources. New Science's orientation toward fostering independent, unconventional science outside institutional constraints suggests a preference for minimal bureaucratic interference, but no direct policy positions on AI regulation are articulated.

2162.notes: The New Venture Fund (NVF), founded in 2006, is a Washington, D.C.-based 501(c)(3) philanthropic intermediary and fiscal sponsor that incubates and supports progressive nonprofits, donor collaboratives, and pooled funds across issue areas including civil rights, climate, democracy, and global health. It is affiliated with Arabella Advisors and has been described by OpenSecrets, Axios, and The New York Times as a 'dark money' vehicle due to limited disclosure of donor identities and pass-through grant-making. With annual revenue of approximately $669 million (2023) and over $500 million in grants made in 2023 alone, NVF is among the largest fiscal sponsors in U.S. philanthropy, with more than half of the 50 largest U.S. grant-making foundations having funded projects it hosts. Its relevance to the AI policy ecosystem stems from its fiscal sponsorship of the Algorithmic Justice League, a prominent AI bias and civil rights advocacy organization, and the Public Interest Technology Universities Network (PIT-UN), which supports civic-minded technology education across 63 universities. NVF has incubated hundreds of projects, including the Malala Fund and We Mean Business, many of which eventually spin off as independent nonprofits. NVF contracts with Sunflower Services, a nonprofit-owned Public Benefit Corporation, for back-office support including finance, HR, and compliance.
2162.regulatory_stance_detail: NVF does not itself articulate a direct AI regulatory stance, but its fiscal sponsorship of the Algorithmic Justice League—which advocates for AI accountability, bias audits, and civil rights protections in AI systems—suggests alignment with a moderate-to-restrictive posture on AI governance. Its broader mission emphasizes equity and civil rights, which typically correlates with support for targeted AI regulation to address bias and discrimination.

2163.notes: New York State is a major U.S. state government that plays a significant role in shaping the AI and technology ecosystem through funding, regulation, and infrastructure investment. As evidenced by the edge context, the state has committed $300 million to Stony Brook University, which hosts the nation's largest quantum network and conducts pioneering research in quantum communication and computing. The state also funds the NYS Office of Information Technology Services with a pipeline of over 350 projects valued at approximately $1.5 billion, indicating large-scale technology modernization efforts. New York Governor Kathy Hochul officially designated Stony Brook University and the University at Buffalo as SUNY flagship institutions in January 2022, accompanied by a $100 million multidisciplinary engineering building commitment. The state's investment in Stony Brook supports R1 research infrastructure co-managing Brookhaven National Laboratory and contributing an estimated $4.65 billion annual economic impact. New York State has historically been active in technology regulation, including AI-related legislation concerning bias, privacy, and automated decision-making, positioning it as one of the most influential state-level actors in U.S. AI governance. The state's approach reflects a moderate-to-targeted regulatory stance, balancing innovation investment with emerging AI oversight frameworks.
2163.regulatory_stance_detail: New York State has enacted and proposed various AI-related regulations including bias auditing requirements for automated employment decision tools (Local Law 144 in NYC, preceded by state-level discussions), and Governor Hochul has pursued AI governance frameworks. The state simultaneously invests heavily in AI and quantum research infrastructure, suggesting a balanced approach of targeted oversight alongside innovation promotion.

2164.notes: Nicholas Tomlin is an NLP researcher and incoming assistant professor at the Toyota Technological Institute at Chicago (TTIC), currently serving as a faculty fellow at NYU's Center for Data Science. He completed his PhD in 2025 from UC Berkeley EECS, where he was part of the Berkeley NLP Group under the advisement of Dan Klein, and received his undergraduate degree from Brown University in math and linguistics. His research focuses on language model reasoning, multi-agent interaction, decision-making, and explainability of AI systems. He is co-creator of the Berkeley Crossword Solver (ACL 2022), the first computer system to achieve superhuman performance at competitive crossword solving, and Ghostbuster (NAACL 2024), a state-of-the-art method for detecting LLM-generated text. His work on explaining game-playing agents like AlphaGo using natural language annotations is directly relevant to the FAR.AI grant ('Explaining Superhuman AI Decisions'). His research has been supported by grants from NSF and FAR.AI and has received media coverage from WIRED, the BBC, and Discover.
2164.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance found in sources. Tomlin's research focus on explainability, human-AI collaboration, and AI safety suggests awareness of risks, but he has not publicly stated positions on AI regulation.

2165.notes: Nick D'Aloisio (born 1 November 1995) is a British computer programmer and internet entrepreneur best known for founding Summly, a mobile app that used NLP algorithms to automatically summarize news articles. He became the world's youngest venture capital-backed entrepreneur at age 16 when he received $300,000 from Li Ka-Shing's Horizon Ventures in 2011, and later raised $1.23M from celebrity investors including Yoko Ono, Ashton Kutcher, and Rupert Murdoch. Yahoo acquired Summly for approximately $30 million in March 2013, when D'Aloisio was 17, making him one of the youngest self-made millionaires in history. He served as a product manager at Yahoo leading the News Digest app (which won an Apple Design Award in 2014) before leaving in late 2015 to study Philosophy and Computer Science at Oxford. In late 2015 he co-founded Sphere Knowledge, a knowledge-sharing startup that raised $30M from Index Ventures and Mike Moritz, which was acquired by Twitter in October 2021. He completed a BPhil in Philosophy at Oxford in July 2021 and is now pursuing a DPhil, with seven papers accepted in peer-reviewed journals. His work in natural language processing and AI-powered summarization places him at the intersection of AI research and entrepreneurship, and his connection to DeepMind's investor network (via Founders Fund/Peter Thiel ecosystem) further ties him to the broader AI funding landscape.
2165.regulatory_stance_detail: No public statements or discernible positions on AI regulation found in available sources. His career has focused on building and selling AI-powered products rather than policy advocacy.

2166.notes: The Nicolas Berggruen Charitable Trust is the philanthropic vehicle of investor and philanthropist Nicolas Berggruen, serving as the primary funder of the Berggruen Institute. In 2016, the Trust made a landmark $500 million endowment grant to the Berggruen Institute on its fifth anniversary, enabling the institute to expand its global mission and build a permanent Los Angeles headquarters. The Trust is administered through Berggruen Holdings, which functions as the investment vehicle of the Trust. As of 2024, the Berggruen Institute's endowment has grown to approximately $1 billion. Nicolas Berggruen is a signatory of The Giving Pledge, reflecting his commitment to dedicating the majority of his wealth to philanthropy. The Trust's support of the Berggruen Institute directly shapes the AI ecosystem through the Institute's research programs on science, technology, synthetic intelligence, and the governance of transformative technologies. The Institute's work explicitly addresses artificial intelligence through its Futurology podcast, planetary governance frameworks, and research themes bridging philosophy, technology, and policy.
2166.regulatory_stance_detail: The Berggruen Institute, funded by the Trust, focuses on 'renovating democracy' and developing governance frameworks for the age of globalization and digital capitalism. Its research addresses AI governance through a philosophical and institutional lens, suggesting a measured, institution-building approach rather than either acceleration or heavy restriction. The Institute's work on planetary governance and technology implies support for thoughtful regulatory frameworks rather than laissez-faire or prohibitive stances.
2166.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Economic inequality

2167.notes: Noemi Dreksler is a Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI), where she leads the organization's survey research program. She holds a DPhil in Experimental Psychology from the University of Oxford (completed 2020), supervised at the Crossmodal Research Laboratory. Her survey work has spanned multiple influential studies including a large 2019 survey of ML researchers on AI progress and ethics (N=524), a two-wave survey of 500+ local US elected officials in 2022 and 2023 capturing pre- and post-ChatGPT attitudes, a survey of 51 leading AGI safety and governance experts, and public opinion surveys across 11 countries with over 13,000 respondents. She also led development of the AI Survey Hub for Attitudes and Research Exchange (AI SHARE) database aggregating 200+ studies from 2014–2023. She received $231,608 from the AI Risk Mitigation Fund for a two-year project conducting public and expert surveys on AI governance and forecasting. More recently her focus has pivoted to AI risk management practices, particularly frontier AI companies' safety frameworks, including co-authoring work on marginal risk assessment methodologies.
2167.regulatory_stance_detail: Dreksler's research explicitly advocates for pre-deployment risk assessments, dangerous capabilities evaluations, third-party model audits, safety restrictions on model usage, and red teaming for frontier/AGI-level systems. Her survey of 51 experts found 'overwhelming support' for these governance practices. Her work on open-sourcing highly capable foundation models argues that for some highly capable models, risks of open-sourcing may outweigh benefits, suggesting support for targeted restrictions on frontier AI.
2167.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, National security

2170.notes: Nscale is a UK-based AI infrastructure hyperscaler founded by Josh Payne and launched from stealth in May 2024. The company builds vertically integrated AI infrastructure spanning GPU compute, networking, data services, and orchestration software, with data centers across Europe, North America, and Asia. In March 2026, Nscale closed a $2 billion Series C round — described as the largest in European history — led by Aker ASA and 8090 Industries, valuing the company at $14.6 billion; prior rounds include a $155 million Series A in late 2024 and a $1.1 billion Series B in September 2025. Investors include NVIDIA, Citadel, Dell, Jane Street, Lenovo, Nokia, Astra Capital Management, Linden Advisors, and Point72. In October 2025, Nscale signed a major multi-year agreement with Microsoft to deploy approximately 200,000 Nvidia GB300 GPUs across Europe and the US, including establishing the UK's largest Nvidia AI supercomputer at its Loughton AI Campus. The company integrated the Aker Nscale joint venture fully into Nscale as part of the Series C transaction, with Aker becoming the largest shareholder at approximately 27.3% of share capital. Notable board additions in 2026 include Sheryl Sandberg, Susan Decker, and Nick Clegg, signaling a strong push toward governance and global policy credibility.
2170.regulatory_stance_detail: Nscale's public messaging focuses on accelerating AI infrastructure buildout as a competitive imperative for nations and companies, with CEO Josh Payne framing AI infrastructure as 'the foundation that the market sits on, the engine of superintelligence.' The company emphasizes speed of deployment and global scale rather than regulatory caution. However, the company does reference EU data sovereignty requirements in its Microsoft agreement, suggesting pragmatic compliance without advocacy for broader regulation.
2170.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration

2172.notes: The Office of Naval Research (ONR) is a U.S. federal agency within the Department of the Navy that funds and manages science and technology programs for naval warfare. The '-MA' suffix in Princeton's collaboration system likely designates a specific program office or grant management code. ONR has been a significant funder of AI and cognitive science research, including a 2023-2026 grant of $750,000 to Tom Griffiths at Princeton for 'Instantiating human inductive biases in machines via metalearning' (grant N00014-23-1-2510). ONR also funded earlier Griffiths work on related AI/cognitive science topics (e.g., grant N00014-18-1-2873 and N00014-13-1-0041). This research directly shapes AI by funding work on how human inductive biases can be instilled in machine learning systems via metalearning and Bayesian approaches. ONR's AI investments span foundational machine learning, human-machine teaming, and cognitive science with national security relevance. ONR has historically been one of the most important funders of foundational AI and cognitive science research in the United States, with roots going back to early computing and AI funding in the postwar era.
2172.regulatory_stance_detail: As a U.S. federal military research agency, ONR does not take public stances on AI regulation per se; its mission is to fund research serving national security. Its funding priorities reflect a preference for advancing AI capabilities, particularly in areas relevant to defense, but it operates within federal oversight frameworks.
2172.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Loss of control

2174.notes: The Okawa Foundation for Information and Telecommunications is a Japanese public foundation established by information technology pioneer Isao Okawa, with a mission to promote and develop the field of information and communications technology. It operates two major programs: the Okawa Prize (a prestigious award with a ¥10 million cash component recognizing outstanding contributions to research, technological development, and business in information and telecommunications) and the Okawa Research Grant, awarded annually to early-career researchers in the United States. The Research Grant has supported numerous AI-relevant researchers at top U.S. universities, including Anca Dragan (robotics/AI safety at UC Berkeley), Chelsea Finn (2023, generality and dexterity in visuomotor control at Stanford), Dawn Song (2008, computer security at UC Berkeley), Amanda Coston (contextual evaluation of AI at UC Berkeley), Azalia Mirhoseini (self-improving AI systems at Stanford), and several others working on LLMs, diffusion models, and AI reasoning. The foundation has been active for over 35 years and consistently funds assistant professors at leading U.S. research universities working on cutting-edge AI and computing topics. Its selection committee includes prominent academics from Waseda University, Osaka University, Carnegie Mellon, and other institutions. Through its grant and prize programs, the foundation meaningfully shapes the U.S. AI research ecosystem by providing early-career funding to researchers working on AI safety, machine learning, and AI systems.
2174.regulatory_stance_detail: The foundation does not appear to take explicit public positions on AI regulation. Its activities are focused on research promotion and awards rather than policy advocacy, making its regulatory stance unclear from available sources.

2175.notes: Okta, Inc. (NASDAQ: OKTA) is the world's leading independent identity management platform, providing authentication, authorization, single sign-on (SSO), and multi-factor authentication (MFA) solutions to enterprises globally. The company describes itself as 'The World's Identity Company' and explicitly positions its technology as securing AI, machine, and human identities. Okta Ventures, the company's investment arm, invests in companies focused on identity, security, and privacy, including workload IAM provider Aembit (2023) and verifiable credentials provider Accredify (2024), directly shaping the AI and cybersecurity ecosystem. Through its philanthropic arm, Okta for Good, the company has committed over $10 million through a donor-advised fund held at Tides Foundation, including a $100,000 grant to UC Berkeley's Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity (CLTC) in June 2023 to support the Cybersecurity Futures 2030 project identifying emerging cybersecurity trends. Okta launched a $1M+ Nonprofit Cybersecurity Portfolio in June 2022 to improve security across the social sector, and in October 2023 announced $1.6 million in philanthropic grants for cybersecurity workforce development targeting underrepresented communities. The company acquired Auth0 in May 2021, expanding its identity platform significantly. Okta's identity infrastructure is foundational to AI deployments, as organizations securing AI agents, users, and workflows depend on its platform.
2175.regulatory_stance_detail: Okta positions itself as a proactive partner in cybersecurity governance, funding cross-sector dialogue on emerging cybersecurity risks via CLTC's Cybersecurity Futures 2030 project and supporting nonprofit and civil society cybersecurity resilience. Their philanthropic investments suggest support for collaborative, multi-stakeholder approaches to security governance rather than purely market-driven solutions, though no explicit public stance on AI-specific legislation is evident from sources.

2176.notes: Omidyar Network is a philanthropic organization founded in 2004, with a mission to 'bend the arc of the digital revolution toward shared power, prosperity, and possibility.' As of their most recent reporting, they have committed more than $1.94 billion to initiatives aligned with their vision. They are a significant funder across the AI policy ecosystem, having supported organizations including the Center for Humane Technology, MIT Media Lab, Encode Justice, and Americans for Responsible Innovation. Since 2023, they have run a Reporters in Residence program that provides freelance journalists with $50,000 stipends to pursue independent reporting on technology, power, and society — including AI governance, labor displacement, and algorithmic bias. They have also launched a Tech Journalism Fund providing $5,000–$25,000 grants for resource-intensive tech reporting projects. Their thematic focus includes scrutinizing concentration of power in AI governance, tech company influence on policy, and the societal impacts of algorithmic systems in housing, education, and criminal justice. Omidyar Network operates as a hybrid foundation and LLC, with programmatic work supported entirely via those internal structures.
2176.regulatory_stance_detail: Omidyar Network funds organizations like Americans for Responsible Innovation and Center for Humane Technology that advocate for meaningful AI oversight and consumer protections. Their journalism program explicitly focuses on 'concentration of power in AI governance,' whether public participation exists in AI decisions, and accountability structures governing technology — signaling support for regulatory frameworks that check corporate power rather than either accelerating development or imposing precautionary pauses.
2176.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

2177.notes: The Ontario Early Researcher Awards (ERA) is a competitive provincial grant program administered by the Ontario Ministry of Research and Innovation (later Ministry of Colleges and Universities) that funds early-career researchers at publicly funded Ontario institutions to build research teams. Each award provides up to $100,000 from the provincial government, matched by $50,000 from the recipient's institution. The program was established as part of Ontario's broader research and innovation strategy; an early round was announced in November 2006, supporting 104 researchers at 22 institutions with a $51 million, five-year commitment. Eligibility requires being a full-time faculty member or principal investigator within the early stages of an academic career. The program is relevant to the AI ecosystem because it has funded researchers in computer science, AI, and related fields — for example, Kate Larson (University of Waterloo, multiagent systems and AI) received an award in 2006, as did Sheila McIllraith (University of Toronto, AI/knowledge representation) and Nikolaos Koudas (U of T Scarborough, data management). The program continues to operate as of 2025, with updated eligibility criteria requiring degree completion on or after January 1, 2015.
2177.regulatory_stance_detail: As a provincial government grant program, the ERA does not have an explicit AI regulatory stance. Its design reflects a pro-investment, innovation-oriented posture — funding early-career researchers to retain talent in Ontario — but it does not directly articulate positions on AI governance or regulation.

2178.notes: Open Society Foundations (OSF) is a global philanthropic network founded by George Soros that funds a wide range of human rights, democracy, and justice initiatives. OSF shapes the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its grantmaking to organizations that analyze algorithmic systems, civil liberties, and AI's impact on marginalized communities. It is a major funder of the Human Rights Data Analysis Group (HRDAG), which studies predictive algorithms in the US criminal justice system, and funds the ACLU, a leading civil liberties organization active in AI-related litigation and advocacy. OSF also contributed $250,000 or more to the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS), a major foreign and security policy think tank with growing AI policy work. OSF's New Executives Fund has directly supported HRDAG leadership for expanding work on police violence data analytics, demonstrating targeted investment in AI accountability work. The foundation's focus on open societies, democratic norms, and human rights positions it as a key funder of AI ethics and bias research, particularly around surveillance, criminal justice algorithms, and civil liberties. OSF has historically been one of the largest private philanthropic funders of civil society organizations globally, giving it substantial indirect influence over the AI policy debate.
2178.regulatory_stance_detail: OSF's funding portfolio strongly favors organizations advocating for civil liberties protections, algorithmic accountability, and scrutiny of AI in criminal justice and surveillance contexts (e.g., HRDAG, ACLU). This pattern indicates support for strong regulatory frameworks around high-risk AI uses, particularly those affecting vulnerable populations, though OSF has not published a singular explicit AI regulation position statement.

2179.notes: Oracle Corporation is a multinational enterprise software and cloud infrastructure company (NYSE: ORCL) that has become a significant player in the AI ecosystem through strategic investments and cloud compute partnerships. In June 2023, Oracle participated as a strategic investor in Cohere's $270 million Series C round, joining NVIDIA, Salesforce Ventures, Inovia Capital, and others to back the enterprise AI platform. Oracle's involvement is strategic rather than purely financial, as Cohere's cloud-agnostic platform can be deployed on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), expanding Oracle's AI compute footprint. Oracle has positioned its cloud infrastructure as a key provider of GPU compute resources for AI workloads, competing with AWS, Azure, and GCP. The company's investment in Cohere reflects its broader strategy to embed AI capabilities into its enterprise software suite and attract AI workloads to OCI. Oracle is considered a 'hyperscaler' in the compute and infrastructure layer that underpins large-scale AI model training and inference.
2179.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is evidenced in these sources, but Oracle's behavior as a strategic investor in AI startups and its positioning as a cloud infrastructure provider suggest a preference for industry-led, market-driven AI development with minimal regulatory friction. Inferred from investment activity and corporate posture.

2180.notes: Oregon State University (OSU) is a major public research university and one of only three institutions in the U.S. designated as a land, sea, space, and sun grant institution, with campuses in Corvallis and Bend, Oregon. OSU is building the Jen-Hsun and Lori Mills Huang Collaborative Innovation Complex (CIC), a $213 million, 143,000-square-foot advanced research and education facility focused on artificial intelligence, materials science, robotics, and semiconductor research, with a groundbreaking ceremony held April 12, 2024. The CIC was catalyzed by a $50 million gift from NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang and his wife Lori (both OSU alumni) announced October 14, 2022, matched by another $50 million from the Wayne and Gladys Valley Foundation, plus $72 million in Oregon state capital bonds. The facility will house one of the nation's most powerful university supercomputers and is expected to open in 2025. OSU's strategic plan targets nearly doubling research expenditures to $600 million (from $367 million in FY2023), with the CIC serving as a centerpiece for AI-driven discovery and industry partnerships, including Oregon's semiconductor sector. The university is also running its 'Believe It: The Campaign for Oregon State University' fundraising campaign seeking $1.75 billion, with over $1 billion already committed as of the campaign's launch.
2180.regulatory_stance_detail: OSU has not made explicit public statements on AI regulation as an institution. Its actions — building a major AI and robotics research complex, partnering with industry (semiconductor, tech), and seeking state and philanthropic funding — suggest a pro-development orientation focused on research and workforce training rather than advocacy for or against regulatory frameworks.
2180.threat_models: National security, Labor displacement, Economic inequality

2182.notes: Overland AI is a defense technology company founded in 2022 and headquartered in Seattle, Washington, specializing in advanced autonomy for uncrewed ground vehicles (UGVs) for U.S. defense and national security applications. The company was founded by CEO Byron Boots, formerly of the University of Washington's Robot Learning Lab, and co-founder and President Stephanie Bonk. In January 2025, Overland raised a $32 million Series A round led by 8VC, with participation from Point72 Ventures, Overmatch Ventures, Shasta Ventures, Ascend, Osage University Partners, and Caprock. In February 2026, the company raised an additional $100 million equity round led by 8VC, including a $20 million venture debt facility from TriplePoint Capital, to scale operations with U.S. Armed Forces. Overland's flagship software product, OverWatch, enables a single operator to coordinate a fleet of autonomous ground vehicles in complex, contested, off-road, and GPS-denied environments. The company partnered with L3Harris Technologies on the Diamondback autonomous ground vehicle, unveiled at the Association of the U.S. Army conference in October 2024. Overland AI shapes the AI ecosystem by developing and deploying AI-driven autonomy software and hardware stacks for military ground systems, competing with players such as Applied Intuition Defense, Forterra, Kodiak Robotics, and Neya Systems.
2182.regulatory_stance_detail: Overland AI's public statements emphasize accelerating deployment of autonomous ground systems to give the U.S. a 'decisive advantage' and 'terrify adversaries,' with no mention of regulatory constraints. Their investor 8VC, which guides their strategic positioning, is associated with a pro-innovation, minimal-regulation stance in defense tech.

2183.notes: Owl Ventures is the world's largest venture capital firm focused exclusively on education technology (EdTech), founded in 2014 and headquartered in San Francisco with additional offices in Boston and London. As of early 2026, the firm manages $2.2B+ AUM across seven funds, having started with a $100M fund in 2015, grown to $1.2B+ AUM by 2021, and surpassed $2B after closing over $1 billion in new funds in January 2022 (including a $640M fifth fund and $270M Opportunity Fund II). The firm invests across all stages from pre-seed through IPO, with initial check sizes typically in the $5M–$50M range, and has backed notable EdTech unicorns including BYJU's, Degreed, MasterClass, Quizlet, Apna, Newsela, Greenlight, and Stash. Owl Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem specifically through its focus on AI-driven learning and workforce development, including investments in Workera (an AI-powered skills assessment platform co-founded by Andrew Ng's AI Fund), Sizzle AI, and Cloudforce (which secured $10M Series A from Owl Ventures and Microsoft for safe, equitable AI in education and healthcare). The firm's investment thesis emphasizes measurable educational outcomes, equity, and global access, with coverage spanning six continents and LP networks including foundations, university endowments, family offices, and sovereign wealth funds. Managing Directors include Ian Chiu, Tom Costin, Amit Patel, and Tory Patterson.
2183.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found in sources, but Owl Ventures' investment focus on AI-driven learning, its backing of companies building 'safe, equitable AI' (e.g., Cloudforce), and its mission-driven framing suggest a preference for industry-led responsible development over heavy regulatory mandates. Their emphasis on outcomes and equity implies acceptance of some guardrails but primarily through market and mission mechanisms.

2184.notes: Parallel is a Paris-based startup founded in 2025 by Paul Lafforgue (CEO, École Polytechnique graduate, former Meta and McKinsey) and Christopher Rydahl (CTO, founder of Hublo, Europe's largest healthcare staffing platform). The company builds AI agents that automate administrative workflows in hospitals — including medical coding, billing, and admissions — by operating as an AI layer on top of existing legacy systems rather than requiring deep integration, enabling deployment in as little as one week. Within less than a year of its $3.5M seed round, Parallel had deployed agents across several dozen public and private hospitals in Europe. On March 19, 2026, the company closed a $20M Series A led by Index Ventures, with participation from Frst, Y Combinator, Hexa, and angels including Arthur Mensch (Mistral AI CEO), Felix Blossier, Quentin de Metz (Pennylane founders), and Vincent Bouvier (Vidal). The company's AI uses Large Language Models and Computer Use Agents to process medical data, improve coding accuracy, and capture appropriate hospital reimbursement revenue. Parallel is a YC alum and plans to expand internationally and broaden its agent portfolio across hospital operations.
2184.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation found in available sources. The company operates in a highly regulated healthcare environment and emphasizes accuracy and compliance in medical coding and billing, suggesting awareness of regulatory constraints, but no formal policy position has been articulated.

2185.notes: Park Foundation is a family foundation headquartered in Ithaca, New York, dedicated to advancing a more just, equitable, and sustainable society both nationally and in the local Ithaca community. The foundation operates multiple grantmaking programs including Democracy, Media, Civic Participation, and Environment. It funded the Center for Humane Technology with $45,000 in general operating support in 2022, which is directly relevant to the AI policy ecosystem through CHT's focus on technology's societal harms and advocacy for tech regulation. The foundation also funds organizations like the Center for Media and Democracy (up to $150,000/year), Project on Government Oversight ($100,000–$295,000/year), Free Speech for People ($75,000–$100,000/year), and Center for Popular Democracy ($50,000–$250,000/year), reflecting a broad commitment to democratic integrity and independent media. Its Democracy Program explicitly aims to challenge powers that threaten independent media and robust democracy, areas increasingly intertwined with AI-driven misinformation and platform accountability. The foundation's grantmaking posture suggests concern about technology's impact on democratic institutions, placing it as an indirect but meaningful funder within the AI governance and accountability ecosystem.
2185.regulatory_stance_detail: Park Foundation's grantmaking pattern—funding the Center for Humane Technology, Center for Media and Democracy, and government oversight organizations—suggests support for robust regulatory oversight of technology platforms and media. Its stated mission to challenge 'powers that threaten an independent media, a robust democracy' aligns with advocacy for stronger accountability mechanisms rather than self-regulation.

2186.notes: The Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI) is a public-private partnership launched on September 23, 2024, by U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken on the margins of the 79th UN General Assembly in New York City. It brings together the U.S. Department of State and eight leading AI companies — Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Google, IBM, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and OpenAI — with a collective commitment of more than $100 million in resources, tools, and funding. The partnership focuses on three pillars: Compute (access to AI models and compute credits), Capacity (building human technical capacity), and Context (expanding local datasets), all aimed at closing the AI access gap in developing countries. The U.S. Department of State committed $10 million in Foreign Assistance and planned an additional $23 million in funding (subject to Congressional approval) to promote responsible AI use and governance globally. Individual company contributions include AWS's $10 million in cloud credits, Google's $120 million Global AI Opportunity Fund, NVIDIA's ~$10 million in free Deep Learning Institute training, and Microsoft's $12 billion in AI infrastructure across Southeast Asia, Africa, and Latin America. The PGIAI is housed under the State Department's Office of the Special Envoy for Critical & Emerging Technology (S/TECH) and complements U.S.-led multilateral AI initiatives including the UN General Assembly's first-ever AI resolution (March 2024) and the Council of Europe's Framework Convention on AI. Three supporting documents — the Risk Management Profile for AI and Human Rights, AI in Global Development Playbook, and Global AI Research Agenda — were developed alongside the partnership to guide the development community.
2186.regulatory_stance_detail: The PGIAI explicitly emphasizes 'safety, security, and trustworthiness' as core commitments alongside AI deployment, and the State Department has supported international governance frameworks including the Council of Europe's Framework Convention on AI and the UN resolution on AI. The partnership promotes responsible AI use and governance globally, reflecting a balanced moderate stance that pursues both AI access expansion and principled safeguards rather than pure acceleration or restrictive control.

2187.notes: The Pasupalak AI Fellowship is a named fellowship at the University of Waterloo's AI Group, established through a $500,000 philanthropic gift from Sam Pasupalak, a Cheriton School of Computer Science alumnus and co-founder of the AI startup Maluuba (acquired by Microsoft in 2017). The fellowship was awarded to AI Group faculty member Kate Larson, a professor in the Cheriton School of Computer Science and Research Scientist at DeepMind, to support her research in areas including AI for Social Good. Pasupalak's broader donation also funded scholarships for the Computational Rhetoric and Robotics labs, Velocity awards for Computer Science Capstone projects, a graduate student start-up award, and the AI Lab renovation. The fellowship reflects Pasupalak's belief in the social value of artificial intelligence and his desire to give back to the institution that shaped his entrepreneurial career. Kate Larson has received recognition including a Province of Ontario Early Researcher Award, the CACS/AIC Outstanding Young Researcher Prize, and a University of Waterloo Research Chair alongside this fellowship. The fellowship directly enables AI research with a social good orientation, placing it within the Canadian AI policy and research ecosystem. It is part of a larger pattern of industry philanthropy channeled into academic AI research at a globally recognized AI hub.
2187.regulatory_stance_detail: No direct regulatory stance is expressed by the fellowship itself. The donor, Sam Pasupalak, has expressed views on the social value of AI but no specific regulatory positions are documented in available sources.

2188.notes: The Patrick J. McGovern Foundation (PJMF) is a major philanthropic organization dedicated to advancing artificial intelligence and data science for public benefit, equity, and sustainability. As of 2025, the Foundation has deployed over $500 million in grants over the past decade, ranking it among the largest global supporters of public-purpose AI. In December 2025, PJMF announced a $75.8 million commitment across 149 grants to organizations working in 13 countries on AI for public purpose, climate resilience, human rights, media, journalism, health equity, and digital literacy. Key grantees in the AI policy ecosystem include AI4ALL ($300,000 in 2025 for youth AI governance storytelling), Black in AI ($300,000 for general operating/capacity building), The Future Society ($170,000), Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), Stanford Digital Economy Lab, ACLU Foundation ($500,000 for AI policy analysis), Center for Democracy & Technology ($500,000), Council on Foreign Relations ($1,000,000 for AI-era governance foresight), and Common Sense Media ($500,000 for youth AI safety). PJMF's grantmaking explicitly targets civic infrastructure, inclusive AI development, AI literacy, and governance frameworks, positioning it as a major force shaping the U.S. and global AI policy ecosystem. Vilas Dhar serves as President of the Foundation.
2188.regulatory_stance_detail: PJMF's grantmaking consistently supports organizations developing AI governance frameworks, civil rights protections, and accountability mechanisms (e.g., CDT AI Governance Lab, ACLU, CAIDP, Common Sense Media youth AI safety). Their 2025 press release explicitly frames the key question as whether AI will 'concentrate power or serve to strengthen public institutions,' suggesting support for structured governance rather than laissez-faire development, but focused on enabling public institutions rather than restrictive regulation.

2189.notes: Pegatron Corporation (stylized PEGΛTRON) is a Taiwanese electronics manufacturer founded January 1, 2008, spun off from Asus on June 1, 2010, specializing in design and manufacturing services (DMS) for computing, communications, and consumer electronics. With revenue of approximately US$40.4 billion (2023) and operations in Taiwan, China, Czech Republic, Mexico, Indonesia, and the US, Pegatron is one of the world's largest contract electronics manufacturers. In October 2025, Pegatron and MIT CSAIL announced a landmark five-year research partnership (2026–2031) focused on developing physically and emotionally intelligent robotic systems, led by CSAIL Director Daniela Rus and Pegatron's Alan Lin. The collaboration spans robotic manipulation, tactile sensing, multimodal perception, adaptive hands, flexible electronic skins, and AI-driven control frameworks, with the goal of deploying robots in precision assembly, collaborative manufacturing, logistics, and assistive robotics. Pegatron's strategic rationale is to gain early access to cutting-edge robotics research and translate it into industrial deployable solutions at scale. The partnership also aims to produce open-source software frameworks and prototype hardware, positioning Pegatron at the intersection of AI research and large-scale manufacturing. Pegatron is also listed as a member of MIT CSAIL's industry affiliates program through the Epoch Foundation's long-standing MIT collaboration network.
2189.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation have been identified from Pegatron. Their actions — investing in a five-year robotics AI research partnership with MIT CSAIL focused on safe, human-collaborative robots — suggest an implicit preference for responsible but accelerated AI development in industrial contexts, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

2190.notes: The Thiel Foundation is a private philanthropic organization founded and chaired by Peter Thiel that supports science, technology, and long-term thinking about the future. Its flagship program, the Thiel Fellowship, was established in 2011 and awards grants to young entrepreneurs to pursue projects outside traditional educational tracks. The grant amount has grown over time — originally $100,000 per fellow, then $200,000, and as of the 2026 class, $250,000 over two years per fellow. The Foundation shapes the AI ecosystem by funding early-stage AI researchers and builders: notable AI-relevant alumni include Chris Olah (mechanistic interpretability researcher at Anthropic), Nicholas Jitkoff, and founders of Anthropic, Cognition, Etched, and other frontier AI companies. Over 300 fellows have been funded since inception, and their companies are collectively worth hundreds of billions of dollars. The Fellowship emphasizes investing in the individual rather than credentials, explicitly positioning itself as an alternative to conventional higher education. The 2026 class includes multiple fellows working on AI, robotics, and compute infrastructure.
2190.regulatory_stance_detail: Peter Thiel, as Chairman, has publicly stated 'The Boomer career tracks no longer lead anywhere worth going,' signaling strong anti-establishment, anti-credentialist ideology. The Foundation's core model — paying young people to skip college and build companies without institutional gatekeeping — reflects a philosophy of minimal regulatory interference and acceleration of innovation outside traditional systems. Thiel has historically been a vocal critic of government overreach and regulatory barriers to technology.

2191.notes: Phil Black is co-founder and General Partner of True Ventures, a Silicon Valley-based early-stage venture capital firm he co-founded in 2005 alongside Jon Callaghan. True Ventures has committed over $3.8 billion across multiple funds, backing more than 1,050 founders across 500+ companies and creating over 85,000 jobs worldwide. The firm invests at seed and Series A stages in technology sectors including AI, robotics, SaaS, consumer tech, climate technologies, and therapeutics, with notable portfolio companies including Fitbit, Peloton, Ring, and Automattic (WordPress). Prior to True Ventures, Black founded Blacksmith Capital, a small angel fund that served as a formative moment in his venture career, and he also held general partner positions for eight years at other firms including Summit Partners. He shapes the AI ecosystem directly through True Ventures investments in AI companies such as Anthropic and Answer.AI, and as an individual investor in Mythos Ventures ($14M round). Black also serves on the boards of StrongDM and Inventables. His investment philosophy emphasizes founder-centric support, risk-taking at the earliest stage, and backing bold, product-driven founders.
2191.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public regulatory statements found in sources, but Black's investment philosophy emphasizes early-stage risk-taking and founder autonomy, and True Ventures' culture is strongly founder-centric. His investments in AI safety-aware companies like Anthropic suggest some awareness of AI risk, but his overall VC orientation is consistent with a light-touch regulatory stance favoring market-driven innovation.

2192.notes: Phylo is an applied AI research lab founded in 2024 by Stanford AI and biology researchers Kexin Huang, Ph.D. and Yuanhao Qu, Ph.D., with the mission to bring agentic intelligence to biomedical scientists. The company emerged from Biomni, an open-source general-purpose AI agent for biomedical research tasks started at Stanford in June 2024, which was adopted by over 7,000 research labs and 4,300+ organizations including 18 of the world's 20 largest pharmaceutical companies. In February 2026, Phylo announced Biomni Lab, described as the first Integrated Biology Environment (IBE), which integrates over 300 databases, software systems, and analytical tools into a single AI-native workspace enabling scientists to plan, author, execute, and collaborate on complex research tasks using agentic AI. Phylo raised a $13.5 million seed round co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Menlo Ventures' Anthology Fund with Anthropic, with participation from Zetta, Conviction, SV Angel, and Valkyrie. Scientific advisors include Nobel laureate Carolyn Bertozzi, CRISPR pioneer Feng Zhang, and computational biologist Fabian Theis. A case study with Ginkgo Bioworks demonstrated that Biomni Lab compressed weeks-long workflows to hours for complex cell-painting and transcriptomic analyses.
