Phylo shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier agentic AI systems for biological research, conducting open-source AI research, and partnering with leading pharmaceutical and biotech organizations.
2192.regulatory_stance_detail: Phylo's materials emphasize rapid deployment, open-source research, and pragmatism in getting AI tools into scientists' hands immediately. Their stated core principle is 'pragmatism' and immediate usefulness, with a focus on fast innovation. They describe deploying 'in a responsible way' but show no advocacy for regulatory frameworks, suggesting a light-touch or self-governance orientation inferred from their actions and framing.

2193.notes: Pivotal Research is a London-based AI safety fellowship program that identifies and enables early-career researchers to work on critical questions in AI safety, AI governance, AIxBio, and technical AI governance. The organization runs 9-week, fully funded, in-person research fellowships twice per year (Q1 and Q3), hosted at the London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA). Fellows receive stipends ranging from £5,000 to £8,000 plus meals, travel, accommodation, and compute support, along with direct mentorship from established AI safety researchers. The program is funded in part by Good Ventures (a philanthropic fund associated with the effective altruism ecosystem), and has supported fellows including Renan Araujo. Past fellows have gone on to top AI safety organizations, PhD programs, and founded impactful initiatives, indicating meaningful pipeline influence within the AI safety research community. The fellowship covers technical AI safety, AI governance and policy, and AI-Bio research areas, positioning it as a talent-development and research-acceleration organization within the broader AI safety ecosystem. Its co-location with LISA and funding through EA-aligned philanthropy situates it firmly in the effective altruism-influenced AI safety community.
2193.regulatory_stance_detail: Pivotal Research's explicit focus on AI safety, AI governance, and biosecurity risks associated with AI strongly implies a precautionary stance—the organization exists to accelerate safety research and governance capacity because current AI development trajectories are seen as insufficiently safe. This is inferred from their mission and research focus rather than explicit policy statements.
2193.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons

2194.notes: Playground Global is an early-stage venture capital firm investing in deep technology companies at the intersection of science and engineering. The firm focuses on pioneering solutions in artificial intelligence, automation, infrastructure, life sciences, decarbonization, and next-generation compute. Playground Global co-led Agility Robotics' $20 million funding round in October 2020 alongside DCVC, joined by TDK Ventures, MFV Partners, ITIC, Sony Innovation Fund, and Safar Partners. The firm then co-led Agility Robotics' $150 million Series B round in April 2022, again alongside DCVC, with the Amazon Industrial Innovation Fund also participating. Bruce Leak, a General Partner at Playground Global, serves on Agility Robotics' board of directors and has been involved since the company's earliest days. Playground's investment thesis emphasizes complex, difficult technical challenges and leverages deep technical and operational expertise to help founders achieve breakthroughs needed to build transformational businesses. Their consistent co-investment with DCVC in robotics and AI-adjacent technologies signals a strong focus on automation and physical AI applications.
2194.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is stated in available sources. However, Playground's investment strategy emphasizing rapid deep-tech innovation, automation, and scaling transformational businesses suggests a preference for light-touch regulation that allows frontier technology companies to develop and deploy quickly. This is inferred from their investment actions rather than explicit public statements.

2195.notes: Plural Platform is a European early-stage venture capital firm launched in June 2022 by a group of prominent tech founders including Ian Hogarth (Songkick co-founder), Taavet Hinrikus (Wise co-founder), Sten Tamkivi (Teleport co-founder), and Khaled Heloui (former Bigpoint CEO). The fund launched with a €250 million vehicle (Plural Platform SCSp RAIF) focused on backing early-stage European tech companies with global ambitions, making investments typically between €1 million and €10 million per deal. Its model is distinctive in that all investors are exclusively former founders and operators, emphasizing 'hands-on' support drawing on direct company-building experience. Plural is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem through its investment in AI safety and alignment companies, most notably its lead role in Conjecture's $5 million seed round in November 2022 — Conjecture being a prominent AI safety research organization. Its investment areas include Artificial Intelligence alongside fintech, digital media, and e-commerce. The fund's portfolio also includes companies like Isometric, Metaview, and CoMind, reflecting a broad technology focus with AI as a key vertical. Ian Hogarth, one of Plural's co-founders, is a well-known AI governance voice in the UK and later chaired the UK AI Safety Institute, lending the fund indirect influence in AI policy circles.
2195.regulatory_stance_detail: Plural's investment in Conjecture, an AI safety-focused organization, signals alignment with responsible AI development. Co-founder Ian Hogarth has publicly written about AI risk and later chaired the UK AI Safety Institute, suggesting the fund's leadership leans toward measured, safety-conscious governance rather than pure acceleration or heavy restriction.
2195.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2196.notes: Polar Semiconductor is a U.S.-based semiconductor manufacturer headquartered in Bloomington, Minnesota, with over 60 years of experience fabricating power semiconductor devices and sensors for automotive, consumer, and industrial markets. The company specializes in sensor, power, and high-voltage wafer production, operating a 310,000-square-foot facility producing approximately 20,000 200mm silicon wafers per month. In May 2024, Polar became the first award recipient under the CHIPS Incentives Program's Funding Opportunity for Commercial Fabrication Facilities, receiving up to $123 million in direct federal funding (increased from initial $120M following due diligence completed September 24, 2024) under the CHIPS and Science Act from the U.S. Department of Commerce, plus $75 million from Minnesota's Forward Fund. As part of the deal, Niobrara Capital and Prysm Capital led a $175 million equity investment, converting Polar from a majority foreign-owned company (previously 70% owned by Japan's Sanken Electric and 30% by Allegro MicroSystems) to a majority U.S.-owned commercial foundry. The total $525 million expansion project aims to double Polar's production capacity to ~40,000 wafers/month, introduce new automation and AI capabilities, and create over 160 new jobs. Polar leads the Minnesota CHIPS Coalition Workforce Partnership, collaborating with over 70 organizations including semiconductor companies, educational institutions, labor organizations, and state/local governments, and received a $3 million dedicated CHIPS award portion for workforce development. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by providing critical semiconductor infrastructure — sensor and power chips — used in aerospace, automotive, defense, and industrial AI applications, and by incorporating AI/automation technologies into its manufacturing modernization.
2196.regulatory_stance_detail: Polar Semiconductor has actively engaged with and benefited from federal industrial policy under the CHIPS and Science Act, suggesting support for government-led semiconductor investment and supply chain security. No explicit public statements on AI regulation policy have been identified; their engagement is primarily around manufacturing incentives rather than AI governance frameworks.

2198.notes: Prairies Economic Development Canada (PrairiesCan) is a Canadian federal government agency established in 2021, headquartered in Edmonton, Alberta, with approximately 241 employees. Its mandate is to diversify and strengthen the economies of Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba through investments in business, innovation, and community economic development. PrairiesCan shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its Regional Innovation Ecosystems (RIE) program, most notably a $3.125 million investment in Amii (Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute) in May 2024 to support AI adoption among up to 30 Prairie-based SMEs over three years. The agency operates multiple funding programs including the Business Scale-up and Productivity (BSP) program, Community Economic Development and Diversification (CEDD), and the Regional Defence Investment Initiative (RDII), a $379.2 million national program delivered by seven Regional Development Agencies. PrairiesCan has committed $200 million over three years through the Prairie Partnership Initiative (PPI), launched in December 2025, and manages a $48.2 million RDII budget for the Prairie provinces to strengthen defence industrial capacity including AI-adjacent technologies such as autonomous drone systems. Minister Eleanor Olszewski currently oversees the agency, with previous Minister Dan Vandal having announced the Amii AI investment in 2024. The agency's AI-related funding is aligned with Canada's 2024 federal budget commitment of $2.4 billion to maintain Canada's competitive edge in AI research and boost SME AI adoption.
2198.regulatory_stance_detail: PrairiesCan operates within Canada's broader federal AI policy framework and directs funding toward responsible AI adoption among SMEs. Its investment in Amii explicitly references Canada's 2024 budget AI commitments and emphasizes guided AI integration, suggesting a balanced, government-directed approach to AI development rather than laissez-faire or heavily restrictive postures.
2198.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

2199.notes: Prime Intellect is a decentralized AI infrastructure and research company founded in 2023 by Vincent Weisser and Johannes Hagemann, focused on building the 'Open Superintelligence Stack' for globally distributed AI training. The company raised $5.5M in seed funding in April 2024 co-led by CoinFund and Distributed Global, followed by a $15M Series A led by Founders Fund with participation from Andrej Karpathy, Clem Delangue (Hugging Face), Tri Dao (Together.AI), and others, bringing total funding to over $20M. Prime Intellect developed PRIME, a fault-tolerant distributed training framework using DiLoCo algorithms that reduces communication bandwidth by 400x compared to traditional data-parallel training. Their landmark achievements include INTELLECT-1 (the first 10B parameter model trained across globally distributed infrastructure, released late 2024), INTELLECT-2 (the first 32B parameter model trained via globally distributed reinforcement learning, May 2025), and INTELLECT-3 (a 100B+ parameter Mixture-of-Experts model, November 2025). The company provides compute grants to independent AI researchers—as evidenced by their support of researcher Theia Vogel—and aggregates GPU supply from centralized and decentralized providers globally. Prime Intellect open-sources its models, checkpoints, training frameworks, and datasets, explicitly aiming to prevent consolidation of AI capabilities within a few large organizations.
2199.regulatory_stance_detail: Prime Intellect's mission explicitly frames decentralized, open-source AI development as a counter to centralized control, stating their work 'prevents the consolidation of AI capabilities within a few organizations.' Their platform invites anyone globally to contribute compute and participate in AI training, and their fundraise announcement focuses on democratizing access rather than governance frameworks. No regulatory advocacy positions are stated; their stance is inferred from strong pro-acceleration, anti-centralization rhetoric and open-source-first strategy.

2200.notes: Principles of Intelligence (PrincInt) is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization incorporated in Minnesota (EIN 33-4129337) focused on building scientific foundations for AI safety through interdisciplinary research. It evolved from PIBBSS (Principles of Intelligent Behavior in Biological and Social Systems), co-founded in late 2021 by Nora Ammann, Tushita (TJ) Jha, and Anna Gajdova, and rebranded as Principles of Intelligence as the umbrella organization expanded. Its flagship program is the PIBBSS Summer Research Fellowship, which recruits researchers from ecology, neuroscience, economics, physics, and other disciplines to work on AI safety problems; since 2025 it has added a dedicated cooperative AI track funded by the Cooperative AI Foundation. Beyond the fellowship, PrincInt runs an Affiliateship program (6-12 months of funding for researchers to develop early-stage ideas into prototypes that can spin off into new organizations) and a Horizon Scanning initiative that proactively identifies novel research directions and senior academic talent for AI safety. The organization is funded by Open Philanthropy, Survival and Flourishing Fund, AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund, and the Cooperative AI Foundation. Alumni have gone on to found organizations (e.g., Simplex), join Anthropic and Epoch AI, and work at the UK AI Security Institute. PrincInt aspires to become a 'Bell Labs of AI Safety,' hosting multiple interdisciplinary research threads and catalyzing a 'Big Science of AI Safety.'
2200.regulatory_stance_detail: PrincInt does not explicitly publish policy positions, but research outputs include work on AI tort liability reform (proposing strict liability, mandatory insurance, and pre-announced liability rules for AI developers) and analysis of AI safety as an emerging scientific paradigm. Their orientation is toward building rigorous scientific and institutional foundations for safe AI rather than advocating for specific regulatory frameworks, suggesting a moderate stance focused on grounding governance in sound science.
2200.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2201.notes:
Public First is a super PAC (FEC ID C00930503) that is part of the broader Public First network founded by former Democratic Congressman Brad Carson and former Republican Congressman Chris Stewart, formed in 2025 to advocate for responsible AI regulation. As of March 2026, the super PAC has raised approximately $1.5 million, with top donors including Michael Cohen ($500,000) and Public First Action ($1 million). The PAC has disbursed funds to aligned PACs including Jobs and Democracy PAC ($740,000) and Defending Our Values PAC ($300,000). It operates as part of a network of three PACs—Public First (bipartisan), Jobs and Democracy (Democratic candidates), and Defending Our Values (Republican candidates)—all supporting candidates who favor AI safety regulation and export controls. The broader Public First Action 501(c)(4) received a $20 million donation from Anthropic in February 2026, with overall network fundraising goals of $50–$75 million for the 2026 midterms. The network directly opposes the pro-deregulation 'Leading the Future' super PAC funded by Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, and Joe Lonsdale.

[Note: This is Public First (research/polling firm). Public First Action (political advocacy, #1439) is a separate entity.]
2201.regulatory_stance_detail: Public First explicitly supports targeted AI regulation including export controls on advanced AI chips, transparency requirements for frontier AI labs, child online safety (KOSA), and mitigating AI-enabled bioweapons and cyberattacks. It opposes federal preemption of state AI laws without adequate federal safeguards, and supports minimum safety standards to prevent a race to the bottom among developers. Co-founder Brad Carson stated: 'We have $50 million and 85% of the public sentiment' in support of responsible AI governance.

2202.notes: The Public Interest Tech Lab (PITL) is a research initiative launched in June 2021 at Harvard Kennedy School's Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, founded and directed by Dr. Latanya Sweeney, the Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology at Harvard. It was established with a $3 million grant from the Ford Foundation. The lab's mission is to ensure technology products are socially responsible, focusing on ways technology can serve—rather than harm—people and society, with particular emphasis on data privacy, algorithmic fairness, and equity. It operates across three pillars: creating and providing public-interest technologies, enabling research and development, and sharing knowledge across institutions and disciplines via networks like the Public Interest Technology University Network. The lab grew out of Sweeney's earlier Data Privacy Lab, founded at Carnegie Mellon in 2001 and brought to Harvard in 2011, reflecting decades of work on technology-society clashes. Sweeney, a former Chief Technology Officer at the U.S. Federal Trade Commission and the first Black woman to earn a PhD in Computer Science from MIT, brings a strong regulatory and policy perspective to the lab's work. Research areas include voting, vaccine access, algorithmic discrimination, and other societal challenges with technological implications, shaping both academic discourse and policy on AI and data systems.
2202.regulatory_stance_detail: The lab's core mission is to ensure technology products are 'socially responsible,' framing harms from algorithms and data systems as unresolved societal clashes requiring active intervention. Sweeney's prior role as FTC CTO and her research on algorithmic discrimination and data privacy signal support for robust oversight and accountability requirements, not voluntary self-governance.

2203.notes: Pulse AI is an early-stage AI startup founded in 2024 in San Francisco by CEO Sid Manchkanti and CTO Ritvik Pandey, specializing in AI-powered document understanding and unstructured data extraction. The company's core product is a document extraction engine that parses complex documents—including PDFs, spreadsheets, and images—and converts them into clean, structured datasets suitable for large language models (LLMs) and machine learning pipelines. On February 19, 2025, Pulse announced a $3.9M seed round led by Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross (NFDG), with participation from Y Combinator, Sequoia Capital Scout, Soma Capital, Liquid 2 Ventures, Olive Tree Capital, Tiferes Ventures, and executives from NVIDIA, OpenAI, and Ramp, bringing total funding to approximately $4.4M. The company claims its technology recovers 20–30% of business-critical information lost through legacy OCR and PDF parsing tools. Pulse's platform is used for RAG pipelines by Fortune 100 companies, automated financial due diligence for YC startups, and ML-driven market intelligence for investment firms. The company plans to expand its engineering team and extend capabilities to multimodal formats including audio and video in 2025. At the time of funding, Pulse was a five-person team and had participated in Y Combinator.
2203.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation have been identified for Pulse AI. As an early-stage infrastructure startup backed by accelerationist investors like Daniel Gross and Nat Friedman, a light-touch stance may be inferred, but there is insufficient direct evidence to assign a firm position.

2204.notes: Qatar Investment Authority (QIA) is the sovereign wealth fund of the State of Qatar, founded in 2005 to diversify Qatar's economy beyond hydrocarbon revenues and manage the country's oil and gas surpluses. As of 2025, QIA manages approximately $557 billion in assets, making it one of the world's largest sovereign wealth funds. QIA has become a significant investor in the global AI ecosystem, participating in xAI's initial funding round and its $6 billion Series C in December 2024, investing in Databricks (January 2025), Instabase (January 2025 $100M Series D), and leading a $125 million funding round for AI platform Cresta in November 2024. The fund also co-invested $105 million in Turkish AI platform Insider (May 2023) and led a $196.5 million round for cybersecurity AI company Snyk (December 2022). QIA operates a $1 billion 'Fund of Funds' venture capital program, having already committed nearly $500 million to six VC firms, with eight additional firms under evaluation as of February 2025. QIA maintains a global presence with advisory offices in the United States and Asia Pacific, reflecting its strategy to deploy capital across private equity, public equity, real estate, infrastructure, venture capital, and alternative investments.
2204.regulatory_stance_detail: As a sovereign wealth fund and financial investor, QIA's stated philosophy emphasizes long-term returns and growth, with its head of funds explicitly stating 'QIA is obviously a financial investor, and so we are still expecting a return to be made from these investments. It's not a charity.' QIA's broad investment in AI companies across multiple jurisdictions without apparent concern for regulatory risk suggests a light-touch orientation, though no explicit regulatory policy stance has been published.

2205.notes: The Quantitative Foundation is a Charlottesville-based private philanthropic foundation founded and led by Jaffray Woodriff, a 1991 UVA (McIntire) graduate and pioneer of data-science-driven quantitative investing. The foundation's most notable act was a landmark $120 million grant to the University of Virginia in 2019 to establish UVA's School of Data Science — the largest individual private grant in UVA's history and one that created the first standalone school of data science in the United States. In December 2024, the foundation committed an additional $20 million to UVA to fund construction of a new building adjacent to the School of Data Science, intended to support expanded research, teaching, and entrepreneurial initiatives including the UVA Innovates program. The foundation shapes the AI ecosystem by directly funding the institutional infrastructure for data science and AI education, research, and workforce development. The 2019 gift also supports faculty endowments, student scholarships, and visiting fellows at the School of Data Science. William Foshay serves as executive director of the foundation and represents it publicly. Through its investments in data science education and AI-focused research capacity, the Quantitative Foundation is a significant philanthropic force in the academic AI pipeline.
2205.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation are found in available sources. The foundation's funding priorities emphasize expanding AI/data science capabilities, entrepreneurship, and public-private partnerships, suggesting a broadly pro-development orientation, but no formal regulatory stance has been articulated.

2206.notes: Qube Research & Technologies (QRT) is a quantitative trading and technology firm that applies advanced mathematics, statistics, computer science, and engineering to financial markets. Co-founded by Laurent Laizet (Chief Investment Officer), QRT has made a major philanthropic commitment to academic research through the launch of QRT Labs in 2026, a coordinated multi-university initiative spanning Imperial College London, the University of Cambridge, and the University of Oxford. Through QRT Labs, the firm directly funds more than 70 early-career researchers including PhD/DPhil students and postdoctoral researchers across the three institutions. Research priorities include foundation AI models, agentic systems, high-performance computing, cybersecurity, hardware design, advanced mathematical modelling, and complex systems — areas directly relevant to the future of AI. The initiative is structured to preserve academic independence while fostering sustained collaboration between QRT technologists and academic researchers. QRT's philanthropic investment positions it as a significant funder of AI-relevant fundamental research in the UK academic ecosystem, with potential long-term influence on the pipeline of AI researchers and the direction of foundational AI science.
2206.regulatory_stance_detail: QRT's public statements emphasize 'intellectual freedom,' 'academic independence,' and long-term trust-based collaboration rather than regulatory frameworks. Their investment model focuses on enabling free scientific inquiry without reference to regulatory oversight of AI, suggesting a preference for self-governance and open research.

2207.notes: The Government of Quebec is the provincial government of Quebec, Canada, and plays a direct role in shaping the AI ecosystem through targeted funding and strategic investment in AI research infrastructure. It provided CAD $36 million in funding to Mila (the Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms), one of the world's leading AI research centers, linking research capacity and talent development to the province's economic ambitions. Quebec Minister for the Economy Christopher Skeete was involved in a $19.35 million funding commitment to McGill University, further supporting AI-adjacent research and talent pipelines. The government is also a backer of Inovia Capital, a major Canadian venture fund, through its investing arm, contributing to a $450 million fund that supports AI and tech startups. The Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec (CDPQ), a public institutional investor created by and accountable to the Quebec government in 1965, manages over $517 billion in assets and is a key vehicle for the province's economic development investments. Quebec has positioned itself as a global hub for AI research and talent, with Montreal serving as home to Mila, making the provincial government a meaningful non-U.S. government actor in the global AI ecosystem.
2207.regulatory_stance_detail: Quebec's government has taken a supportive, enabling stance toward AI by investing heavily in research institutions like Mila and universities, suggesting a balanced approach that promotes development while maintaining public oversight through institutions like CDPQ. No explicit restrictive AI regulation positions are evident from the sources, but the government retains a public interest guardian role in its investment frameworks.

2208.notes: QuintessenceLabs was founded in 2008 in Canberra, Australia as the country's first quantum technology company, by Dr. Vikram Sharma (Founder and CEO). The company is a global leader in quantum cybersecurity, offering hardware and software solutions including quantum random number generation (QRNG), encryption key and policy management (KMS), and quantum key distribution (QKD) under product lines such as TSF, qStream, qOptica, qConnect, and qProtect. It is relevant to the AI policy ecosystem primarily through its cybersecurity infrastructure work — particularly its post-quantum cryptographic protections — which directly addresses risks to AI systems and critical government data from quantum computing attacks. The company received an investment from In-Q-Tel (IQT), the U.S. intelligence community's nonprofit venture arm, underscoring its national security relevance. In April 2024, QuintessenceLabs partnered with Carahsoft to distribute quantum-resilient cybersecurity solutions to U.S. government agencies via procurement vehicles including SEWP V, ITES-SW2, and NASPO ValuePoint. In 2025, it secured a $15M investment from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) as part of a $20M funding round, following an earlier A$25M Series B round led by Main Sequence and TELUS Ventures. The company has also been named an inaugural private-sector member of the Quantum Security Alliance (QSA) Strategic Information Sharing Partnership and has been recognized by the World Economic Forum as a Global Innovator.
2208.regulatory_stance_detail: QuintessenceLabs actively aligns its products with U.S. federal mandates such as the Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act (signed December 2022), explicitly marketing solutions as compliant with federal requirements to protect critical infrastructure. The company supports government-driven quantum security standards and partners with public sector distributors, suggesting support for targeted, proactive regulation around quantum-era cybersecurity rather than opposition to regulatory frameworks.

2210.notes: RTX Ventures is the venture capital arm of RTX (formerly Raytheon Technologies, NYSE: RTX), one of the largest aerospace and defense contractors in the world. Led by President and Managing Director Dan (Daniel) Ateya, RTX Ventures invests in early-stage companies focused on transforming aerospace and defense, with portfolio areas including hypersonics, artificial intelligence, machine learning, advanced manufacturing, sustainable aviation, and space. The fund made its first eight investments in 2022, backing companies such as H55 (electric propulsion, October 2022) and Hermeus (hypersonics). In July 2023, RTX Ventures led a $45 million Series A round for Impulse Space, an in-space transportation company. In June 2025, RTX Ventures co-led a $5.5 million seed round for Phoenix Semiconductor, focused on domestically manufactured legacy chips for national security applications. In July 2025, RTX Ventures led a $55 million Series Seed+ round for Radical AI, a NYC-based company building autonomous labs for materials R&D using AI, alongside NVIDIA's NVentures, noa, Eni Next, Infinite Capital, and AlleyCorp. RTX Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its role as a funder of AI-driven defense, materials science, and aerospace startups, leveraging RTX's engineering resources to accelerate portfolio company growth.
2210.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation found in sources. RTX Ventures' investment focus on defense and national security AI applications suggests comfort with government oversight frameworks, but no specific regulatory stance has been articulated publicly.

2211.notes: Rachel Weinberg is the co-founder and lead engineer of Manifund, the charitable funding platform that emerged from Manifold Markets around 2022. She was recruited by Austin Chen (her then-boyfriend, now husband) to build the impact certificate platform for Scott Alexander's ACX Grants program, launching manifund.org within two weeks. Her background includes founding and running the EA group at Tufts University where she studied mathematics (partial degree), and doing operations for small-to-medium EA events. She received a grant from the Long-Term Future Fund for time spent learning web development. At Manifund she serves as the primary engineer building grantmaking infrastructure and also served as a regrantor with a $50k budget specifically to 'dogfood' the platform and improve UX. She co-organized the Manifest forecasting conferences (250 attendees in September 2023, 600 in June 2024). Weinberg created OpenBook, an EA grants database, and has shaped the EA/AI safety funding ecosystem by building transparent, fast-turnaround infrastructure for grants of $5k–$75k, enabling projects that often go on to receive larger grants from major funders like Open Philanthropy.
2211.regulatory_stance_detail: Weinberg has not made explicit regulatory policy statements. Her Manifund profile states she believes 'the most important thing in the world right now is making sure the development of advanced AI goes as well as possible,' and she noted updating toward AI governance being more neglected and tractable, suggesting concern-driven but not specifically regulatory positioning.

2212.notes: Rad AI is a San Francisco-based healthcare AI company founded in 2018 by Jeff Chang, M.D., described as the youngest US radiologist in history, and co-founded with CEO Doktor Gurson. The company pioneered one of the first commercial generative AI products in healthcare, building AI tools that automatically generate radiology reports customized to each radiologist's language and style. Its flagship products include Rad AI Reporting (AI-enabled radiology reporting workflow) and Rad AI Continuity (patient follow-up management), which collectively impact nearly 50 million patients annually. In May 2024, Rad AI closed a $50 million Series B round led by Khosla Ventures, with participation from WiL, ARTIS Ventures, OCV Partners, Kickstart Fund, and Gradient Ventures (Google's AI fund), bringing total funding to over $80 million. The company is used by more than one-third of all US health systems and 9 of the 10 largest US radiology practices, with revenue tripling year-over-year. Rad AI has partnered with Google to use MedLM and cloud infrastructure, and has trained proprietary LLMs on some of the world's largest radiology datasets. The company has been recognized by CB Insights (Digital Health 50, AI 100) and AuntMinnie (Best New Radiology Software 2023, Best New Radiology Vendor 2021), and an Accenture report named it the highest-influence private company in radiology as of December 2023.
2212.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy positions are stated in available sources. The company's focus on reducing errors, improving patient outcomes, and partnering with major health systems suggests comfort operating within existing healthcare regulatory frameworks, but no formal stance on AI regulation has been publicly articulated.

2213.notes: Radical Ventures is a Toronto-based AI-focused venture capital firm founded in 2017 by Jordan Jacobs (co-founder of the Vector Institute for AI and Layer 6, acquired by TD Bank) and co-founders. It was among the first institutional VC funds exclusively dedicated to AI, launching its debut fund around 2019. Radical is best known as the first investor in Cohere, writing its seed check in 2019/2020 before the generative AI boom and continuing to lead subsequent rounds including a co-led US$500M round valuing Cohere at US$6.8B. The firm has also backed World Labs, Waabi Innovation, Untether AI, and Xanadu, among others. In 2024, Radical closed a US$650M early-stage AI fund and separately raised a near-US$800M venture growth fund, reflecting its expansion from seed-stage to growth investing. Partners include Jordan Jacobs, Dominic Barton (former McKinsey Global Managing Partner and Canadian Ambassador to China; current Chairman of Rio Tinto), and Rob Toews (investment partner and Forbes AI columnist). Radical has offices in Toronto and San Francisco with partners in London and New York, making it a globally connected but Canada-rooted AI investment platform.
2213.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public policy positions found in sources, but Radical's framing of AI as a transformational economic force ('AI Eats Software') and its aggressive early-stage investment posture across frontier AI companies suggests a preference for minimal regulatory friction. Their support for companies like Cohere, positioned as competitive with OpenAI and Google DeepMind, implies belief that rapid AI commercialization should proceed with limited regulatory constraint.
2213.threat_models: Economic inequality, National security, Labor displacement

2214.notes: Recraft is an AI-native generative design platform founded in 2022 and headquartered in London, UK. The company was founded by Anna Dorogush Veronika and focuses on enabling creative teams to generate and edit digital illustrations, vector art, icons, and 3D graphics with consistent brand styling. Recraft's proprietary model, Recraft V3 (released late October 2024), topped the Artificial Analysis leaderboard for text-to-image generation models, outperforming competitors including OpenAI's DALL-E and Midjourney. The company raised a $12M Series A in January 2024 led by Khosla Ventures, followed by a $30M Series B in May 2025 led by Accel with participation from Madrona Ventures, Khosla Ventures, Nat Friedman, Elad Gil, and RTP Global, bringing total funding to approximately $42M. As of mid-2025, Recraft has surpassed 4 million users and exceeded $5 million in annual recurring revenue (ARR). The platform shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier image-generation models and providing professional designers with AI-powered tools that directly compete with major generative AI image platforms.
2214.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the available sources. As a VC-backed startup competing in the generative AI design space, the company's actions suggest a pro-growth orientation, but no formal regulatory stance has been articulated.

2215.notes: Recursion Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: RXRX) is a clinical-stage TechBio company founded in 2013 by Chris Gibson, Blake Borgeson, and Dean Li at the University of Utah, focused on using AI and automation to industrialize drug discovery and development. The company operates the Recursion OS, a closed-loop platform combining high-throughput wet-lab biology with machine learning to generate one of the world's largest proprietary biological and chemical datasets (50+ petabytes as of 2025). Recursion completed its IPO on NASDAQ in 2021 and has raised funding across multiple rounds, with Lux Capital leading its Series A in 2017 and a $60M Series B shortly after. The company has built BioHive-2, ranked as the 76th most powerful supercomputer globally (Top500, 2025), developed in partnership with NVIDIA, and has expanded partnerships with Google Cloud and Tempus. In 2023-2024, Recursion acquired Cyclica and Valence to bolster generative AI and digital chemistry capabilities, and announced a combination with Exscientia to advance AI-driven drug industrialization. The company is a prominent example of AI applied to pharmaceutical R&D at scale, with a pipeline of 50+ programs across oncology and rare diseases, and serves as a key builder and deployer of AI in biotech.
2215.regulatory_stance_detail: Recursion's public messaging emphasizes speed, disruption of the status quo, and removing human bias through AI-driven empiricism. The company frames itself as challenging conservative norms in pharma and advocates for industrializing drug discovery through automation and AI with minimal friction. No explicit support for AI regulation has been identified in available sources; their philosophy centers on accelerating innovation.

2216.notes: Renaissance Philanthropy is a philanthropic advisory organization founded in May 2024 by Tom Kalil (CEO) and Kumar Garg (President), both veterans of the Obama-era White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) and Schmidt Futures. The organization was established with founding donations from Eric and Wendy Schmidt, though the exact amount has not been disclosed. Its core mission is to fuel a '21st-century renaissance' by connecting high-net-worth individuals, families, and foundations with transformative science, technology, and innovation projects that fall through the gaps between venture capital and government funding. Renaissance builds time-bound, thesis-driven philanthropic funds, allowing donors to support breakthrough ideas without needing large in-house expert teams. AI is explicitly within its scope, as the organization is affiliated with Schmidt Futures initiatives such as Future House, a 'moonshot' aimed at creating an 'AI Scientist.' Kalil previously played key roles in the $40 billion National Nanotechnology Initiative and the BRAIN Initiative under Clinton and Obama. In its first year (2025 Annual Report), Renaissance reported progress moving from 'proof-of-concept to proof-of-impact,' with funding partners including philanthropists, foundations, and government agencies.
2216.regulatory_stance_detail: Renaissance Philanthropy's mission centers on accelerating science and technology innovation through private philanthropy, explicitly framing declining public R&D investment as an opportunity for philanthropy to step up. Its focus on funding projects that venture capital and government agencies pass over suggests a preference for philanthropic and market-driven solutions over regulatory intervention. No explicit regulatory policy positions are stated.

2217.notes: The MacArthur Foundation Research Network on Opening Governance was a research network convened and organized by the GovLab (NYU) and funded by a $5 million, three-year grant from the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation, with additional support from Google.org, active from 2014 to 2018. Chaired by Beth Simone Noveck and coordinated by Andrew Young, the Network brought together twelve experts in computer science, political science, policy informatics, social psychology, law, and communications — including Sir Tim Berners-Lee, Karim Lakhani, Henry Farrell, and Geoff Mulgan — to study how democratic institutions can be redesigned for greater effectiveness and legitimacy. Its core research agenda, released in May 2015, focused on two strands: data-driven governance (leveraging big, open, and small data to improve public decision-making) and collaborative governance (opening institutions to diverse participation and expertise). The Network conducted empirical research combined with real-world experiments with governments and NGOs, and used Google.org funding to build technology platforms enabling more open problem-solving. Its work is directly relevant to AI policy through its focus on how governments can use data and algorithmic tools responsibly, how open data creates public value, and how institutions should be structured to incorporate technological innovation. The Network produced research publications, a blog, and curriculum development efforts aimed at building capacity for data-driven governance across public institutions.
2217.regulatory_stance_detail: The Network's research agenda supports institutionalizing data-driven and collaborative governance practices, advocating for redesigning public institutions to be more open and evidence-based. It does not oppose data use or technology in government but emphasizes legitimacy, diverse participation, and empirical accountability — suggesting a balanced, structured approach to governing technology rather than either permissive or restrictive extremes.

2218.notes: Respan (formerly Keywords AI) is a San Francisco-based AI observability and evaluation platform founded in 2023 by Andy Li (CEO) and Raymond Huang (CTO), both former UIUC engineering students, who joined Y Combinator's Winter 2024 batch. The company rebranded from Keywords AI to Respan in February 2026, reflecting its pivot from LLM API routing to a full-stack proactive observability platform for AI agents. In March 2026, Respan announced a $5M seed round led by Gradient Ventures, with participation from Y Combinator, Hat-Trick Capital, XIAOXIAO FUND, Antigravity Capital, and Alpen Capital. The platform processes over 1 billion logs and 2 trillion tokens per month, supporting 6.5M+ end users across 100+ AI startup and enterprise customers. Respan's core offering includes three integrated workflows: trace-and-monitor (capturing agent execution trees via spans), evaluate-and-optimize (online and offline LLM evaluations), and prompt-and-gateway (managing prompts and routing traffic through an AI gateway supporting 250+ models). The platform shapes the AI ecosystem by providing the tooling layer that enables AI teams to deploy, monitor, and iteratively improve LLM agents in production, directly influencing how AI systems behave at scale.
2218.regulatory_stance_detail: No public statements or positions on AI regulation found in available sources. The company is focused on developer tooling and has not taken public stances on regulatory matters.

2219.notes: The Responsible Technology Youth Power Fund (RTYPF) is a first-of-its-kind philanthropic grantmaking initiative launched in 2023 to support youth and intergenerationally led 501(c)(3) organizations shaping the responsible technology movement. The fund focuses on issues spanning AI, mental health and well-being, online safety, and climate. Now in its third year, RTYPF has raised over $7 million from 19 co-funders and distributed grants to 33 organizations, with award amounts ranging from $25,000 to $150,000 per organization. In February 2025, RTYPF announced $2.4 million in grants to 18 youth-led organizations working on tech accountability, responsible AI, and online safety. Co-funders include Omidyar Network, Archewell Foundation, Hopelab, Susan Crown Exchange, Arthur M. Blank Family Foundation, Oak Foundation, Pinterest, Pivotal, and others. RTYPF is directly connected to the AI policy ecosystem through its funding of organizations like Encode Justice, a prominent youth-led AI policy advocacy group. The fund's Steering Committee—comprising funders and previous grantees—selects grantees using criteria of Possibility, Creativity, and Equity.
2219.regulatory_stance_detail: RTYPF funds youth-led organizations working on tech accountability, responsible AI, and online safety, suggesting support for meaningful but balanced regulation. Its focus on 'responsible technology' and funding of AI policy advocacy groups like Encode Justice implies a moderate stance favoring targeted safeguards rather than either accelerationism or broad precautionary halts.

2220.notes: The Richard King Mellon Foundation is a major Pittsburgh-based philanthropic foundation with a 74+ year history of supporting science, technology, and regional development in western Pennsylvania. In May 2021, the Foundation approved a $150 million grant to Carnegie Mellon University — the largest single grant in its history — to accelerate CMU's science and technology leadership. The grant was split into two $75 million tranches: one as a lead gift for a new cutting-edge science building on CMU's Oakland campus, and the other to seed the Robotics Innovation Center (RIC) and the Manufacturing Futures Institute at Hazelwood Green, a former riverside steel mill site. Within the $75 million robotics/manufacturing allocation, $45 million serves as the lead grant for the RIC, a planned 150,000 square-foot translational robotics research facility estimated to cost over $100 million total. The RIC is designed to support robotics research, integration, iteration, and commercialization, including pre-incubator space for CMU-affiliated robotics startups. Through these investments, the Foundation is a significant indirect shaper of the AI and robotics research ecosystem, funding infrastructure that supports one of the world's leading robotics and AI universities. The Foundation's focus on Pittsburgh's economic transformation through advanced manufacturing, robotics, and science aligns its philanthropic strategy closely with the future of AI and automation.
2220.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit statements on AI regulation are present in the available sources. The Foundation's actions suggest a pro-development, acceleration-oriented stance through large investments in robotics and AI research infrastructure at CMU, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

2221.notes: The Rockefeller Foundation is one of the world's oldest and largest philanthropic organizations, founded in 1913, with a mission to 'promote the well-being of humanity.' It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through strategic grantmaking, including a $300,000 grant to Black in AI for elevating marginalized talent and instilling equity in the AI field, and a $250,000 grant to RadicalxChange Foundation (2023–2025) focused on governance and technology reform. The Foundation's Big Bets Fellowship programs across the US, Africa, Latin America, and Asia-Pacific explicitly emphasize AI and policy innovation as priority areas, particularly for underserved communities. Its 2026 US Fellowship call specifically highlights 'a strong emphasis on artificial intelligence, policy innovation and data access to strengthen civic institutions, governance, and service delivery for vulnerable communities.' The Foundation operates the Bellagio Center residency program, which convenes global leaders including policymakers, technologists, and artists working on transformative societal challenges. It has a longstanding history of backing leadership development and inter-organizational networks, and its grantmaking reflects concern about economic inequality, racial equity, and the governance of emerging technologies. The Foundation's philanthropy is backed by an endowment and it operates global offices.
2221.regulatory_stance_detail: The Foundation's grantmaking to Black in AI and RadicalxChange, combined with its explicit fellowship focus on 'policy innovation and data access' for AI governance, suggests a moderate stance favoring targeted, equity-oriented AI governance frameworks rather than either permissive or broadly restrictive regulation. It funds organizations working on democratic and equitable AI governance rather than opposing or demanding sweeping regulation.

2222.notes: Ronald D. Sugar is a prominent aerospace and defense executive who served as Chairman and CEO of Northrop Grumman Corporation from 2003 until his retirement in 2010, one of the largest defense contractors in the United States. He holds BS (summa cum laude, 1968), MS (1969), and PhD (1971) degrees in electrical engineering from UCLA, completing his doctorate at age 23. Sugar shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through philanthropic investment in advanced computing education: in 2024 he and his wife Valerie donated $5 million to the USC School of Advanced Computing, and he previously gave $5 million to UCLA Engineering (2016) establishing the Ronald and Valerie Sugar Dean endowment, and $1 million to create an endowed faculty chair at UCLA Samueli (2011). He served as Chairman of the Board of Uber Technologies during a pivotal period of that company's technology development, and sits on the board of Apple Inc., giving him direct influence over major technology and AI-adjacent platforms. He was appointed by the President of the United States to the National Security Telecommunications Advisory Committee, reflecting his national security and technology policy relevance. His membership in the National Academy of Engineering and his governance roles at multiple major corporations position him as a connector and funder in the broader technology and AI ecosystem.
2222.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. His background in defense contracting (Northrop Grumman) and board membership at Apple and Uber suggest familiarity with technology governance, but no specific AI regulatory stance is documented.

2223.notes: Runway AI is a New York-based generative AI company founded in 2018, focused on advancing creative tools for filmmakers, artists, and storytellers through artificial intelligence. The company is best known for its AI-powered video generation models, including Gen-1 (2023) and Gen-2, which were among the first of their kind and gained adoption in the TV and film industry, including use by 'The Late Show' on CBS. Runway was also a co-creator of Stable Diffusion, the widely used open-source image generation model. The company has raised approximately $860 million to date, including a $141M Series C extension with participation from Google, NVIDIA, and Salesforce Ventures, and a $315M Series E led by General Atlantic in February 2026, reaching a $5.3 billion valuation. Investors include NVIDIA, Google, Qatar Investment Authority, Felicis Ventures, Lux Capital, Adobe Ventures, and Fidelity Investments, among others. In March 2026, Runway launched a $10M venture fund (Runway Fund) to back early-stage startups in AI, media, and world simulation, having quietly backed companies like Cartesia, LanceDB, and Tamarind Bio. Runway is actively developing 'world models'—next-generation AI systems capable of understanding and simulating physical reality—with applications targeted at medicine, robotics, and autonomous vehicles.
2223.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory policy positions are stated in available sources. Runway's posture is inferred from its actions: rapid model releases, open ecosystem building (Runway Fund, Builders program), and partnerships with major tech firms suggest a preference for industry-led development with minimal regulatory friction. The company's focus on democratizing creative AI tools aligns with an accelerationist-to-light-touch orientation.

2224.notes: The Russell Sage Foundation (RSF) was established in 1907 by Margaret Olivia Sage 'for the improvement of social and living conditions in the United States' and is the principal American foundation devoted exclusively to social sciences. RSF funds social science research across four main programs: Behavioral Science and Decision Making in Context; the Future of Work; Race, Ethnicity, and Immigration; and Social, Political, and Economic Inequality. It shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its Future of Work program, which funds research on how AI and automation affect jobs, wages, and inequality — including a fellowship supporting Andrew McAfee (MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy) to write a book on AI's economic and social implications, and grants studying AI's impact on labor markets using longitudinal data. RSF also supported the Stanford Digital Economy Lab, which conducts empirical research on AI's labor market effects. RSF publishes books and the peer-reviewed, open-access journal RSF: The Russell Sage Foundation Journal of the Social Sciences, and hosts visiting scholars at its New York headquarters. Notable funded research includes Till von Wachter's study of AI's effects on employment and earnings in California over 20 years, and multiple grants examining technological change, wage transparency, and labor market restructuring. RSF's work contributes directly to national policy discussions on AI-driven economic disruption.
2224.regulatory_stance_detail: RSF does not take explicit regulatory positions itself, but consistently funds research examining the labor market and inequality consequences of AI — implying concern that policy responses may be needed. The foundation's Future of Work program explicitly seeks research on 'the role of public policies on employment, earnings, and job quality,' suggesting a belief that targeted policy intervention is warranted, though RSF does not advocate directly.

2225.notes: Ryan Kidd is the Co-Executive Director of MATS Research (ML Alignment & Theory Scholars Program), a nonprofit AI safety research fellowship and talent pipeline based in Berkeley and London, which he has helped scale from 15 scholars to 175+ fellows and has supported 530+ researchers producing 180+ research publications. He co-founded the London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA) in 2023, described as the largest AI safety hub in Europe. As a Manifund Regrantor, he has awarded approximately $400,000 to AI safety startups and projects, helping seed organizations including Catalyze Impact, Timaeus, and AI Safety Atlas. Kidd holds a PhD in Physics from the University of Queensland and previously conducted AI alignment research at the Stanford Existential Risks Initiative before pivoting to field-building. He advises multiple AI safety organizations including Halcyon Futures, Pivotal Research, AI Safety ANZ, and BASE. He is a prominent voice arguing that AI safety systematically undervalues founders and field-builders relative to researchers, and has published influential essays on this theme on LessWrong. MATS alumni have founded 30+ AI safety organizations and teams, including Apollo Research, Timaeus, and Workshop Labs (acquired by Thinking Machines).
2225.regulatory_stance_detail: Kidd advocates for a 'coordinated, careful AI rollout that preserves human agency and meaning' and is deeply concerned about x-risk and the long-term future. He supports building governance infrastructure (safety orgs, auditing orgs, gov-funded AI safety teams) rather than explicit hard regulation, suggesting a moderate/targeted stance focused on building the ecosystem of safety-oriented institutions rather than mandating restrictions on developers.
2225.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2226.notes: California State University, Sacramento (CSUS), commonly known as Sacramento State or Sac State, is a public university within the California State University system, currently enrolling approximately 31,000 students. In January 2026, the university secured a historic $50 million investment from Meta (led by CEO Mark Zuckerberg) — the largest gift in CSUS history — to transform three excess state-owned buildings on Capitol Mall in downtown Sacramento into a mixed-use academic and affordable housing district. The development plan includes state-of-the-art STEM facilities, a dedicated AI Center to support a new bachelor's degree in AI Business Management, a new School of Public Affairs, affordable student and faculty housing, a mixed-use performing arts space, and a boutique hotel. The project is a partnership between CSUS, the California Department of General Services, and Governor Gavin Newsom's administration, with Meta providing catalyst funding for abatement and demolition of existing structures. University President Dr. Luke Wood has framed the project as positioning Sacramento State as a 'flagship university' and an engine of economic vitality for downtown Sacramento. The AI Center represents the university's direct entry into AI education infrastructure, making it a meaningful node in California's emerging AI talent pipeline.
2226.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public stance on AI regulation is evident from the sources. The university's engagement with AI is primarily through education and infrastructure (AI Center, AI Business Management degree), and its partnership with Meta suggests an innovation-forward orientation, but no formal regulatory position has been stated.

2227.notes: The Safeguarded AI Programme is a £59 million (~$60M USD) R&D initiative housed within the UK's Advanced Research and Invention Agency (ARIA), launched in 2024 and led by Programme Director David 'davidad' Dalrymple. The programme sits within ARIA's 'Mathematics for Safe AI' opportunity space and aims to develop quantitative safety guarantees for AI systems—analogous to rigorous safety standards in nuclear power and passenger aviation. Its core thesis involves constructing a 'gatekeeper' AI that uses scientific world models and mathematical proofs to verify that other AI agents operate only within agreed-upon safety guardrails in a given application (e.g., balancing electricity grids, optimising clinical trials). The programme is divided into three Technical Areas: TA1 (Scaffolding, including theory, backend, HCI, and sociotechnical integration), TA2 (Machine Learning, awarded £18M to a single non-profit entity to develop verifiable ML capabilities), and TA3 (Applications, initially funded at £5.4M across ~10 teams to prototype domain-specific deployments). Notably, ARIA redirected its efforts in late 2024 away from TA2 as originally planned, doubling down on TA1 and pivoting TA3 focus toward cybersecurity. The programme has issued multiple solicitations including a £14.2M call for TA1.2 and TA1.3 software developers (deadline April 2025) and a £1M Phase 1 for TA2 organizations. The programme represents one of the most substantial government-funded efforts globally focused specifically on formal verification and guaranteed-safe AI methods.
2227.regulatory_stance_detail: The programme does not advocate for restrictive AI regulation per se, but aims to develop the technical safety standards and quantitative guarantees that would underpin responsible AI governance—analogous to engineering safety standards in aviation and nuclear power. This implies support for a structured, standards-based regulatory framework rather than either laissez-faire or prescriptive mandates.
2227.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity

2228.notes: Sage Bergerson is an independent AI policy and governance researcher and freelance writer based in San Francisco. She holds an MS in machine learning from University College London, where her research focused on speech recognition and natural language processing, and a BS in neuroscience and computer science from NYU, where she was a research fellow with the Machine Learning for Language group. She received a $2,500 stipend from the AI Risk Mitigation Fund (via the Long-Term Future Fund) for a 5-month part-time project collaborating with Epoch, FutureTech (MIT CSAIL), and GovAI on a research paper analyzing the implications of compute access. She previously worked as a Research Data Analyst at Stanford's Knight Initiative Brain Resilience Laboratory, applying deep learning to brain resilience and aging research. She has been active on LessWrong and the AI Alignment Forum since 2021, posting on topics including multi-agent inverse reinforcement learning and interpretability questions. Her work bridges technical ML expertise with AI governance and safety concerns, placing her at the intersection of technical research and policy.
2228.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from her research focus on compute governance in collaboration with GovAI and Epoch, and her engagement with AI safety and alignment topics on LessWrong/Alignment Forum. No explicit regulatory stance statements found, but her work on compute access implications suggests interest in targeted governance mechanisms.

2229.notes: Salesforce Ventures is the corporate venture capital arm of Salesforce (CRM), investing in enterprise software and AI companies. In March 2023, it launched a dedicated $250 million Generative AI Fund focused on funding foundational and enterprise AI companies. Cohere was among the first investments from this new fund, with Salesforce Ventures participating in Cohere's $270 million Series C led by Inovia Capital in June 2023, alongside NVIDIA, Oracle, and others. Salesforce Ventures doubled down on Cohere in a subsequent $450 million round in mid-2024, alongside Nvidia, Cisco, and PSP Investments, at a reported $5 billion valuation. The firm's investment thesis emphasizes enterprise-focused AI companies whose go-to-market approach aligns with Salesforce's own CRM and enterprise software ecosystem. By deploying dedicated generative AI capital and publishing detailed investment rationales on its website, Salesforce Ventures actively shapes the enterprise AI funding landscape and signals strategic priorities for the broader Salesforce platform.
2229.regulatory_stance_detail: Salesforce Ventures' investment posture—rapidly deploying a $250M generative AI fund and backing foundational model companies like Cohere—reflects a preference for accelerating AI development in the enterprise with minimal friction. No explicit advocacy for heavy regulation is evidenced in the sources; the focus is on commercialization and scaling enterprise AI.
2229.threat_models: Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Power concentration

2230.notes: Sally Gao is a community organizer focused on AI safety in New York City. She received a $1,000 Rapid Grant from BlueDot Impact to run AI Safety meetups in NYC, as disclosed in BlueDot's April 2026 blog post about their $50,000 rapid grants program. Her events have hosted notable guests such as Alex Bores, a New York State Assemblymember active in AI policy. One of her events, titled 'The State of AI Safety,' was scheduled for April 23rd (2026). Her work is oriented toward field-building, community outreach, and spreading public awareness about AI safety in the New York ecosystem. She is associated with the broader BlueDot Impact community of course participants and alumni engaged in AI safety work. Her activities align with organizations like the AI Safety Awareness Group NYC and related New York AI safety community infrastructure.
2230.regulatory_stance_detail: Inferred from her organizing work: she runs AI safety awareness meetups aimed at educating the public and connecting stakeholders on AI governance, suggesting support for engaged, informed oversight of AI rather than opposition to regulation or extreme precautionary positions.
2230.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

2231.notes: Samuel Benjamin Bankman-Fried (born March 6, 1992) is the founder of FTX Trading Ltd., a cryptocurrency derivatives exchange, and co-founder of Alameda Research, a quantitative crypto trading firm. He was a major funder of effective altruism (EA)-aligned organizations working on AI safety and existential risk, including 80,000 Hours and Conjecture, making him a significant financial actor in the AI safety/policy ecosystem. Introduced to effective altruism at MIT around 2012–2013 through Will MacAskill, he pursued an 'earning to give' strategy, first at Jane Street Capital (2014–2017) and then through FTX, donating tens of millions to causes including AI safety, biosecurity, and pandemic preparedness. At his peak in 2021, his net worth reached approximately $26 billion, enabling massive philanthropic influence through the FTX Future Fund, which funded AI safety and longtermist projects. FTX collapsed in November 2022 amid revelations of mismanagement and commingling of customer funds with Alameda Research; Bankman-Fried was arrested in December 2022, convicted on seven counts of fraud, conspiracy, and money laundering in November 2023, and sentenced to 25 years in prison in March 2024, with forfeiture of over $11 billion. His collapse severely disrupted EA-aligned AI safety funding pipelines and tarnished the reputations of organizations he had supported.
2231.regulatory_stance_detail: Bankman-Fried engaged extensively with U.S. Congress on cryptocurrency regulation, generally advocating for frameworks that would legitimize crypto markets without heavy restrictions. His broader EA-aligned philosophy emphasized maximizing impact through market mechanisms rather than government intervention, though he did participate in regulatory discussions suggesting openness to some industry-specific oversight.
2231.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, National security

2233.notes: Samsung Advanced Institute of Technology (SAIT) is Samsung Electronics' dedicated R&D hub focused on cutting-edge future technologies, including artificial intelligence. SAIT hosted Day 1 of the annual Samsung AI Forum 2020 (November 2-3, 2020), bringing together globally renowned AI researchers including Yoshua Bengio, Yann LeCun, and Chelsea Finn. SAIT presents the 'Samsung AI Researcher of the Year' award, with Kyunghyun Cho being one of five inaugural recipients in November 2020. The institute employs Fellows such as Professor Donhee Ham of Harvard University, who conducts research on neuromorphic computing and brain-inspired AI. SAIT's president Dr. Sungwoo Hwang co-chairs the Samsung AI Forum alongside designated 'Samsung AI Professors' such as Yoshua Bengio, reflecting the institute's strategy of partnering with top academic AI researchers worldwide. SAIT's research agenda spans foundational AI, neuromorphic computing, and cutting-edge future technologies relevant to Samsung's device and semiconductor businesses.
2233.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is articulated in the sources. SAIT's public communications focus on advancing AI research and real-world applications, emphasizing human-centered AI and beneficial societal impact, but no specific policy positions on AI regulation are stated.

2234.notes: Samsung Austin Semiconductor, LLC is the U.S.-based subsidiary of Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., responsible for semiconductor fabrication operations in Texas. The company has operated in Texas for approximately 30 years and is currently constructing a major semiconductor fabrication facility in Taylor, Texas, originally announced in November 2021 as a $17 billion investment—the largest foreign direct investment in Texas history. In September 2025, Governor Greg Abbott announced a $250 million Texas Semiconductor Innovation Fund (TSIF) grant to the company for their Taylor facility, part of a broader $4.73 billion new initiative to manufacture 2-nanometer leading-edge logic chips. The Taylor facility, projected to open in 2026, is expected to contribute $26.8 billion to the Central Texas economy and create tens of thousands of jobs. Samsung's total Texas investment stands at approximately $40 billion, including plans for a second chip-manufacturing fab, an advanced packaging facility, and an R&D center in Taylor. The company's production of 2nm chips is directly relevant to AI infrastructure, as these chips are designed to power next-generation technologies including 5G, artificial intelligence, and high-performance computing. Samsung Austin Semiconductor shapes the AI ecosystem primarily as a critical semiconductor manufacturer supplying the compute hardware foundational to AI development.
2234.regulatory_stance_detail: Samsung Austin Semiconductor has actively engaged with state-level incentive programs such as the Texas CHIPS Act and Texas Enterprise Fund, suggesting a preference for government partnership through grants and subsidies rather than regulation. Their public statements emphasize economic growth, job creation, and supply chain security, consistent with a light-touch regulatory orientation focused on enabling semiconductor manufacturing expansion.

2235.notes: Samsung Electronics is a South Korean multinational conglomerate and the world's largest semiconductor manufacturer, uniquely positioned as a leader in both advanced memory and advanced logic chip technologies. In April 2024, the Biden-Harris Administration announced a preliminary memorandum of terms to provide Samsung up to $6.4 billion in direct funding under the CHIPS and Science Act, making it the third-largest CHIPS Act award recipient. Samsung has been manufacturing semiconductors in Texas since 1996, investing $18 billion in its Austin campus over nearly 30 years. In 2021, Samsung announced a minimum $17 billion expansion into Taylor, Texas, and with the CHIPS Act support, total expected investment in the region exceeds $40–45 billion through the end of the decade—one of the largest foreign direct investments for a greenfield project in U.S. history. The CHIPS Act funding supports two chip production facilities, a research center, and an advanced packaging facility in Taylor, Texas, with production expected to begin in 2026, initially at 4-nanometer and eventually scaling to 2-nanometer process nodes. These chips are explicitly cited as essential for AI applications, aerospace, defense, and automotive industries, directly shaping U.S. AI infrastructure capacity. The investment is projected to create over 20,000 jobs and leverage up to $40 million in CHIPS workforce training funding.
2235.regulatory_stance_detail: Samsung has engaged constructively with U.S. government CHIPS Act policy, accepting $6.4 billion in federal funding tied to regulatory and national security requirements. Co-CEO Kyung Kye Hyun emphasized Samsung's role in securing the U.S. semiconductor supply chain for AI chips, suggesting alignment with government-directed industrial policy. However, no explicit public statements on AI regulation specifically have been identified in the available sources.

2236.notes: The San Francisco Compute Company (also known as SF Compute) is an AI infrastructure startup founded in 2023 by Evan Conrad and Alex Gajewski, who pivoted from an AI music startup after struggling to access affordable GPU compute. The company operates as a marketplace and order book for AI computing power, enabling startups, graduate students, and research labs to access pre-training clusters and large supercomputers on a short-term, flexible basis without requiring long-term contracts. Rather than owning GPU clusters, SF Compute manages them on behalf of owners and collects a small fee per transaction, helping heavy GPU users reduce financial exposure by subleasing contracted but unused resources. In July 2024, the company raised $12 million in seed funding led by Altman Capital (Jack Altman), valuing it at approximately $70 million, with additional investors including Terrence Rohan. By September 2024, Macroscopic Ventures participated in a follow-on Early Stage VC round of $15 million. In November 2025, SF Compute raised a further $40 million as reported by the Wall Street Journal. The company shapes the AI ecosystem by democratizing access to compute for smaller players priced out of traditional GPU markets, directly enabling AI model training at scale for organizations that lack the capital or connections to secure long-term contracts with major cloud providers.
2236.regulatory_stance_detail: SF Compute's founders explicitly framed their mission as democratizing compute access, criticizing a market where only 'the holy few' can afford GPU clusters. Their platform model emphasizes market-based solutions (an order book/trading platform) over regulatory intervention, suggesting a preference for market mechanisms and light-touch governance.

2237.notes: Sands Capital is a growth-oriented investment management firm based in Arlington, Virginia, known for long-term equity investing in innovative companies. In the AI policy ecosystem, Sands Capital is notable as an existing investor in Credo AI, a Palo Alto-based AI governance software company. Sands Capital participated in Credo AI's $21 million Series B funding round announced on July 30, 2024, which also included CrimsoNox Capital, Mozilla Ventures, FPV Ventures, Decibel VC, Booz Allen Hamilton, and AI Fund, bringing Credo AI's total funding to $41.3 million. Scott Frederick, Managing Partner at Sands Capital, publicly commented on the investment, emphasizing the importance of responsible AI governance and multi-stakeholder oversight. Frederick's statement highlights Sands Capital's alignment with the responsible AI movement and their view that enterprises urgently need to adopt AI governance frameworks. Their investment in Credo AI — a platform focused on AI risk management, compliance with regulations like the EU AI Act and NIST standards — signals a deliberate strategic interest in AI governance and safety infrastructure.
2237.regulatory_stance_detail: Scott Frederick, Managing Partner at Sands Capital, stated that 'the Responsible AI movement finds its strongest industry advocate in Credo AI, championing holistic governance that integrates multi-stakeholder oversight,' and praised Credo AI's work aligning AI with 'industry-specific standards and regulations, ensuring swift and safe adoption.' This suggests support for structured AI governance frameworks and compliance regimes, consistent with a moderate regulatory stance.

2238.notes: Sapphire Ventures is a global software-focused venture capital firm with more than $10 billion in AUM and offices in Austin, London, Menlo Park, and San Francisco. Founded over two decades ago, the firm focuses on Series B through late-stage enterprise technology investments across the U.S., Europe, and Israel. Since its founding, Sapphire has invested in more than 170 companies globally, resulting in more than 30 public listings and 45 acquisitions. In November 2021, the firm announced its largest fundraise to date, bringing AUM to $8.8 billion with the closing of Sapphire Ventures Fund VI. Sapphire shapes the AI ecosystem through targeted investments in AI-driven companies, including as an existing investor in EliseAI's $250 million Series E round (August 2025), a lead investor in Constructor's $25 million Series B (June 2024), and a participant in Temporal's $300 million Series D at a $5 billion valuation. Its investment strategies span Sapphire Ventures, Sapphire Partners, and Sapphire Sport, complemented by a Portfolio Growth team that provides operational support to portfolio companies. The firm's consistent backing of vertical AI, AI infrastructure, and AI-powered enterprise software positions it as a meaningful capital allocator in the AI ecosystem.
2238.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit policy statements found in sources. As a growth-stage enterprise technology VC actively deploying capital into AI companies across healthcare, housing, and infrastructure, Sapphire's investment behavior strongly implies a preference for a permissive regulatory environment that enables rapid AI commercialization and scaling.

2239.notes: The National Infrastructure Fund, known as 'Infra,' is Saudi Arabia's lead development financing partner for infrastructure, operating as a supervised entity under the National Development Fund. Infra's mandate is to catalyze higher levels of private sector investment to accelerate delivery of infrastructure projects critical to Saudi Arabia's social and economic transformation. In January 2026, at the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting in Davos, Infra signed a Strategic Financing Framework Agreement of up to $1.2 billion with Humain, the PIF-owned national AI company, to fund development of up to 250 MW of hyperscale AI data center capacity. The agreement includes exploration of a joint AI data center investment platform designed to attract global and local institutional investors to further scale Humain's AI strategy. Infra's CEO Esmail Alsallom described the deal as 'an important step in expanding Infra's role of unlocking infrastructure investment opportunities in the Kingdom.' Infra's financing covers infrastructure projects across all sectors with a focus on achieving Vision 2030 goals and supporting the Saudi Green Initiative. Its involvement in AI infrastructure financing places it as a key enabler of Saudi Arabia's ambition to become a global AI and digital economy hub.
2239.regulatory_stance_detail: Infra's actions strongly suggest an accelerationist stance: it is providing up to $1.2 billion in development financing to rapidly scale AI data center capacity in Saudi Arabia, explicitly aiming to deploy compute 'with speed and scale' and attract global institutional capital. No regulatory caution or restriction language appears in any source; all framing is about enabling and expanding AI infrastructure as fast as possible.

2240.notes: Schmidt Sciences is a philanthropic organization founded in 2024 by Eric and Wendy Schmidt, bringing together their science-focused philanthropic efforts previously housed under Schmidt Futures. The organization is led by Dr. Stuart Feldman as President and Chief Scientist and prioritizes research across six centers: AI and Advanced Computing, Astrophysics, Biosciences, Climate, Science Systems, and Space. Its flagship AI program, AI2050, is conceived and co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika, and supports exceptional researchers working on beneficial AI with the central question: 'It's 2050. AI has turned out to be hugely beneficial to society. What happened?' In December 2024, Schmidt Sciences announced its third cohort of 25 AI2050 Fellows — 5 senior fellows and 20 early career fellows — awarding up to $12 million to researchers across global institutions including MIT, Cornell, Stanford, Oxford, and the University of Toronto.
